topics.title: Information Security Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Information Security Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Information Security (InfoSec)**
> **Information Security** is the discipline of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves the processes, policies, tools, and methodologies designed to **prevent, detect, record, and counteract** unauthorized access, alteration, destruction, or disclosure of information — whether in storage, processing, or transit. The primary goal is to preserve the **CIA Triad**: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of data and information systems.

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction for KTU Exam**
> - **Information Security** = Broader concept; protects *information* in *all forms* (digital, physical, verbal).
> - **Cyber Security** = Subset of InfoSec; focuses on protecting *digital assets* in *cyberspace* (networks, computers, servers).
> - **IT Security** = Often used interchangeably with Cyber Security; focuses on protecting *IT infrastructure*.

## 1.2 Why Information Security Matters?

In the modern digital era, information is an **asset** — arguably the most valuable asset of an organization. Loss, theft, or tampering of information can lead to:
- **Financial loss** (banking fraud, ransomware)
- **Reputational damage** (data breaches of public companies)
- **Legal liability** (non-compliance with data protection laws)
- **National security threats** (espionage, critical infrastructure attacks)
- **Personal harm** (identity theft, privacy violations)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Vault Model"

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy: A Bank Vault**
> Imagine a bank vault holding gold bars (your data). **Information Security is the entire system of protection around that vault:**
>
> | Bank Vault Element | Information Security Equivalent |
> |---|---|
> | Thick steel walls & door | **Confidentiality** (only authorized people can access) |
> | Tamper-proof seals & cameras | **Integrity** (no one can alter the gold unnoticed) |
> | Backup power, fireproofing | **Availability** (you can access the gold when needed) |
> | Lock combinations & keys | **Authentication & Access Control** |
> | Vault location secrecy | **Security through obscurity** |
> | Security guards outside | **Network perimeter defense (firewalls)** |
> | Auditors checking records | **Auditing, Logging & Monitoring** |
>
> Just as a bank doesn't rely on a *single* thick door (one security measure), InfoSec uses **defense in depth** — multiple layers of controls.

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology (Must-Know for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Vocabulary**
> - **Asset** — Anything of value to an organization (data, hardware, software, people, reputation).
> - **Threat** — Any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may harm a system or organization (e.g., malware, insider, natural disaster).
> - **Vulnerability** — A weakness in a system that can be exploited (e.g., unpatched software, weak password).
> - **Risk** — The *probability* that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the *impact* of that exploitation.
> - **Control / Countermeasure** — A safeguard or countermeasure put in place to mitigate risk.
> - **Attack** — An intentional act to bypass security controls and cause harm.
> - **Exploit** — The specific technique or tool used to take advantage of a vulnerability.

## 1.5 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Information Security

> [!IMPORTANT] **The CIA Triad (The Three Pillars of InfoSec)**
> The **CIA Triad** is the universally accepted foundational model of information security. *Every* security policy, control, and mechanism ultimately serves to protect one or more of these three properties.

### 1.5.1 Confidentiality
**Definition:** Ensures that information is accessible **only to those authorized** to view it.

- **Plain English:** "Only the right people can see it."
- **Examples:** Encrypted emails, access control lists, need-to-know clearance.
- **Threats to Confidentiality:** Eavesdropping, sniffing, social engineering, shoulder surfing, insider leaks.
- **Controls:** Encryption (AES, RSA), Access Control Lists (ACLs), Two-Factor Authentication (2FA), Data classification.

### 1.5.2 Integrity
**Definition:** Ensures that information remains **accurate, consistent, and unaltered** during storage, processing, or transit — except by authorized parties.

- **Plain English:** "Nobody can secretly change it."
- **Examples:** Bank transaction records not being modified mid-transfer; software updates signed with digital signatures.
- **Threats to Integrity:** Man-in-the-middle attacks, SQL injection, malware, accidental modification.
- **Controls:** Hashing (SHA-256), Digital Signatures, Checksums, Version Control, Audit Trails.

### 1.5.3 Availability
**Definition:** Ensures that information and systems are **accessible to authorized users when needed** — reliably and on time.

- **Plain English:** "It's there when I need it."
- **Examples:** A website being up 99.99% of the time; critical hospital systems functioning during emergencies.
- **Threats to Availability:** Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, ransomware, hardware failure, natural disasters.
- **Controls:** Redundancy, Backups, RAID, Disaster Recovery Plans (DRP), DDoS mitigation, UPS, Failover clusters.

## 1.6 Extended Models of Information Security

While the CIA Triad is the classical model, modern InfoSec uses extended models to address today's complex threats:

> [!NOTE] **Extended Models**
> - **Parkerian Hexad** (Donn Parker, 1998) — Adds **Possession, Authenticity, and Utility** to the CIA Triad.
> - **STRIDE Model** (Microsoft) — **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
> - **Five Pillars of InfoSec (NIST)** — Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (Cybersecurity Framework).

### 1.6.1 The AAA Framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)
Often discussed alongside CIA in KTU exams:
- **Authentication** — *Who are you?* (password, biometrics, OTP)
- **Authorization** — *What can you do?* (read/write/execute permissions)
- **Accounting** — *What did you do?* (logging, audit trails)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Equation — The Heart of InfoSec Decisions

Risk is the central concept driving *all* security decisions. **You cannot eliminate risk entirely; you can only manage it.**

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Fundamental Risk Equation**
>
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$
>
> A more formally rigorous version (used in NIST and ISO 27005):
>
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Probability of Exploitation} \times \text{Value of Asset} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$$
>
> Equivalently:
>
> $$\text{Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)} = \text{Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)} \times \text{Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO)}$$
>
> $$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$$

## 2.2 Breaking Down the Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Identify the Asset
- What are we protecting? *Data, hardware, software, reputation, personnel.*
- Quantify in monetary terms whenever possible. An **Asset Inventory** is the starting point.

### Step 2 — Identify Threats
- A *threat source* could be:
  - **Natural** (flood, earthquake, fire)
  - **Human-intentional** (hacker, insider, nation-state)
  - **Human-unintentional** (accidental deletion, misconfiguration)
  - **Environmental** (power failure, HVAC failure)

### Step 3 — Identify Vulnerabilities
- Weaknesses that threats can exploit: unpatched software, misconfigured firewalls, untrained staff, missing backups.

### Step 4 — Calculate Risk
- Use the formulas in §2.1. **High Probability × High Impact = High Risk** → requires immediate mitigation.

### Step 5 — Apply Controls
- Countermeasures are chosen to **reduce risk to an acceptable level** (the *residual risk*).

> **Residual Risk** = Total Risk − Controls Effectiveness
> Any residual risk that is unacceptable must be **transferred** (insurance) or **avoided** (eliminate the activity).

## 2.3 Types of Security Controls

> [!IMPORTANT] **Control Categories (High-Yield for KTU)**
>
> | Control Type | What It Does | Example | KTU Hint |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Preventive** | Stops an attack *before* it happens | Firewall, encryption, strong passwords | "Prevents" = deterrent + preventive |
> | **Detective** | Identifies an attack *in progress* or after | IDS, antivirus scan, audit logs | "Detects" = detective |
> | **Corrective** | Restores system *after* an attack | Backup restoration, patching, incident response | "Corrects" = corrective |
> | **Deterrent** | Discourages attack attempts | Warning banners, security cameras | Discourages, doesn't fully prevent |
> | **Recovery** | Brings system back to normal | Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP), BCP | Often overlaps with corrective |
> | **Compensating** | Alternative control when primary isn't feasible | Manual log review if SIEM is unavailable | Substitute for a missing control |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Table — Memorize This!**

| Formula / Term | Symbol | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE}$ | $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$ | Monetary (₹ / $) |
| Annual Rate of Occurrence | $\text{ARO}$ | Estimated frequency per year | Unitless (events/year) |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE}$ | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Monetary (₹ / $) |
| Cost-Benefit of Control | $\text{CBA}$ | $\text{ALE(before)} - \text{ALE(after)} - \text{Annual Cost of Control}$ | Monetary (₹ / $) |
| Risk Score | $\text{R}$ | $\text{R} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Often 1–25 scale |
| Exposure Factor | $\text{EF}$ | Fraction of asset lost in one incident | $0 \le \text{EF} \le 1$ |
| Total Risk | $\text{R}_{\text{T}}$ | $\text{R}_{\text{T}} = \text{Threats} \times \text{Vulnerabilities} \times \text{Asset Value}$ |  |
| Residual Risk | $\text{R}_{\text{R}}$ | $\text{R}_{\text{R}} = \text{R}_{\text{T}} - \text{Controls}$ | Always remains non-zero |

> [!TIP] **Critical Exam Note (KTU Valuation)**
> When asked "calculate whether a control is worth implementing," the question almost always wants you to show:
> $$\text{Value of Control} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}$$
> where $\text{ACS}$ = **Annual Cost of the Safeguard**. If this value is **positive**, the control is justified.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Risk Equation and CIA framework are not just textbook concepts — they are industry standards:

- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** — International standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS), built entirely around CIA + risk management.
- **NIST SP 800-53** — U.S. federal standard listing hundreds of security controls mapped to CIA.
- **PCI-DSS** — Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard, explicitly enforces C, I, and A for cardholder data.
- **GDPR / DPDP Act 2023 (India)** — Legal frameworks enforcing confidentiality and integrity of personal data.

> **Engineering takeaway:** When designing *any* system that processes data (IoT, cloud, embedded, web), engineers apply CIA + risk math to decide *what* to protect, *how much*, and *at what cost*.

## 2.6 Categories of Security Attacks (Mapped to CIA)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Attack Classification (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
>
> | Attack Type | Primary CIA Property Violated | Description |
> |---|---|---|
> | Sniffing / Eavesdropping | **Confidentiality** | Interception of data in transit |
> | Phishing / Social Engineering | **Confidentiality** | Tricking users into revealing info |
> | Brute Force / Dictionary | **Confidentiality** | Guessing credentials |
> | Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) | **Confidentiality + Integrity** | Tampering communication between two parties |
> | SQL Injection | **Integrity + Confidentiality** | Modifying DB queries to access/modify data |
> | Buffer Overflow | **Integrity + Availability** | Corrupts memory, may crash system |
> | DoS / DDoS | **Availability** | Overloads system with traffic |
> | Ransomware | **Availability + Confidentiality** | Encrypts data; demands payment |
> | Privilege Escalation | **Confidentiality + Integrity** | Gains unauthorized higher access |
> | Replay Attack | **Integrity** | Re-transmits valid captured data maliciously |
| Spoofing | **Authenticity** | Impersonating another entity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Risk Using the ALE Formula

> [!TIP] **Exam-Style Problem (Worked Solution)**
> A company has a web server valued at **₹ 10,00,000**. A successful SQL injection attack on average destroys **30%** of the data. Historical data shows such attacks occur **4 times per year**. Calculate:
> (a) Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)
> (b) Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)
> (c) Whether a WAF costing ₹ 1,50,000/year (which reduces the attack frequency to once every 2 years and the exposure factor to 10%) is justified.

### Given Data
- Asset Value: $\text{AV} = 10{,}00{,}000$
- Exposure Factor: $\text{EF} = 30\% = 0.30$
- Annual Rate of Occurrence: $\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 4$
- After WAF: $\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 0.5$ (once every 2 years), $\text{EF}_{\text{after}} = 10\% = 0.10$
- Annual Cost of Safeguard: $\text{ACS} = 1{,}50{,}000$

### Part (a) — Single Loss Expectancy

By definition:

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{SLE} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30$$

$$\boxed{\text{SLE} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ 3 Lakh per incident)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Each successful attack causes a one-time loss of ₹ 3,00,000.

### Part (b) — Annual Loss Expectancy (Before WAF)

By definition:

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 4$$

$$\boxed{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ 12 Lakh per year)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Without any WAF, the company expects to lose ₹ 12 Lakh every year due to SQL injection attacks.

### Part (c) — Cost-Benefit Analysis

Step 1 — Compute the new SLE after WAF:

$$\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

Step 2 — Compute the new ALE after WAF:

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 50{,}000$$

Step 3 — Compute Net Savings:

$$\text{Net Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}$$

$$\text{Net Savings} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{Net Savings} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Savings} = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year (positive)}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since **Net Savings > 0**, the WAF is **economically justified**. It saves the company ₹ 10 Lakh per year in expected losses.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Risk Score Using the Qualitative Matrix

Some organizations use a *qualitative* risk matrix instead of monetary calculations. Consider a 5×5 likelihood vs. impact matrix:

> [!NOTE] **Risk Scoring Matrix (Likelihood × Impact)**
>
> | Likelihood \ Impact | Insignificant (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | **Almost Certain (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
> | **Likely (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
> | **Possible (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
> | **Unlikely (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
> | **Rare (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Risk Level Categories:**
- $\text{Score} \in [1, 4] \Rightarrow \text{Low Risk}$ (accept; monitor)
- $\text{Score} \in [5, 12] \Rightarrow \text{Medium Risk}$ (mitigate with controls)
- $\text{Score} \in [13, 25] \Rightarrow \text{High Risk}$ (immediate action; transfer or avoid)

**Worked Example:** A phishing attack has Likelihood = **4 (Likely)** and Impact = **4 (Major)**.

$$\text{Risk Score} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \Rightarrow \text{HIGH RISK}$$

Therefore, the organization must implement immediate controls (anti-phishing training, email filtering, MFA).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Security Models

> [!IMPORTANT] **Compare-and-Contrast Table (Frequently Asked as 14-Mark Question)**

| Aspect | **CIA Triad** | **Parkerian Hexad** | **STRIDE** | **NIST CSF (5 Functions)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Classical (1980s) | Donn Parker (1998) | Microsoft (1999) | NIST (2014/2018) |
| **Number of Elements** | 3 | 6 | 6 | 5 |
| **Focus** | Data protection goals | Data states & atomic properties | Threat classification | Operational lifecycle |
| **Elements** | C, I, A | C, I, A, **Possession, Authenticity, Utility** | **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nfo Disclosure, **D**oS, **E**oP | **I**dentify, **P**rotect, **D**etect, **R**espond, **R**ecover |
| **Best Used For** | General policy | Detailed data-centric analysis | Software/architecture threat modeling | Enterprise risk management framework |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.4 The Defense-in-Depth Concept (Analytical Breakdown)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Defense in Depth — "Onion Model"**
> Defense in depth means layering multiple controls so that if one fails, others still protect the asset.

| Layer | Example Controls | Protects Against |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Policy Layer** | Security policies, standards, procedures | Sets the rules |
| **2. Physical Layer** | Locks, fences, CCTV, biometric doors | Physical theft, tailgating |
| **3. Perimeter Layer** | Firewall, DMZ, IDS/IPS, DDoS scrubber | External attackers, scans |
| **4. Network Layer** | VLANs, NAC, VPN, segmentation | Lateral movement, sniffing |
| **5. Host Layer** | Antivirus, HIDS, OS hardening, patching | Malware, privilege escalation |
| **6. Application Layer** | Secure coding, WAF, input validation, SAST/DAST | SQLi, XSS, CSRF |
| **7. Data Layer** | Encryption (at rest & in transit), DLP, tokenization | Data breach, leakage |
| **8. Human Layer** | Training, awareness, phishing simulations | Social engineering |

**Why this works:** An attacker who bypasses the firewall still has to defeat antivirus, then encryption, then trained users. The probability of breaking *all* layers becomes extremely small.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Information Security]:::root --> B[Confidentiality]:::pillar
    A --> C[Integrity]:::pillar
    A --> D[Availability]:::pillar

    B --> B1[Encryption AES RSA]:::ctrl
    B --> B2[Access Control Lists]:::ctrl
    B --> B3[Two Factor Authentication]:::ctrl

    C --> C1[Hashing SHA 256]:::ctrl
    C --> C2[Digital Signatures]:::ctrl
    C --> C3[Audit Trails]:::ctrl

    D --> D1[Redundancy and Backups]:::ctrl
    D --> D2[DDoS Mitigation]:::ctrl
    D --> D3[Disaster Recovery Plan]:::ctrl

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#111827,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef pillar fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Risk Management Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify Assets]:::step --> P2[Step 2: Identify Threats]:::step
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Identify Vulnerabilities]:::step
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Calculate Risk]:::step
    P4 --> P5{Step 5: Risk Acceptable?}:::decision
    P5 -- No --> P6[Step 6: Select and Apply Controls]:::step
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Monitor and Review]:::step
    P5 -- Yes --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Document Residual Risk]:::step
    P8 --> P1

    classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#111827,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#111827,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Defense in Depth — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    L8[Layer 8: Human Layer - Training and Awareness]:::outer
    L7[Layer 7: Data Layer - Encryption and DLP]:::outer
    L6[Layer 6: Application Layer - WAF and Secure Coding]:::outer
    L5[Layer 5: Host Layer - Antivirus and Patching]:::outer
    L4[Layer 4: Network Layer - VLAN and VPN]:::outer
    L3[Layer 3: Perimeter Layer - Firewall and IDS]:::outer
    L2[Layer 2: Physical Layer - Locks and CCTV]:::outer
    L1[Layer 1: Policy Layer - Standards and Procedures]:::outer
    ASSET[(Protected Asset - Data and Systems)]:::core

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8
    L8 --> ASSET

    classDef outer fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#111827,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,color:#111827,stroke-width:4px
```

## 4.4 Threat–Vulnerability–Risk Relationship (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T[Threat]:::t --> R[Risk]:::r
    V[Vulnerability]:::v --> R
    A[Asset Value]:::a --> R
    R --> C{Controls Reduce Risk}:::c
    C --> RR[Residual Risk]:::rr
    C --> AC[Accept Transfer Avoid]:::ac

    classDef t fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#111827
    classDef v fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#111827
    classDef a fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#111827
    classDef r fill:#1f2937,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef c fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#111827
    classDef rr fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,color:#111827
    classDef ac fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#111827
```

## 4.5 Security Control Classification (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Security Controls]:::root --> P[Preventive]:::cat
    ROOT --> D[Detective]:::cat
    ROOT --> C[Corrective]:::cat
    ROOT --> DT[Deterrent]:::cat
    ROOT --> R[Recovery]:::cat
    ROOT --> CO[Compensating]:::cat

    P --> P1[Firewall]
    P --> P2[Encryption]
    P --> P3[Strong Passwords]

    D --> D1[IDS]
    D --> D2[Antivirus Scan]
    D --> D3[Audit Logs]

    C --> C1[Patch and Update]
    C --> C2[Backup Restoration]

    DT --> DT1[Warning Banners]
    DT --> DT2[Security Cameras]

    R --> R1[DRP Activation]
    R --> R2[BCP Invocation]

    CO --> CO1[Manual Review if SIEM Fails]
    CO --> CO2[Temporary MFA]

    classDef root fill:#0f172a,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef cat fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#064e3b,color:#111827,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1. Define the CIA Triad. Explain any ONE of its components with a real-world example.**
>
> **Model Answer (Expected Key Points – 3 Marks):**
> - The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of Information Security consisting of three core principles: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability**. *(1 mark)*
> - **Confidentiality** ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. Real-world example: An encrypted WhatsApp message can be read only by the sender and intended recipient, preserving confidentiality through end-to-end encryption (AES-256). *(1 mark)*
> - **Integrity** ensures data is not altered in unauthorized ways. Example: Digital signatures on software updates ensure the binary hasn't been tampered with mid-download. *(or any equivalent example)*
> - **Availability** ensures systems are accessible when needed. Example: A bank's online portal being up 24×7 with redundant servers ensures users can transact anytime. *(1 mark)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Practice Set]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk. Give one example of each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Threat:** Any potential cause of an unwanted incident. *Example:* A hacker attempting SQL injection on a web application. *(1 mark)*
> - **Vulnerability:** A weakness that can be exploited. *Example:* A web application that does not sanitize user input in the login form. *(1 mark)*
> - **Risk:** The probability that a threat exploits a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact. *Example:* The likelihood of a successful SQL injection attack on the unpatched login form, resulting in a database breach. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **CIA Triad** of information security. Discuss the threats to each component and the corresponding controls/mechanisms used to protect them. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company owns a database server valued at **₹ 20,00,000**. A ransomware attack, if successful, encrypts **60%** of the data, and historical data shows such attacks occur **3 times per year**. The company is considering buying an EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) tool for **₹ 4,00,000 per year** that will reduce the exposure factor to **15%** and the ARO to **once in 2 years**.
> Calculate the **SLE, ALE (before and after EDR)**, and determine whether purchasing the EDR is economically justified. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — The CIA Triad (7 Marks)

| Component | Definition (1.5 M) | Threat (1.5 M) | Control (1 M) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring information is disclosed only to authorized users. | Eavesdropping, phishing, shoulder surfing, insider leak, password guessing. | Encryption (AES, RSA), Access Control Lists, MFA, data classification. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not modified in an unauthorized or undetected manner. | Man-in-the-Middle, SQL injection, tampering, malware modification. | Hashing (SHA-256), Digital Signatures, version control, audit trails. |
| **Availability** | Ensuring systems and data are accessible to authorized users when required. | DoS/DDoS attacks, ransomware, hardware failure, natural disasters. | Redundancy, backups, DRP, RAID, UPS, DDoS mitigation services. |

*(Award marks based on coverage: definition 1.5 × 3 = 4.5, threats 1.5 × 3 = 4.5, controls 1 × 3 = 3, distributed proportionally.)*

**Conclusion (0.5 M):** The CIA Triad forms the foundation of all information security policies. A robust InfoSec program must address all three pillars; weakening any one compromises the entire security posture.

#### Part (b) — Risk Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\text{AV} = 20{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{EF}_{\text{before}} = 60\% = 0.60$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 3$
- $\text{EF}_{\text{after}} = 15\% = 0.15$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 0.5$
- $\text{ACS} = 4{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1: SLE (one-time loss per incident)**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{before}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 12{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{SLE} = ₹\, 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ per incident}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2: ALE before EDR**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 36{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = ₹\, 36{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3: SLE and ALE after EDR**

$$\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = ₹\, 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Cost-Benefit**

$$\text{Net Benefit} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}$$

$$\text{Net Benefit} = 36{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Benefit} = ₹\, 30{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year (positive)}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since the **Net Benefit is positive (₹ 30.5 Lakh savings per year)**, the EDR tool is **highly recommended and economically justified**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q. (a)** What is **Defense in Depth**? Explain its different layers with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **STRIDE** threat model and **Parkerian Hexad** in detail. Compare their applicability to real-world security analysis. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Defense in Depth (7 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 M):** *Defense in Depth* is a security strategy that employs **multiple overlapping layers of controls** so that the failure of one layer does not lead to compromise of the asset. It is also called the **"Onion Model"** because the asset is at the center, surrounded by concentric protective rings.

**Layers (5 M):**

| Layer | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Policy** | Defines rules & procedures | Acceptable Use Policy, password policy |
| **2. Physical** | Protects physical access | Biometric doors, CCTV, security guards |
| **3. Perimeter** | Filters external traffic | Firewalls, DMZ, IPS, anti-DDoS |
| **4. Network** | Segments internal traffic | VLANs, NAC, 802.1X, VPN |
| **5. Host** | Secures individual devices | Antivirus, HIDS, OS hardening, patching |
| **6. Application** | Secures software | WAF, secure coding, input validation |
| **7. Data** | Protects the data itself | AES encryption, tokenization, DLP |
| **8. Human** | Secures the user factor | Phishing simulations, security awareness |

**Conclusion (0.5 M):** No single layer is sufficient. Attackers must defeat *all* layers to compromise the asset, drastically reducing the probability of a successful breach.

#### Part (b) — STRIDE & Parkerian Hexad (7 Marks)

**STRIDE (3.5 M):**
Developed by Microsoft, **STRIDE** is a threat classification model with six categories:
- **S – Spoofing:** Impersonating another user/system (e.g., IP spoofing, email spoofing).
- **T – Tampering:** Modifying data or code (e.g., MITM altering HTTP traffic).
- **R – Repudiation:** Denying having performed an action (e.g., user denies transaction; mitigated by logs/signatures).
- **I – Information Disclosure:** Unauthorized data exposure (e.g., data breach, error messages leaking paths).
- **D – Denial of Service:** Disrupting service (e.g., SYN flood, DDoS).
- **E – Elevation of Privilege:** Gaining higher access (e.g., buffer overflow to gain root).

**Parkerian Hexad (3 M):**
Proposed by Donn Parker in 1998, this model adds three elements to the CIA Triad:
- **Possession** – Control over the asset (e.g., stolen laptop is "lost" even if not data-leaked).
- **Authenticity** – Verifiable origin (e.g., digital signature on documents).
- **Utility** – Usefulness of data (e.g., encrypted data is useless without the key → low utility to attacker).

**Comparison (0.5 M):** STRIDE is best for **threat modeling in software design**, while Parkerian Hexad is best for **data-centric risk analysis**. Both extend the classical CIA model in complementary ways.

---

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Formula Misuse:** Students often confuse $\text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ (correct) with $\text{AV} \times \text{ARO}$ (wrong — SLE must be calculated first). Always show SLE as a separate step. *(Loses 1–2 marks per question.)*
> 2. **Confusing CIA with AAA:** The **CIA Triad** is about *properties of information*; the **AAA Framework** (Auth/Authz/Accounting) is about *access control processes*. Do not mix them in a 14-mark answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Residual Risk:** Any risk-based answer must mention that *complete elimination of risk is impossible*; the goal is to reduce risk to an *acceptable residual level*.
> 4. **Listing without explaining:** Simply writing "Confidentiality = encryption" is insufficient. KTU examiners expect a **definition + threat + control** triplet for full marks.
> 5. **No mention of ISO/NIST:** A high-quality answer briefly references **ISO 27001** or **NIST CSF** to show industry awareness. This is often a tie-breaker in valuation.
> 6. **Quantitative vs Qualitative Risk:** Know when to use ALE (quantitative, needs ₹/$ values) vs Risk Score Matrix (qualitative, uses likelihood × impact scales).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **🔁 Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**
>
> - **Information Security** = Protecting information in *all* forms (digital, physical, verbal).
> - **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability. *This is the single most-tested concept in Module 1.*
> - **Extended models:** Parkerian Hexad adds Possession, Authenticity, Utility. STRIDE is for threat classification. NIST CSF has 5 functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover).
> - **Risk Equation:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$.
> - **ALE Formula:** $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = (\text{AV} \times \text{EF}) \times \text{ARO}$.
> - **Control Justification:** $\text{Net Benefit} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}$. If positive → control is justified.
> - **Control Types (Mnemonic – "PD-CDRC"):** Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Recovery, Compensating.
> - **Defense in Depth:** Multiple layers (Policy → Physical → Perimeter → Network → Host → App → Data → Human).
> - **AAA Framework:** Authentication (who), Authorization (what), Accounting (what did).
> - **Residual Risk** = Total Risk − Effectiveness of Controls (always non-zero; must be *accepted, transferred, or avoided*).
> - **Real-world standards:** ISO/IEC 27001, NIST SP 800-53, PCI-DSS, GDPR / DPDP Act 2023.
> - **Key distinction:** *InfoSec* ⊃ *Cyber Security* ⊃ *IT Security* (broadest to narrowest).
> - **Always end risk answers with a conclusion** stating whether the control is justified and why.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threats to Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Threats to Information Systems ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Threat (KTU Definition):** A *threat* to an information system is any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to the system, its data, or its users. Formally, it is a set of circumstances, an agent, or an event that has the **potential** to breach security and cause damage to an information asset. > [!NOTE] > **Working Definitions for the Module:** > - **Asset:** Anything of value to the organization (hardware, software, data, people, reputation). > - **Vulnerability:** A weakness or gap in the protection efforts of a system that can be exploited to cause harm. > - **Threat:** Any potential danger that can exploit a vulnerability to breach security and cause damage. > - **Risk:** The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact of such exploitation. > - **Attack:** The deliberate act of exploiting a vulnerability to achieve a security breach. The classic relationship is captured as: $$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$$ --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Think of your **home** as an information system. - The **house** is the **asset** (your system/data). - The **unlocked back door** is the **vulnerability**. - The **burglar roaming the neighbourhood** is the **threat**. - The **actual break-in** is the **attack**. - The **value of stolen goods plus emotional trauma** is the **impact**. - The **risk** is the probability the burglar finds that unlocked door **and** steals something valuable. > [!TIP] > If there is *no vulnerability*, a threat cannot materialize into an attack. If there is *no threat*, even a vulnerable system stays safe. Security is therefore the *art of reducing the overlap* between threats and vulnerabilities. --- ### 1.3 The CIA Triad vs. Threats Every threat to an information system ultimately attacks at least one pillar of the **CIA Triad**: | Pillar | What It Protects | Example Threat | |--------|------------------|----------------| | **Confidentiality** | Privacy of data | Eavesdropping, insider leakage | | **Integrity** | Accuracy & trustworthiness of data | Data tampering, man-in-the-middle | | **Availability** | Accessibility of the system & data | Denial-of-Service (DoS), ransomware | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Auxiliary Security Goals (Syllabus Highlight):** *Authenticity, Accountability, Non-repudiation, and Reliability* — modern threats also target these extended properties. --- ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Threat Analysis > [!NOTE] > Key numerical metrics you must know for KTU exams: > - **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** — measured in monetary units (e.g., ₹, $, €). > - **Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** — dimensionless, expressed as events/year. > - **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** — measured in monetary units per year. > - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — expressed in hours. > - **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)** — expressed in hours. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Threats (Syllabus-Aligned Taxonomy) Threats to information systems are classified along **multiple orthogonal axes**: #### A. Based on the Source / Origin 1. **Human Threats** — intentional (malicious attackers, insiders) or unintentional (careless employees). 2. **Natural / Environmental Threats** — floods, earthquakes, fire, lightning. 3. **Technical / System Threats** — hardware failure, software bugs, OS crashes. 4. **Physical Threats** — theft, vandalism, unauthorized physical access. #### B. Based on Intent 1. **Intentional / Malicious Threats** — hacking, malware, social engineering. 2. **Unintentional / Accidental Threats** — human error, misconfiguration, accidental data deletion. #### C. Based on the Target Layer (Network Security Stack) 1. **Application Layer Threats** — SQL injection, XSS, CSRF. 2. **Transport Layer Threats** — session hijacking, SYN flooding. 3. **Network Layer Threats** — IP spoofing, smurf attacks. 4. **Physical / Link Layer Threats** — wiretapping, MAC flooding. --- ### 2.2 Detailed Threat Categories (KTU High-Yield) #### 2.2.1 Malware (Malicious Software) Malware is the **most prevalent** threat class. Sub-types include: - **Virus** — attaches to a legitimate program; needs a host. - **Worm** — self-replicating, spreads over networks without a host. - **Trojan Horse** — disguises as legitimate software; carries hidden payload. - **Ransomware** — encrypts user data and demands payment for the decryption key. - **Spyware** — covertly collects user information. - **Adware** — displays unwanted advertisements. - **Rootkit** — provides privileged (root) access while hiding its existence. - **Bot / Botnet** — a compromised machine controlled remotely; a network of bots is a *botnet*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KAV (Kernel Authentic Virus) / Keylogger distinction:** A keylogger is a *type of spyware* that records keystrokes. In KTU exams, mark it under **Spyware → Confidentiality breach**. #### 2.2.2 Network-Based Threats - **Eavesdropping / Sniffing** — passive interception of network traffic. - **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** — attacker secretly relays/alters communication. - **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** — overwhelming a system to deny legitimate service. - **Distributed DoS (DDoS)** — DoS launched from many distributed agents (botnets). - **Spoofing** — forging source identity (IP, MAC, email, DNS). - **Replay Attack** — malicious retransmission of valid data. - **Session Hijacking** — stealing a valid session token to impersonate a user. #### 2.2.3 Application & Web-Based Threats - **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — inserting malicious SQL into queries. - **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — injecting client-side scripts into web pages. - **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** — tricking an authenticated user into executing unwanted actions. - **Buffer Overflow** — writing beyond allocated memory to overwrite adjacent regions. - **Zero-Day Exploit** — exploits a vulnerability unknown to the software vendor. #### 2.2.4 Human-Centric Threats - **Social Engineering** — psychological manipulation of people. - **Phishing** — fraudulent emails/websites mimicking trusted entities. - **Spear Phishing** — targeted phishing at a specific individual/organization. - **Whaling** — phishing aimed at high-profile executives. - **Vishing** — voice-based phishing (phone calls). - **Smishing** — SMS-based phishing. - **Pretexting, Baiting, Tailgating** — other social engineering tactics. - **Insider Threat** — current/former employees, contractors, or business associates who have inside access. #### 2.2.5 Physical Threats - **Theft** of laptops, mobile devices, storage media. - **Unauthorized Physical Access** to server rooms. - **Hardware Destruction** (sabotage). - **Environmental Threats** — power failure, overheating, water damage, EMI. --- ### 2.3 The Threat–Vulnerability–Control Triangle > [!TIP] > Threats and vulnerabilities are *not the same*. The KTU examiner expects students to clearly distinguish them. - A **threat** is the *agent that causes damage* (e.g., a hacker, a flood, malware). - A **vulnerability** is the *weakness that the agent exploits* (e.g., unpatched OS, open server-room door). - A **control** (or countermeasure) *reduces* either the threat or the vulnerability. The presence of a threat **without** a vulnerability yields no attack. The presence of a vulnerability **without** a threat yields no attack (today). Security professionals must manage **both** simultaneously. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All quantitative questions on "Threats to Information Systems" reduce to the formulas below.** Memorize them with units. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|--------|---------------| | 1 | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Single Loss Expectancy | Monetary units (₹, $) | | 2 | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | Monetary units / year | | 3 | $AV$ = Asset Value | — | Monetary units | | 4 | $EF$ = Exposure Factor | — | Fraction or % (0 to 1) | | 5 | $ARO$ = Annual Rate of Occurrence | — | Dimensionless (events/year) | | 6 | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | — | Qualitative or quantitative | | 7 | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS}{ACS}$ | Return on Security Investment | Decimal or % | | 8 | $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours | | 9 | $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Recover | Hours | | 10 | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability | Fraction (0 to 1) | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Table Pitfall:** All absolute-value bars in this table have been replaced with `\vert`/`\times` notation so that markdown pipe characters do not break the table syntax. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Utility These quantitative models are used by: - **Risk Analysts** in banks to compute Annual Loss Expectancy for cyber-insurance premiums. - **CISO offices** to justify security budgets via ROSI calculations. - **Data Centre Engineers** to compute system availability SLAs (typically ≥ 99.999%). - **Government CERTs** to publish national threat-level advisories. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Quantification Formula The KTU 2024 syllabus treats risk as a function of threat, vulnerability, and impact. We derive each component step by step. --- #### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) **Definition:** SLE is the monetary loss expected from a *single* occurrence of a threat. **Derivation Logic:** - Let $AV$ = total monetary value of the asset. - Let $EF$ = exposure factor, the fraction of the asset that is lost in a single event, expressed as a value in $[0, 1]$ (or as a percentage $0\%$–$100\%$). Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} SLE &= AV \times EF \\[4pt] \text{Units} &: \; [\text{Money}] \times [\text{dimensionless}] = [\text{Money}] \end{aligned} $$ **Example:** A database server worth ₹10,00,000 ($AV = 10^{6}$ ₹) suffers a hard-disk failure that wipes $40\%$ of the data. $$EF = 0.40, \quad SLE = 10^{6} \times 0.40 = 4 \times 10^{5} \text{ ₹}$$ --- #### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO) **Definition:** ARO is the estimated frequency with which a threat is expected to occur per year, measured in events/year. - If a virus hits a system, on average, **once every 4 years** → $ARO = 0.25$. - If DDoS attacks are observed **12 times per year** → $ARO = 12$. --- #### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) **Derivation:** Multiplying the loss per event by the number of events per year: $$ \begin{aligned} ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\[4pt] \text{Units} &: \; [\text{Money}] \times [\text{events/year}] = [\text{Money/year}] \end{aligned} $$ **Example (continuing above):** If disk-failure occurs on average once every 5 years, $$ARO = \tfrac{1}{5} = 0.2, \quad ALE = 4 \times 10^{5} \times 0.2 = 8 \times 10^{4} \text{ ₹/year}$$ --- #### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Return on Security Investment (ROSI) If a security control costing $ACS$ (Annual Cost of the Control) reduces ALE from $ALE_{before}$ to $ALE_{after}$, the savings are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Savings} &= ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS \\[4pt] ROSI &= \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS}{ACS} \end{aligned} $$ A **positive** ROSI indicates a financially justified control. --- #### 3.1.5 Step 5 — System Availability For physical / hardware threats, availability is defined as: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\[4pt] A_{\%} &= A \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ **Example:** $MTBF = 3000$ hours, $MTTR = 3$ hours. $$A = \dfrac{3000}{3000 + 3} = \dfrac{3000}{3003} \approx 0.999 \; \Rightarrow \; 99.9\%$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem (Worked Model):** > A laptop containing customer data is valued at ₹80,000. A phishing attack that succeeds is expected to expose $25\%$ of the data's value. Historically, such successful attacks occur 4 times per year. The IT department proposes an anti-phishing control costing ₹20,000 per year, projected to halve the number of successful attacks. Compute SLE, ALE before, ALE after, and ROSI. Decide if the control is justified. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} AV &= 80{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\ EF &= 0.25 \\ SLE &= AV \times EF = 80{,}000 \times 0.25 = 20{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\ ARO_{before} &= 4 \; \text{events/year} \\ ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO_{before} = 20{,}000 \times 4 = 80{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ARO_{after} &= \dfrac{4}{2} = 2 \; \text{events/year} \\ ALE_{after} &= 20{,}000 \times 2 = 40{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ACS &= 20{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ROSI &= \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS}{ACS} \\ &= \dfrac{80{,}000 - 40{,}000 - 20{,}000}{20{,}000} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{20{,}000} = 1.0 \; (100\%) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A ROSI of $+1.0$ means every rupee invested saves one rupee; the control is **financially justified**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. CIA Triad Mapping > [!TIP] > This matrix is a *very high-yield* KTU diagram. Examiners frequently ask: "Which pillar of the CIA triad does threat X violate?" | Threat | Primary CIA Violation | Secondary Violation | |--------|----------------------|---------------------| | Phishing | Confidentiality | Authenticity | | Ransomware | Availability | Integrity | | SQL Injection | Integrity | Confidentiality | | DoS / DDoS | Availability | — | | Man-in-the-Middle | Confidentiality | Integrity | | Replay Attack | Integrity | Authenticity | | Keylogger (Spyware) | Confidentiality | — | | Insider Data Theft | Confidentiality | Non-repudiation | | Hardware Failure | Availability | Integrity | | Natural Disaster | Availability | Integrity | --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defense Layers | Layer | Common Threats | Typical Defense | |-------|----------------|-----------------| | Physical | Theft, vandalism, fire | Locks, CCTV, fire suppression | | Network | Sniffing, DoS, spoofing | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPN, TLS | | Host / OS | Malware, privilege escalation | Antivirus, patching, least privilege | | Application | SQLi, XSS, CSRF | Input validation, WAF, secure coding | | Data | Tampering, leakage | Encryption, hashing, DLP, backups | | Human | Social engineering, insider threat | Awareness training, MFA, audit logs | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `tA`, `tB`, `tC`); no reserved keywords used as node names; all labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown formatting inside the quoted strings. ### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Threats to Information Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD tRoot["THREATS TO INFORMATION SYSTEMS"] --> tA["PHYSICAL AND ENVIRONMENTAL"] tRoot --> tB["LOGICAL AND TECHNICAL"] tRoot --> tC["HUMAN AND SOCIAL"] tA --> tA1["THEFT OF HARDWARE"] tA --> tA2["FIRE OR FLOOD"] tA --> tA3["POWER FAILURE"] tA --> tA4["ELECTROMAGNETIC INTERFERENCE"] tB --> tB1["MALWARE"] tB --> tB2["NETWORK ATTACKS"] tB --> tB3["APPLICATION ATTACKS"] tB --> tB4["SYSTEM AND SOFTWARE FAILURES"] tB1 --> tB1a["VIRUS"] tB1 --> tB1b["WORM"] tB1 --> tB1c["TROJAN HORSE"] tB1 --> tB1d["RANSOMWARE"] tB1 --> tB1e["SPYWARE"] tB1 --> tB1f["ROOTKIT"] tB2 --> tB2a["DOS AND DDOS"] tB2 --> tB2b["MAN IN THE MIDDLE"] tB2 --> tB2c["SPOOFING"] tB2 --> tB2d["SNIFFING"] tB2 --> tB2e["REPLAY ATTACK"] tB2 --> tB2f["SESSION HIJACKING"] tB3 --> tB3a["SQL INJECTION"] tB3 --> tB3b["CROSS SITE SCRIPTING"] tB3 --> tB3c["CROSS SITE REQUEST FORGERY"] tB3 --> tB3d["BUFFER OVERFLOW"] tB3 --> tB3e["ZERO DAY EXPLOIT"] tC --> tC1["SOCIAL ENGINEERING"] tC --> tC2["PHISHING FAMILY"] tC --> tC3["INSIDER THREAT"] tC --> tC4["HUMAN ERROR"] tC2 --> tC2a["PHISHING"] tC2 --> tC2b["SPEAR PHISHING"] tC2 --> tC2c["WHALING"] tC2 --> tC2d["VISHING"] tC2 --> tC2e["SMISHING"] ``` --- ### 4.2 Threat–Vulnerability–Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR a1["THREAT AGENT
HACKER FLOOD MALWARE"] --> b1["EXPLOITS"] a2["VULNERABILITY
UNPATCHED OS OPEN PORT"] --> b1 b1 --> c1["ATTACK OCCURS"] c1 --> d1["IMPACT ON CIA"] d1 --> e1["RISK MATERIALIZED"] e1 --> f1["APPLY CONTROL"] f1 --> a2 f1 --> a1 classDef riskBox fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef controlBox fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#007700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class e1 riskBox class f1 controlBox ``` --- ### 4.3 OSI-Layer Threat Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD l7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION"] --> t7["SQLI XSS CSRF BUFFER OVERFLOW"] l6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION"] --> t6["TLS ATTACKS CERT SPOOFING"] l5["LAYER 5 SESSION"] --> t5["SESSION HIJACKING REPLAY"] l4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT"] --> t4["SYN FLOOD TCP HIJACK"] l3["LAYER 3 NETWORK"] --> t3["IP SPOOFING SMURF ICMP FLOOD"] l2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK"] --> t2["MAC FLOODING ARP POISONING"] l1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL"] --> t1["WIRETAPPING CABLE CUT"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Risk Quantification Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["IDENTIFY ASSET"] --> s2["COMPUTE AV"] s2 --> s3["ESTIMATE EF"] s3 --> s4["SLE = AV x EF"] s4 --> s5["ESTIMATE ARO"] s5 --> s6["ALE = SLE x ARO"] s6 --> s7["DECIDE CONTROL"] s7 --> s8["COMPUTE ROSI"] s8 --> s9{"IS ROSI POSITIVE"} s9 -->|YES| s10["IMPLEMENT CONTROL"] s9 -->|NO| s11["REJECT OR RECONSIDER"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Q1.** Differentiate between a **threat**, a **vulnerability**, and an **attack**. Illustrate with a one-line example each. **Model Answer (3 marks distribution):** - **Threat (1 mark):** Any potential cause that can exploit a system to cause damage. *Example:* An attacker scanning the internet for open SSH ports. - **Vulnerability (1 mark):** A weakness in the system that can be exploited. *Example:* A server running an outdated SSH service with a known CVE. - **Attack (1 mark):** The actual exploitation of a vulnerability by a threat. *Example:* The attacker exploits the outdated SSH service and gains root access. > [!TIP] > Examiners expect *three distinct sentences* — one per definition. Avoid defining two in one sentence. --- **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]** **Q2.** List and briefly explain **any three** categories of malware. **Model Answer (3 × 1 mark):** 1. **Virus (1 m):** Malicious code that attaches itself to a legitimate program and propagates only when the host program runs. It typically requires user action. 2. **Worm (1 m):** Self-replicating malware that spreads over networks automatically without human intervention, exploiting OS or protocol vulnerabilities. 3. **Ransomware (1 m):** Malware that encrypts user data and demands payment (usually in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. *Example families:* WannaCry, LockBit. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 End-Semester pattern, students answer **one** question from each module-pair. Each Part-B has internal choice — Question A or Question B. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]* **(a) [7 marks]** Classify the various **threats to information systems** in detail. Compare **logical threats** and **physical threats** with suitable examples. **Model Solution:** **(i) Classification of threats (4 marks):** Threats can be classified along multiple axes: 1. **By source:** Human, Natural/Environmental, Technical, Physical. 2. **By intent:** Intentional (malicious) vs. Unintentional (accidental). 3. **By target layer:** Application, Network, Host, Data, Physical. **(ii) Logical vs. Physical threats (3 marks):** | Aspect | Logical Threats | Physical Threats | |--------|----------------|------------------| | Target | Software, data, network | Hardware, premises, environment | | Vector | Network, USB, email | Direct access, disasters | | Examples | Malware, DDoS, SQLi | Theft, fire, power loss | | Defense | Firewall, antivirus, patching | Locks, CCTV, UPS, fire suppression | --- **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **CIA Triad** and identify which CIA pillar each of the following violates: *(i) Ransomware, (ii) Sniffing, (iii) SQL Injection, (iv) DoS, (v) Phishing, (vi) Insider data theft, (vii) Hardware failure.* **Model Solution:** **CIA Triad diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR c["CONFIDENTIALITY
Privacy of Data"] --> tri["INFORMATION SECURITY"] i["INTEGRITY
Trustworthiness of Data"] --> tri a["AVAILABILITY
Accessibility of System"] --> tri ``` **Mapping (5 marks — 1/2 mark each, no full credit for wrong entries):** | Threat | CIA Violated | |--------|--------------| | (i) Ransomware | **Availability** (also Integrity) | | (ii) Sniffing | **Confidentiality** | | (iii) SQL Injection | **Integrity** (also Confidentiality) | | (iv) DoS | **Availability** | | (v) Phishing | **Confidentiality** (also Authenticity) | | (vi) Insider data theft | **Confidentiality** | | (vii) Hardware failure | **Availability** | --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]* — **Internal Choice Alternative** **(a) [7 marks]** Define **Risk**. Compute **SLE**, **ALE before**, **ALE after**, and **ROSI** for the following scenario, and comment on whether the proposed control is justified. > *Scenario:* A web application valued at ₹5,00,000 suffers an SQL injection attack that exposes 50% of its data. Such attacks occur 6 times a year. A Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing ₹1,50,000/year is proposed, expected to reduce successful attacks to once a year. **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} AV &= 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\ EF &= 0.50 \\ SLE &= AV \times EF = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 2{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\ ARO_{before} &= 6 \\ ARO_{after} &= 1 \\ ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO_{before} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 6 = 15{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ALE_{after} &= SLE \times ARO_{after} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 1 = 2{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ACS &= 1{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹/year} \\ ROSI &= \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS}{ACS} \\ &= \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{11{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \approx 7.33 \end{aligned} $$ **Comment (2 marks):** $ROSI \approx 7.33$ means each ₹1 invested saves ₹7.33. The WAF is **strongly justified**. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the following **types of malware** with one distinguishing feature each: **Virus, Worm, Trojan Horse, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Botnet**. **Model Solution (7 marks — 1 per malware):** 1. **Virus (1 m):** Attaches to a host program; needs user execution to spread; cannot self-propagate. 2. **Worm (1 m):** Self-replicating; propagates over networks without user action; consumes bandwidth. 3. **Trojan Horse (1 m):** Disguises as legitimate software; carries a hidden malicious payload; does not self-replicate. 4. **Ransomware (1 m):** Encrypts user files and demands ransom; uses strong cryptography (e.g., RSA-2048). 5. **Spyware (1 m):** Covertly monitors user activity; keyloggers, browser-hijackers fall under this class. 6. **Rootkit (1 m):** Gains and hides privileged (root/admin) access; often evades antivirus by subverting OS internals. 7. **Botnet (1 m):** A network of compromised machines (bots/zombies) controlled by a single *botmaster* via C2 channels; used for DDoS, spam, mining. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions on "Threats to Information Systems" Questions:** > 1. **Threat ≠ Vulnerability ≠ Attack.** Many students use these terms interchangeably and lose 1–2 marks per question. Always give *separate, single-line definitions*. > 2. **CIA Triad mapping.** Examiners expect the *primary* pillar; do not list all three for every threat — pick the dominant one and optionally mention the secondary in brackets. > 3. **Formula Units.** When solving ALE problems, *always state units* (₹/year, events/year). Missing units cost a half-mark in valuation. > 4. **ROSI Sign Convention.** A negative ROSI means the control is *not* justified. Do not write "the control is justified" without showing the sign and magnitude. > 5. **SQLi vs. XSS.** SQL Injection targets the *database server*; XSS targets *other users' browsers*. Confusing these costs a mark on web-security questions. > 6. **Worm vs. Virus.** A worm does *not* need a host file; a virus *does*. This distinction is the most-tested differentiator. > 7. **Insider Threat.** Do not categorize it only as "malware" — it is a **human threat** that bypasses many technical controls. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Threats to Information Systems" (Module 1):** - **Core triad:** Threat → Vulnerability → Attack → Impact → Risk. - **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (extended: Authenticity, Non-repudiation, Accountability). - **Threat classes:** Physical, Logical/Technical, Human (Social Engineering, Insider). - **Malware types to memorize (7):** Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Botnet. - **Network threats (6):** DoS/DDoS, MitM, Spoofing, Sniffing, Replay, Session Hijacking. - **Web threats (4):** SQLi, XSS, CSRF, Buffer Overflow. - **Social Engineering (5):** Phishing, Spear Phishing, Whaling, Vishing, Smishing. - **Quantitative formulas (must-know):** - $SLE = AV \times EF$ - $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ - $ROSI = (ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - ACS)/ACS$ - $Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ - **Distinguish clearly:** Threat ≠ Vulnerability ≠ Attack ≠ Risk. - **Mapping rule:** Malware primarily violates **Availability & Integrity**; Phishing/Sniffing violates **Confidentiality**; DoS violates **Availability**; SQLi/XSS violates **Integrity**. - **Defensive layers:** Physical → Network → Host → Application → Data → Human (defense-in-depth). - **Insider threat** is the *hardest* to defend against using only technical controls; it requires audit logs, MFA, and least-privilege policies. - **Risk is a function, not a number** — it depends on threat, vulnerability, and impact simultaneously.

topics.title: Cyber Security and Security Risk Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cyber Security and Security Risk Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Cyber Security? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (ISO/IEC 27032 Aligned):** > **Cyber Security** is the collection of tools, policies, security concepts, security safeguards, guidelines, risk management approaches, actions, training, best practices, assurance, and technologies that can be used to protect the cyber environment, organization, and user's assets against cyber threats. In simpler words, it is the practice of defending computers, servers, mobile devices, electronic systems, networks, and data from malicious digital attacks. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "Your Digital House" 🏠** > Imagine your computer system is a **house**: > - **Doors & Windows (Confidentiality)** → Locks that prevent strangers from peeking inside your house. > - **Walls (Integrity)** → The structural integrity of the house ensuring nothing inside is altered or broken. > - **Power Supply (Availability)** → Electricity that ensures lights, fans, and appliances work when you need them. > - **Burglar (Threat)** → A potential attacker trying to break in. > - **Weak Door Lock (Vulnerability)** → A flaw that a burglar can exploit. > - **Risk** = The probability that the burglar actually breaks in AND steals something valuable. > > **Cyber Security = The complete security system** of this digital house (locks, CCTV, alarm, security guard). ## 1.2 The Three Pillars — CIA Triad The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security. Every cyber security decision revolves around these three goals: | Pillar | Meaning | Real-World Analogy | |---|---|---| | **Confidentiality** | Ensuring that data is accessible only to authorized users. | A sealed envelope — only the intended recipient can read it. | | **Integrity** | Ensuring that data is accurate, consistent, and unaltered during storage or transmission. | A notarized document — proof it hasn't been tampered with. | | **Availability** | Ensuring that systems and data are accessible to authorized users whenever needed. | A 24/7 hospital emergency room — always open when needed. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Extended Triad (Parkerian Hexad) — Beyond CIA:** > Some models add **Possession, Authenticity, and Utility** for more comprehensive coverage, but for KTU 2024, focus on **CIA + Authenticity + Non-repudiation**. ## 1.3 What is Security Risk Analysis? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **Security Risk Analysis** (also called Risk Assessment) is the systematic process of identifying, estimating, and prioritizing risks to organizational assets (information, systems, people) resulting from the operation of an information system. It forms part of **Risk Management**. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "Insurance Underwriting" 🛡️** > Before an insurance company insures your car, they assess: > 1. **What can go wrong?** (Threat — accident, theft) > 2. **What weakness exists?** (Vulnerability — old brakes, no anti-theft) > 3. **What is the impact?** (Consequence — total loss) > 4. **How often can it happen?** (Likelihood) > 5. **Should we accept, avoid, mitigate, or transfer the risk?** > > **Cyber Risk Analysis does the EXACT SAME thing for digital assets!** ## 1.4 Core Security Terminology (Syllabus Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know KTU Definitions:** > > - **Asset** — Anything of value to the organization (hardware, data, software, reputation). > - **Threat** — Any potential event that can cause harm (virus, insider, natural disaster). > - **Vulnerability** — A weakness in a system that can be exploited (unpatched software, weak password). > - **Risk** — The potential for loss, damage, or destruction of assets as a result of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. > - **Likelihood** — The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability. > - **Impact** — The magnitude of harm that would result if the risk materializes. > - **Control / Countermeasure** — A safeguard put in place to reduce risk (firewall, encryption, policy). > - **Residual Risk** — The risk remaining **after** controls have been applied. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Risk Equation At the heart of risk analysis lies a single conceptual relationship: $$R = f(T, V, I)$$ Where: - $R$ = Risk - $T$ = Threat (what can go wrong) - $V$ = Vulnerability (the weakness) - $I$ = Impact (the damage caused) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic Step Decomposition:** > 1. **No Threat + No Vulnerability = No Risk.** (If neither exists, nothing bad can happen.) > 2. **High Threat + High Vulnerability + High Impact = Critical Risk.** > 3. **Risk only materializes when a Threat ACTS ON a Vulnerability.** > 4. **Controls reduce either T, V, or I — thereby reducing R.** ## 2.2 Quantitative Risk Analysis Formulas (KTU High-Yield) Quantitative Risk Analysis assigns **monetary values** to risk components. This is a **guaranteed KTU Part B topic**. | Formula | Description | Variables | |---|---|---| | $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$ | **Single Loss Expectancy** (loss from ONE occurrence) | $AV$ = Asset Value; $EF$ = Exposure Factor (fraction of asset lost, e.g., 0.6) | | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | **Annual Loss Expectancy** (expected yearly loss) | $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy; $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (how many times per year) | | $\text{Total ALE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ALE}_i$ | Total annualized loss across all risk scenarios | Sum of individual ALEs | | $\text{ROS} = \text{ALE} - \text{ACS}$ | **Return on Security Investment** | $ACS$ = Annual Cost of Safeguard | | $\text{Risk Score} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$ | Qualitative/quantitative hybrid scoring | Likelihood (1–5); Impact (1–5) | | $\text{Risk Reduction} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}}$ | Effectiveness of a control | Difference in expected losses | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - Banks use **ALE** to justify multi-million dollar cybersecurity budgets. > - Insurance companies use **SLE** to set cyber-insurance premiums. > - ISO 27001 auditors require **quantitative justification** for every control implemented. ## 2.3 The Risk Management Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-30) The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)**. Risk management is a continuous, iterative 6-step process: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six Phases of Risk Management:** > 1. **Frame the Risk** — Establish risk context, criteria, and tolerance. > 2. **Assess the Risk** — Identify threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and impact. > 3. **Respond to the Risk** — Accept, Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, or Share. > 4. **Monitor the Risk** — Continuously review and update. > 5. **Communicate the Risk** — Report to stakeholders. > 6. **Identify Assets & Threats** — Catalog everything of value. ## 2.4 Risk Response Strategies (The "5 Ts") > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Must-Know Risk Treatment Options:** > > | Strategy | Meaning | Example | > |---|---|---| > | **Accept** | Acknowledge the risk and do nothing (low-impact, low-cost). | Tolerating a brief website downtime. | > | **Avoid** | Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the cause. | Discontinuing a vulnerable service. | > | **Mitigate** | Reduce likelihood or impact through controls. | Installing a firewall, applying patches. | > | **Transfer** | Shift risk to a third party. | Purchasing cyber insurance. | > | **Share / Diversify** | Distribute risk across parties. | Cloud providers, joint ventures. | ## 2.5 Qualitative vs. Quantitative Risk Analysis > [!NOTE] > **Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU:** > > | Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative | > |---|---|---| > | **Nature** | Subjective, descriptive | Objective, numerical | > | **Scale** | Low / Medium / High (or 1–5) | Monetary values (₹, $) | > | **Data Required** | Expert judgment, experience | Historical data, statistics | > | **Cost** | Low | High | > | **Examples** | Risk Matrix, OCTAVE | ALE/SLE/ARO, FAIR | > | **Best For** | Initial screening | Budget justification | ## 2.6 Popular Risk Analysis Methodologies (KTU Frequently Asked) | Methodology | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **NIST SP 800-30** | Framework | U.S. government standard; comprehensive RMF guide. | | **OCTAVE** (Operationally Critical Threat, Asset, and Vulnerability Evaluation) | Qualitative | Carnegie Mellon; self-directed, asset-driven. | | **FAIR** (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) | Quantitative | Decomposes risk into measurable factors. | | **STRIDE** | Threat Modeling | Microsoft-developed; categorizes threats into 6 types. | | **DREAD** | Threat Scoring | Old Microsoft model; ranks threats 0–10 across 5 criteria. | | **ISO 27005** | Framework | International standard aligned with ISO 27001. | | **CRAMM** (CCTA Risk Analysis and Management Method) | Hybrid | UK government; 3-stage structured process. | > [!NOTE] > **STRIDE Mnemonic (Bonus for KTU):** > **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Solved Problem: ALE-Based Risk Justification (Full Worked-Out Example) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Style Quantitative Problem (14-Mark Standard):** > An e-commerce company has a customer database worth **₹10,00,000**. A SQL Injection attack could expose 70% of the records. Statistically, such attacks occur **3 times per year**. The company is evaluating a Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing **₹2,00,000 per year** that is expected to reduce the attack frequency to **0.5 times per year** and the exposure factor to **30%**. > > **(a)** Calculate the ALE before and after the WAF. > **(b)** Is the WAF a financially justified control? Justify using ROS. > **(c)** Calculate the Risk Reduction achieved. ### Given Data (Step 0) | Variable | Symbol | Pre-Control Value | Post-Control Value | |---|---|---|---| | Asset Value | $AV$ | ₹10,00,000 | ₹10,00,000 | | Exposure Factor | $EF$ | 0.70 | 0.30 | | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO$ | 3 | 0.5 | | Annual Cost of Safeguard | $ACS$ | — | ₹2,00,000 | ### Part (a) — ALE Calculations **Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) before WAF** $$\text{SLE}_{\text{pre}} = AV \times EF_{\text{pre}}$$ $$\text{SLE}_{\text{pre}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70$$ $$\text{SLE}_{\text{pre}} = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ in INR)}$$ **Step 2: Compute ALE before WAF** $$\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = \text{SLE}_{\text{pre}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{pre}}$$ $$\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 7{,}00{,}000 \times 3$$ $$\boxed{\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 21{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$ **Step 3: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) after WAF** $$\text{SLE}_{\text{post}} = AV \times EF_{\text{post}}$$ $$\text{SLE}_{\text{post}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30$$ $$\text{SLE}_{\text{post}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ in INR)}$$ **Step 4: Compute ALE after WAF** $$\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \text{SLE}_{\text{post}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{post}}$$ $$\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5$$ $$\boxed{\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$ ### Part (b) — Return on Security Investment (ROS) **Step 5: Compute ROS** $$\text{ROS} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{ACS}$$ $$\text{ROS} = 21{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{ROS} = 17{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year (POSITIVE → Justified!)}}$$ **Interpretation:** A positive ROS means the WAF pays for itself **17.5 times** in expected annual savings. The control is **strongly recommended**. ### Part (c) — Risk Reduction **Step 6: Compute Risk Reduction** $$\text{Risk Reduction} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}}$$ $$\text{Risk Reduction} = 21{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{Risk Reduction} = 19{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Percentage Risk Reduction:** > $$\text{\% Reduction} = \frac{19{,}50{,}000}{21{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 92.86\%$$ ## 3.2 Qualitative Risk Matrix (Analytical Scoring Method) When exact values are unavailable, risks are scored on a **5×5 matrix**: | Likelihood ↓ / Impact → | Negligible (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Severe (5) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Rare (1)** | 1 — Low | 2 — Low | 3 — Low | 4 — Medium | 5 — Medium | | **Unlikely (2)** | 2 — Low | 4 — Low | 6 — Medium | 8 — Medium | 10 — High | | **Possible (3)** | 3 — Low | 6 — Medium | 9 — High | 12 — High | 15 — Critical | | **Likely (4)** | 4 — Medium | 8 — Medium | 12 — High | 16 — Critical | 20 — Critical | | **Almost Certain (5)** | 5 — Medium | 10 — High | 15 — Critical | 20 — Critical | 25 — Critical | **Risk Score Formula:** $$\text{Risk Score} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$$ **Decision Bands (Typical KTU Threshold):** - **Low (1–5):** Accept the risk. - **Medium (6–10):** Plan mitigation within 6 months. - **High (11–15):** Mitigate immediately. - **Critical (16–25):** Avoid or transfer immediately. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Risk Frameworks | Framework | Origin | Quantitative? | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---|---| | **NIST SP 800-30** | NIST (USA) | Hybrid | Comprehensive, regulatory acceptance | Complex, requires expertise | | **OCTAVE** | CERT/CMU | Qualitative | Self-directed, asset-focused | Not suited for large enterprises | | **FAIR** | Risk Management Insight | Quantitative | Precise, financial focus | Requires rich data | | **ISO 27005** | ISO | Flexible | Globally recognized, certifiable | Generic, needs customization | | **CRAMM** | UK CCTA | Hybrid | Structured 3-stage process | UK-specific legacy | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CIA Triad — Visual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CORE["CORE SECURITY GOALS"] direction TB C["CONFIDENTIALITY
Data Privacy
Encryption, Access Control"] I["INTEGRITY
Data Accuracy
Hashing, Digital Signatures"] A["AVAILABILITY
System Uptime
Redundancy, DDoS Protection"] end subgraph SUPPORTS["SUPPORTING PROPERTIES"] AU["AUTHENTICITY"] NR["NON-REPUDIATION"] end AU --> C NR --> I CORE --> SUPPORTS classDef primary fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef support fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff class C,I,A primary class AU,NR support ``` ## 4.2 Risk Management Process Flow (NIST RMF) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STEP 1
FRAME RISK
Context and Criteria"] --> B["STEP 2
ASSESS RISK
Identify Threats and
Vulnerabilities"] B --> C["STEP 3
RESPOND TO RISK
Accept / Avoid /
Mitigate / Transfer"] C --> D["STEP 4
MONITOR RISK
Continuous Review"] D --> E["STEP 5
COMMUNICATE
Stakeholder Reporting"] E --> A B1["Threat ID"] --> B B2["Vulnerability ID"] --> B B3["Likelihood"] --> B B4["Impact Analysis"] --> B C1["Accept"] --> C C2["Avoid"] --> C C3["Mitigate"] --> C C4["Transfer"] --> C classDef stepNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef subNode fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff class A,B,C,D,E stepNode class B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,C4 subNode ``` ## 4.3 Risk Treatment Decision Tree (The 5 Ts) ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["IDENTIFIED RISK"]) --> Q1{"Is risk
low impact
and low cost?"} Q1 -- YES --> ACCEPT["ACCEPT
Document and monitor"] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Can we
eliminate the
cause entirely?"} Q2 -- YES --> AVOID["AVOID
Remove the asset or activity"] Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Is a control
cost-effective?"} Q3 -- YES --> MITIGATE["MITIGATE
Apply safeguards
Firewall, Encryption, Patches"] Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Can third party
absorb the loss?"} Q4 -- YES --> TRANSFER["TRANSFER
Cyber Insurance, Outsourcing"] Q4 -- NO --> SHARE["SHARE
Joint risk ownership"] classDef decision fill:#facc15,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000000 classDef action fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff classDef start fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision class ACCEPT,AVOID,MITIGATE,TRANSFER,SHARE action class START start ``` ## 4.4 STRIDE Threat Model — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SYS["INFORMATION SYSTEM"] --> S["S
SPOOFING
Fake identity"] SYS --> T["T
TAMPERING
Modify data"] SYS --> R["R
REPUDIATION
Deny actions"] SYS --> I["I
INFORMATION
DISCLOSURE
Expose data"] SYS --> D["D
DENIAL OF
SERVICE
Disrupt service"] SYS --> E["E
ELEVATION OF
PRIVILEGE
Gain admin access"] S --> COUNTER1["Counter: Authentication, MFA"] T --> COUNTER2["Counter: Integrity checks, Hashing"] R --> COUNTER3["Counter: Logs, Digital Signatures"] I --> COUNTER4["Counter: Encryption, Access Control"] D --> COUNTER5["Counter: Redundancy, Rate Limiting"] E --> COUNTER6["Counter: Least Privilege, RBAC"] classDef threatNode fill:#be123c,stroke:#881337,color:#ffffff classDef counterNode fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff classDef systemNode fill:#312e81,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff class S,T,R,I,D,E threatNode class COUNTER1,COUNTER2,COUNTER3,COUNTER4,COUNTER5,COUNTER6 counterNode class SYS systemNode ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define Cyber Security. List any four cyber security goals.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Cyber Security** is the collection of tools, policies, safeguards, guidelines, risk management approaches, and technologies used to protect the cyber environment, organization, and user assets against cyber threats. > > **Four Cyber Security Goals (CIA + A):** > 1. **Confidentiality** — Preventing unauthorized disclosure of information. > 2. **Integrity** — Ensuring data is accurate and unaltered. > 3. **Availability** — Ensuring systems and data are accessible when needed. > 4. **Authenticity** — Verifying the identity of users and systems. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Threat** | A potential event that can cause harm. | A phishing email sent to an employee. | | **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited. | An unpatched version of Apache Server. | | **Risk** | The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. | A phishing email (threat) exploiting an untrained employee (vulnerability) leading to credential theft. | > **Key Relation:** $\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$ --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice 1]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail with suitable examples. Briefly discuss any two **supporting security properties** (Authenticity and Non-Repudiation). **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** What is **Security Risk Analysis**? Explain the steps involved in conducting a qualitative risk assessment. **(7 Marks)** #### 📘 Part (a) — CIA Triad Model Answer (7 Marks) The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security representing three core goals: **1.
