- Typically **28 bits of entropy** on 64-bit Linux.
- Bypassable via *information leak* vulnerabilities (defeats in isolation).

**(ii) NX / DEP (No-Execute / Data Execution Prevention):**
- Marks memory regions (e.g., stack) as **non-executable** via the NX bit in page tables.
- Injected shellcode on the stack **cannot execute**.
- Forces attacker to use existing code (ROP, ret2libc) instead of injecting new code.
- Bypassable via return-oriented programming (ROP).

**(iii) Safe C Functions:**
- Replace unsafe functions with bounds-checked alternatives:
  - `gets()` → `fgets(buf, size, stdin)`
  - `strcpy()` → `strncpy(dest, src, n)` or `strlcpy()`
  - `sprintf()` → `snprintf(buf, size, ...)`
  - `strcat()` → `strncat(dest, src, n)`
- These functions accept an explicit maximum length, preventing overflow at the source.
- Most effective because they eliminate the root cause.

---

### **CHOICE B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q4 (a). Differentiate between Stack-Based and Heap-Based buffer overflow attacks. List at least three differences. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Stack-Based Buffer Overflow | Heap-Based Buffer Overflow |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory Region Affected** | Stack (local variables, return address) | Heap (dynamically allocated memory) |
| 2 | **Primary Target** | Return address → control-flow hijack | Heap metadata (chunk headers, free lists) |
| 3 | **Triggering Functions** | `gets()`, `strcpy()` on local arrays | `malloc()`, `strcpy()` on heap pointers |
| 4 | **Detection Difficulty** | Easier (canaries, ASLR effective) | Harder (canaries don't cover heap) |
| 5 | **Exploitation Technique** | Overwrite return address + shellcode | Write-what-where via corrupted heap metadata |
| 6 | **Famous Example** | Morris Worm (1988), Code Red (2001) | Heap spraying in browser exploits |
| 7 | **Impact** | Direct RCE / control-flow hijack | Arbitrary write / RCE after metadata corruption |

---

#### **Q4 (b). What is a Format String Attack? How is it related to buffer overflow? Explain with a suitable example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A **Format String Attack** is a vulnerability that occurs when user-controlled input is passed directly as the *format string* argument to functions like `printf()`, `scanf()`, or `syslog()`. The attacker can include format specifiers (`%x`, `%s`, `%n`) to leak stack memory or write arbitrary values to arbitrary memory addresses.

**Relation to Buffer Overflow:**
- Both are memory corruption vulnerabilities.
- `%n` allows writing to arbitrary memory locations — effectively a **write-what-where** primitive.
- This can overwrite the return address (analogous to a buffer overflow) or modify GOT entries to redirect execution.

**Example:**

```c
void vuln() {
    char buf[100];
    gets(buf);
    printf(buf);   // VULNERABLE — user input used as format string
}
```

**Attacker Input:** `%x %x %x %n`

- `%x` leaks stack data (information disclosure).
- `%n` writes the number of bytes printed so far to an address provided on the stack.
- With proper payload construction, the attacker can overwrite the return address or a function pointer, achieving control-flow hijack.

**Mitigation:**
- Never pass user input as the format string.
- Use `printf("%s", user_input);` instead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse *stack overflow* (excessive stack recursion) with *buffer overflow* (data overwriting memory). Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** write only the definition without explaining the *mechanism* (saved EBP, return address corruption). Conceptual depth is mandatory for full marks.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget to mention **mitigations** in long answers. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes defense-in-depth.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** skip diagrams. A neat stack frame diagram is worth **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> - ✅ **DO** mention **defense-in-depth**, real-world examples (Morris Worm, Code Red), and modern bypasses (ROP) for top-band scoring.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Buffer Overflow** = writing data beyond a buffer's allocated size, corrupting adjacent memory.
- [ ] **Stack frame** contains (low → high): local buffers → saved EBP → return address.
- [ ] Stack grows **downward**, so buffer overflow proceeds **upward** into the return address.
- [ ] **Offset to return address** on 32-bit: $L_{buf} + 4$ bytes; on 64-bit: $L_{buf} + 8$ bytes.
- [ ] **Unsafe functions** to remember: `gets`, `strcpy`, `sprintf`, `strcat`, `scanf("%s", ...)`.
- [ ] **Safe replacements**: `fgets`, `strncpy`/`strlcpy`, `snprintf`, `strncat`.
- [ ] **Stack Canary** = random value between buffer and return address; verified before `ret`.
- [ ] **ASLR** randomizes memory base addresses (~28-bit entropy on 64-bit Linux).
- [ ] **NX/DEP** marks stack/heap as non-executable; bypassable via ROP.
- [ ] **ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)** chains existing code gadgets; defeats NX.
- [ ] **ret2libc** redirects to existing `system("/bin/sh")`; bypasses NX.
- [ ] **Format string** vulnerabilities (`printf(buf)`) are a related memory corruption class.
- [ ] **Defense-in-depth** = stack canary + ASLR + NX + CFI + safe coding (no single defense suffices).
- [ ] **Famous exploits**: Morris Worm (1988), Code Red (2001), Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160, adjacent class).
- [ ] Always draw the **stack frame diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is the single highest-value visual aid.

### 🎯 Memory Anchors (One-Liners)

> *"Buffers overflow upward; return addresses sit above; attackers reach them first."*

> *"Canary protects, ASLR randomizes, NX disables, CFI verifies — together they defend in depth."*

> *"A format string with `%n` is a buffer overflow in disguise."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack Overflow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack Overflow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Overflow (Buffer Overflow Attack):** A *stack-based buffer overflow* is a memory-corruption vulnerability that occurs when a program writes more data into a buffer allocated on the **call stack** than the buffer was originally sized to hold. The excess data overwrites adjacent memory regions — most critically, the **saved frame pointer** and the **return address** — thereby allowing an attacker to hijack the program's control flow, inject arbitrary code, or crash the executing process.

In the context of the KTU 2024 *Fundamentals of Cyber Security* syllabus, Stack Overflow is classified under **Module 1: Vulnerabilities and Attacks**, and is treated as one of the foundational **memory-safety flaws** that arise from insecure low-level programming in languages such as C and C++ (which lack built-in bounds checking on array/pointer operations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology used by KTU Board Examiners:**
> - **Vulnerability** — A weakness in a system (the bug).
> - **Exploit** — The mechanism/code used to leverage the vulnerability.
> - **Payload** — The malicious data delivered through the overflow.
> - **Shellcode** — Machine-level instructions inserted by the attacker to spawn a shell or execute arbitrary commands.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Stack of Post-it Notes

Imagine a vertical stack of Post-it notes pinned to a wall. Each function call is a **newer note pinned on top** of older ones, and the bottommost notes are the oldest function calls. Now imagine that each Post-it note has a strict **height limit of 5 lines** (the buffer size).

If you write **10 lines** on a single Post-it, the ink bleeds *downward* onto the Post-it pinned just below it, **scribbling over the "Return To: Main Office" instruction** that was written on that lower note. When the program (the office worker) finally removes the top Post-it, it doesn't go back to the *Main Office* anymore — it follows your scribbled-over instruction and walks into your trap.

This is **exactly** what happens in a stack overflow:

| Real-world Object | Program Memory Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Stack of Post-it notes | Call Stack in RAM |
| 5-line height limit | Allocated buffer size (e.g., 64 bytes) |
| Ink bleeding downward | Writing past buffer end → overwriting adjacent stack frame |
| Scribbled return address | Overwritten return address in stack frame |
| Walking into a trap | Hijacked execution to attacker's shellcode |

## 1.3 Why Stack Overflow is a Pillar Topic in Cyber Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Stack overflows have been ranked consistently in the **OWASP Top 10** and **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration)** lists under **CWE-121: Stack-based Buffer Overflow**. Famous real-world examples include the **Morris Worm (1988)**, the **Code Red Worm (2001)**, and the **Heartbleed-style family of memory bugs**. Mastering this topic is essential for KTU Module 1, as it is a frequently asked 14-mark question in ESE.

**Standard Metrics used in Cyber Security Literature:**
- **Stack Canary Size** = **4 bytes (32-bit)** or **8 bytes (64-bit)**
- **Typical vulnerable function family** = `gets()`, `strcpy()`, `sprintf()`, `scanf("%s")`
- **Default GCC stack protection level** = **`-fstack-protector`** (canary) or **`-fstack-protector-strong`**
- **DEP/NX bit default on modern OS** = **Enabled**
- **ASLR entropy on 64-bit Linux** = **28 bits** for stack, **28 bits** for libraries

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Program Memory Layout — The Battlefield

Every C/C++ program, when loaded into RAM, is divided into distinct **segments**. A stack overflow attack operates strictly within the **Stack segment**, but the layout context is essential to understand *why* an overflow on the stack is dangerous.

```
   LOW MEMORY ADDRESSES (0x00000000)
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │       TEXT / CODE           │  ← Compiled machine instructions (read-only)
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │     INITIALIZED DATA        │  ← Global/static variables with values
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │    UNINITIALIZED DATA (BSS) │  ← Global/static variables = 0
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │          HEAP               │  ← malloc(), new (grows UPWARD ↑)
   │            ⋮                │
   │           (free)            │
   │            ⋮                │
   │          STACK              │  ← Local variables, return addresses (grows DOWNWARD ↓)
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │   ENV / ARGS / KERNEL       │
   └─────────────────────────────┘
   HIGH MEMORY ADDRESSES (0xFFFFFFFF)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **heap grows upward** (towards higher addresses) while the **stack grows downward** (towards lower addresses). They meet in the middle. This opposing growth is what makes *heap overflow* and *stack overflow* topologically different attacks.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Stack Frame — Step by Step

When a function `victim()` is called from `main()`, a **stack frame** is pushed onto the call stack. From **low address to high address** (i.e., from top-of-stack to bottom), the frame contains:

1. **Function arguments** pushed by the caller (right-to-left in cdecl calling convention).
2. **Return address** — the address in `main()` to which execution must resume after `victim()` finishes.
3. **Saved Frame Pointer (SFP / EBP / RBP)** — the base pointer of the calling function's frame.
4. **Local variables** of `victim()`, including any buffers.

```
   HIGH ADDRESS (Bottom of Stack)
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │   Arguments to victim()      │
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   Return Address (RET)       │  ← TARGET OF OVERFLOW
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   Saved Frame Pointer (SFP)  │
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   Local Variable: int x      │
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   char buf[64]               │  ← BUFFER (overflow starts here)
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   ...                        │
   LOW ADDRESS (Top of Stack / Stack Pointer)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The attacker writes **more than 64 bytes** into `buf`. The extra bytes first overwrite `x`, then `SFP`, then — most critically — the **Return Address (RET)**. When `victim()` executes the `ret` instruction, the CPU pops the corrupted address from the stack and jumps there, redirecting execution to attacker-controlled code.

## 2.3 The 5-Stage Attack Lifecycle

A KTU examiner expects the following logical breakdown of *how* a stack overflow exploit is constructed:

1. **Reconnaissance (Vulnerability Discovery):** Identify a function using unsafe library calls such as `gets()` or `strcpy()`.
2. **Buffer Size Determination:** Disassemble the binary or use fuzzing to determine the buffer's exact byte size.
3. **Offset Calculation:** Compute the exact number of padding bytes needed to reach the return address.
4. **Payload Construction:** Build a string of the form:
$$\text{Payload} = \underbrace{\text{Padding}}_{N \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{Fake RET}}_{4 \text{ or } 8 \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{NOP Sled} + \text{Shellcode}}_{\text{attacker code}}$$

5. **Delivery & Execution:** Inject the payload as input. The `ret` instruction jumps to the NOP sled, slides into the shellcode, and grants the attacker control (typically a remote shell).

## 2.4 Defense Mechanisms (High-Yield for KTU)

| Defense | Acronym | Function | Where Implemented |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stack Canary** | — | Places a random sentinel value between buffer and RET; aborts if overwritten | Compiler (`-fstack-protector`) |
| **Address Space Layout Randomization** | **ASLR** | Randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries at each run | OS Kernel |
| **Data Execution Prevention** | **DEP / NX Bit** | Marks stack and heap as **non-executable**; CPU refuses to run code from those regions | CPU + OS |
| **Position Independent Executables** | **PIE** | Randomizes the text segment's base address (ASLR for code) | Compiler + OS |
| **Safe Library Functions** | — | Replace `gets()` → `fgets()`, `strcpy()` → `strncpy()` | Source Code |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **only quantitative relationships** required for solving stack-overflow numerical/analytical problems in KTU ESE. Memorize these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Offset to Return Address | $\text{Offset} = \text{Buffer Size} + \text{SFP Size} + \text{Alignment}$ | bytes |
| 2 | Return Address Size (32-bit) | $\vert RET \vert = 4$ | bytes |
| 3 | Return Address Size (64-bit) | $\vert RET \vert = 8$ | bytes |
| 4 | Saved Frame Pointer Size (32-bit) | $\vert SFP \vert = 4$ | bytes |
| 5 | Saved Frame Pointer Size (64-bit) | $\vert SFP \vert = 8$ | bytes |
| 6 | Total Payload Size | $\vert P \vert = \text{Offset} + \vert RET \vert + \vert \text{Shellcode} \vert$ | bytes |
| 7 | Stack Canary Size | $\vert C \vert = 4 \text{ (32-bit)} \text{ or } 8 \text{ (64-bit)}$ | bytes |
| 8 | ASLR Entropy (64-bit Linux) | $E = 2^{28}$ | possible stack bases |
| 9 | Memory Address (Little-Endian Word) | $\text{addr} = b_0 + b_1 \cdot 256 + b_2 \cdot 256^2 + b_3 \cdot 256^3$ | decimal representation |
| 10 | Address Boundary Check | $0 \leq \text{addr} \leq 2^{32}-1$ (32-bit) | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Engineering:** Mandatory secure-coding training in industries writing firmware, OS kernels, and embedded controllers.
- **Penetration Testing:** Stack overflow is the foundational exploit taught in **OSCP / CEH / CompTIA PenTest+** curricula.
- **Defensive Engineering:** The same memory-layout understanding underpins sandboxing, container isolation, and the design of Rust's ownership model (which *eliminates* this class of bug at compile time).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Offset to the Return Address

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most repeated 14-mark working** in KTU ESE for this topic. Practice writing it longhand until it becomes muscle memory.

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU-style):**
Consider a C function with the following stack frame:

```c
void vulnerable() {
    char buf[64];        // 64-byte buffer
    int  guard;          // 4-byte integer
    gets(buf);           // unsafe, no bounds check
}
```

Assume a **32-bit x86 architecture** (cdecl calling convention, no canary, no ASLR for the sake of the calculation).

**Goal:** Calculate the number of padding bytes required to overwrite the **Return Address**.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Stack Frame Layout (low → high address)

The compiler arranges the local variables in the frame such that the buffer sits at the **lowest address**, and the return address sits at the **highest address** in the frame.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lowest address (buf)}   &\longrightarrow \text{Highest address (RET)} \\
\text{buf[64]} \;\Big\vert\; \text{guard (4 B)} \;\Big\vert\; \text{SFP (4 B)} \;\Big\vert\; \text{RET (4 B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Intermediate Bytes (between buf and RET)

We must overflow `buf`, then overwrite `guard`, then overwrite `SFP`, before we can start writing into the return address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Intermediate Bytes} &= \vert \text{guard} \vert + \vert \text{SFP} \vert \\
&= 4 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 8 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Total Offset to Reach the Return Address

The offset is the number of bytes we must write *before* our fake return address begins.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= \vert \text{buf} \vert + \text{Intermediate Bytes} \\
&= 64 + 8 \\
&= 72 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Final Payload (with shellcode)

Suppose we have an 8-byte shellcode block. The total payload is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert P \vert &= \text{Offset} + \vert \text{RET} \vert + \vert \text{Shellcode} \vert \\
&= 72 + 4 + 8 \\
&= 84 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Little-Endian Conversion of the Fake Return Address

Suppose we want the CPU to jump to address `0x080491A2`. The bytes must be written to the stack in **little-endian** order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address } 0x080491A2 &= 0x08 \cdot 16^6 + 0x04 \cdot 16^4 + 0x91 \cdot 16^2 + 0xA2 \cdot 16^0 \\
&= 134512802 \text{ (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In memory (low → high byte), this address is stored as:

$$
\text{Byte stream} = \texttt{A2} \;\vert\; \texttt{91} \;\vert\; \texttt{04} \;\vert\; \texttt{08}
$$

This 4-byte sequence is appended directly after the 72-byte padding.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A C function contains `char buffer[80]`, an `int dummy = 0x41414141`, and a saved frame pointer. On a 32-bit system with no compiler protections, calculate:
> (a) The offset from the start of `buffer` to the return address.
> (b) The minimum length of input string that would overwrite the return address with at least one byte.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Offset Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= \vert \text{buffer} \vert + \vert \text{dummy} \vert + \vert \text{SFP} \vert \\
&= 80 + 4 + 4 \\
&= 88 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Minimum Length to Touch the Return Address**

To overwrite **at least the first byte** of the return address, the attacker must write 88 bytes of padding plus 1 byte. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\min} &= \text{Offset} + 1 \\
&= 88 + 1 \\
&= 89 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Attackers vs. Defenders

| Layer | Attacker Action | Defender Countermeasure | Effect of Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source Code | Inject `> 80` bytes into `buffer` via `gets()` | Replace with `fgets(buf, 80, stdin)` | Overflow becomes *impossible* by construction |
| Compile Time | Rely on fixed stack layout | Insert **stack canary** between buffer and SFP | Program aborts before `ret` executes |
| Link Time | Hardcode `jmp esp` gadgets | Enable **PIE** + **ASLR** | Gadget address becomes unpredictable |
| Run Time | Place shellcode on stack | Enable **DEP / NX bit** | CPU raises #PF exception, no code execution |
| OS Kernel | Brute-force ASLR | Increase ASLR entropy to 28+ bits | Brute-force time becomes infeasible |
| Hardware | Use ROP chains to call `mprotect()` | **Intel CET** (Shadow Stack + IBT) | Return addresses are *hardware-verified* |

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive End-to-End Exploit Walkthrough

**Step A — The Vulnerable Source Code (fictitious KTU lab example):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
void greet() {
    char name[32];
    gets(name);                       // vulnerable
    printf("Hello, %s!\n", name);
}
int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    greet();
    return 0;
}
```

**Step B — Compilation (assume no protections for the lab):**

```
gcc -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -no-pie -o vuln vuln.c
```

**Step C — Disassembly (relevant portion of `greet()`):**

```
sub    esp, 0x28           ; allocate 40 bytes on stack (32 buf + alignment)
lea    eax, [ebp-0x20]     ; address of name[32]
mov    [esp], eax
call   gets                ; unsafe call
...
leave                      ; mov esp, ebp; pop ebp
ret                        ; pops the (now-corrupted) return address
```

**Step D — Compute Offset (32-bit, 4-byte SFP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= 32 + 4 = 36 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Construct the Python-style Payload (described in words, since this is not a CS coding module):**

The payload is conceptually:

$$
\text{Payload} = \underbrace{\texttt{"A" \times 36}}_{\text{padding}} + \underbrace{\texttt{0xFFFFD0E0}}_{\text{fake RET (little-endian)}} + \underbrace{\texttt{NOP sled} + \texttt{shellcode}}_{\text{attacker code}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fake return address points into the NOP sled (a long sequence of the `0x90` x86 opcode, which is a harmless "do nothing" instruction). The CPU slides through the NOPs and lands in the shellcode, which — for example — invokes `execve("/bin/sh", NULL, NULL)` to spawn a shell.

**Step F — Execution Outcome:**

On a system with **no protections**, the attacker gains a shell. On a hardened system with **ASLR + NX + Canary**, the same payload is either:
- Refused by the CPU (NX → page fault),
- Rejected by the program (canary mismatch → `__stack_chk_fail`),
- Misdirected by address randomization (RET points to garbage).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stack Overflow Exploit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Attacker Crafts Malicious Input] --> B[Identify Vulnerable Function e.g. gets]
    B --> C[Compute Offset Buffer + SFP bytes]
    C --> D[Build Payload: Padding + Fake RET + NOP Sled + Shellcode]
    D --> E[Deliver Payload as Input to Target Program]
    E --> F[gets copies payload onto stack]
    F --> G[Buffer Overflow: extra bytes overwrite SFP and RET]
    G --> H[victim function executes ret instruction]
    H --> I{RET address corrupted}
    I --> J[CPU pops fake RET from stack]
    J --> K[Control transfers to NOP sled]
    K --> L[Slides into Shellcode]
    L --> M[Shellcode executes e.g. execve /bin/sh]
    M --> N[Attacker gains remote shell on victim host]

    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style N fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000000
    style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000000
    style G fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style M fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Memory Layout Diagram — Before and After Overflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Overflow: Normal Stack Frame]
        direction TB
        B1[Higher Address: Return Address = 0x080491F6]
        B2[Saved Frame Pointer = 0xFFFFD120]
        B3[Local Var: int x = 5]
        B4[Buffer: buf = Hello, 0 bytes used]
    end

    subgraph AFTER[After Overflow: Corrupted Stack Frame]
        direction TB
        A1[Higher Address: Return Address = 0xFFFFD0E0 OVERWRITTEN]
        A2[Saved Frame Pointer = 0x41414141 OVERWRITTEN]
        A3[Local Var: int x = 0x41414141 OVERWRITTEN]
        A4[Buffer: buf = AAAA...AAAA 80 bytes OVERFLOWED]
    end

    BEFORE -.gets input.-> AFTER

    style B1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style B3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style B4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style A1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style A2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style A4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Source Code Level] --> SC[Use fgets, strncpy, snprintf]
    CT[Compile Time] --> CO[Enable Stack Canary -fstack-protector]
    CT --> PIE[Enable PIE Position Independent Executable]
    LT[Link Time] --> RELRO[Enable Full RELRO]
    RT[Run Time - OS] --> ASLR[Randomize Stack Heap Lib bases]
    RT --> NX[DEP NX Bit Non-executable memory]
    HW[Hardware Level] --> CET[Intel CET Shadow Stack + IBT]

    ATT[Attacker Payload] --> S
    S --> CT
    CT --> LT
    LT --> RT
    RT --> HW
    HW --> BLOCK[Overflow Attack Blocked]

    style ATT fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style BLOCK fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style SC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style CO fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style PIE fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style RELRO fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style ASLR fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style NX fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style CET fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Stack Frame Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME[Function Stack Frame - Low to High Address]
        direction TB
        SP[Stack Pointer - Lowest Address] --> BUF[char buffer 32 to 256 bytes]
        BUF --> LOC[Other Local Variables]
        LOC --> SFP[Saved Frame Pointer 4 or 8 bytes]
        SFP --> RET[Return Address 4 or 8 bytes CRITICAL TARGET]
        RET --> ARG[Function Arguments]
        ARG --> BP[Base Pointer - Highest Address]
    end

    RET -.overwritten by.-> OV[Attacker Payload Bytes]
    OV -.redirects CPU to.-> SC[Shellcode in NOP Sled]

    style RET fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style OV fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style SC fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style BUF fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style SFP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
**Define stack overflow attack. List any two unsafe C library functions that enable it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **stack overflow attack** is a memory-corruption exploit in which a program writes more data into a stack-allocated buffer than the buffer was sized to hold, causing the excess data to overwrite adjacent stack memory — including the saved frame pointer and the function's return address. This allows the attacker to redirect the program's control flow to attacker-supplied code.
>
> Two unsafe C library functions:
> 1. **`gets()`** — reads input from stdin with no length limit.
> 2. **`strcpy()`** — copies a source string into a destination buffer with no length limit.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two functions.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Differentiate between Stack Overflow and Heap Overflow attacks (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Stack Overflow | Heap Overflow |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Targets the **call stack** (local variables, return addresses). | Targets the **heap** (dynamically allocated memory via `malloc`/`new`). |
| 2 | Overwrites the **return address** to hijack control flow. | Overwrites **heap metadata** (chunk headers, free-list pointers) to corrupt allocator state. |
| 3 | Easier to exploit for **code execution** (direct control over RET). | Harder to leverage for code execution; typically used for **arbitrary write** or to corrupt adjacent objects. |

*(1 mark per valid point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module-level questions follow the **internal choice** pattern. You are given two alternatives; you attempt **one** of them. Each alternative is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the layout of a typical process address space in Linux. Identify the segment in which a stack overflow occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step procedure by which an attacker exploits a stack-based buffer overflow. How is the offset to the return address calculated? Derive the offset for a function containing `char buffer[100]`, `int flag = 0`, and a 32-bit saved frame pointer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Process Address Space Layout (7 marks)**

The Linux process address space is divided into distinct segments (low → high address):

- **Text Segment:** Contains the compiled machine instructions of the program. Marked read-only and executable.
- **Initialized Data Segment:** Stores global and static variables that are explicitly initialized.
- **Uninitialized Data Segment (BSS):** Stores global and static variables that default to zero.
- **Heap Segment:** Region used for dynamic memory allocation via `malloc()`, `calloc()`, and `new`. The heap **grows upward** towards higher addresses as more memory is allocated.
- **Stack Segment:** Region used for function-call management, local variables, and return addresses. The stack **grows downward** towards lower addresses. Each function call pushes a new **stack frame** containing the return address, saved frame pointer, and local variables.

> A **stack overflow** occurs in the **Stack Segment** when a buffer declared as a local variable is overrun.

*(4 marks for diagram, 2 marks for segment explanation, 1 mark for identifying the stack segment.)*

---

**Part (b) — Exploitation Steps and Offset Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step exploitation procedure:**

1. **Identify vulnerability:** Locate a C function using unsafe input routines (`gets`, `strcpy`, `sprintf`).
2. **Determine buffer size:** Disassemble the binary or use static analysis.
3. **Compute offset:** Calculate the number of bytes between the buffer's start and the return address's location in the stack frame.
4. **Craft payload:** Construct input as `padding + fake_RET + NOP_sled + shellcode`.
5. **Deliver and hijack:** Feed the payload; the corrupted return address redirects execution to the shellcode, granting the attacker control.

**Offset Calculation for the given function (32-bit, 4-byte SFP):**

The function's stack frame contains, from low to high address:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{buffer[100]} \;\Big\vert\; \text{flag (4 B)} \;\Big\vert\; \text{SFP (4 B)} \;\Big\vert\; \text{RET (4 B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Bytes between the start of `buffer` and the start of `RET`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= \vert \text{buffer} \vert + \vert \text{flag} \vert + \vert \text{SFP} \vert \\
&= 100 + 4 + 4 \\
&= 108 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the attacker must write 108 bytes of padding, followed by a 4-byte fake return address (in little-endian format), to overwrite the return address.

*(3 marks for procedure, 3 marks for derivation, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** What is a stack canary? Explain how it detects a stack overflow attack. Mention the compiler flag used to enable it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the roles of **ASLR** and **DEP/NX bit** in defending against stack overflow attacks. How does the **Intel CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology)** provide hardware-level protection? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Stack Canary (7 marks)**

A **stack canary** (or *stack cookie*) is a small, randomly generated integer value placed by the compiler between a function's local buffer and its saved frame pointer / return address. The canary is unknown to the attacker and is checked immediately before the function returns.

**Detection Mechanism:**

1. At function entry, the compiler generates code to place a random canary value on the stack just *above* the local buffer.
2. The function's local variables (including the buffer) are placed *below* the canary.
3. Before executing `ret`, the compiler inserts a check: if the canary value has changed, it calls `__stack_chk_fail()`, which terminates the process with an error.
4. A normal buffer overflow that overwrites the return address must also overwrite the canary, changing its value and triggering the abort.

**Compiler flag:** **`-fstack-protector`** (GCC). Variants include `-fstack-protector-strong` and `-fstack-protector-all`.

*(3 marks for definition, 3 marks for mechanism, 1 mark for the compiler flag.)*

---

**Part (b) — ASLR, DEP, and Intel CET (7 marks)**

**ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization):**
- Implemented at the **OS kernel level**.
- At every program load, the kernel randomizes the **base addresses** of the stack, heap, and shared libraries.
- On 64-bit Linux, ASLR provides approximately **28 bits of entropy** for the stack, making brute-force prediction infeasible.
- An attacker can no longer hardcode a "jump to NOP sled" address, since the target address differs on every run.

**DEP / NX Bit (Data Execution Prevention / No-Execute):**
- A hardware + OS feature that **marks stack and heap pages as non-executable**.
- If the CPU's instruction pointer ever tries to fetch and execute an opcode from a non-executable page, a **#PF (Page Fault)** exception is raised, and the OS terminates the offending process.
- DEP renders classic shellcode-on-the-stack attacks infeasible.

**Intel CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology):**
- A **hardware-level** extension introduced in newer Intel and AMD processors.
- Provides two protections:
  1. **Shadow Stack:** A separate, hardware-protected stack stores an exact copy of every return address. On `ret`, the CPU verifies that the return address popped from the data stack matches the one in the shadow stack. A mismatch triggers a **#CP (Control Protection)** exception.
  2. **Indirect Branch Tracking (IBT):** Validates indirect jump and call targets using `ENDBR64/ENDBR32` instructions.
- Even a sophisticated ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) attack is blocked because the attacker cannot forge a valid return address on the shadow stack.

*(2 marks for ASLR, 2 marks for DEP/NX, 3 marks for Intel CET.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU ESE for Stack Overflow:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Saved Frame Pointer (SFP) in the offset calculation** — losing 1 to 2 marks. Always include the 4-byte (32-bit) or 8-byte (64-bit) SFP in your offset.
> 2. **Writing the return address in big-endian format** — the x86 architecture is little-endian; writing the address byte-reversed gives an incorrect exploit. State explicitly "little-endian" in your answer.
> 3. **Confusing stack overflow with stack data structure overflow** — the KTU examiner interprets the term in the **security context**, not the data-structures context.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — a stack frame diagram is worth at least 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. Always draw one.
> 5. **Not mentioning defenses** — even if the question focuses on the attack, a brief mention of canaries, ASLR, and DEP earns bonus comprehension marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition:** Stack overflow = writing past a stack-allocated buffer to overwrite the return address.
- **Primary target:** The 4-byte (32-bit) or 8-byte (64-bit) **return address** in the function's stack frame.
- **Unsafe functions to remember:** `gets()`, `strcpy()`, `sprintf()`, `scanf("%s")`.
- **Safe replacements:** `fgets()`, `strncpy()`, `snprintf()`, `scanf("%31s", …)`.
- **Offset formula (32-bit):** $\text{Offset} = \vert \text{buffer} \vert + \text{variable sizes} + 4_{\text{SFP}}$.
- **Offset formula (64-bit):** $\text{Offset} = \vert \text{buffer} \vert + \text{variable sizes} + 8_{\text{SFP}}$.
- **Payload anatomy:** Padding + Fake RET (little-endian) + NOP Sled (0x90) + Shellcode.
- **Memory segments:** Text → Data → BSS → Heap (↑) ↔ Stack (↓) → Env/Args.
- **Stack frame order (low → high):** Buffer → Local Vars → SFP → RET → Arguments.
- **Defenses (4 essential):** Stack Canary, ASLR, DEP/NX, Intel CET Shadow Stack.
- **Compiler flags:** `-fstack-protector`, `-fno-stack-protector`, `-z execstack`, `-no-pie`, `-fpie`.
- **Hardware flag:** NX bit in the page-table entry.
- **Real-world examples:** Morris Worm (1988), Code Red (2001), Blaster (2003).
- **CWE classification:** **CWE-121 — Stack-based Buffer Overflow**.
- **Exam mantra:** Always draw a labelled stack-frame diagram; always state "little-endian"; always include SFP in the offset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Format String
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Format String

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Format String Vulnerability** is a class of software vulnerability that occurs when an application uses *unfiltered* or *unsanitized* user input as the **format string argument** of a C-family function from the `printf` family (e.g., `printf`, `fprintf`, `sprintf`, `snprintf`, `syslog`). The attacker crafts an input containing malicious **format specifiers** (e.g., `%x`, `%s`, `%n`) that cause the program to either leak memory contents from the process stack or perform arbitrary memory writes, leading to information disclosure, denial of service, or full remote code execution.

The root cause lies in the C calling convention for variadic functions. The compiler does **not** know at compile time how many arguments the format string will consume, so it pushes *potential arguments* onto the stack in advance. When a user-controlled string is used directly, those "potential arguments" are actually whatever happens to be on the stack — registers, return addresses, local variables — which the attacker can read or overwrite.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postman and the Form Letter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a post office clerk. Your job is to read a "form letter" template and fill in the blanks (named `%d`, `%s`, `%x`). Normally, the letter arrives with a sealed envelope containing the actual values to be plugged in — these are the trusted *arguments*.

A **format string vulnerability** happens when a stranger hands you a *handwritten* letter and tells you, "Just read this out loud to the recipient — no envelope, no values provided." Being an obedient clerk, you start reading: "Dear `%x`, your account `%s` has been debited by `%n`..." Without realizing it, you start grabbing whatever lies on your desk (scratch pads, diaries, confidential files) to satisfy each `%x` and `%n` placeholder. The stranger has effectively turned you into a **memory-leaking and memory-writing machine** — exactly what a format string exploit does to a vulnerable C program.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Format Specifier** — A token beginning with `%` that tells `printf` how to interpret and print the next argument on the stack (e.g., `%d`, `%s`, `%n`).
>
> **Variadic Function** — A function that accepts a variable number of arguments (declared with `...` in C). `printf(const char *format, ...)` is the canonical example.
>
> **Format String Exploit** — A payload constructed by the attacker that abuses format specifiers to read or write arbitrary process memory.
>
> **Stack Smashing (variant)** — Format string attacks that use `%n` to overwrite the return address or a function pointer on the stack, achieving control-flow hijack without requiring a traditional buffer overflow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the `printf` Family

The C standard library exposes a family of formatted output functions whose first argument is always a **format control string**. The C99/POSIX signatures are:

$$\text{printf}(\text{const char}* \text{ format}, \dots)$$

$$\text{fprintf}(\text{FILE}* \text{ stream}, \text{const char}* \text{ format}, \dots)$$

$$\text{sprintf}(\text{char}* \text{ str}, \text{const char}* \text{ format}, \dots)$$

$$\text{snprintf}(\text{char}* \text{ str}, \text{size\_t size}, \text{const char}* \text{ format}, \dots)$$

The function follows a strict operational contract:

1. The **format string** is read from left to right.
2. Whenever a literal character is encountered, it is copied to the output.
3. Whenever a `%` is encountered, the next *value on the stack* is consumed and rendered according to the *conversion specifier*.
4. The function does **not** verify whether a corresponding argument actually exists — it blindly pops values from the stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Security Insight:** Because `printf` does not know how many arguments were *actually* passed, it will keep popping values from the stack — including stack frames, return addresses, and unrelated local variables — until the format string is exhausted. The attacker simply *is* the format string.

## 2.2 Common Format Specifiers Used in Exploitation

| Specifier | Function in Normal Use | Abused Behavior in Vulnerability |
|:---------|:----------------------|:---------------------------------|
| `%d`     | Print next 4 bytes as signed decimal integer | Leaks the next stack value as a decimal number |
| `%u`     | Print next 4 bytes as unsigned integer | Leaks the next stack value (unsigned) |
| `%x`     | Print next 4 bytes as lowercase hexadecimal | Leaks the next stack value in hex — ideal for reconnaissance |
| `%p`     | Print pointer value | Leaks a pointer-sized value (8 bytes on 64-bit) |
| `%s`     | Print string at next stack address | Reads memory at attacker-controlled address (info disclosure) |
| `%n`     | **None in safe code** — stores byte-count written so far into next stack location | **Arbitrary 4-byte write primitive** — the most dangerous specifier |
| `%hn`    | Variant of `%n` writing 2 bytes | Arbitrary 2-byte write (used to avoid bad chars) |
| `%hhn`   | Variant of `%n` writing 1 byte | Arbitrary 1-byte write (used in modern exploits) |
| `%lx`, `%ld` | 64-bit variants on LP64 systems | Leak 8-byte stack slots on Linux x86-64 |

## 2.3 Stack Layout During a Vulnerable Call

Consider the following vulnerable C snippet:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    printf(argv[1]);
    return 0;
}
```

When compiled (32-bit x86, GCC), the stack at the moment of the `printf` call looks like this:

| Stack Address (grows down) | Content | Role in Exploitation |
|:---------------------------|:--------|:---------------------|
| `[ebp+8]`  | Pointer to `argv[1]` | **The format string** — attacker-controlled |
| `[ebp+4]`  | Saved return address | May be read with `%x` or overwritten with `%n` |
| `[ebp]`    | Saved EBP | Local frame pointer |
| `[ebp-4]`  | `argc` (often) | Leaked by first `%x` |
| `[ebp-8]` … | Other locals / padding | Attacker reads up the stack using repeated `%x` |

On **64-bit (LP64) Linux**, the first six arguments are passed in **registers** (`rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9`) per the System V AMD64 ABI. Only arguments beyond the sixth land on the stack. Therefore:

- `%1$p` to `%5$p` → reads from `rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9` (registers)
- `%6$p` and above → reads from the caller's stack frame

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The attacker must first determine the *offset* of his injected payload on the stack. A common technique is to send `AAAA%6$p%7$p...` and look for the output `0x41414141` — that index is the *direct parameter access offset* and is the target for `%n` writes.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the table below as your one-page revision sheet. All symbols are KTU-board standard.

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|:--------|:-----------------|:-------------|
| Number of stack bytes consumed by a single `%x` on 32-bit | $\Delta_{32} = 4$ bytes | One full word |
| Number of stack bytes consumed by a single `%lx` on 64-bit | $\Delta_{64} = 8$ bytes | One full qword |
| Output count before `%n` triggers write | $N = \text{(number of printed chars so far)}$ | Bytes written to stdout |
| Value written by `%n` to target address | $V = N$ | 4-byte write by default |
| Value written by `%hn` to target address | $V = N \bmod 2^{16}$ | 2-byte write |
| Value written by `%hhn` to target address | $V = N \bmod 2^{8}$ | 1-byte write |
| Direct parameter access (POSIX) | `%k\$x` reads the $k$-th argument | Avoids consuming intermediate slots |
| Pointer size on LP64 | $\vert p \vert = 8$ bytes | Used for 64-bit address packing |
| Pointer size on ILP32 | $\vert p \vert = 4$ bytes | Used for 32-bit address packing |
| Defensive constant — never trust user input | Always use `printf("%s", user_input)` | Eliminates the vulnerability entirely |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Format string vulnerabilities have been weaponized in:

- **CVE-2000-0573** — WU-FTPD 2.6.0 `site exec` format string (remote root).
- **CVE-2012-1823** — PHP `php.cgi` query string parsing format bug.
- **CVE-2018-6789** — Exim MTA base64-decoder format string (pre-auth RCE).
- **Modern IoT firmware** — many routers still ship MIPS/ARM binaries with `syslog(user_input)` calls.

The same primitive powers the offensive tools **Metasploit `format_string` mixin**, **pwntools `fmtstr_payload`**, and detection in **Coverity**, **CodeQL**, and **Semgrep** static analyzers. Every KTU cybersecurity graduate is expected to recognize this class on sight during code review.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Derivation

## 3.1 Worked Example — Discovering the Stack Offset (32-bit)

Consider the program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    printf(argv[1]);
    return 0;
}
```

**Goal:** Find which positional argument index points to memory the attacker controls.

**Step 1 — Inject a probe string.** The attacker runs:

```
./vuln AAAA%8$x
```

**Step 2 — Observe output.** The terminal prints:

```
AAAA41414141
```

**Step 3 — Interpret the output.**

- The literal `AAAA` is the four printable characters `0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41`.
- The output `41414141` is the hexadecimal representation of those same four bytes.
- This means the **8th positional argument** to `printf` was the attacker's buffer itself.

**Step 4 — Generalize.** On this binary, the attacker's input is at direct parameter index $k = 8$. Any payload supplied at this offset can be referenced with `%8$`.

> [!NOTE]
> On a 64-bit binary the offset is typically larger (often $k = 6, 7, 8$ depending on register usage). Use `%p` repeatedly or `AAAA%6$p%7$p%8$p%9$p%10$p%11$p` and search for the `0x4141414141414141` sentinel to locate the offset.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Arbitrary Memory Read (Information Disclosure)

Suppose the attacker has determined $k = 8$ and wishes to read a 4-byte value from a *known* memory address $A$ (e.g., the saved return address of a privileged function, or a flag string).

**Step 1 — Construct a payload that places $A$ on the stack at the right slot.**

The attacker's buffer (32-bit) is:

$$\text{buf} = \underbrace{A_{b3}\,A_{b2}\,A_{b1}\,A_{b0}}_{4 \text{ bytes of address}} \,\|\, \text{"%8\$s"}$$

where $\|$ denotes string concatenation. The first 4 bytes are the raw little-endian address; the format specifier `%8$s` will then read a string starting at memory address $A$.

**Step 2 — Submit the payload.**

```bash
./vuln $(python2 -c 'import sys; sys.stdout.write("\x90\x84\x04\x08" + "%8$s")')
```

**Step 3 — The program executes:**

$$\text{printf}("{\tt 0x90, 0x84, 0x04, 0x08, \%8\$s}") \; \text{(conceptually)}$$

`printf` consumes the first 4 bytes as the format string, encounters `%8$s`, fetches the 8th argument (which equals the 4 bytes of the address $A$ just read as a pointer), dereferences it, and prints the resulting C string.

**Step 4 — Result.** The secret value at $A$ is leaked to stdout. The attacker now has an *arbitrary read* primitive.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Arbitrary Memory Write (4-byte `%n`)

This is the core primitive for control-flow hijack.

**Setup:** The attacker wants to write the value $V = 0x00000001$ into a target memory address $T$ (e.g., a saved return address slot, a `__free_hook` pointer in glibc, or a C++ vtable entry).

**Step 1 — Choose a write size.** We will use `%hhn` (1-byte writes) to avoid generating extremely long output.

**Step 2 — Layout the payload.**

The payload buffer (32-bit) is structured as:

$$
\text{buf} = T_{b3} T_{b2} T_{b1} T_{b0} \,\|\, \text{"%"} \, x_1 \, \text{"c%"} \, k \, \text{"\$hhn"} \,\|\, T'_{b3} T'_{b2} T'_{b1} T'_{b0} \,\|\, \text{"%"} \, x_2 \, \text{"c%"} \, (k{+}1) \, \text{"\$hhn"} \,\|\, \dots
$$

Each group `[4 address bytes] [%Xc%K$hhn]` writes one byte of the desired 32-bit value.

**Step 3 — Compute $x_i$ for each byte.**

Let $b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3$ be the four bytes of $V$ (little-endian). Let $N_{i-1}$ be the total number of bytes already printed before the $i$-th `%hhn`. Then the required padding for the $i$-th byte is:

$$
x_i = (b_i - N_{i-1}) \bmod 2^{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the closed-form for a 4-byte write.**

The cumulative byte count before each `%hhn` write is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= 0 \\
N_1 &= 4 + x_1 \\
N_2 &= 4 + x_1 + 4 + x_2 = 8 + x_1 + x_2 \\
N_3 &= 4 + x_1 + 4 + x_2 + 4 + x_3 = 12 + x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \\
N_4 &= 4 + x_1 + 4 + x_2 + 4 + x_3 + 4 + x_4 = 16 + x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4
\end{aligned}
$$

We require $N_i \equiv b_i \pmod{2^{8}}$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$. Solving iteratively (because $x_i \in [0, 255]$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= (b_1 - 4) \bmod 256 \\
x_2 &= (b_2 - 4 - x_1) \bmod 256 \\
x_3 &= (b_3 - 4 - x_1 - x_2) \bmod 256 \\
x_4 &= (b_4 - 4 - x_1 - x_2 - x_3) \bmod 256
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **canonical `%hhn` padding equations** for format-string exploit construction. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to derive them — they are the algebraic heart of the topic.

**Step 5 — Numerical demonstration.** Let $T = 0x0804a020$ and $V = 0x00000100$ (write the byte `\x01\x00\x00\x00` at address $T$). Then:

- $b_0 = 0x00$, $b_1 = 0x00$, $b_2 = 0x01$, $b_3 = 0x00$
- $x_1 = (0 - 4) \bmod 256 = 252$
- $x_2 = (0 - 4 - 252) \bmod 256 = 0$
- $x_3 = (1 - 4 - 252 - 0) \bmod 256 = 1$
- $x_4 = (0 - 4 - 252 - 0 - 1) \bmod 256 = 255$

The constructed payload is:

```
\x20\xa0\x04\x08 %252c%8$hhn \x21\xa0\x04\x08 %0c%9$hhn \x22\xa0\x04\x08 %1c%10$hhn \x23\xa0\x04\x08 %255c%11$hhn
```

> [!NOTE]
> The address is incremented by 1 between writes because each `%hhn` writes one byte and the next address slot points at the next byte of the 4-byte target word.

## 3.4 Summary of the Exploit Lifecycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Reconnaissance} &\rightarrow \text{Inject probe strings, locate offset } k \\
\text{2. Read Primitive} &\rightarrow \text{Use } \%k\$s \text{ or } \%k\$p \text{ to leak memory} \\
\text{3. Write Primitive} &\rightarrow \text{Use } \%k\$n / \%k\$hn / \%k\$hhn \text{ to overwrite memory} \\
\text{4. Control-Flow Hijack} &\rightarrow \text{Overwrite return address / GOT / vtable / \_\_free\_hook} \\
\text{5. Shell} &\rightarrow \text{Transfer execution to attacker-supplied shellcode}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Format String Attack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Crafts Malicious Input] --> B[Vulnerable C Program Invokes printf]
    B --> C{Is User Input Used Directly as Format String?}
    C -- Yes --> D[printf Walks the Format String]
    C -- No --> E[Safe Execution - No Vulnerability]
    D --> F[Encounter Percent Specifier]
    F --> G{Which Specifier Type?}
    G -- percent x or percent p or percent s --> H[Stack Read Primitive - Information Disclosure]
    G -- percent n or percent hn or percent hhn --> I[Stack Write Primitive - Arbitrary Memory Write]
    H --> J[Attacker Captures Leaked Data]
    I --> K[Attacker Overwrites GOT or Return Address or Hook Pointer]
    K --> L[Control Flow Redirected to Attacker Code]
    L --> M[Remote Code Execution Achieved]
    J --> N[Reconnaissance - Identify Sensitive Addresses]
    N --> I
    E --> O[Program Returns Normally]
```

## 4.2 Stack Memory Layout During Vulnerable `printf` Call

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighAddr["High Memory Addresses"]
        HA1[argv Pointer]
        HA2[argc Value]
        HA3[Return Address of main]
        HA4[Saved EBP of main]
    end

    subgraph FormatString["Format String Region - Attacker Controlled"]
        FS1[Address Bytes 0x0804a020]
        FS2[Percent 252c Percent 8 dollar hhn]
        FS3[Address Bytes 0x0804a021]
        FS4[Percent 0c Percent 9 dollar hhn]
    end

    subgraph LowAddr["Low Memory Addresses"]
        LA1[Stack Canary]
        LA2[Local Variable argc]
        LA3[Local Variable argv Pointer]
    end

    HA1 --> HA2 --> HA3 --> HA4 --> FS1 --> FS2 --> FS3 --> FS4 --> LA1 --> LA2 --> LA3

    style HighAddr fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style FormatString fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style LowAddr fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003399
```

## 4.3 Exploit Decision Flow — From Discovery to Shell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Source Code Review] --> S2[Step 2: Identify printf Family with User Input]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compile and Test Binary]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Send Probe AAAA Percent k dollar x]
    S4 --> S5{See 41414141 in Output?}
    S5 -- No --> S4
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5: Offset k Confirmed]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Build Read Payload using Percent k dollar s]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Leak Target Address Value]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Build Write Payload using Percent k dollar hhn]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Overwrite Function Pointer or Return Address]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Trigger Hijacked Function]
    S11 --> S12[Step 11: Shellcode Executes - Root Shell Obtained]

    style S1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    style S6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style S12 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Format String Vulnerability. Give one example of a vulnerable C statement and its safe equivalent.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Format String Vulnerability* is a coding defect where untrusted user input is passed directly as the format control string of a `printf`-family function, allowing attackers to inject format specifiers that read or write arbitrary process memory.

**Vulnerable statement:**

```c
printf(argv[1]);
```

**Safe equivalent:**

```c
printf("%s", argv[1]);
```

The safe version treats the user input purely as data, never as a format directive. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for vulnerable example, 1 for safe fix.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the role of the `%n` format specifier? Why is it considered the most dangerous specifier from a security standpoint?**

**Model Answer:**

The `%n` specifier does **not** print anything. Instead, it stores the number of characters written to the output so far into the memory address pointed to by the corresponding argument. For example, `printf("AB%n", &count);` sets `count = 2`.

It is the most dangerous specifier because an attacker controlling the format string can:

1. Place an arbitrary address on the stack using payload bytes.
2. Use `%n` to write a 4-byte integer value (or `%hn`/`%hhn` for 2/1 byte) into that address.
3. Overwrite critical pointers such as GOT entries, return addresses, or `__free_hook`, leading to arbitrary code execution.

Because there is **no legitimate use case** for `%n` in modern secure C coding, most production codebases ban it entirely via compiler flags like `-Wformat-security`. **(3 marks: 1 for behaviour, 1 for exploitation use, 1 for defensive note.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Format String vulnerability in detail. Discuss the operational mechanism of `%n`, `%x`, and `%s` when used in an attack. Also explain stack layout during a vulnerable call and list three defensive measures.**

#### Part (a) — Mechanism and Stack Layout (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the vulnerability** (1 mark).
A Format String Vulnerability arises when user input is used as the format argument of `printf`, `fprintf`, `sprintf`, or `syslog`. The function interprets any `%` tokens in the input as format specifiers and consumes whatever data is on the stack to satisfy them.

**Step 2 — Explain the C calling convention** (2 marks).
In C, variadic arguments are passed on the stack (32-bit) or in registers and on the stack (64-bit). The callee has no way of knowing how many were actually supplied; it relies entirely on the format string to dictate consumption. Thus a manipulated format string is the attacker's primary weapon.

**Step 3 — Draw the stack layout** (3 marks).
```
        [Higher Addresses]
        |  argv[1] pointer |
        |  argc            |
        |  saved EIP       |  ← Attacker can leak with %x
        |  saved EBP       |
        |  format string   |  ← Attacker controlled
        |  <padding>       |
        |  local vars      |  ← Read by %x, overwritten by %n
        [Lower Addresses]
```

**Step 4 — Explain `%x` and `%s` exploitation** (1 mark).
- `%x` consumes 4 bytes from the next stack slot and prints them as hex — useful for reconnaissance.
- `%s` consumes 4 bytes (interpreted as a pointer) and prints the C string at that address — used for arbitrary read.

#### Part (b) — `%n` Exploitation and Defences (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Behaviour of `%n`** (2 marks).
`%n` writes the integer count of characters printed so far into the memory location pointed to by the next stack argument. Variants `%hn` (2 bytes) and `%hhn` (1 byte) allow fine-grained writes.

**Step 2 — Write primitive construction** (3 marks).
The attacker places the *target address* on the stack (typically in his own input buffer) and uses direct parameter access (`%k$n`) to write a value into it. The padding equations are:

$$
x_i = (b_i - 4i - \sum_{j < i} x_j) \bmod 256
$$

where $b_i$ is the $i$-th byte of the desired 32-bit value. This produces a 4-address + 4-format-specifier payload that overwrites the target memory atomically.

**Step 3 — Three defensive measures** (2 marks).
1. **Coding standard:** Always use `printf("%s", user_input)`, never `printf(user_input)`.
2. **Compiler hardening:** Use `-Wformat-security` and `-Werror=format-security` in GCC/Clang; enable `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2`.
3. **Static analysis & banning:** Use tools like `Coverity`, `CodeQL`, or `Semgrep`; forbid `%n` in glibc with `FORTIFY_SOURCE`; employ `grsecurity`/`PaX`/`RELRO` (Full RELRO) to make GOT read-only after process startup.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a) Explain how an attacker discovers the direct parameter access offset of his buffer on the stack during a format string attack. Provide the probing technique with an example payload for both 32-bit and 64-bit binaries.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Construct a complete format string exploit payload to overwrite a 4-byte value `0x00000100` at address `0x0804a020` using `%hhn`, given the buffer is at direct parameter index $k = 8$. Show all padding calculations explicitly. Also state two real-world CVEs that exploited format string bugs.** (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Offset Discovery (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — The sentinel technique** (2 marks).
The attacker injects a known 4-byte or 8-byte pattern (the *sentinel*) at the start of his input, followed by a sequence of `%p` or `%x` placeholders. The classic pattern is `AAAA` (4 bytes) or `AAAAAAAA` (8 bytes).

**Step 2 — 32-bit example** (2 marks).
Payload: `AAAA%8$x`.
If the output is `AAAA41414141`, the 8th argument equals `0x41414141`, confirming the attacker's buffer is at direct parameter index $k = 8$ (32-bit).

**Step 3 — 64-bit example** (3 marks).
On 64-bit Linux, the first 5 variadic args live in registers (`rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9`), so the stack offset is typically higher:

```
Payload: AAAAAAAA%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p
Output : AAAAAAAA0x7ffd...0x7ffd...0x4141414141414141
```

When the output reveals `0x4141414141414141` at the 9th placeholder, the buffer is at direct parameter index $k = 9$ on that binary. A faster technique is direct-access: `AAAAAAAA%9$p` which yields `0x4141414141414141` in one shot.

#### Part (b) — Complete Payload Construction (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Target address $T = 0x0804a020$
- Desired 4-byte value $V = 0x00000100$
- Buffer offset $k = 8$
- Write method: `%hhn` (1-byte writes)

**Step 1 — Decompose value into bytes (little-endian)** (1 mark).
$$b_0 = 0x00, \quad b_1 = 0x00, \quad b_2 = 0x01, \quad b_3 = 0x00$$

**Step 2 — Compute padding using the canonical formula** (3 marks).

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= (b_1 - 4) \bmod 256 = (0 - 4) \bmod 256 = 252 \\
x_2 &= (b_2 - 4 - x_1) \bmod 256 = (1 - 4 - 252) \bmod 256 = 1 \\
x_3 &= (b_3 - 4 - x_1 - x_2) \bmod 256 = (0 - 4 - 252 - 1) \bmod 256 = 3 \\
x_4 &= (b_4 - 4 - x_1 - x_2 - x_3) \bmod 256 = (0 - 4 - 252 - 1 - 3) \bmod 256 = 252
\end{aligned}
$$

(For `$b_4$` we treat the un-specified upper byte as `0x00`.)

**Step 3 — Construct the payload** (2 marks).

```
\x20\xa0\x04\x08 %252c%8$hhn
\x21\xa0\x04\x08 %1c%9$hhn
\x22\xa0\x04\x08 %3c%10$hhn
\x23\xa0\x04\x08 %252c%11$hhn
```

Each `[4 address bytes]` places the next sequential address at the corresponding `%k$` slot, and each `%Xc%K$hhn` writes exactly one byte of the value.

**Step 4 — Real-world CVEs** (1 mark).
1. **CVE-2000-0573** — WU-FTPD 2.6.0 `site exec` format string; allowed remote root.
2. **CVE-2018-6789** — Exim MTA base64-decoder format string; pre-auth remote code execution on millions of mail servers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Format String questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `%s` (read) with `%n` (write).** Examiners expect students to clearly state that `%s` only *leaks* memory, while `%n` *writes* to memory. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the little-endian byte order** when placing target addresses in a payload. On x86, `0x0804a020` must be packed as `\x20\xa0\x04\x08`, not `\x08\x04\xa0\x20`.
> 3. **Skipping the 4-byte address consumption in the padding formula.** Each address slot costs 4 bytes of "output" before the first `%hhn` — students often forget this and their calculations go off by 4.
> 4. **Not mentioning `FORTIFY_SOURCE` / `RELRO` as defenses.** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists "defensive coding" as part of the topic. A complete answer must include at least one compile-time defence.
> 5. **Drawing the stack in the wrong direction.** The stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). Examiners deduct marks for inverted diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Format String Vulnerability**
>
> - A **Format String Vulnerability** exists when user input is passed directly as the format argument of `printf`/`fprintf`/`sprintf`/`syslog`.
> - The safe idiom is **always** `printf("%s", user_input)`.
> - The **C calling convention** does not let the callee know the real argument count — `printf` blindly consumes stack values to satisfy each `%` specifier.
> - **Dangerous specifiers:** `%n`, `%hn`, `%hhn` (writes); `%s` (arbitrary read); `%x`, `%p` (reconnaissance leaks).
> - On **32-bit** binaries, arguments after the format string are on the stack; the buffer is usually at offset $k \in [6, 12]$.
> - On **64-bit** binaries, the first five variadic arguments are in registers (`rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9`); the buffer is usually at offset $k \in [6, 12]$ as well but indices are 1-based.
> - **Direct parameter access** (`%k$x`) lets the attacker skip intermediate arguments cleanly.
> - The **sentinel technique** (`AAAA%k$x`) reveals the offset of the attacker's buffer.
> - The **canonical `%hhn` padding formula** is:
>   $$x_i = (b_i - 4i - \sum_{j<i} x_j) \bmod 256$$
> - **Defences** (memorize all three): (i) `printf("%s", ...)` coding discipline, (ii) `-Wformat-security -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` compiler flags, (iii) **Full RELRO** + banning `%n` + static analysis.
> - **Real-world exploits:** WU-FTPD 2000, Exim 2018, PHP-CGI 2012, and countless IoT firmware bugs.
> - The vulnerability is **CWE-134** in the MITRE Common Weakness Enumeration.
> - KTU expects both **theoretical mechanism** and **practical exploit construction** in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing: Burpsuite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing: Burpsuite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burp Suite** is an integrated, Java-based **web vulnerability scanner** and **interception proxy platform** developed by PortSwigger Web Security. It functions as a comprehensive graphical toolset for performing **security testing of web applications** by allowing a penetration tester to inspect, manipulate, and replay HTTP/HTTPS traffic between the browser (client) and the target web server.

In the KTU 2024 *Fundamentals of Cyber Security* syllabus, Burp Suite is positioned as the **de-facto industry standard tool** for executing the **Penetration Testing (Pentest)** phase of the VAPT (Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing) lifecycle on web-based targets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Custom Post Office" for Web Traffic**
>
> Imagine the internet is a postal system. Normally, your browser (the sender) drops a letter into a mailbox, and it goes directly to the web server (the receiver) without anyone reading it. **Burp Suite acts like a "Custom Post Office Inspector"** sitting invisibly in the middle of this route.
>
> - **Every letter (request) is opened, photocopied, and can be modified** before being resealed and forwarded.
> - **Every reply (response) is also opened, photocopied, and forwarded** back to you.
> - You get to **read, alter, replay, and even forge** every single message.
>
> This "man-in-the-middle" capability is what allows a security tester to find weaknesses — just like a post office inspector looks for forged stamps, tampered envelopes, or suspicious contents.

### 1.3 Why Burp Suite in the VAPT Lifecycle?

VAPT = **V**ulnerability **A**ssessment + **P**enetration **T**esting.

| VAPT Phase | Burp Suite Component Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Reconnaissance | **Spider / Target** | Map the web application's surface (URLs, parameters, cookies) |
| Scanning | **Scanner (Pro)** | Automated detection of SQLi, XSS, etc. |
| Exploitation | **Intruder / Repeater** | Manual & automated payload delivery |
| Reporting | **Logger / Reporters** | Audit log of every request-response pair |

### 1.4 Editions Available

> [!NOTE]
> - **Community Edition (Free)** — Manual tools only (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder-limited, Decoder, Comparer, Sequencer).
> - **Professional Edition (Paid)** — Adds **Automated Scanner** for active/passive vulnerability detection.
> - **Enterprise Edition** — CI/CD-integrated dynamic scanning at scale.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Working Principle — The Intercepting Proxy

Burp Suite is fundamentally an **intercepting proxy**. To understand *why* it works, we must first understand the normal HTTP transaction:

$$
\text{Browser} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Request}} \text{Web Server} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Response}} \text{Browser}
$$

When Burp Suite is launched and the browser is configured to route through it (default `127.0.0.1:8080`), the topology becomes:

$$
\text{Browser} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP}} \text{Burp Proxy :8080} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP}} \text{Web Server}
$$

The **Why**: This interposition gives the tester a *choke point* where every byte passing between client and server can be observed, paused, edited, and forwarded.

The **How**: Burp achieves this by:
1. **Binding a TCP listener** on a configurable port (default **8080**).
2. **Generating a per-session CA certificate** (for HTTPS interception) that the browser must trust.
3. **Terminating the TLS session** locally, exposing the plaintext HTTP for analysis, then **re-encrypting** the traffic before sending it to the origin server.

### 2.2 Burp Suite Architecture — Tab-by-Tab Breakdown

Burp Suite is organized into a tabbed workspace. Each tab represents a specialized module:

| # | Tab | Type | Function | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dashboard** | Aggregator | Shows task queue, scan results, event log | Low |
| 2 | **Target** | Site Map | Hierarchical view of all crawled URLs, parameters, and requests | High |
| 3 | **Proxy** | Interception Engine | Capture & modify in-flight HTTP/S messages | **Very High** |
| 4 | **Intruder** | Fuzzing Engine | Automated customized attacks (brute-force, fuzzing) | **Very High** |
| 5 | **Repeater** | Manual Replayer | Re-send a single request with arbitrary edits | High |
| 6 | **Sequencer** | Token Analyzer | Statistical randomness analysis of session tokens/cookies | Medium |
| 7 | **Decoder** | Encoding Tool | Convert between Base64, URL, HTML, Hex, Binary | Medium |
| 8 | **Comparer** | Diff Tool | Word/byte-level diff between two responses | Low |
| 9 | **Extender** | Plugin Manager | Load BApps (Burp extensions in Python/Java/Ruby) | Medium |
| 10 | **Logger** | Event Recorder | Append-only full traffic log for audit/evidence | Medium |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Configurations & Limits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every default value, port, and configuration that KTU examiners love to ask. Memorize this **verbatim**.

| Parameter | Default Value | KTU-Expected Symbol/Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Burp Proxy Listener Address | `127.0.0.1` (loopback) | Local binding |
| Burp Proxy Listener Port | **`8080`** | TCP port $P_{proxy} = 8080$ |
| Invisible Proxy Port (non-proxy-aware clients) | `8080` with ` invisible = true` | — |
| SOCKS Proxy Port (Enterprise) | `0` (disabled by default) | — |
| CA Certificate File | `cacert.der` (per-user) | Export from *Proxy → Options* |
| Intruder Max Concurrent Requests (Community) | **Single-threaded** (rate-limited) | $T_{concurrency} = 1$ |
| Intruder Throttling (default) | `0` ms delay (Professional) | $t_{delay} \in [0, \infty)$ |
| Sequencer Sample Size (default) | `100` | $N_{samples} = 100$ |
| Scanner Crawl Depth (default) | **Unlimited** | — |
| Scanner Audit Insertion Point | `URL params, cookies, headers, body` | — |
| Session Handling Cookie Jar | Per-project | In-memory by default |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind port 8080**: Port **8080** is the IANA-recognized "HTTP Alternate" port — non-privileged ($P > 1024$, so no root/admin needed) and unlikely to conflict with the target web server on port **80/443**.

### 2.4 The Four Attack Modes of Intruder (Examination Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **4 Intruder Attack Types**. Each controls *how* payloads are distributed across the marked positions in a request template.

Let the request template have $k$ marked positions $\lbrace p_1, p_2, \dots, p_k \rbrace$, and the payload set have $n$ entries $\lbrace v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n \rbrace$.

| Attack Type | Iterations Formula | Real-World Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sniper** | $N = n$ (single position, others fixed) | Fuzzing one parameter at a time (e.g., testing a single login field) |
| **Battering Ram** | $N = n$ (all marked positions get *same* payload) | Password spray where username & password change together |
| **Pitchfork** | $N = \min(n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k)$ (paired payloads) | Credential stuffing with matched user:pass lists |
| **Cluster Bomb** | $N = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ (all combinations) | Brute-forcing username AND password independently |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Burp Suite is used in:
- **Bug Bounty Programs** (HackerOne, Bugcrowd) — to find vulnerabilities in production web apps.
- **Red Team Engagements** — simulating real attacker behavior on corporate web assets.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines** — Burp Enterprise is integrated into Jenkins/GitHub Actions for continuous scanning.
- **Compliance Audits** — PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, and OWASP ASVS Level testing require web app pen-testing; Burp provides the audit trail.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Canonical Burp Suite Pen-Test Workflow (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **golden methodology** you must reproduce in a 14-mark KTU answer. Each step is exhaustive; do not skip a step in the exam.

#### **Step 1 — Environment Configuration**

The browser is configured to send all traffic to Burp's proxy listener.

$$
\text{Browser Proxy Settings} = \lbrace \text{HTTP Proxy} = 127.0.0.1, \ \text{Port} = 8080, \ \text{SSL Proxy} = \text{ENABLED} \rbrace
$$

Additionally, the **Burp CA certificate** (`cacert.der`) is imported into the browser's trust store. Without this step, HTTPS sites will show certificate warnings and traffic will not be intercepted.

**Conversion Logic**: Trusting the CA makes the browser believe Burp is the legitimate origin server for *any* domain, allowing TLS termination at Burp.

#### **Step 2 — Passive Reconnaissance (Target / Site Map)**

The tester browses the target application normally. Burp **passively** records every URL, parameter, cookie, and response code visited.

**What gets captured** for each request:
- HTTP method ($\text{GET}, \text{POST}, \text{PUT}, \text{DELETE}$)
- URL path and query string
- Request headers (Cookies, User-Agent, Authorization)
- Request body (form data, JSON, XML)
- Response status code $S \in \lbrace 200, 301, 302, 401, 403, 500, \dots \rbrace$
- Response body length $\vert B_{resp} \vert$ in bytes

This populates the **Site Map** — a tree-view of the entire application surface.

#### **Step 3 — Active Reconnaissance (Spider / Crawl)**

Burp's crawler (or the Pro **Scanner → Crawl** module) automatically:
1. Parses every response HTML/JS.
2. Extracts new links $\rightarrow$ adds them to the queue.
3. Submits discovered forms with default/baseline values.
4. Records discovered directories and parameters.

The **Crawl Depth** $D$ controls recursion:
$$
D = 0 \Rightarrow \text{seed URLs only} \quad ; \quad D = \infty \Rightarrow \text{unlimited recursion}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Vulnerability Scanning (Pro Only)**

The Scanner performs two audit passes:

$$
\text{Scan}(\text{target}) = \text{Crawl} \cup \text{Audit} \cup \text{Report}
$$

- **Passive audit** — Analyzes already-observed traffic for issues (e.g., missing security headers).
- **Active audit** — Sends crafted probe requests with **insertion points** $\mathcal{I}$ = {URL params, cookies, headers, body fields} to trigger vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, etc.).

#### **Step 5 — Manual Verification (Repeater)**

A suspicious request is sent to **Repeater**. The tester edits headers/parameters and re-issues the request manually to confirm the vulnerability, isolating the exact payload.

**Example verification flow** for reflected XSS:
$$
\text{Request}: \text{?search=}\langle \text{alert(1)} \rangle \quad \xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \quad \text{Response: } \dots \langle \text{alert(1)} \rangle \dots
$$

If the response reflects the script tag unescaped → vulnerability confirmed.

#### **Step 6 — Exploitation (Intruder)**

The verified request is sent to **Intruder**. Markers $(\S)$ are placed around the vulnerable parameter:

$$
\text{?search=}\S \langle \text{payload} \rangle \S
$$

Payloads are loaded from a list (e.g., 10,000 XSS strings), and one of the four attack types from §2.4 is executed.

**Result analysis** uses the response **length** $\vert B_{resp} \vert$ or **status code** $S$ as the anomaly detector. A response that differs from the baseline in length/keywords (e.g., contains "XSS" in title) indicates exploitation success.

#### **Step 7 — Session Token Analysis (Sequencer)**

If the application issues session cookies $\lbrace C_i \rbrace_{i=1}^{N}$, the tester captures $N \geq 100$ samples via Proxy/Intruder and feeds them to **Sequencer**. It computes:

- **Shannon Entropy** $H$ of the token distribution:
$$
H = -\sum_{j=1}^{m} p_j \log_2 p_j \quad \text{(bits per symbol)}
$$
- **Effective Entropy** $H_{eff} = H \cdot L$ where $L$ = token length.
- **FIPS 140-2 randomness tests** (monobit, poker, runs, long-run, autocorrelation).

A low $H_{eff}$ (e.g., $< 64$ bits) means the token is **predictable** → session-hijackable.

#### **Step 8 — Decoding & Comparison**

**Decoder** performs base transformations:
$$
\text{Base64}(x) \leftrightarrow x \leftrightarrow \text{URL}(x) \leftrightarrow \text{Hex}(x) \leftrightarrow \text{HTML}(x)
$$

**Comparer** computes diff:
$$
\Delta(x, y) = \lbrace i : x_i \neq y_i \rbrace
$$
highlighting changed bytes — useful for blind-injection tests where only a 1-byte difference indicates success.

#### **Step 9 — Reporting**

The **Logger** exports a complete audit trail (`.xml` or `.csv`) of every request/response for compliance evidence. Professional Edition generates HTML/PDF vulnerability reports with severity ratings (High/Medium/Low/Info) using the **CVSS v3.1** scoring framework.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Matching Vulnerability to Burp Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in some form. Memorize it.

| Vulnerability (OWASP Top 10 Ref.) | Primary Burp Tool | Secondary Burp Tool |
|---|---|---|
| A01 — Broken Access Control | Repeater, Intruder | Target (compare role-based URLs) |
