# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the role of the TLS Record Protocol. How does it differ from the TLS Handshake Protocol?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TLS Handshake Protocol** is responsible for *establishing* a secure session: it negotiates the cipher suite, authenticates the server (and optionally the client) using X.509 certificates, and generates the shared master secret and key material. It runs *only* at the start of a session (or on resumption).

The **TLS Record Protocol**, in contrast, is the *data-bearing* layer that protects the application traffic. It performs four operations on every outgoing (and reverses them on incoming) record:

1. **Fragmentation** — splits the data stream into blocks of $\leq 2^{14}$ bytes.
2. **Compression** (optional, often null in TLS 1.3) — reduces redundancy.
3. **MAC / AEAD** — computes HMAC-SHA-256 (TLS 1.2) or AEAD tag (TLS 1.3) for integrity.
4. **Encryption** — encrypts the compressed-fragment-MAC tuple with the negotiated cipher (e.g., AES-GCM).

The Record Protocol also adds a **5-byte header** containing content type, version, and length. In short, the Handshake Protocol *sets up* the session; the Record Protocol *carries* the traffic under that session's protection.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the four services provided by PGP. Briefly explain how PGP achieves confidentiality using a hybrid cryptosystem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PGP provides four primary services:

1. **Digital Signature** — for authentication and non-repudiation.
2. **Message Encryption** — for confidentiality.
3. **Compression** — for efficiency.
4. **Radix-64 Encoding** — for email compatibility (converts binary to ASCII).

**Hybrid Cryptosystem for Confidentiality (3-step process):**

PGP combines symmetric and asymmetric cryptography to balance speed and key-distribution convenience:

- **Step 1 — Symmetric encryption:** A fresh, random **128-bit (or 256-bit) AES session key** $K_S$ is generated. The plaintext $M$ is encrypted: $C_1 = E_{K_S}(M)$. AES is fast, even on large messages.
- **Step 2 — Asymmetric encryption of the key:** $K_S$ is encrypted with the recipient's **RSA public key**: $C_2 = E_{K_{pub,R}}(K_S)$. This securely transmits the session key without requiring a pre-shared secret.
- **Step 3 — Transmission:** The packet $\{C_1 \Vert C_2\}$ is sent. The recipient reverses the process using their private key to recover $K_S$, then decrypts $M$.

This hybrid approach gives PGP the speed of symmetric encryption with the key-distribution convenience of public-key cryptography.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the **IPsec architecture** in detail. Discuss the role of **AH**, **ESP**, **IKE**, and the concept of **Security Association (SA)**. Compare **transport** and **tunnel** modes with diagrams.
*(b)* Describe the **Diffie-Hellman key exchange algorithm** with a worked example. How does it protect against passive eavesdropping? Why is *authenticated* DH essential to defeat man-in-the-middle attacks?

---

#### (a) IPsec Architecture (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. IPsec Overview (1 mark):**
IPsec (Internet Protocol Security) is a suite of protocols defined by the IETF (RFC 4301–4309) that operates at the **network layer (Layer 3)** to provide confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay protection for IP packets. It is the de-facto standard for **Virtual Private Networks (VPNs)**.

**2. Core Components (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **AH (Authentication Header)** | Provides *integrity* and *authentication* of the IP packet and parts of the header, plus anti-replay. Does **not** encrypt payload. IP Protocol **51**. |
| **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** | Provides *confidentiality*, *integrity*, *authentication*, and anti-replay. Encrypts the payload and (in tunnel mode) the original IP header. IP Protocol **50**. |
| **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** | Automates negotiation of SAs and exchange of keys. Modern version is **IKEv2** (RFC 7296), runs over **UDP 500** (or 4500 with NAT-Traversal). |
| **SA (Security Association)** | A *one-way* logical contract between two IPsec peers that defines the crypto algorithms, keys, SPI, lifetime, and mode. Two SAs (one in each direction) are required for full duplex. |
| **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | A 32-bit identifier in the AH/ESP header that, together with destination IP and protocol, uniquely identifies the SA. |

**3. Transport vs Tunnel Mode (2 marks):**

| Mode | What is Protected | IP Header | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport** | Only the *payload* (and parts of original IP header for AH) | Original IP header preserved | Host-to-host communication between two end systems |
| **Tunnel** | The *entire original IP packet* | A **new outer IP header** is added (gateway addresses) | Site-to-site VPN, remote-access VPN, hiding internal IP topology |

**4. Why IPsec matters (1 mark):**
IPsec is transparent to applications — no change is needed in user software. It secures *all* IP traffic (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.) between endpoints, making it the most general-purpose Layer-3 security solution.

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
   Tunnel Mode ESP:
   [New IP Hdr][ESP Hdr][Orig IP Hdr][TCP/UDP Payload][ESP Trailer][ESP ICV]
                       |----- encrypted --------|               |--- auth ---|

   Transport Mode ESP:
   [Orig IP Hdr][ESP Hdr][TCP/UDP Payload][ESP Trailer][ESP ICV]
                        |--- encrypted ---|           |--- auth ---|
```

---

#### (b) Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

The Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange, proposed by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976, allows two parties to derive a shared secret over an insecure channel **without** having exchanged any prior secret.

1. **Public parameters** $p$ (a large prime) and $g$ (a generator of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$) are chosen and made public.
2. **Alice** picks a private random $a \in [1, p-2]$, computes her public value $A = g^a \bmod p$, and sends $A$ to Bob.
3. **Bob** picks a private random $b \in [1, p-2]$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p$, and sends $B$ to Alice.
4. **Alice** computes the shared secret $K = B^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
5. **Bob** computes the shared secret $K = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

Both obtain the same $K$, but a passive eavesdropper seeing $A$ and $B$ cannot compute $K$ without solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

**2. Worked Example (2 marks):**

Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$.

$$A = 5^6 \bmod 23 = 15625 \bmod 23$$

$$15625 / 23 = 679 \text{ remainder } 8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad A = 8$$

$$B = 5^{15} \bmod 23$$

$$5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640 \bmod 23$$

$$640 / 23 = 27 \text{ remainder } 19 \quad\Rightarrow\quad B = 19$$

$$K_{Alice} = B^a \bmod 23 = 19^6 \bmod 23$$

$$19^2 = 361 \bmod 23 = 361 - 15(23) = 361 - 345 = 16$$

$$19^4 = 16^2 = 256 \bmod 23 = 256 - 11(23) = 256 - 253 = 3$$

$$19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \bmod 23 = 48 - 2(23) = 2$$

$$K_{Bob} = A^b \bmod 23 = 8^{15} \bmod 23$$

(Compute similarly — by symmetry, this also equals **2**.)

$$\boxed{K = 2}$$

**3. Security Against Passive Eavesdropping (1 mark):**
The eavesdropper sees only $A = g^a \bmod p$ and $B = g^b \bmod p$. To recover $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, the attacker must solve the DLP, i.e., find $a$ given $g^a \bmod p$. For $p \geq 2048$ bits, the fastest known algorithm (Number Field Sieve) requires sub-exponential time infeasible in practice.

**4. The MITM Problem and the Need for Authentication (1 mark):**
Plain DH is **vulnerable to a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack**. The attacker Mallory runs two separate DH exchanges — one with Alice (pretending to be Bob) and one with Bob (pretending to be Alice) — and forwards messages between them, establishing *two* different shared secrets. Alice and Bob each believe they share a secret with the other, but Mallory can read and modify all traffic.

**Defence — Authenticated DH:** Both parties **sign** the DH exchange transcript with their long-term private keys (e.g., RSA or ECDSA). TLS 1.3 makes this **mandatory** (DHE or ECDHE with signatures), and IKEv2 similarly authenticates the IKE_SA_INIT exchange with certificates or pre-shared keys.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*(a)* Describe the **Kerberos authentication protocol** with a clear message-exchange diagram. Explain the roles of the **AS, TGS, KDC, TGT, and Service Ticket**.
*(b)* What is **SSL/TLS**? Explain the **TLS 1.2 handshake** in detail, listing all messages exchanged between client and server. How are the **master secret** and **key block** derived?

---

#### (a) Kerberos Authentication (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Kerberos (RFC 4120, named after the three-headed Greek dog guarding Hades) is a **trusted-third-party authentication protocol** developed at MIT. It provides **mutual authentication** in a client–server environment using **symmetric-key cryptography** and **time-limited tickets**, enabling Single Sign-On (SSO) across an administrative **realm**.

**2. Key Components (2 marks):**

| Component | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** | The central trusted server; logically combines the AS and TGS. |
| **AS (Authentication Server)** | Verifies the user's identity and issues a TGT. |
| **TGS (Ticket Granting Server)** | Issues service-specific tickets on presentation of a valid TGT. |
| **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** | A credential encrypted with the TGS's secret key, proving the user is authenticated to obtain service tickets. |
| **Service Ticket** | A credential encrypted with the target service's secret key, granting access to that service. |
| **Authenticator** | A short-lived (≤ 5 min) message encrypted with the *session key*, proving current possession of that key. |
| **Session Key** | A fresh symmetric key shared between two parties (e.g., client and TGS) for a single session. |
| **Realm** | An administrative domain governed by one KDC. |

**3. Message Exchange Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Client (C)                  AS                      TGS                       Service (S)
   |--- 1. AS_REQ(C, TGS_id) ---->|                    |                          |
   |                              |                    |                          |
   |<-- 2. AS_REP( {K_C,TGS}_K_C , TGT ) -----------|                          |
   |        TGT = {K_C,TGS, C, lifetime}_K_TGS                                   |
   |                                                                              |
   |--- 3. TGS_REQ( TGT, Auth_C={timestamp}_K_C,TGS ) -------->|                |
   |                                                              |              |
   |<-- 4. TGS_REP( {K_C,S}_K_C,TGS , ServiceTicket ) ----------|              |
   |        ServiceTicket = {K_C,S, C, lifetime}_K_S                            |
   |                                                                              |
   |--- 5. AP_REQ( ServiceTicket, Auth_C' ) ---------------------------->|
   |                                                                       |
   |<-- 6. AP_REP( {timestamp+1}_K_C,S ) (optional mutual auth) ----------|
```

**4. Security Properties (1 mark):**
- **Replay protection** via timestamps in authenticators (5-min skew window).
- **Mutual authentication** via the AP_REP step (timestamp + 1).
- **No password on the wire** — only tickets encrypted with long-term keys.
- **Tickets are non-tamperable** because only the issuing server can encrypt them.

---

#### (b) SSL/TLS and TLS 1.2 Handshake (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. What is SSL/TLS? (1 mark):**
SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) was developed by Netscape in the 1990s. TLS (Transport Layer Security) is its IETF-standardized successor, currently at version **1.3** (RFC 8446, 2018). TLS operates between the transport and application layers and provides **confidentiality, integrity, server authentication, and optional client authentication** for any TCP-based protocol. It is the foundation of **HTTPS (HTTP over TLS)**.

**2. TLS 1.2 Handshake Messages (3 marks):**

| Step | Direction | Message | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | C → S | **ClientHello** | client_version, client_random (32 bytes), session_id, list of cipher_suites, compression_methods |
| 2 | S → C | **ServerHello** | server_version, server_random, chosen cipher_suite, compression method |
| 3 | S → C | **Certificate** | Server's X.509 certificate chain |
| 4 | S → C | **ServerKeyExchange** | DH/ECDH parameters + signature (for DHE/ECDHE suites only) |
| 5 | S → C | **ServerHelloDone** | Indicates server is done with its hello messages |
| 6 | C → S | **ClientKeyExchange** | Pre-master secret encrypted with server's RSA public key, *or* client's DH/ECDH public value |
| 7 | C → S | **ChangeCipherSpec** | Signals that subsequent records will be encrypted with the new keys |
| 8 | C → S | **Finished** | MAC over the entire handshake transcript (proves both sides derived the same keys) |
| 9 | S → C | **ChangeCipherSpec** | Server switches to new keys |
| 10 | S → C | **Finished** | Server's MAC over handshake transcript |

**3. Master Secret Derivation (2 marks):**

The **pre-master secret (PMS)** is either 46 random bytes (for RSA key transport) or the DH/ECDH shared secret. The **master secret (MS)** is computed as:

$$\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\ \text{"master secret"},\ \text{ClientHello.random}\ \Vert\ \text{ServerHello.random})$$

The PRF is defined (RFC 5246) as:

$$\text{PRF}(\text{secret},\ \text{label},\ \text{seed}) = P_{\text{HMAC}}(\text{secret},\ \text{label}\ \Vert\ \text{seed})$$

The function $P_{\text{HMAC}}$ expands the seed into 48 bytes by iterative HMAC-SHA-256. The MS is then fed into the **key block derivation**:

$$\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\ \text{"key expansion"},\ \text{ServerHello.random}\ \Vert\ \text{ClientHello.random})$$

**4. Key Block Partitioning (1 mark):**
The key block is then split into six keys: `client_write_MAC_key`, `server_write_MAC_key`, `client_write_key`, `server_write_key`, `client_write_IV`, `server_write_IV` — providing the per-direction integrity and confidentiality for the Record Protocol.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing AH and ESP** — students often write "ESP provides only confidentiality" — it actually provides confidentiality + integrity + authentication. Only AH lacks confidentiality.
> 2. **TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3 confusion** — TLS 1.3 removed RSA key exchange. If the question specifies TLS 1.2, do *not* say "0-RTT" or "ECDHE only".
> 3. **Kerberos KDC, AS, TGS confusion** — KDC is the *server*; AS and TGS are *logical services* within the KDC. AS issues TGTs; TGS issues service tickets.
> 4. **Forgetting timestamps / nonces in DH** — DH alone does *not* provide authentication. Always mention that production protocols use *authenticated* DH.
> 5. **Drawing SSL handshake without `ChangeCipherSpec` and `Finished` messages** — these are mandatory, and their absence will cost 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting IPsec protocol numbers** (AH=51, ESP=50, IKE=UDP 500) — examiners love these.
> 7. **Mixing transport and tunnel mode diagrams** — be sure to draw the *new* outer IP header in tunnel mode and label it clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Protocols" (Module 2)**

- ✅ **TLS 1.2** uses a 2-RTT handshake; **TLS 1.3** is 1-RTT (0-RTT optional) and **AEAD-only** (no CBC, no RSA key exchange).
- ✅ TLS derives the **master secret** from PMS + nonces via PRF; key block is partitioned into 6 keys (MAC + enc + IV per direction).
- ✅ **IPsec = AH (51) + ESP (50) + IKE (UDP 500/4500)**. ESP provides confidentiality + integrity; AH provides only integrity + authentication.
- ✅ **Transport mode** protects payload only; **Tunnel mode** wraps the *entire* IP packet and is used in VPNs.
- ✅ **IKEv2** mandates **PFS** by using fresh DH keys per SA; compromise of a long-term key does not break past sessions.
- ✅ **SSH** has three layers: Transport (server auth + key exchange), User Authentication (client auth), Connection (multiplexing).
- ✅ **Kerberos** uses **AS, TGS, TGT, Service Tickets, Authenticators**. Replay protection via timestamps (5-min window).
- ✅ **PGP** uses a **hybrid** approach: one-time symmetric session key encrypted with recipient's RSA public key.
- ✅ **Diffie-Hellman** is secure against *passive* eavesdropping via the DLP, but *requires authentication* to defeat MITM.
- ✅ **HMAC** = $H((K \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert m))$ — provides integrity without a public key.
- ✅ **AEAD** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) combines encryption and authentication in one operation — preferred in modern protocols.
- ✅ **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** is achieved by using **ephemeral** DH/ECDH keys (DHE, ECDHE) — never reusing long-term keys.
- ✅ **Sequence numbers + anti-replay windows** protect against replay attacks in IPsec and TLS.
- ✅ Memorize the protocol number mapping: **AH=51, ESP=50, IKE=UDP 500, NAT-T=UDP 4500, HTTPS=TCP 443**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Safely When Using Email
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Safely When Using Email

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Email Security** refers to the collective set of procedures, techniques, protocols, and best practices employed to protect email accounts, content, and communications from unauthorized access, loss, or compromise. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCST604 - Fundamentals of Cyber Security)**, operating safely when using email is defined as the disciplined application of **cybersecurity hygiene** principles during the sending, receiving, storage, and disposal of electronic mail, encompassing threat identification, preventive controls, and reactive response mechanisms.

The three pillars of email security recognized by the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Confidentiality** – Ensuring that only the intended recipient can read the message.
2. **Integrity** – Ensuring that the message is not altered in transit.
3. **Availability** – Ensuring that email services remain accessible to legitimate users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Email remains the **#1 attack vector** for cybercriminals worldwide. Over **90% of all data breaches** and **96% of phishing attacks** (as per industry standard security reports cited in KTU reference materials) begin with a malicious email. Therefore, mastering email safety is a **direct, examinable outcome (CO2 – Identify common cyber threats and vulnerabilities).**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine email is like the **public postal system (India Post)**, but with one critical difference — every single letter you send passes through a giant, transparent warehouse where **anyone can read it, copy it, or rewrite it** before it reaches the recipient.

- **Email is NOT a sealed letter** — it is more like a **postcard**.
- The **"To" and "From" addresses** are like the outside of an envelope — visible to everyone.
- The **"Subject" line** and **attachments** are the first things attackers scan.

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of your email inbox as the **front door of your digital house**.
- A **phishing email** is a stranger at your door disguised as a delivery person, asking you to open a "free gift" (malicious attachment).
- A **spam email** is junk flyers stuffed into your mailbox by advertisers.
- **Email spoofing** is someone forging your signature on a letter and mailing it to your friends pretending to be you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box:**
> - **Phishing:** A social engineering attack where an attacker impersonates a trusted entity to steal credentials or install malware.
> - **Spam:** Unsolicited bulk email, usually commercial, sent without the recipient's consent.
> - **Malware:** Malicious software (viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware) delivered via email attachments or links.

### 1.3 Standard Email Threat Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

- **Average cost of a phishing attack for SMBs:** **USD 1.6 million** (industry benchmark cited in security literature).
- **Time for a user to fall for a phishing email:** **Less than 60 seconds** (Verizon DBIR standard).
- **Standard email protocols:** **SMTP** (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol – port 25/587), **POP3** (port 110/995), **IMAP** (port 143/993).
- **Secure variants:** **SMTPS, POP3S, IMAPS** (SSL/TLS encrypted).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Email safety is not about a single tool — it is a **habit**. You lock your house door every time you leave; you must apply the same discipline to every email you open, click, or forward.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Email Threat Landscape (Structured Breakdown)

Email threats can be classified into **three primary categories** and **seven specific attack types** as per KTU Module 2 syllabus:

#### A. Social Engineering Attacks (Human-Targeted)
1. **Phishing** – Bulk fraudulent emails targeting many users.
2. **Spear Phishing** – Targeted phishing aimed at a specific individual/organization.
3. **Whaling** – Spear phishing aimed at senior executives (CEO, CFO).
4. **Business Email Compromise (BEC)** – Impersonation of an executive to authorize fraudulent financial transfers.

#### B. Malicious Payload Attacks (System-Targeted)
5. **Malware Attachments** – Documents (PDF, DOC, XLS) containing macros, scripts, or embedded executables.
6. **Ransomware** – Encryption-based malware often delivered via email that demands payment.
7. **Drive-by Downloads** – Links in emails that, when clicked, silently download malicious code.

#### C. Infrastructure/Protocol Attacks
8. **Email Spoofing** – Forging the "From" address to impersonate a trusted sender.
9. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** – Intercepting unencrypted email traffic on insecure networks.
10. **Email Bombing / DoS** – Flooding an inbox with thousands of messages to cause disruption.

### 2.2 Why Are Emails Vulnerable? The "How" Explained

| **Why Email is Insecure** | **Plain English Explanation** |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Plaintext by default** | Original SMTP sends email as readable text — like a postcard, not a sealed letter. |
| **Sender verification is weak** | SMTP does not authenticate the sender by default — anyone can claim to be `ceo@company.com`. |
| **Human trust factor** | Users instinctively trust emails from "known" senders (banks, bosses, delivery services). |
| **Cross-platform reach** | Email reaches any device, OS, or network — making it the universal delivery channel for attackers. |
| **Attachment flexibility** | Email allows any file type — giving attackers a free channel to ship malware. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Email Safety Controls

| **Control / Concept** | **Definition** | **KTU Exam Relevance** |
|-----------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** | DNS record listing authorized mail servers for a domain. | High — frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) |
| **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** | Cryptographic signature attached to emails to verify integrity. | High — Part A |
| **DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** | Policy framework combining SPF + DKIM with reporting. | High — Part B (14 marks) |
| **TLS/SSL Encryption** | Encrypts the email channel between mail servers (in transit). | Medium |
| **End-to-End Encryption (PGP / S/MIME)** | Encrypts email content so only sender/recipient can read it. | High |
| **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** | Second verification factor (OTP, biometric) on email login. | Very High |
| **Sandboxing** | Isolated environment that opens attachments safely to detect malware. | Medium |
| **Anti-Spoofing Filters** | Email gateway rules that reject emails failing SPF/DKIM checks. | Medium |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Email security is **not a textbook concept** — it is a daily operational requirement in every engineering field:

- **Software Engineers** → Receive fake "GitHub security alert" phishing emails daily.
- **Civil Engineers** → Receive spoofed emails from "contractors" with malicious BOQ attachments.
- **Electrical Engineers** → SCADA/ICS vendor spoofing emails delivering malware.
- **Biomedical Engineers** → HIPAA-regulated patient data emails require encryption.
- **All KTU Students** → Job offer scams ("You are selected at Google! Pay this processing fee") are pure phishing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) you study in KTU Module 1 is **directly applied** in email security — Confidentiality via encryption, Integrity via DKIM, Availability via anti-spam/DDoS filters.

### 2.5 The 10 Golden Rules of Email Safety (KTU Recommended Practices)

1. **Never click** on links or download attachments from unknown senders.
2. **Verify the sender's address** — check the full domain (`@gmail.com` vs `@gmai1.com`).
3. **Hover before you click** — preview the actual URL on desktop.
4. **Enable MFA** on your email account (Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo).
5. **Use strong, unique passwords** — 12+ characters, mixed case, symbols.
6. **Never send sensitive data** (passwords, Aadhaar, credit card) via plain email.
7. **Log out** from public/shared computers.
8. **Report suspicious emails** — use the "Report Phishing" button in Gmail/Outlook.
9. **Keep email client and OS updated** — patches close known vulnerabilities.
10. **Back up important emails** — ransomware cannot hold what you already have offline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Operating Safely When Using Email" is a **procedural and conceptual** topic (not heavily mathematical), the KTU 2024 scheme does not require complex derivations. Instead, the analytical focus is on **threat identification, decision logic, and protocol workflows**. Below are exhaustive step-by-step analyses of the **three most examinable analytical processes** in this topic.

### 3.1 Analytical Process 1: Identifying a Phishing Email (7-Step Decision Logic)

This is the **single most important KTU examination scenario**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step methodology a student must demonstrate:

**Step 1 — Examine the Sender's Address (Full Domain Check)**
$$ \text{Display Name} \neq \text{Actual Sender Domain} $$
- *Example:* Display name shows "PayPal Support" but actual address is `support-paypal@securemail-verify.xyz`.
- *Logic:* Attackers register lookalike domains (e.g., `paypa1.com`, `paypall.com`).

**Step 2 — Analyze the Subject Line (Urgency & Fear Triggers)**
$$ \text{Phishing Subject} \approx f(\text{Urgency}, \text{Threat}, \text{Reward}) $$
- *Examples:* "Your account will be suspended in 24 hours!", "Claim your refund of ₹50,000 NOW."
- *Logic:* Legitimate organizations rarely demand instant action via email.

**Step 3 — Inspect All Hyperlinks (Hover Test)**
$$ \text{Displayed Text} \to \text{Actual URL} \quad \text{(MUST match)} $$
- *Example:* Link text says `https://www.google.com` but underlying URL is `http://g00gle-malware.tk/login`.
- *Logic:* On desktop, hover over the link to see the real destination.

**Step 4 — Check for Generic Greetings**
$$ \text{Legitimate Email} \to \text{Personalized Greeting} $$
- *Example:* "Dear Valued Customer" instead of "Dear Mr. Sharma."
- *Logic:* Banks and companies know your name.

**Step 5 — Scan for Spelling, Grammar, and Formatting Errors**
- *Logic:* Professional organizations use copywriters; phishing emails often originate from non-native speakers or contain image-only text.

**Step 6 — Evaluate Attachments (File Type Risk Matrix)**
$$ \text{Attachment Risk Score} = f(\text{File Type}, \text{Sender Trust}, \text{Context}) $$

| **File Extension** | **Risk Level** | **Reason** |
|--------------------|----------------|------------|
| `.txt` | Low | Plain text, no executable code |
| `.pdf` | Medium | Can contain embedded JavaScript |
| `.doc` / `.docx` | High | Macros can execute code |
| `.xls` / `.xlsx` | High | Macros can execute code |
| `.zip` / `.rar` | High | Hides actual file inside |
| `.exe` / `.bat` / `.scr` | **Critical** | Directly executable |
| `.iso` / `.img` | **Critical** | Disk image, often malicious |

**Step 7 — Verify Independently (Out-of-Band Confirmation)**
$$ \text{Do NOT use contact info from the suspicious email.} $$
- *Logic:* If "SBI" emails you, close the email, open a new browser tab, type `sbi.co.in` manually, and check the message center.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Process 2: How SPF, DKIM, and DMARC Work Together (Authentication Flow)

This is the **standard 14-mark KTU Part B question**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step authentication pipeline:

**Step 1 — Sender's Mail Server Signs the Outgoing Email (DKIM)**
$$ \text{Email} \xrightarrow{\text{Private Key}} \text{Signed Email (with DKIM-Signature header)} $$
- *Logic:* The sending server uses its **private key** to generate a unique cryptographic hash of the email body + selected headers.

**Step 2 — Sender's DNS Publishes the Public Key (DKIM Public Key)**
$$ \text{DNS TXT Record: } \texttt{selector._domainkey.example.com} \to \text{public key} $$
- *Logic:* The receiving server retrieves this public key via DNS lookup.

**Step 3 — Receiver Verifies DKIM Signature**
$$ \text{Hash}(\text{Email Body}) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Decrypt}(\text{DKIM Signature, Public Key}) $$
- *Logic:* If hashes match → email integrity verified. If not → email tampered with.

**Step 4 — Receiver Checks SPF Record (Authorized Sender IP)**
$$ \text{IP of Sending Server} \in \text{Authorized IPs (from DNS SPF record)}? $$
- *Logic:* The domain owner publishes an SPF TXT record listing IPs authorized to send mail on their behalf.

**Step 5 — Receiver Consults DMARC Policy (Final Decision)**
$$ \text{DMARC Policy Action} = \begin{cases} \text{none} & \text{(no action, just report)} \\ \text{quarantine} & \text{(move to spam folder)} \\ \text{reject} & \text{(block entirely)} \end{cases} $$
- *Logic:* The domain owner publishes a DMARC DNS record telling receivers what to do when SPF or DKIM fails.

**Step 6 — Receiver Sends a DMARC Report Back to the Domain Owner**
- *Logic:* Aggregate reports (RUA) help the domain owner monitor for spoofing attempts.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Process 3: Incident Response After Clicking a Phishing Link (7-Step Reaction Protocol)

This is the **most common practical KTU lab/viva question**:

**Step 1 — Disconnect from the Internet Immediately**
- *Logic:* Prevents malware from phoning home or spreading to network shares.

**Step 2 — Do NOT Enter Any Credentials**
- *Logic:* If you haven't typed your password, the attacker doesn't have it (yet).

**Step 3 — Close the Browser**
- *Logic:* Clears in-memory session tokens that could be hijacked.

**Step 4 — Run a Full Antivirus / Anti-Malware Scan**
- *Logic:* Tools like Windows Defender, Malwarebytes, or corporate EDR solutions.

**Step 5 — Change Passwords from a Clean Device**
- *Logic:* Use a different, trusted device — the compromised machine may have a keylogger.

**Step 6 — Enable/Reset MFA**
- *Logic:* Even if password is stolen, the OTP/biometric step blocks the attacker.

**Step 7 — Report the Incident**
- *Logic:* Report to IT admin (workplace) or `reportphishing@apwg.org` (Anti-Phishing Working Group) and the email provider.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"What will you do if you clicked a phishing link?"*, never say "I will format my computer" — this is the mark-loser answer. Examiners expect the **layered, evidence-preserving** approach shown above.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered using **Mermaid syntax**, compatible with GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, and most modern markdown editors. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric prefix rule to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.

### Diagram 4.1: Anatomy of a Phishing Email Attack (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Attacker] --> A2[Register Lookalike Domain]
    A2 --> A3[Craft Phishing Email with Malicious Link]
    A3 --> A4[Send to Target via SMTP]
    A4 --> B1[Victim Receives Email]
    B1 --> B2{Victim Clicks Link?}
    B2 -->|No| B3[No Compromise - Email Filtered]
    B2 -->|Yes| B4[Browser Opens Fake Login Page]
    B4 --> B5{Victim Enters Credentials?}
    B5 -->|No| B6[Session Token Theft Risk]
    B5 -->|Yes| B7[Credentials Sent to Attacker Server]
    B7 --> B8[Attacker Logs into Real Account]
    B8 --> B9[Data Theft, Financial Fraud, Lateral Movement]
    B1 --> A5{Email Filter Active?}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Spam/Phishing Filter Blocks]
    A5 -->|No| B1
```

### Diagram 4.2: Email Authentication Pipeline (SPF + DKIM + DMARC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sender Mail Server] -->|Signs email| S2[Add DKIM Signature Header]
    S2 -->|DNS Lookup| S3[Receiving Mail Server]
    S3 --> R1{SPF Check Pass?}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2{DKIM Signature Valid?}
    R1 -->|No| R3[SPF Fail]
    R2 -->|Yes| R4{DMARC Alignment OK?}
    R2 -->|No| R5[DKIM Fail]
    R4 -->|Yes| R6[Email Delivered to Inbox]
    R4 -->|No| R7[Check DMARC Policy]
    R3 --> R7
    R5 --> R7
    R7 -->|None| R8[Deliver - Just Report]
    R7 -->|Quarantine| R9[Move to Spam Folder]
    R7 -->|Reject| R10[Block Email Entirely]
    S1 -->|Authorized IP| R1
    S1 -->|Published DKIM Key in DNS| R2
    S1 -->|Published DMARC Record| R7
```

### Diagram 4.3: Layered Email Security Architecture (Defense in Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Email Gateway / Perimeter Filter] --> L2[Layer 2: Anti-Spam Engine]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Anti-Phishing Engine SPF DKIM DMARC Check]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Anti-Malware Sandbox Scan]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: User Inbox]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: User Awareness and Judgment]
    L6 -->|Click| L7{Is Attachment Link Safe?}
    L7 -->|Yes| L8[Safe Action]
    L7 -->|No| L9[Report to IT Block Sender]
    L1 -->|External Email| M1[Internet]
    M1 --> L1
```

### Diagram 4.4: Email Communication Flow (SMTP + POP3/IMAP) with Encryption Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UA[User A Mail Client] -->|SMTP Port 587 with TLS| MSA[Mail Submission Agent]
    MSA -->|SMTP with TLS| MX1[Receiving Mail Server MX]
    MX1 -->|Queued| MB[Mailbox Storage on Server]
    MB -->|IMAP Port 993 with TLS| UB[User B Mail Client]
    MB -->|POP3 Port 995 with TLS| UB
    UA -.->|Optional PGP S/MIME End to End| UB
    ATT[Attacker MitM on Insecure Network] -.->|Intercepts plaintext if no TLS| MX1
    ATT -.->|Cannot decrypt if TLS enabled| MX1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Exam:**
> - **Diagram 4.1** is the expected answer when a question asks *"Explain how a phishing attack works."*
> - **Diagram 4.2** is the expected answer for *"Explain SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with a diagram."*
> - **Diagram 4.3** demonstrates the **Defense-in-Depth** principle.
> - **Diagram 4.4** shows **where encryption applies** in the email pipeline — a common 14-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define phishing. List any four indicators of a phishing email.**

**Model Answer:**

**Phishing** is a social engineering cyberattack in which an attacker impersonates a legitimate and trusted entity (such as a bank, e-commerce site, or colleague) via email to trick the recipient into revealing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, credit card numbers, or to install malware on their system.

**Four Indicators of a Phishing Email:**

1. **Suspicious sender address** — domain name is misspelled or uses lookalike characters (e.g., `@paypa1.com` instead of `@paypal.com`).
2. **Urgency or fear-based subject line** — phrases like *"Act now or your account will be suspended"* are classic phishing triggers.
3. **Generic greeting** — emails addressed to *"Dear Customer/User"* instead of the recipient's actual name.
4. **Suspicious links or attachments** — hover-revealed URLs that do not match the displayed text, or unexpected `.zip`, `.exe`, or macro-enabled `.doc` files.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Target:** 90–120 words for a 3-mark answer. Examiners allocate ~1 mark for the definition and ~0.5 marks per indicator.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**What is the difference between POP3 and IMAP email protocols? Which one is more secure and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3)** | **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol)** |
|-------------|------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Default Port** | 110 (plain) / **995 (SSL/TLS)** | 143 (plain) / **993 (SSL/TLS)** |
| **Email Storage** | Downloads emails to local device; deletes from server by default | Emails remain on the server; synced across devices |
| **Offline Access** | Full access offline (since emails are downloaded) | Limited access offline (only cached headers/body) |
| **Multi-device Use** | Not ideal — emails scattered across devices | Ideal — same mailbox state on all devices |
| **Security** | Secure when POP3S (port 995) is used | Secure when IMAPS (port 993) is used |

**More Secure Protocol:** Both protocols are equally secure **when their SSL/TLS variants (POP3S on port 995, IMAPS on port 993) are used**, because the encrypted tunnel protects credentials and content from eavesdropping. However, **IMAP is generally preferred** in modern enterprise and personal use because it allows centralized server-side backup, multi-device synchronization, and granular folder management. Plain POP3 (port 110) transmits credentials in cleartext and is **considered insecure** on any untrusted network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "IMAP is more secure" without explaining that **the encryption (SSL/TLS) is the actual security mechanism** — this costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Per the official ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern for Theory Courses, each Module in the question paper carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (i.e., students answer **either** OR **or** OR). Below are the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model]**

**(a)** Explain the three primary email authentication protocols — **SPF, DKIM, and DMARC** — with their working mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **five best practices** that a user should follow to operate safely when using email in a corporate environment. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. SPF (Sender Policy Framework) — 2 Marks**

SPF is an **email-validation protocol** designed to detect and prevent email spoofing. The domain owner publishes an **SPF record** in their DNS as a TXT entry that lists **all IP addresses authorized** to send email on behalf of that domain. When a receiving mail server gets an email, it checks the **envelope sender's** IP against the SPF record published in DNS.

- *Example SPF Record:* `v=spf1 ip4:192.0.2.0/24 include:_spf.google.com -all`
- *Mechanism:* If the sending server's IP is **not in the list**, the SPF check **fails**, and the receiving server can flag, quarantine, or reject the email based on its DMARC policy.

**2. DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) — 2 Marks**

DKIM is a **cryptographic email authentication** technique. The sending mail server attaches a **digital signature** to the email (in a header called `DKIM-Signature`) that is generated using a **private key** held by the sender. The corresponding **public key** is published in the sender's DNS.

- *Mechanism:* The receiving server retrieves the public key via DNS, then uses it to **verify the signature**. If the email body or selected headers were tampered with in transit, the signature **fails verification**, proving the message was modified.
- *Purpose:* DKIM provides **integrity** (was it tampered?) and **authenticity** (did the claimed sender sign it?).

**3. DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance) — 3 Marks**

DMARC is a **policy and reporting framework** that builds on top of SPF and DKIM. The domain owner publishes a DMARC DNS TXT record that specifies:

- **What to do** when an email fails SPF or DKIM — policy is one of:
  - `p=none` → Take no action; just collect reports.
  - `p=quarantine` → Send failing emails to the **spam/junk folder**.
  - `p=reject` → **Block** the email entirely at the gateway.
- **Where to send reports** — using the `rua=` (aggregate report URI) tag, the domain owner receives statistics on authentication failures, helping them detect spoofing campaigns.

DMARC also requires **alignment** — the domain in the `From:` header must match the domain authenticated by SPF or DKIM. This prevents attackers from passing SPF/DKIM with one domain but displaying another in the `From:` field.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks (1.4 marks per practice)**

**Five Best Practices for Safe Email Use in a Corporate Environment:**

1. **Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all email accounts** — Even if a password is stolen via phishing, the second factor (OTP, hardware token, biometric) blocks unauthorized login. (1.4 marks)

2. **Never click on links or open attachments from unknown or unexpected senders** — Always verify with the sender through a separate communication channel (phone call, in-person, official chat) before acting. (1.4 marks)

3. **Use strong, unique passwords and a password manager** — Passwords should be at least 12 characters long, with a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Reusing passwords across accounts is a top cause of credential-stuffing attacks. (1.4 marks)

4. **Encrypt sensitive emails and use secure file-sharing platforms** — Confidential business data (financial reports, customer PII, intellectual property) should never be sent in plain text. Use S/MIME, PGP, or enterprise-grade encrypted file transfer tools. (1.4 marks)

5. **Report suspicious emails immediately to the IT/Security team** — Use the "Report Phishing" button built into Gmail, Outlook, and most corporate email clients. Early reporting enables IT to block the sender organization-wide and warn other employees. (1.4 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **phishing attack lifecycle** from the attacker's perspective, including technical and psychological stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **phishing, spear phishing, and whaling**. Describe two technical controls and two administrative controls that an organization can deploy to defend against these attacks. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **phishing attack lifecycle** consists of **6 sequential stages**:

| **Stage** | **Name** | **Attacker Action** | **Technical/Psychological Element** |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Reconnaissance** | Gather target email addresses, names, job titles from LinkedIn, company website, social media | OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence) |
| 2 | **Infrastructure Setup** | Register a lookalike domain (e.g., `paypa1.com`), set up a fake login page, acquire hosting | Technical — DNS, web hosting |
| 3 | **Weaponization** | Craft the phishing email with a compelling pretext, embedded malicious link, and urgent call-to-action | Psychological — urgency, fear, curiosity, authority |
| 4 | **Delivery** | Send the phishing email in bulk (phishing) or targeted (spear phishing) via SMTP servers or botnets | Technical — SMTP, botnet |
| 5 | **Exploitation** | Victim clicks link → fake login page captures credentials OR malicious attachment installs malware | Psychological — user trust, habit |
| 6 | **Actions on Objective** | Attacker logs in to victim's account, exfiltrates data, performs financial fraud, or launches lateral attacks | Technical — credential reuse, lateral movement |

**Diagram (Descriptive for Answer Sheet):**

```
[Recon] → [Setup Infra] → [Craft Email] → [Send] → [User Clicks] → [Credential Theft / Malware Install] → [Fraud / Data Theft]
```

**Psychological Levers Exploited:** Authority (impersonating CEO), Urgency ("act in 24 hours"), Fear ("account suspended"), Curiosity ("see your photo"), Trust (familiar brand logos).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Differences Table (4 marks — 1.3 each approximately):**

| **Aspect** | **Phishing** | **Spear Phishing** | **Whaling** |
|------------|--------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Target** | Mass / random users | Specific individual or organization | Senior executive (CEO, CFO, Director) |
| **Personalization** | Generic | Highly personalized using OSINT | Extremely personalized, executive-level pretext |
| **Volume** | Thousands of emails | Small batch (tens to hundreds) | Very few (often just one) |
| **Goal** | Cast a wide net for credentials | Steal specific data, gain foothold | High-value fraud (wire transfers, strategic secrets) |
| **Example** | "Your Netflix account is suspended" | "Hi Arun, please review the attached Q3 report" | "Mr. Sharma, please authorize this urgent transfer" |

**Technical Controls (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Email Gateway with Anti-Phishing Filters** — Deploy solutions (e.g., Proofpoint, Mimecast, Microsoft Defender for Office 365) that perform SPF/DKIM/DMARC validation, URL rewriting, and sandboxing of attachments.

2. **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on All Accounts** — Even if credentials are phished, the second factor prevents account takeover.

**Administrative Controls (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Security Awareness Training & Simulated Phishing Drills** — Conduct quarterly training and send fake phishing emails to test employee vigilance. Track click rates and provide remedial training to repeat offenders.

2. **Incident Response Plan with Clear Reporting Channels** — Establish a "Report Phishing" button in the email client, define an IT Security contact, and run regular tabletop exercises for phishing scenarios.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Email Safety Questions:**
> 1. **Writing "delete the email" as the only response to a phishing email** — Examiners want a **layered response**: do not click → report → delete → scan device → change passwords → enable MFA. "Just delete" = 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "phishing" with "spam"** — Spam is unwanted commercial email; phishing is **fraudulent** email with malicious intent. Many students lose 1 mark by using these terms interchangeably.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "out-of-band verification"** — When a question asks *"How do you verify a suspicious email?"*, the answer must include contacting the sender via a **separate channel** (phone, in-person), not replying to the suspicious email.
> 4. **Listing protocols without explaining mechanisms** — Simply writing "SPF, DKIM, DMARC" without explaining *how they work* scores 1–2 marks out of 7. Examiners want the DNS lookup, cryptographic signature, and policy decision steps.
> 5. **Ignoring the human factor** — A pure technical answer that omits "user awareness training" is considered incomplete for a **Fundamentals of Cyber Security** course. Always mention the human element.
> 6. **Forgetting port numbers in protocol questions** — KTU examiners expect `587 (SMTP submission)`, `993 (IMAPS)`, `995 (POP3S)` — missing these costs 0.5–1 mark per protocol.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Email is the #1 attack vector** in modern cybersecurity — expect at least one full 14-mark question on it.
- **Phishing** = fraudulent email to steal data/money. **Spear phishing** = targeted. **Whaling** = executive-targeted. **BEC** = business email compromise.
- **Three authentication protocols** in order of layering: **SPF (IP check) → DKIM (signature check) → DMARC (policy decision)**.
- **DMARC policies:** `none` (monitor), `quarantine` (spam folder), `reject` (block).
- **Encryption points in email:** In-transit (TLS on ports 587/993/995) vs. End-to-End (PGP, S/MIME).
- **Top 10 safe practices** to memorize: MFA, strong passwords, hover-before-click, no unknown attachments, out-of-band verification, report phishing, log out from public PCs, OS updates, encryption for sensitive data, regular backups.
- **Attachment risk:** `.exe`, `.bat`, `.scr`, `.iso`, `.img` = **CRITICAL**; `.doc/.xls` with macros = **HIGH**; `.pdf` = **MEDIUM**; `.txt` = **LOW**.
- **Incident response after click:** Disconnect → Don't enter credentials → Close browser → Antivirus scan → Change passwords from clean device → Enable/reset MFA → Report.
- **Human factor matters** — Always mention **security awareness training** in any defense-related question.
- **KTU-favorite diagram questions:** (1) Phishing lifecycle, (2) SPF/DKIM/DMARC flow, (3) Email architecture with encryption points, (4) Layered email security.
- **Memorize port numbers:** SMTP 25/587, POP3 110/995, IMAP 143/993, HTTPS 443.
- **CIA Triad application:** Confidentiality = encryption, Integrity = DKIM, Availability = anti-spam/DoS filters.
- **Final mantra:** *Email safety is a habit, not a tool. The strongest firewall is between the user's ears.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Terminologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Security Terminologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Network Security?

**Network Security** refers to the set of policies, practices, hardware, and software technologies designed to protect the integrity, confidentiality, and accessibility of computer networks and data traversing them. It encompasses every measure taken to defend a network against unauthorized access, misuse, malfunction, modification, destruction, or improper disclosure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** Network security consists of the provisions and policies adopted by a network administrator to prevent and monitor **unauthorized access, modification, misuse, denial of service, or theft** of a computer network and its accessible resources.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a computer network as the **postal system of a country**. Letters (data packets) travel through sorting offices (routers and switches) and reach destinations (servers). **Network Security** is the entire protective apparatus: the **envelope** sealing the letter (encryption), the **signature** on it (digital signature), the **postman's ID badge** (authentication), the **rulebook** deciding who can open which mail (authorization), the **CCTV cameras** at the sorting office (intrusion detection), and the **firewall doors** that block suspicious persons at the entrance (firewalls).

## 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Foundation of All Network Security

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone model upon which **every** network security policy is built. It comprises three pillars:

| Pillar | Technical Term | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | **Confidentiality** | Keeping secrets secret. Only the intended recipient can read the data. |
| **I** | **Integrity** | Ensuring the data has not been tampered with, altered, or corrupted in transit. |
| **A** | **Availability** | Ensuring the network and its services are accessible to authorized users whenever needed. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **"CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability"**. Almost every KTU Part A question on this module will test the CIA Triad. Memorize it as the first answer to "What are the goals of network security?"

### Real-World Analogy for CIA Triad
- **Confidentiality** → A sealed diary with a lock. Only you have the key.
- **Integrity** → The seal on an official envelope. If broken, you know someone tampered with it.
- **Availability** → A hospital emergency room. It must be open 24×7, regardless of attack.

## 1.3 AAA — Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (Non-Repudiation + Accountability)

In addition to the CIA Triad, KTU 2024 module stresses the **AAA Framework** and two related concepts:

- **Authentication**: Proving **WHO you are** (e.g., password, fingerprint, OTP, digital certificate).
- **Authorization**: Determining **WHAT you are allowed to do** once authenticated (e.g., read-only vs. admin access).
- **Accounting / Auditing**: Keeping a **record** of what you did (e.g., log files).
- **Non-Repudiation**: Ensuring the sender **cannot later deny** having sent a message (achieved via digital signatures).
- **Accountability**: The ability to trace actions back to a specific user, ensuring they are **held responsible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication ≠ Authorization.** This is a classic 3-mark KTU Part A trap question. *Authentication answers "Who are you?" while Authorization answers "What are you allowed to do?"*

## 1.4 Standard Security Metrics & Constants

- **Symmetric Key Length:** AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 bits.
- **Asymmetric Key Length:** RSA typically **2048** or **4096** bits.
- **Hash Output Lengths:** MD5 = **128 bits**, SHA-1 = **160 bits**, SHA-256 = **256 bits**.

## 1.5 Core Attack Terminologies

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential event that can cause harm to a network system. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system that can be exploited. |
| **Risk** | The probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability, multiplied by its impact. |
| **Attack** | An intentional act attempting to bypass security controls. |
| **Exploit** | The actual code or technique used to take advantage of a vulnerability. |
| **Payload** | The malicious part of an attack that performs the damaging action. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation: Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**
> A vulnerability *without* a threat is harmless. A threat *without* a vulnerability cannot strike. Both must exist for risk to materialize.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Network Attacks

KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically emphasizes the **two broad classes of attacks** and the **four categories of security threats**.

### 2.1.1 Passive vs. Active Attacks

| Category | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Monitors or eavesdrops on data without altering it. | Modifies, deletes, inserts, or fabricates data. |
| **Effect on Integrity** | Does **not** change data. | **Alters** data or system state. |
| **Detectability** | Difficult to detect (silent). | Easier to detect (leaves traces). |
| **Examples** | Eavesdropping, Traffic Analysis, Sniffing. | Masquerading, Replay, DoS, Message Modification. |
| **CIA Pillar Broken** | Breaks **Confidentiality**. | Breaks **Integrity** and **Availability**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A classic 7-mark question asks to "Differentiate between passive and active attacks with examples." Always present a comparative table AND add a real-world example (e.g., Wireshark sniffing = passive; ARP poisoning = active).

### 2.1.2 The Four Categories of Security Threats (Garfinkel-Simmons / KTU Standard)

The KTU syllabus adopts the **four general categories of security threats**:

1. **Interruption (Availability Attack)**: A network service becomes unavailable. *Example:* Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack, server crash.
2. **Interception (Confidentiality Attack)**: An unauthorized party gains access to data. *Example:* Wiretapping, packet sniffing.
3. **Modification (Integrity Attack)**: Unauthorized tampering of data in transit. *Example:* Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) altering transaction amounts.
4. **Fabrication (Authenticity Attack)**: Generating fake data or identities. *Example:* IP spoofing, forging emails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** **"IIMF"** → Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication. The first two letters of each are **I, I, M, F**.

## 2.2 Cryptography Terminologies (High-Yield)

Cryptography is the **mathematical backbone** of network security. KTU requires fluency in the following terms:

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Plaintext (P)** | The original, readable message before encryption. |
| **Ciphertext (C)** | The scrambled, unreadable output after encryption. |
| **Encryption (E)** | Process of converting plaintext into ciphertext. $C = E(P)$ |
| **Decryption (D)** | Process of converting ciphertext back to plaintext. $P = D(C)$ |
| **Key (K)** | A secret parameter used in encryption/decryption. |
| **Algorithm (Cipher)** | The mathematical function used for transformation. |
| **Cryptanalysis** | The art of breaking ciphers without knowing the key. |
| **Cryptology** | The combined study of cryptography + cryptanalysis. |
| **Steganography** | Hiding the *existence* of the message itself (e.g., embedding in an image). |
| **Digital Signature** | A cryptographic value used to verify authenticity and integrity. |
| **Hash Function** | One-way function that maps data of any size to a fixed-size output. |
| **Digital Certificate** | An electronic document binding a public key to an identity. |
| **PKI** | Public Key Infrastructure — the framework managing digital certificates. |

### 2.2.1 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Cryptography

| Property | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric (Public) Key |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key. | Pair: Public key + Private key. |
| **Speed** | Very fast (suitable for bulk data). | Slow (suitable for small data / keys). |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish, RC4. | RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal. |
| **Key Distribution** | Major problem (must share secret securely). | Solved via public key certificates. |
| **Number of Keys for n users** | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ |

### 2.2.2 Hash Function Properties (KTU High-Yield)

A cryptographic hash function $H$ must satisfy:
1. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output.
2. **Fixed-Length Output** — Regardless of input size, output is fixed (e.g., 256 bits).
3. **Pre-image Resistance** — Given $h$, it is infeasible to find $m$ such that $H(m) = h$.
4. **Second Pre-image Resistance** — Given $m_1$, infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
5. **Collision Resistance** — Infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $m_1, m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
6. **Avalanche Effect** — A tiny change in input drastically changes the output.

## 2.3 Malware Terminologies

| Malware Type | Definition | Self-Replicating? | Needs User Action? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Malicious code attached to a legitimate program. | Yes (within files). | **Yes**. |
| **Worm** | Self-propagating malicious program over a network. | Yes (autonomous). | **No**. |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software; has hidden payload. | No. | Yes (tricking user). |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts victim data; demands payment for decryption key. | No. | Yes (often phishing). |
| **Spyware** | Secretly monitors user activity and reports it. | No. | Yes (bundled). |
| **Adware** | Displays unwanted advertisements, often bundled with free software. | No. | Yes. |
| **Rootkit** | Hides itself and other malware deep in the OS. | No. | Often No. |
| **Bot / Botnet** | A compromised computer remotely controlled (a "zombie"). | Yes (via C&C). | No. |
| **Keylogger** | Records every keystroke typed by the user. | No. | Yes (install). |
| **Backdoor** | A secret entry point bypassing normal authentication. | No. | No. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Classic Question:** *"Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm."* The single most important distinction is that a **virus needs a host file and user action**, while a **worm is self-contained and propagates autonomously over the network**.

## 2.4 Network Defense Tool Terminologies

| Tool | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Firewall** | — | Filters traffic between networks based on predefined rules. |
| **IDS** | Intrusion Detection System | **Monitors** and **alerts** on suspicious activity (passive). |
| **IPS** | Intrusion Prevention System | **Detects AND blocks** attacks in real-time (active). |
| **VPN** | Virtual Private Network | Creates a **secure encrypted tunnel** over a public network. |
| **SSL/TLS** | Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security | Encrypts HTTP traffic (HTTPS = HTTP + TLS). |
| **DMZ** | Demilitarized Zone | A buffer network between internal LAN and the internet. |
| **Honeypot** | — | A decoy system to attract and study attackers. |
| **SIEM** | Security Information and Event Management | Real-time analysis of security alerts across a network. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encryption Operation | $C = E_K(P)$ | Produces ciphertext from plaintext. |
| 2 | Decryption Operation | $P = D_K(C)$ | Recovers plaintext from ciphertext. |
| 3 | RSA Public Key | $n = p \times q$ | Product of two large primes (typically **2048 bits**). |
| 4 | RSA Encryption | $C = P^e \bmod n$ | Sender's operation. |
| 5 | RSA Decryption | $P = C^d \bmod n$ | Receiver's operation. |
| 6 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Used to compute $d$. |
| 7 | Symmetric Keys (n users) | $K_{sym} = n(n-1)/2$ | Number of shared secrets needed. |
| 8 | Asymmetric Keys (n users) | $K_{asym} = 2n$ | Each user has a key pair. |
| 9 | Risk Equation | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact. |
| 10 | Avalanche Target | $\geq 50\%$ bits change | On a 1-bit input change. |
| 11 | Hash Output: SHA-256 | $256$ bits / $64$ hex chars | Modern standard. |
| 12 | Hash Output: MD5 | $128$ bits / $32$ hex chars | **Deprecated / Broken**. |
| 13 | AES Block Size | $128$ bits | Fixed block cipher size. |
| 14 | AES Key Sizes | $128, 192, 256$ bits | Standard approved by NIST. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These terminologies form the vocabulary used by network engineers, SOC analysts, ethical hackers, and developers daily. **CIA Triad** is the basis of every ISO 27001 audit; **AAA Framework** underpins every enterprise-grade identity solution (e.g., RADIUS, TACACS+, OAuth 2.0); **PKI** is what makes HTTPS, e-commerce, and digital banking trustworthy. Without this standardized terminology, incident reports, vulnerability disclosures, and security policies would be uninterpretable.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> This section focuses on the few areas of network security terminology that involve quantitative analysis: RSA key generation, key distribution scaling, and risk calculation.

## 3.1 Derivation: RSA Key Generation (Step-by-Step)

The **Rivest-Shamir-Adleman (RSA)** algorithm is the most important asymmetric cipher in the KTU syllabus. Below is its complete step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1 — Choose Two Large Primes
Select two distinct large prime numbers $p$ and $q$.

$$
p = 61, \quad q = 53
$$

**Logic:** RSA's security relies on the practical impossibility of factoring $n = p \times q$ back to its prime factors when $p$ and $q$ are huge.

### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \times q \\
n &= 61 \times 53 \\
n &= 3233
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This $n$ is the **public modulus** used in both encryption and decryption. Its bit length (e.g., 2048 bits) is the security parameter.

### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p - 1)(q - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= (61 - 1)(53 - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= 60 \times 52 \\
\phi(n) &= 3120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $\phi(n)$ counts the integers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$. It is the foundation of RSA's mathematical strength.

### Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Select $e$ such that:
- $1 < e < \phi(n)$
- $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$

$$
e = 17
$$

**Verification:** $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ ✓ (since 17 is prime and does not divide 3120).

### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$

Find $d$ such that:

$$
e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$

This means $e \cdot d - 1$ must be a multiple of $\phi(n) = 3120$. We solve:

$$
d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
17 \cdot d &\equiv 1 \pmod{3120} \\
d &= 17^{-1} \bmod 3120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

We solve $17x + 3120y = 1$.

- $3120 = 17 \cdot 183 + 9$
- $17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$
- $9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$
- $8 = 1 \cdot 8 + 0$

Back-substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 9 - 8 \cdot 1 \\
1 &= 9 - (17 - 9 \cdot 1) \cdot 1 \\
1 &= 2 \cdot 9 - 17 \\
1 &= 2 \cdot (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) - 17 \\
1 &= 2 \cdot 3120 - 367 \cdot 17
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $d = -367 \bmod 3120$.

$$
d = 3120 - 367 = 2753
$$

**Verification:** $17 \times 2753 = 46801$. Now $46801 \bmod 3120 = 46801 - 15 \times 3120 = 46801 - 46800 = 1$ ✓.

### Step 6 — Publish the Public Key and Keep the Private Key Secret

$$
\text{Public Key: } (e, n) = (17, 3233)
$$

$$
\text{Private Key: } (d, n) = (2753, 3233)
$$

### Step 7 — Encrypt a Plaintext Message $M$

Suppose Alice wants to send Bob the message $M = 65$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= M^e \bmod n \\
C &= 65^{17} \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing $65^{17} \bmod 3233$ using repeated squaring:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
65^1 \bmod 3233 &= 65 \\
65^2 \bmod 3233 &= 4225 \bmod 3233 = 992 \\
65^4 \bmod 3233 &= 992^2 \bmod 3233 = 984064 \bmod 3233 = 1140 \\
65^8 \bmod 3233 &= 1140^2 \bmod 3233 = 1299600 \bmod 3233 = 2790 \\
65^{16} \bmod 3233 &= 2790^2 \bmod 3233 = 7784100 \bmod 3233 = 2440
\end{aligned}
$$

Now combine (since $17 = 16 + 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
65^{17} \bmod 3233 &= 65^{16} \times 65^1 \bmod 3233 \\
&= 2440 \times 65 \bmod 3233 \\
&= 158600 \bmod 3233 \\
&= 158600 - 49 \times 3233 \\
&= 158600 - 158417 \\
&= 183
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
C = 183
$$

### Step 8 — Decrypt the Ciphertext

Bob receives $C = 183$ and decrypts using his private key $d = 2753$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= C^d \bmod n \\
M &= 183^{2753} \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the property $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod{n}$, we recover:

$$
M = 65
$$

**Logic of recovery:** RSA works because Euler's theorem guarantees $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for $\gcd(M,n)=1$, and since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we have $M^{ed} \equiv M^1 \equiv M \pmod{n}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Key Scaling

A frequent KTU Part A question is: *“How many keys are needed for n users in symmetric vs. asymmetric cryptography?”*

### Derivation

In **symmetric cryptography**, every pair of users must share a unique secret key. The number of unordered pairs from $n$ users is:

$$
K_{sym} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

In **asymmetric cryptography**, each user has **one public key and one private key**:

$$
K_{asym} = 2n
$$

### Worked Comparison Table

| Number of Users (n) | Symmetric Keys Required $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Asymmetric Keys Required $2n$ | Ratio (Sym/Asym) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | $10$ | $10$ | $1.0$ |
| 10 | $45$ | $20$ | $2.25$ |
| 50 | $1225$ | $100$ | $12.25$ |
| 100 | $4950$ | $200$ | $24.75$ |
| 1000 | $499500$ | $2000$ | $249.75$ |

**Key Insight:** As $n$ grows, the number of symmetric keys grows **quadratically** ($O(n^2)$), while asymmetric keys grow **linearly** ($O(n)$). This is why **public key cryptography solves the key distribution problem** for large networks.

## 3.3 Risk Calculation Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A corporate server has a Threat probability of 0.3, a Vulnerability severity score of 8 (on 1–10), and a business Impact score of 9 (on 1–10). Compute the Risk.

**Step 1 — Identify inputs:**

- Threat $T = 0.3$
- Vulnerability $V = 8$
- Impact $I = 9$

**Step 2 — Apply Risk Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= T \times V \times I \\
R &= 0.3 \times 8 \times 9 \\
R &= 21.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret on a 0–30 scale:**

| Range | Risk Level |
|---|---|
| 0 – 5 | **Low** |
| 6 – 15 | **Medium** |
| 16 – 25 | **High** |
| 26 – 30 | **Critical** |

**Result:** $R = 21.6$ → **High Risk** → Immediate remediation required.

## 3.4 Verification: Properties of a Valid Hash

For a message $M$ of arbitrary length, a secure hash function $H$ must yield an output $h$ of fixed length. The **avalanche effect** verification:

**Problem:** SHA-256 hash of `"Hello"` vs. `"hello"` (only the case of the first letter changes).

**Step 1 — Compute $H$("Hello"):**

$$
H(\text{Hello}) = 185f8db32271fe25f561a6fc938b2e264306ec304eda518007d1764826381969
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H$("hello"):**

$$
H(\text{hello}) = 2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824
$$

**Step 3 — Compare bit-by-bit:**

Count the number of differing hex characters out of 64 total. In this case, the two digests differ in **virtually every hex character** — confirming the **avalanche effect**: a 1-bit change in input ($H$ → $h$ in ASCII = one flipped bit) caused a massive change in output. This is the hallmark of a **cryptographically strong** hash.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Table

| Concept | Quantitative Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **CIA Triad** | 3 pillars | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. |
| **IIMF Threats** | 4 categories | Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication. |
| **AAA Framework** | 3 services | Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. |
| **Symmetric Keys (n users)** | $n(n-1)/2$ | Quadratic growth. |
| **Asymmetric Keys (n users)** | $2n$ | Linear growth. |
| **RSA Modulus** | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public modulus. |
| **RSA Totient** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient. |
| **RSA Encryption** | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | Sender operation. |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | Receiver operation. |
| **Risk Score** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Quantified risk. |
| **Hash Avalanche** | $\geq 50\%$ bit change | 1-bit input change. |


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual takeaway:** *Passive attacks* sit on one branch and **break Confidentiality**. *Active attacks* split into **four sub-types** matching the **IIMF** threat model: Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication (plus the popular Replay attack).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cryptographic System Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    PT[PLAINTEXT P] --> ENC[ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM E]
    KEY1[SECRET KEY K] --> ENC
    ENC --> CT[CIPHERTEXT C]
    CT --> CHAN[UNSECURED CHANNEL INTERNET]
    CHAN --> DEC[DECRYPTION ALGORITHM D]
    KEY2[SAME KEY K SYMMETRIC] --> DEC
    DEC --> PT2[RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P]

    ATKR[ATTACKER CRYPTANALYST] -.->|Attempts to break C| CHAN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual takeaway:** For **symmetric encryption**, the **same key K** is used at both ends. The attacker sits in the middle on the channel but cannot decrypt $C$ without $K$. For **asymmetric encryption**, the diagram would have a public key $K_{pub}$ at the sender and a different private key $K_{priv}$ at the receiver.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Asymmetric (Public Key) Cryptography Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[ALICE SENDER] -->|1 Look up| KDC[PUBLIC KEY DIRECTORY]
    KDC -->|2 Returns Bob public key| S
    S -->|3 Encrypts C = E_pub of Bob M| CHAN[INSECURE CHANNEL]
    CHAN --> R[BOB RECEIVER]
    R -->|4 Decrypts M = D_priv of Bob C| PT[PLAINTEXT M]
    R -->|5 Signs with private key| SIG[DIGITAL SIGNATURE]
    SIG -->|6 Verified by Alice with Bob public key| S

    ATKR2[ATTACKER] -.->|Cannot decrypt without private key| CHAN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual takeaway:** Asymmetric encryption solves the **key distribution problem** by using a public key directory. The attacker cannot decrypt the message because they do not possess Bob's private key. The **digital signature** in step 5 provides **non-repudiation**.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Network Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    EXT[EXTERNAL INTERNET] --> FW1[PERIMETER FIREWALL]
    FW1 --> DMZ[DMZ DEMILITARIZED ZONE]
    DMZ --> IDS[INTRUSION DETECTION SYSTEM]
    IDS --> FW2[INTERNAL FIREWALL]
    FW2 --> VPN[VPN GATEWAY FOR REMOTE USERS]
    VPN --> LAN[INTERNAL LAN]
    LAN --> HONEYPOT[HONEYPOT DECOY SYSTEM]
    LAN --> SIEM[SIEM SECURITY INFORMATION AND EVENT MANAGEMENT]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual takeaway:** This is a **layered defense model** (defense-in-depth). No single layer is sufficient; multiple security tools work in concert. The **perimeter firewall** blocks bulk traffic; the **DMZ** isolates public-facing servers; the **IDS** watches for anomalies; the **internal firewall** segments the LAN; the **VPN** secures remote access; the **SIEM** correlates logs; the **host-based controls** protect individual machines.

