# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define the CIA Triad. Why is it considered the foundation of network security?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CIA Triad** is a model representing the three core goals of network security:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that data is accessible only to those authorized to view it. (1 mark)
2. **Integrity** — Ensuring that data is not altered or tampered with during storage or transmission. (1 mark)
3. **Availability** — Ensuring that network services and data are accessible to authorized users whenever required. (1 mark)

It is considered the foundation of network security because every security policy, mechanism, and technology is designed to protect one or more of these three properties. Any compromise of CIA represents a successful attack.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Malicious code that attaches itself to a legitimate host program/file. | Standalone malicious program that propagates over a network. |
| **Self-Replicating** | Yes, but **requires a host** to spread. | Yes, **autonomously** without a host. |
| **User Action Required** | Yes (e.g., opening an infected file). | **No** (spreads automatically). |
| **Speed of Spread** | Slow. | Very fast (can infect thousands in minutes). |
| **Example** | ILOVEYOU email virus. | Blaster, Conficker, Morris Worm. |

(3 marks for the comparative table and 1 example each.)

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ **QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** — Choose this OR Question B.

#### **Q.A (a) Explain the four general categories of security threats (IIMF) with suitable examples.** **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Network security threats can be classified into four general categories. Each category represents a different way in which the security of a system can be compromised.

**1. Interruption (Availability Attack) [1.5 marks]**
- A network service or system becomes unavailable or destroyed.
- This is an attack on the **Availability** pillar of the CIA Triad.
- **Example:** A **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** attack floods a server with traffic, making it unable to respond to legitimate users. Other examples include hardware failure, file deletion, or cutting of communication lines.

**2. Interception (Confidentiality Attack) [1.5 marks]**
- An unauthorized party gains access to data while it is being transmitted or stored.
- This is an attack on the **Confidentiality** pillar of the CIA Triad.
- **Example:** A hacker using a **packet sniffer** (e.g., Wireshark) on an unencrypted Wi-Fi network to capture credit card numbers, or an insider copying confidential files.

**3. Modification (Integrity Attack) [1.5 marks]**
- An unauthorized party alters data in transit or at rest.
- This is an attack on the **Integrity** pillar of the CIA Triad.
- **Example:** A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacker intercepts a bank transaction and changes the destination account number to their own. Another example: changing grades in a university database.

**4. Fabrication (Authenticity Attack) [1.5 marks]**
- An unauthorized party generates fake objects, identities, or data that appear legitimate.
- This is an attack on **Authenticity**, often linked to **Authentication**.
- **Example:** **IP Spoofing** where an attacker forges the source IP address of packets. Another example: **email spoofing** where phishing emails appear to come from a trusted sender.

**Diagram:**
A clear block diagram mapping IIMF threats to the broken CIA pillar is recommended for full marks.

---

#### **Q.A (b) Explain symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. Compare them and give two examples of each.** **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Symmetric Key Cryptography [3 marks]**
- Uses a **single shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption.
- The sender and receiver must agree on and securely exchange the key beforehand.
- **Mathematical Representation:**
  - Encryption: $C = E_K(P)$
  - Decryption: $P = D_K(C)$
  - The same $K$ is used in both.
- **Advantages:** Very fast; suitable for encrypting bulk data.
- **Disadvantages:** Key distribution is difficult; number of keys grows as $n(n-1)/2$.
- **Examples:** **AES** (128/192/256-bit keys), **DES** (56-bit), **3DES**, **Blowfish**, **RC4**.

**Asymmetric Key Cryptography [3 marks]**
- Uses a **pair of keys**: a **public key** (freely distributed) and a **private key** (kept secret).
- Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the corresponding private key.
- **Mathematical Representation:**
  - Encryption: $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$
  - Decryption: $P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$
- **Advantages:** Solves key distribution; enables digital signatures; only $2n$ keys for $n$ users.
- **Disadvantages:** Much slower than symmetric; computationally expensive.
- **Examples:** **RSA** (2048-bit), **ECC** (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), **Diffie-Hellman**, **ElGamal**.

**Comparison Table [1 mark]**

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| Keys | 1 shared | 2 (public + private pair) |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Key Distribution | Hard | Easy (public key directory) |
| Key Count for n users | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ |
| Primary Use | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

---

### ❓ **QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** — Alternative Choice.

#### **Q.B (a) Define the following terms with examples: (i) Threat (ii) Vulnerability (iii) Risk (iv) Exploit (v) Payload (vi) Non-Repudiation (vii) Social Engineering.** **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (1 mark each):**

1. **Threat** — Any potential event or actor that can cause harm to a system. *Example:* A hacker group, a malware campaign, or a natural disaster.

2. **Vulnerability** — A weakness or flaw in a system that can be exploited. *Example:* An unpatched Windows server with a known SMB buffer overflow bug.

3. **Risk** — The likelihood that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact. *Example:* Risk = (Threat Probability 0.4) × (Vulnerability Severity 8) × (Impact 9) = **28.8 (Critical)**.

4. **Exploit** — The specific code, technique, or tool used to take advantage of a vulnerability. *Example:* **EternalBlue** exploit targeting SMBv1 vulnerability.

5. **Payload** — The malicious component of an attack that performs the actual damage after the exploit succeeds. *Example:* A **ransomware** payload that encrypts the victim's hard drive.

6. **Non-Repudiation** — A security property ensuring that the sender of a message **cannot later deny** having sent it. *Example:* A **digital signature** on a contract.

7. **Social Engineering** — Psychological manipulation of people into performing actions or revealing confidential information. *Example:* A **phishing email** pretending to be from a bank, asking the user to click a link and enter credentials.

---

#### **Q.B (b) Describe the various types of malware. Differentiate clearly between Virus, Worm, and Trojan Horse.** **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Malware (5 marks):**
- **Virus** — Attaches to a host program; spreads when the program runs.
- **Worm** — Self-propagates over the network; no host or user action needed.
- **Trojan Horse** — Disguised as legitimate software; has a hidden malicious payload.
- **Ransomware** — Encrypts victim data and demands a ransom (e.g., WannaCry).
- **Spyware** — Secretly monitors user activity and reports it back.
- **Adware** — Displays unwanted advertisements, often bundled with freeware.
- **Rootkit** — Gains privileged access and hides itself and other malware.
- **Bot / Botnet** — A network of "zombie" computers controlled by an attacker (bot-herder).
- **Keylogger** — Records every keystroke typed by the user.
- **Backdoor** — Secret entry point bypassing normal authentication.

**Comparison: Virus vs. Worm vs. Trojan [2 marks]**

| Property | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|---|---|---|---|
| Needs host file | **Yes** | **No** | **No** (appears as legit software) |
| Needs user action | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** (to install) |
| Self-replicating | Yes (with host) | Yes (autonomous) | **No** |
| Spreads via | Infected files | Network connections | Tricking user to install |
| Example | ILOVEYOU | Blaster | Fake game installer |

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Authentication with Authorization.** Authentication = "Who are you?" Authorization = "What are you allowed to do?" Examiners specifically deduct marks for this swap.
>
> 2. **Writing "Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability" without explaining each.** Listing the CIA Triad alone is worth only 1 mark; you must briefly explain each pillar to earn 3 full marks in Part A.
>
> 3. **Confusing Risk with Threat.** A *threat* is a potential danger; *risk* is the probability × impact. Examiners will mark you down if you treat them as synonyms.
>
> 4. **In Part B, writing only one example per threat category.** KTU examiners expect **at least two examples** for the four IIMF threats to score full 7 marks. Use real-world attacks (e.g., WannaCry, Mirai Botnet, Heartbleed).
>
> 5. **Skipping the comparison table in "Differentiate between X and Y" questions.** KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly awards 1–2 marks for a clean comparative table.
>
> 6. **For RSA, forgetting to verify that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.** This verification step is a **mandatory** 1-mark item.
>
> 7. **Calling Malware types as "computer bugs"** — Examiners expect the formal terms: *Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Rootkit, Spyware, etc.*
>
> 8. **Spelling mistakes in technical terms** (e.g., "Asymetric" instead of "Asymmetric"; "Malwere" instead of "Malware") lead to -0.5 mark penalties per occurrence.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. The bedrock of all security.
- ✅ **AAA Framework** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. Plus **Non-Repudiation** and **Accountability**.
- ✅ **Four Threat Categories (IIMF)** = Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication.
- ✅ **Passive vs. Active** = Passive monitors (sniffing); Active alters (DoS, MITM).
- ✅ **Cryptography Terms** = Plaintext, Ciphertext, Key, Algorithm, Encryption, Decryption.
- ✅ **Symmetric Key Count** = $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users (quadratic growth).
- ✅ **Asymmetric Key Count** = $2n$ keys for $n$ users (linear growth). Public + Private.
- ✅ **RSA Equation** = $C = P^e \bmod n$, $P = C^d \bmod n$, $n = p \cdot q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- ✅ **Hash Function Properties** = Deterministic, Fixed-length, Pre-image resistant, Collision resistant, Avalanche effect.
- ✅ **Common Hash Algorithms** = MD5 (broken, 128-bit), SHA-1 (deprecated, 160-bit), SHA-256 (secure, 256-bit).
- ✅ **Malware** = Virus (host+user), Worm (self), Trojan (disguised), Ransomware (encrypts), Spyware (monitors), Rootkit (hides), Botnet (zombies).
- ✅ **Defense Tools** = Firewall, IDS (alerts), IPS (blocks), VPN (tunnel), DMZ (buffer), SIEM (correlation), Honeypot (decoy).
- ✅ **Risk Equation** = $R = T \times V \times I$ (Threat × Vulnerability × Impact).
- ✅ **Authentication ≠ Authorization** — Memorize this distinction.
- ✅ **Active attacks** can break **Integrity, Availability, and Authenticity**; **Passive attacks** mainly break **Confidentiality**.
- ✅ **Confidentiality** is protected by **Encryption**; **Integrity** by **Hashes + Digital Signatures**; **Availability** by **Redundancy + Anti-DoS**.
- ✅ **Non-Repudiation** is uniquely provided by **Digital Signatures**, not by simple passwords.
- ✅ **PKI** = the entire trust framework of certificates, CAs, and public keys that makes HTTPS, e-commerce, and secure email possible.
- ✅ Always **provide a diagram or comparative table** in Part B answers — KTU examiners reward visual structure with bonus marks.
- ✅ Real-world example for every concept (WannaCry, Mirai, Heartbleed, ILOVEYOU) — Examiners appreciate applied knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DDoS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DDoS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** attack is a coordinated, malicious attempt to render a targeted computing resource (server, network, service, or application) unavailable to its legitimate end-users by simultaneously flooding it with an overwhelming volume of traffic or resource-exhausting requests from multiple, geographically distributed compromised machines.

Formally, if $R_{capacity}$ represents the maximum service capacity of a target system (measured in requests per second, packets per second, or bandwidth in bps), then a DDoS attack succeeds when the aggregate attack traffic $T_{attack}$ generated by $n$ distributed attacker nodes satisfies:

$$
T_{attack} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i} \;\gg\; R_{capacity}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A DDoS attack uses multiple compromised systems (botnets/zombies) as sources of attack traffic. The "distributed" nature is what differentiates it from a single-source DoS attack, making it exponentially harder to defend against.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single-lane highway toll booth** that can normally process **100 cars per minute**.

- A **DoS attack** is like **one crazy driver** standing at the toll booth, blocking all other cars from passing.
- A **DDoS attack** is like **10,000 crazy drivers** simultaneously arriving at that single toll booth from **every direction, every road, and every state** — all trying to pay their toll at the exact same moment. The police (defenders) cannot simply block one road; they must block thousands of incoming roads simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Key Ingredients of a DDoS Attack:**
> 1. **Victim** — the target server/service.
> 2. **Attack Infrastructure** — the botnet (army of compromised "zombie" machines).
> 3. **Attack Vectors** — the communication mechanism (e.g., SYN flood, UDP flood, HTTP flood).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in DDoS Quantification

- **Bandwidth (Gbps / Tbps):** Volume-based measurement of total throughput.
- **Packets per Second (Pps):** Protocol-layer measurement.
- **Requests per Second (Rps):** Application-layer measurement.
- **Bit Rate (bps):** Raw data rate.
- **Botnet Size ($n$):** Number of compromised hosts controlled by the attacker.
- **Attack Duration ($t_a$):** Time window in seconds the attack is sustained.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Scale:** The largest recorded DDoS attack peaked at **~5.6 Tbps** (Cloudflare mitigated in 2024). For comparison, the entire global average Internet traffic is measured in tens of Tbps — meaning a single attack can disrupt large fractions of country-level connectivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of a DDoS Attack

A DDoS attack does not occur spontaneously. It follows a structured **multi-stage operational pipeline**:

1. **Reconnaissance Phase**
   - The attacker scans the network to identify vulnerable hosts.
   - Tools: Nmap, mass-scanners (e.g., Mirai's telnet brute-forcer).
   - Goal: Identify candidate machines for recruitment.

2. **Botnet Recruitment (Weaponization)**
   - Vulnerable IoT devices, PCs, and servers are infected with malware.
   - The malware installs a **handler-agent** architecture: the attacker commands a small set of **Command-and-Control (C2) servers**, which then relay instructions to thousands of **bots/zombies**.

3. **C2 Communication Establishment**
   - **Centralized Model:** Bots connect to a fixed C2 server (easy to dismantle).
   - **Decentralized/P2P Model:** Bots form an overlay network; no single takedown point.
   - **IRC / HTTP / HTTPS / Tor-based** channels are commonly abused.

4. **Attack Initiation**
   - Attacker triggers synchronized flooding from all bots toward the victim IP/domain.
   - Spoofed source IPs are often used to obscure the true origin.

5. **Sustained Disruption**
   - The victim exhausts CPU cycles, memory buffers, link bandwidth, or socket connections.
   - Legitimate users experience latency spikes, failed handshakes, and timeout errors.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of DDoS Attack Vectors

DDoS attacks are universally classified into **three primary categories** by the KTU syllabus and by OWASP/RFC 4732:

### Category 1 — Volume-Based (Bandwidth Saturation)
- **Goal:** Consume the network's bandwidth.
- **Mechanism:** Saturate the link with high-rate UDP/ICMP packets.
- **Metrics:** Measured in **Gbps**.
- **Examples:** UDP flood, ICMP flood (Ping of Death), Amplification (DNS, NTP, Memcached).

### Category 2 — Protocol (State-Exhaustion) Attacks
- **Goal:** Exhaust server resources, connection state tables, or firewall state.
- **Mechanism:** Exploit weaknesses in Layer 3/4 protocol stacks.
- **Metrics:** Measured in **Pps**.
- **Examples:** SYN flood, Smurf attack, Ping of Death, Fragmented packet attacks.

### Category 3 — Application-Layer Attacks
- **Goal:** Crash the web server / database by exhausting application-level resources.
- **Mechanism:** Mimic legitimate HTTP/HTTPS/DNS queries; appear as "real users."
- **Metrics:** Measured in **Rps**.
- **Examples:** HTTP GET/POST flood, Slowloris, RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet), DNS query flood.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Application-Layer is Hardest to Defend:** Application-layer DDoS uses low-bandwidth, legitimate-looking traffic. A single attacker generating 10 HTTP requests/second can sometimes cripple a server, because each request triggers expensive DB queries or session-establishment work.

## 2.3 The Botnet Architecture (Attack Infrastructure)

The **botnet** is the engine of any DDoS attack. It is a network of internet-connected devices, each running a piece of malicious code (a **bot** or **zombie**).

$$
\text{Botnet} = \{B_1, B_2, B_3, \ldots, B_n\}
$$

where each $B_i$ is an infected host running the attacker's payload. The aggregate throughput is:

$$
T_{botnet} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}^{rate}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous Botnet Case Study (KTU Favorite):** The **Mirai botnet (2016)** infected ~600,000 IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) by brute-forcing default telnet credentials. It launched a **~1.2 Tbps** attack on Dyn DNS, taking down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub across the US East Coast.

## 2.4 DDoS Amplification — The "Free Lunch" Attack

Amplification attacks exploit **UDP-based protocols with asymmetric response ratios**. A small spoofed request generates a **much larger** response directed at the victim.

The **Amplification Factor (AF)** is defined as:

$$
AF = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}
$$

The **Bandwidth Amplification Factor (BAF)** is:

$$
BAF = \frac{\text{Response Bandwidth (bps)}}{\text{Request Bandwidth (bps)}}
$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Memcached Amplification (2018):** An attacker sends a 15-byte spoofed request → memcached server returns a **750 KB** response.
> $AF = 750{,}000 / 15 = 50{,}000\times$ amplification. With a small botnet, multi-Tbps attacks become feasible.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attack Volume Threshold | $T_{attack} > R_{capacity}$ | bps / pps / rps | Defines successful DDoS |
| Aggregate Botnet Rate | $T_{botnet} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}^{rate}$ | bps | Sum of individual bot throughputs |
| Amplification Factor | $AF = \text{Response}_{bytes} / \text{Request}_{bytes}$ | dimensionless | Higher = more dangerous |
| Bandwidth Amp. Factor | $BAF = \text{Response}_{bps} / \text{Request}_{bps}$ | dimensionless | Used in UDP amplification |
| Reflection Multiplier | $M = N_{reflectors}$ | count | Number of servers fooled |
| Legitimate Ratio | $L_{ratio} = R_{legit} / (R_{legit} + R_{attack})$ | fraction | Goal of attacker: minimize this |
| Half-Open Connection Rate | $\Delta C = R_{syn} - R_{syn-ack} \cdot P_{accept}$ | conn/s | Used in SYN flood modeling |
| TCP State-Table Exhaustion | $S_{max} = M_{buffer} / S_{per-conn}$ | connections | Each half-open conn uses memory |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Tip:** For KTU 2-mark questions, you only need to remember the **three attack categories** (Volume / Protocol / Application) and **two metrics** (Gbps for volume, Rps for application). Protocol attacks use Pps.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Traffic Engineering:** ISPs use DDoS analytics to provision scrubbing capacity.
- **CDN/Edge Networks:** Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield integrate DDoS defense at edge POPs.
- **Cybersecurity Operations Centers (SOCs):** SIEM rules correlate anomalies in flow records to detect DDoS in real time.
- **IoT Security Standards:** Post-Mirai, NIST issued SP 800-183 emphasizing IoT device hardening.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> DDoS is fundamentally a **systems / network engineering** topic. While it lacks classical "math derivations," it is rich in **quantitative analytical models** that KTU examiners frequently test. Below are the most important derivations and analyses.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SYN Flood — State-Table Exhaustion Model

In a TCP three-way handshake, the server allocates a state-table entry ($S_{entry}$) for every incoming SYN. Legitimate clients complete the handshake; attackers never send the final ACK.

**Given:**
- Server memory buffer for half-open connections: $M_{buffer}$ (in bytes)
- Memory per half-open connection: $S_{per-conn}$ (in bytes)
- Attack SYN rate: $R_{syn}$ (connections per second)
- Legitimate completion rate: $R_{complete}$ (connections per second)

**Step 1:** Maximum number of half-open connections the server can hold:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{M_{buffer}}{S_{per-conn}}
$$

**Step 2:** Net accumulation rate of half-open connections under attack:

$$
\Delta S = R_{syn} - R_{complete}
$$

**Step 3:** Time-to-exhaustion ($t_{exhaust}$) of the state table:

$$
t_{exhaust} = \frac{S_{max}}{\Delta S} = \frac{M_{buffer} / S_{per-conn}}{R_{syn} - R_{complete}}
$$

**Step 4:** Example numerical evaluation.

Suppose: $M_{buffer} = 64\,\text{MB} = 67{,}108{,}864\,\text{bytes}$, $S_{per-conn} = 1024\,\text{bytes}$, $R_{syn} = 1000\,\text{syn/s}$, $R_{complete} = 10\,\text{conn/s}$ (mostly attackers, few legit).

$$
S_{max} = \frac{67{,}108{,}864}{1024} = 65{,}536\ \text{connections}
$$

$$
\Delta S = 1000 - 10 = 990\ \text{conn/s}
$$

$$
t_{exhaust} = \frac{65{,}536}{990} \approx 66.2\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** Within just over a minute, the server's TCP state table is full, and **legitimate clients are refused** (SYN cookies or backlog tuning required as mitigation).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amplification Attack — Total Victim Throughput

A DNS amplification attack uses open DNS resolvers as reflectors.

**Given:**
- Number of reflectors abused: $N_{reflectors}$
- Per-reflector request rate sent by attacker: $R_{req}$ (req/s)
- Amplification factor: $AF$
- Average request size: $P_{req}$ (bytes)
- Average response size: $P_{resp} = AF \cdot P_{req}$ (bytes)

**Step 1:** Total request bandwidth the attacker must generate:

$$
B_{attacker} = N_{reflectors} \times R_{req} \times P_{req} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte}\ \ (\text{bps})
$$

**Step 2:** Total response bandwidth hitting the victim (the DDoS traffic):

$$
B_{victim} = N_{reflectors} \times R_{req} \times P_{resp} \times 8 = B_{attacker} \times AF
$$

**Step 3:** Example calculation — DNS amplification.

$N_{reflectors} = 50{,}000$, $R_{req} = 10\,\text{req/s}$, $P_{req} = 60\,\text{bytes}$, $AF = 50$.

$$
B_{attacker} = 50{,}000 \times 10 \times 60 \times 8 = 240{,}000{,}000\ \text{bps} = 240\ \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{victim} = 240\ \text{Mbps} \times 50 = 12{,}000\ \text{Mbps} = 12\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 240 Mbps attacker link can flood a victim with **12 Gbps** of DNS-reflection traffic — the **free asymmetric weapon** of DDoS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Application-Layer HTTP Flood — Legitimate User Rejection

Application-layer attacks target expensive server-side operations.

**Given:**
- Server capacity: $C_{server}$ HTTP requests/second
- Attack request rate: $R_{attack}$ (rps)
- Legitimate request rate: $R_{legit}$ (rps)
- Each attack request causes: $W$ units of server work (e.g., DB queries, session creation)

**Step 1:** Effective server capacity consumed by attack:

$$
C_{consumed} = R_{attack} \times W
$$

**Step 2:** Available capacity for legitimate users:

$$
C_{available} = C_{server} - C_{consumed}
$$

**Step 3:** Fraction of legitimate users that can be served (the **Goodput**):

$$
G_{ratio} = \max\!\left(0,\ \frac{C_{available}}{R_{legit}}\right) = \max\!\left(0,\ \frac{C_{server} - R_{attack} \cdot W}{R_{legit}}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Example.

$C_{server} = 10{,}000\ \text{rps}$, $W = 5$ work units, $R_{attack} = 1500\ \text{rps}$, $R_{legit} = 500\ \text{rps}$.

$$
C_{consumed} = 1500 \times 5 = 7500\ \text{work units}
$$

$$
C_{available} = 10{,}000 - 7500 = 2500\ \text{units}
$$

$$
G_{ratio} = \frac{2500}{500} = 5
$$

Wait — the result $\gt 1$ means all legit users are served (over-provisioned). Let's try a more realistic case:

$R_{attack} = 1900\ \text{rps}$:

$$
C_{consumed} = 1900 \times 5 = 9500,\quad C_{available} = 500
$$

$$
G_{ratio} = \frac{500}{500} = 1 \quad \text{(at saturation)}
$$

$R_{attack} = 2000\ \text{rps}$:

$$
C_{available} = 10{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 0 \Rightarrow G_{ratio} = 0\ \text{(total outage)}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 20% increase in request rate causes a **100% outage for legitimate users**. This is the **asymmetric devastation** of application-layer DDoS.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DoS vs. DDoS

| Parameter | Denial-of-Service (DoS) | Distributed DoS (DDoS) |
|---|---|---|
| Source nodes | **1** attacker | **n** (botnet) |
| Source IPs | Single / spoofed | Thousands of unique IPs |
| Traceability | Easier | Very difficult |
| Bandwidth | Limited by attacker's link | Sum of botnet bandwidths |
| Defense | Block single IP | Requires distributed mitigation |
| Cost to attacker | Low–Medium | High (botnet maintenance) |
| Volume of attack | Mbps range | Gbps–Tbps range |
| Detection | Easy (anomaly from 1 IP) | Hard (distributed signature) |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack Categories (Syllabus-Critical)

| Category | OSI Layer | Target Resource | Metric | Example Attack | Defense |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volume-Based | L3 / L4 | Bandwidth link | **Gbps** | UDP flood, DNS amp | Blackhole, Scrubbing |
| Protocol | L3 / L4 | Server state, firewall | **Pps** | SYN flood, Ping of Death | SYN cookies, RST, Rate-limit |
| Application | L7 | Web app / DB | **Rps** | HTTP flood, Slowloris | WAF, CAPTCHA, JS challenge |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DDoS Attack Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Attacker Master Console] --> B[Command and Control C2 Server]
    B --> C1[Bot Node 1 - IoT Camera]
    B --> C2[Bot Node 2 - Compromised PC]
    B --> C3[Bot Node 3 - Vulnerable Server]
    B --> C4[Bot Node N - Mobile Device]
    C1 --> D[Coordinated Attack Traffic]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Victim Server or Network]
    E --> F[Legitimate User Requests Blocked]
    E --> G[Service Outage]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#e8590c,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style C3 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style C4 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style D fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#b197fc,stroke:#6741d9,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#e03131,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Traffic] --> B[Edge Router ACL]
    B --> C[ISP Scrubbing Center]
    C --> D[CDN and WAF Layer]
    D --> E[Load Balancer]
    E --> F[Origin Server]

    B -.Blocks.-> X[Blacklisted IPs]
    C -.Filters.-> Y[Anomalous Packets]
    D -.Challenges.-> Z[Bots and Scripts]
    E -.Distributes.-> W[Healthy Backends]
    F -.Serves.-> V[Legitimate Users]

    style A fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style C fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style D fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style E fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000000
    style F fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff
    style X fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style Y fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style Z fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style W fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 SYN Flood State-Exhaustion Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Botnet Attacker
    participant Server as Victim Server
    participant Legit as Legitimate Client

    Note over Server: State Table S max equals 65,536 entries

    loop Continuous SYN flood
        Attacker->>Server: SYN packet
        Server->>Attacker: SYN ACK (allocates state entry)
        Note right of Server: Attacker never sends final ACK
        Note over Server: Half open connection persists
    end

    Attacker->>Server: SYN packet 65,537
    Server--xAttacker: Connection dropped (state table full)
    Legit->>Server: SYN packet
    Server--xLegit: Service refused
    Note over Legit: Legitimate user denied service
```

## 4.4 DDoS Attack Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DDoS Attacks] --> B[Volume Based - L3 L4]
    A --> C[Protocol Based - L3 L4]
    A --> D[Application Based - L7]

    B --> B1[UDP Flood]
    B --> B2[ICMP Flood]
    B --> B3[DNS Amplification]
    B --> B4[NTP Amplification]
    B --> B5[Memcached Amplification]

    C --> C1[SYN Flood]
    C --> C2[Smurf Attack]
    C --> C3[Ping of Death]
    C --> C4[Fragmented Packet Attack]

    D --> D1[HTTP GET Flood]
    D --> D2[HTTP POST Flood]
    D --> D3[Slowloris]
    D --> D4[RUDY Attack]
    D --> D5[DNS Query Flood]

    style A fill:#1c7ed6,stroke:#0b4d8c,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#e8590c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#b197fc,stroke:#6741d9,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.5 DDoS Defense Strategy — Layered Mitigation Stack

| Layer | Defense Mechanism | Attack Type Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Network Edge** | Access Control Lists (ACL), uRPF | IP spoofing, simple floods |
| **L2 — ISP / Upstream** | BGP blackholing, RTBH | Volume-based attacks |
| **L3 — Scrubbing Center** | Deep Packet Inspection, anomaly detection | Amplification, protocol attacks |
| **L4 — CDN / Anycast** | Geographic load distribution | All volumetric |
| **L5 — WAF** | Signature, behavioral rules | Application-layer |
| **L6 — Application** | CAPTCHA, JS challenges, rate limits | Slowloris, HTTP flood |
| **L7 — Server** | SYN cookies, resource pooling | SYN flood, state exhaustion |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **confuse DoS with DDoS** — remember: DDoS uses **multiple** distributed sources.
> - Many students incorrectly classify **SYN flood as a volume-based attack** — it is **protocol-based** (state exhaustion), not bandwidth exhaustion.
> - Do **not** state that DDoS is "hacking the server" — DDoS **does not** breach confidentiality; it disrupts **availability** (one of the three CIA triad pillars).
> - When asked for "types of DDoS," always answer with the **three categories**: Volume, Protocol, Application. Listing only examples (UDP flood, SYN flood) without categories is a common mark-deduction mistake.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack. How does it differ from a traditional Denial of Service (DoS) attack?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack** is a malicious attempt to make an online service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic from **multiple compromised systems distributed across the internet**, typically controlled through a **botnet** and a **Command-and-Control (C2)** infrastructure.

**Key differences from DoS:**

| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Number of attack sources | Single source | Multiple (hundreds to millions) |
| Traceability | Easier to trace | Very difficult |
| Traffic volume | Limited to attacker's link | Aggregated botnet throughput |
| Defense complexity | Low (block one IP) | High (distributed mitigation) |

DDoS is fundamentally a **scalability weapon**: while DoS attacks are limited by a single machine's bandwidth, DDoS multiplies the attack volume across a botnet, often reaching **terabits per second**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three main categories of DDoS attacks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three main categories of DDoS attacks (per KTU/OWASP classification) are:

1. **Volume-Based Attacks (Bandwidth Saturation):** Flood the target with massive UDP/ICMP traffic or use amplification (DNS, NTP, Memcached) to saturate the network link. Measured in **Gbps**.

2. **Protocol Attacks (State Exhaustion):** Exploit weaknesses in Layer 3/4 protocols (e.g., TCP state table, firewall sessions). The classic example is the **SYN flood**, which exhausts the half-open connection table. Measured in **Pps**.

3. **Application-Layer Attacks:** Target the web server or database with legitimate-looking HTTP/HTTPS requests (e.g., HTTP GET/POST flood, Slowloris). These are **low-bandwidth but high-impact** attacks measured in **Rps**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and working of a **botnet-based DDoS attack**. Describe the role of the **Command-and-Control (C2) server**, the **bot/zombie**, and the **handler** in detail. Use a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **SYN flood attack**. Derive the formula for **time-to-exhaustion** of the TCP state table. Assume a server with $M_{buffer} = 32\,\text{MB}$ and memory per connection $S_{per-conn} = 2048\,\text{bytes}$. If the attack rate is $R_{syn} = 500\,\text{syn/s}$ and the legitimate completion rate is $R_{complete} = 5\,\text{conn/s}$, calculate the time to exhaustion.

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Botnet Architecture

**Definition:** A **botnet** is a collection of internet-connected computers (bots/zombies) infected with malware that allows an attacker to control them remotely.

**Architecture Layers:**

1. **Attacker / Botmaster:** The mastermind who orchestrates the attack. Operates from a hidden location, often over Tor or through chained proxies.

2. **Command-and-Control (C2) Server:** The central relay that issues commands to the bots. C2 communication uses HTTP/HTTPS, IRC, P2P overlays, or even Twitter feeds.

3. **Bots / Zombies:** Compromised machines (PCs, IoT cameras, routers, DVRs, mobile phones) running a malicious payload that awaits C2 instructions.

4. **Victim:** The target server, network, or service.

**Working:**

- The attacker recruits bots by scanning for vulnerable devices and infecting them via malware, brute-forcing default credentials, or exploiting known CVEs.
- Once the botnet is ready, the attacker pushes an attack command (e.g., "flood victim X with UDP packets on port 80") via the C2 channel.
- All bots **simultaneously** send malicious traffic to the victim.
- The victim's bandwidth, CPU, memory, or state tables are exhausted, denying service to legitimate users.

**Key Topologies:**
- **Centralized (Star):** All bots → 1 C2 server (single point of failure).
- **Hierarchical:** Tiered C2 servers.
- **Decentralized (P2P):** Bots act as both client and server; resilient against takedowns.

> **Marking Allocation (7 Marks):** Definition (1), Botmaster (1), C2 (1), Bots (1), Working (2), Diagram (1).

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — SYN Flood Derivation

**Working of SYN Flood:**

In a normal TCP 3-way handshake:

1. Client sends **SYN** → Server allocates state, replies **SYN-ACK**.
2. Client sends **ACK** → Connection established.

In a **SYN flood attack**, the attacker (or its bots) sends a flood of **SYN packets** with **spoofed source IPs** and **never sends the final ACK**. The server's state table fills with half-open connections, and new legitimate clients are dropped.

**Derivation of Time-to-Exhaustion:**

**Step 1:** Maximum half-open connections the server can hold:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{M_{buffer}}{S_{per-conn}}
$$

**Step 2:** Net accumulation rate of half-open connections:

$$
\Delta S = R_{syn} - R_{complete}
$$

**Step 3:** Time to exhaustion:

$$
t_{exhaust} = \frac{S_{max}}{\Delta S} = \frac{M_{buffer} / S_{per-conn}}{R_{syn} - R_{complete}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation:**

Given: $M_{buffer} = 32\,\text{MB} = 33{,}554{,}432\,\text{bytes}$, $S_{per-conn} = 2048\,\text{bytes}$, $R_{syn} = 500\,\text{syn/s}$, $R_{complete} = 5\,\text{conn/s}$.

$$
S_{max} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{2048} = 16{,}384\ \text{half-open connections}
$$

$$
\Delta S = 500 - 5 = 495\ \text{connections/second}
$$

$$
t_{exhaust} = \frac{16{,}384}{495} \approx 33.1\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** The server's state table is fully exhausted in approximately **33 seconds**, after which **all legitimate TCP connection requests are refused**.

> **Marking Allocation (7 Marks):** Working of SYN flood (2), Step 1 derivation (1), Step 2 derivation (1), Step 3 derivation (1), Numerical calculation (2).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **DNS amplification attack** in detail. With a suitable diagram, describe how the attacker exploits open DNS resolvers to flood the victim. Define **Amplification Factor (AF)** and compute the attack bandwidth for the following: 40,000 reflectors, $R_{req} = 5\,\text{req/s}$, request size $= 80\,\text{bytes}$, $AF = 60$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss various **DDoS defense mechanisms**. Explain in detail: (i) Blackholing, (ii) Scrubbing Centers, (iii) Anycast-based CDN, and (iv) Web Application Firewalls (WAF). State one limitation of each.

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — DNS Amplification

**Concept:** DNS amplification is a **reflection-based volumetric DDoS attack** where the attacker sends small DNS queries (with the **victim's spoofed IP** as the source) to many open DNS resolvers, which then send **much larger DNS responses** to the victim.

**Working:**

1. Attacker uses a botnet or a single machine to craft DNS queries with:
   - Source IP spoofed to the **victim's IP**.
   - Query type `ANY` (maximizes response size).
2. Open resolvers on the internet receive the query and reply to the **spoofed source** (i.e., the victim).
3. The victim is flooded with large DNS responses from thousands of resolvers.

**Amplification Factor (AF):**

$$
AF = \frac{\text{Avg. DNS Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Avg. DNS Query Size (bytes)}}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $N_{reflectors} = 40{,}000$, $R_{req} = 5\,\text{req/s}$, $P_{req} = 80\,\text{bytes}$, $AF = 60$.

**Step 1:** Response size per query:

$$
P_{resp} = AF \times P_{req} = 60 \times 80 = 4800\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Total request rate hitting all reflectors:

$$
R_{total} = N_{reflectors} \times R_{req} = 40{,}000 \times 5 = 200{,}000\ \text{queries/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Total bandwidth hitting the victim:

$$
B_{victim} = R_{total} \times P_{resp} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte}
$$

$$
B_{victim} = 200{,}000 \times 4800 \times 8 = 7{,}680{,}000{,}000\ \text{bps} \approx 7.68\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with attacker's outgoing bandwidth:

$$
B_{attacker} = 200{,}000 \times 80 \times 8 = 128{,}000{,}000\ \text{bps} = 128\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** The attacker generates only **128 Mbps** of upstream traffic but causes **7.68 Gbps** of flood at the victim — a **60× bandwidth multiplier**, demonstrating the **asymmetric devastation** of amplification attacks.

> **Marking Allocation (7 Marks):** Concept (1), Working steps (2), Diagram (1), AF definition (1), Numerical calculation (2).

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — DDoS Defense Mechanisms

**(i) Blackholing (Null Routing):**
- The victim's ISP advertises the victim's IP as a "blackhole" route, dropping all traffic to that IP.
- **Limitation:** Drops **all** traffic — including legitimate users. The victim effectively goes offline, which "succeeds" in stopping the attack but **denies service to everyone**.

**(ii) Scrubbing Centers:**
- A **scrubbing center** is a specialized facility that receives the victim's traffic, analyzes it using DPI and behavioral analytics, filters out attack traffic, and forwards clean traffic back to the victim via a **GRE tunnel** or VPN.
- **Limitation:** Adds **latency** (typically 5–50 ms) and is **expensive** for high-bandwidth tenants; also requires the attack to be **rerouted** (BGP announcements), which can take minutes.

**(iii) Anycast-based CDN:**
- Content Delivery Networks (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS CloudFront) use **anycast** addressing — the same IP is announced from many geographic POPs (Points of Presence). Traffic is naturally **distributed** across the globe.
- **Limitation:** Effective for **application-layer** content delivery, but for **stateful services** (e.g., game servers, banking) anycast is less suitable because TCP sessions cannot be easily migrated.

**(iv) Web Application Firewalls (WAF):**
- A WAF operates at **Layer 7**, inspecting HTTP headers, cookies, and request bodies to detect attack patterns (e.g., known SQLi, XSS, or HTTP flood signatures). It can issue **CAPTCHA**, **JS challenges**, or **rate-limit** aggressive clients.
- **Limitation:** Can be **bypassed** by slow-and-low attacks (e.g., Slowloris) and requires continuous rule updates; also, legitimate users may be challenged, hurting UX.

> **Marking Allocation (7 Marks):** Each mechanism (1.5 marks) + Limitation (0.25 each) ≈ Total 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — DDoS**

- **Definition:** DDoS = multiple distributed sources overwhelm a target's resources.
- **CIA Triad:** DDoS attacks violate **Availability**.
- **Three Categories (MUST remember):**
  - **Volume-Based** → measured in **Gbps** (UDP flood, ICMP flood, Amplification).
  - **Protocol** → measured in **Pps** (SYN flood, Smurf, Ping of Death).
  - **Application** → measured in **Rps** (HTTP flood, Slowloris, DNS query flood).
- **Botnet Components:** Botmaster → C2 Server → Bots/Zombies → Victim.
- **Mirai Botnet (2016):** 600K IoT devices, ~1.2 Tbps attack on Dyn DNS.
- **Amplification Factor Formula:** $AF = \text{Response Size} / \text{Request Size}$.
- **Memcached AF:** Up to **51,200×** (15-byte request → 750 KB response).
- **SYN Flood Time-to-Exhaustion:** $t_{exhaust} = S_{max} / (R_{syn} - R_{complete})$.
- **DoS vs. DDoS:** DoS = single source; DDoS = many distributed sources.
- **Defenses (in order of layers):** ACL → Blackholing → Scrubbing → CDN/Anycast → WAF → SYN cookies → CAPTCHA.
- **Reflection Attack:** Spoofed source IP directs responses to the victim (e.g., DNS, NTP, SSDP).
- **Slow-and-Low Attacks:** Defeat rate-limiting by using long-lived, low-rate connections (e.g., Slowloris).
- **HTTP Flood vs. SYN Flood:** HTTP flood mimics real users; SYN flood never completes handshakes.
- **Why DDoS is hard to defend:** Distributed sources, spoofed IPs, application-layer attacks mimic legitimate traffic, reflection amplifies attack bandwidth.
- **Famous Incidents to remember:** Mirai (2016), Dyn DNS (2016), GitHub (2018 — 1.35 Tbps), Cloudflare (2024 — 5.6 Tbps).

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, **always classify** any DDoS attack you describe into one of the **three categories** before explaining. Examiners award marks for correct categorization, not just listing attack names.

---
