topics.title: ARP Spoofing and Session Hijacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** ARP Spoofing and Session Hijacking # Module 3 | ARP Spoofing and Session Hijacking ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) — The Foundational Concept Before we discuss **ARP Spoofing**, we must first understand what **ARP** does inside a Local Area Network (LAN). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a Link-Layer (Layer 2) protocol defined in **RFC 826** that maps a known 32-bit **IPv4 address** to its corresponding 48-bit **physical MAC address** within a broadcast domain (LAN). It operates as a *request-response* protocol: a host broadcasts an ARP Request, and the owner of the queried IP responds unicast with its MAC address. - **Layer**: Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI Model - **Scope**: Local Area Network (LAN) only — ARP packets are **not** routed. - **Cache**: Each host maintains a dynamic **ARP Cache / ARP Table** mapping `IP → MAC` entries. #### Conceptual Analogy — The Telephone Directory of a LAN > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine a college seminar hall where every student has a *Name* (IP Address) and a *Roll Number* (MAC Address). If you want to pass a note to "Aditya" (IP `192.168.1.5`) but only know his name, you stand up and shout, *"Hey, who has roll number for Aditya?"* (ARP Request). Aditya shouts back, *"That's me, my roll number is CSB2107"* (ARP Reply). You now write his roll number into your **personal notebook** (ARP Cache) so that you don't have to shout again. > > **ARP Spoofing** is the equivalent of a malicious student standing up and shouting, *"Hey, I'm Aditya, my roll number is CSB9999!"* — and you naively scribble the wrong roll number into your notebook, sending all subsequent notes to the wrong person. ### 1.2 ARP Spoofing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **ARP Spoofing** (also called **ARP Poisoning** or **ARP Cache Poisoning**) is a Layer 2 Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack in which an attacker sends **forged ARP Reply packets** onto a LAN, falsely associating the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate host (typically the default gateway). This causes victim hosts to update their ARP Cache with the attacker's MAC, redirecting traffic through the attacker. ### 1.3 Session Hijacking — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **Session Hijacking** is a network security attack where an adversary takes over (steals) an **existing authenticated session** between a client and a server, thereby impersonating the legitimate user without needing their credentials. The attacker exploits weaknesses in session token generation, transmission, or TCP sequence number predictability. #### Conceptual Analogy — The Stolen Concert Wristband > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine you enter a 3-day music festival and are given a **wristband** (Session ID/Token) that proves you paid for entry. You walk around, eat, drink, and the wristband gets you back in. A pickpocket, without ever stealing your credit card, **clones your wristband** (steals the session token) and walks around the festival spending *your* money, ordering *your* drinks, and enjoying *your* privileges. You, meanwhile, are bewildered when the bartender says, *"You already used your 3rd free drink."* ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Values (Highlighted) | Component | Standard Value | |---|---| | IPv4 Address Length | **32 bits** | | MAC Address Length | **48 bits** | | ARP Cache Timeout (Windows Default) | **15 to 45 seconds** | | ARP Cache Timeout (Linux Default) | **20 to 600 seconds** | | Default TCP Session Port (HTTP) | **Port 80** | | Secure TCP Session Port (HTTPS) | **Port 443** | | RFC Defining ARP | **RFC 826 (1982)** | | RFC Defining TCP | **RFC 793 (1981)** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 How ARP Works — The Legitimate Flow 1. **Host A** wants to send an IP packet to **Host B (IP: 192.168.1.5)** but only knows the IP. 2. Host A checks its **ARP Cache**. If no entry exists for `192.168.1.5`, it proceeds. 3. Host A broadcasts an **ARP Request**: *"Who has 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.2 (Host A's IP)."* — sent to broadcast MAC `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`. 4. Every host on the LAN receives the broadcast, but **only Host B** replies. 5. Host B sends a **unicast ARP Reply**: *"192.168.1.5 is at MAC-AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF"*. 6. Host A stores the mapping in its ARP Cache (e.g., for the next 15–45 seconds on Windows). > [!NOTE] > **Critical ARP Weakness:** ARP has **no authentication**. Any host can send an unsolicited ARP Reply ("Gratuitous ARP") and a victim will trust it and update its cache. This is the root vulnerability exploited in ARP Spoofing. ## 2.2 ARP Spoofing — Step-by-Step Attack Logic ### Phase 1: Reconnaissance - Attacker uses tools like **Wireshark**, **arping**, or **ettercap** to map the LAN. - Identifies the **Default Gateway IP** and victim's IP/MAC. ### Phase 2: Cache Poisoning - Attacker sends **forged Gratuitous ARP Replies** to the victim: - *"IP of Gateway (192.168.1.1) is at MAC-Attacker:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"* - Simultaneously sends to the gateway: - *"IP of Victim (192.168.1.50) is at MAC-Attacker:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"* ### Phase 3: Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) Position - All traffic between victim and gateway now flows **through the attacker**. - Attacker enables **IP Forwarding** to remain invisible (otherwise DoS results). ### Phase 4: Eavesdropping / Manipulation - Attacker sniffs credentials, injects malicious content, or modifies packets in transit. ## 2.3 Types of ARP Spoofing Attacks | Type | Description | Impact | |---|---|---| | **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** | Attacker intercepts bidirectional traffic | Eavesdropping, data theft | | **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Attacker updates cache with bogus MAC; packets dropped | Network outage for victim | | **MAC Flooding** | Attacker floods switch with fake MACs; switch fails open into hub mode | Performance collapse, traffic broadcast | | **Session Hijacking (via ARP)** | Combined with session theft at upper layers | Full account takeover | ## 2.4 Session Hijacking — Deep Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.4.1 Classification of Session Hijacking **A. Network Level Session Hijacking (TCP Hijacking)** - Exploits the predictability of TCP **Sequence Numbers (SEQ)** and **Acknowledgment Numbers (ACK)**. - The attacker desynchronizes the connection and injects packets with the correct sequence number. **B. Application Level Session Hijacking** - Steals the **Session Token / Session ID / Cookie** (e.g., JSESSIONID, PHPSESSID). - The attacker presents the stolen token to the server, which trusts it as the legitimate user. ### 2.4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake (Foundation for TCP Hijacking) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, SEQ}=x} \text{Server} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK, SEQ}=y, \text{ACK}=x+1} \text{Client} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, SEQ}=x+1, \text{ACK}=y+1} \text{Server} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If the attacker can predict `x+1` and `y+1`, they can inject packets that the server believes come from the legitimate client. Older OS kernels used **incrementally predictable** sequence numbers (RFC 793 specified only that they be increasing; not how). Modern systems use cryptographic random offsets. ### 2.4.3 Session Hijacking Attack Steps (Application Layer) 1. **Sniffing**: Attacker captures network traffic (often after an ARP Spoof). 2. **Session ID Extraction**: Pulls `Cookie: SESSIONID=abc123xyz` from HTTP header. 3. **Session Token Replay**: Uses the stolen token via a tool like **Burp Suite** or **OWASP ZAP**. 4. **Impersonation**: Server accepts the token; attacker is now logged in as the victim. ### 2.4.4 Session Fixation vs Session Hijacking — Distinction | Aspect | Session Fixation | Session Hijacking | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Attacker pre-sets a known session ID for the victim | Attacker steals an existing valid session ID | | **Victim Awareness** | Victim "logs in" with attacker's ID | Victim's active session is hijacked mid-flight | | **Mitigation** | Regenerate session ID on login | Use HTTPS, secure HttpOnly cookies | ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ARP Request Delivery | Broadcast MAC = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | Reaches **all** hosts in broadcast domain | | 2 | TCP SEQ Predictability | $\text{SEQ}_{n+1} = \text{SEQ}_n + \text{Bytes Sent}$ | Older kernels used simple increment | | 3 | Expected ACK | $\text{ACK} = \text{SEQ}_{received} + 1$ | Mandatory in TCP three-way handshake | | 4 | IPv4 to MAC | $\text{ARP}: \text{IP}_{32bit} \rightarrow \text{MAC}_{48bit}$ | One-way mapping per request | | 5 | Session ID Entropy | $H \geq 128 \text{ bits}$ | Recommended minimum for unguessability | | 6 | Cache Timeout (Windows) | $T_{\text{cache}} = 15\text{–}45 \text{ seconds}$ | Dynamic, replacable during attack | | 7 | TCP Window Size | $W \in [1, 65535] \text{ bytes}$ | Defines flow control threshold | | 8 | Cookie Flags (Secure) | $\text{HttpOnly} \land \text{Secure} \land \text{SameSite}$ | Defeats XSS and sniff-based theft | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** ARP Spoofing and Session Hijacking form the **practical foundation of LAN penetration testing**. They are tested extensively in **CEH, OSCP, CompTIA Security+**, and in KTU's lab modules on offensive security. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations ## 3.1 ARP Packet Structure (RFC 826) An ARP message is encapsulated directly inside an Ethernet frame. The structure is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hardware Type (HTYPE)} &= 0x0001 \;\; (\text{Ethernet}) \\ \text{Protocol Type (PTYPE)} &= 0x0800 \;\; (\text{IPv4}) \\ \text{Hardware Address Length (HLEN)} &= 6 \;\; (\text{MAC bytes}) \\ \text{Protocol Address Length (PLEN)} &= 4 \;\; (\text{IPv4 bytes}) \\ \text{Operation (OPER)} &= 1 \;\; (\text{Request}) \;\; \text{or} \;\; 2 \;\; (\text{Reply}) \\ \text{Sender Hardware Address (SHA)} &= \text{MAC of sender} \\ \text{Sender Protocol Address (SPA)} &= \text{IPv4 of sender} \\ \text{Target Hardware Address (THA)} &= \text{MAC of target (unknown = 00:00:00:00:00:00 in Request)} \\ \text{Target Protocol Address (TPA)} &= \text{IPv4 of target} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Total ARP Packet Size Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Size} &= \text{HTYPE} + \text{PTYPE} + \text{HLEN} + \text{PLEN} + \text{OPER} + \text{SHA} + \text{SPA} + \text{THA} + \text{TPA} \\ &= 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 \\ &= 28 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > When encapsulated inside an Ethernet II frame (header 14 bytes + FCS 4 bytes), the total frame length is **28 + 14 + 4 = 46 bytes** (padded to minimum Ethernet payload 46 bytes). ## 3.2 ARP Spoofing — Exhaustive Numerical / Logical Walkthrough ### Scenario Setup - **Victim IP** = `192.168.1.50`, **Victim MAC** = `V:VV:VV:VV:VV:VV` - **Gateway IP** = `192.168.1.1`, **Gateway MAC** = `G:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG` - **Attacker IP** = `192.168.1.77`, **Attacker MAC** = `A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA` ### Step 1: Attacker Crafts Forged ARP Reply (Victim-bound) The attacker constructs a packet with: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{OPER} &= 2 \;\; (\text{Reply}) \\ \text{SHA} &= \text{A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA} \;\; (\text{Attacker's MAC}) \\ \text{SPA} &= 192.168.1.1 \;\; (\text{Gateway's IP — LIES}) \\ \text{THA} &= \text{V:VV:VV:VV:VV:VV} \;\; (\text{Victim's MAC}) \\ \text{TPA} &= 192.168.1.50 \;\; (\text{Victim's IP}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Logic**: The attacker is telling the victim: *"Hey 192.168.1.50, the host with IP 192.168.1.1 (the gateway) has MAC A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA."* The victim trusts this and updates its ARP cache: $$ \text{Victim ARP Cache: } 192.168.1.1 \rightarrow \text{A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA (WRONG)} $$ ### Step 2: Attacker Sends Forged Reply to Gateway (Symmetric Poisoning) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SHA} &= \text{A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA} \\ \text{SPA} &= 192.168.1.50 \\ \text{THA} &= \text{G:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG} \\ \text{TPA} &= 192.168.1.1 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Result**: The gateway now believes the victim's IP is at the attacker's MAC: $$ \text{Gateway ARP Cache: } 192.168.1.50 \rightarrow \text{A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA (WRONG)} $$ ### Step 3: IP Forwarding Activation The attacker issues (on Linux): $$ \text{Command: } \texttt{echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip\_forward} $$ This enables the attacker's machine to **forward** packets between the victim and gateway, ensuring the connection does not break (avoiding DoS detection). ### Step 4: Packet Flow After Poisoning $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Victim} \xrightarrow{\text{Frame with dst MAC = A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA, dst IP = 192.168.1.1}} \text{Attacker} \\ \text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{\text{Re-encapsulates with dst MAC = G:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG, dst IP = 192.168.1.1}} \text{Gateway} \\ \end{aligned} $$ The attacker can now **read, log, or modify** every packet during the re-encapsulation. ## 3.3 TCP Sequence Number Prediction — Analytical Derivation Per RFC 793, the sequence number is a 32-bit unsigned counter. The simplest (and historic) implementation in older systems is: $$ \text{SEQ}_{n+1} = \text{SEQ}_n + \Delta_{\text{bytes}} $$ Where $\Delta_{\text{bytes}}$ is the number of payload bytes in the last segment. If the attacker observes **3 handshake packets** and computes the increments: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ISN}_{\text{client}} &= X_0 \\ \text{ISN}_{\text{server}} &= Y_0 \\ \text{Delta} &= Y_0 - X_0 \;\; (\text{or some pattern}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Modern Operating Systems** (Linux 2.6+, Windows Vista+, BSD) use a **cryptographically random ISN generator** (RFC 6528), making pure prediction infeasible. However, **blind TCP hijacking** can still occur via: > - ARP Spoofing (placing attacker on-path) → **preferred modern vector** > - Side-channel timing leaks > - NAT/Proxy predictability in embedded systems ## 3.4 Session Token Entropy Calculation A session ID is considered secure if its entropy $H$ exceeds 64 bits (NIST minimum) and ideally 128 bits: $$ H = \log_2(N^L) $$ Where $N$ is the alphabet size and $L$ is the token length. **Example**: A 16-character alphanumeric token (A–Z, a–z, 0–9) has: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 62 \\ L &= 16 \\ H &= \log_2(62^{16}) = 16 \times \log_2(62) \\ \log_2(62) &\approx 5.954 \\ H &\approx 16 \times 5.954 = 95.27 \text{ bits} \;\; \text{(ACCEPTABLE, but not optimal)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Brute-force resistance at 95 bits**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time} &= \frac{2^{95}}{10^{12} \text{ guesses/sec (1 T/s)}} = 3.8 \times 10^{16} \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 1.2 \text{ million years} \;\; (\text{computationally infeasible}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > If $L$ is reduced or $N$ shrinks (e.g., 8-char hex = 32 bits), brute force becomes trivial. This is why KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is short session ID dangerous?"* # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ARP Spoofing — Man-in-the-Middle Attack Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["VICTIM HOST
IP 192.168.1.50
MAC VV:VV:VV:VV:VV:VV"] -- "Step 1 Forged ARP Reply
Says Gateway IP is at Attacker MAC" --> B["ATTACKER HOST
IP 192.168.1.77
MAC AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"] B -- "Step 2 Forged ARP Reply
Says Victim IP is at Attacker MAC" --> C["GATEWAY ROUTER
IP 192.168.1.1
MAC GG:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG"] A -- "Step 3 Victim traffic sent to attacker MAC" --> B B -- "Step 4 Attacker forwards to real gateway
with IP forward enabled" --> C C -- "Step 5 Gateway replies to attacker MAC" --> B B -- "Step 6 Attacker forwards to victim" --> A A -. "Step 7 Victim unaware
connection works normally" .-> A B -. "Step 8 Attacker logs
all traffic in clear" .-> B ``` ## 4.2 Legitimate vs ARP Spoofed Communication Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L["LEGITIMATE FLOW"] L1["VICTIM"] -- "dst MAC G:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG" --> L2["GATEWAY"] L2 -- "direct reply" --> L1 end subgraph S["SPOOFED FLOW"] S1["VICTIM"] -- "dst MAC A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA" --> S2["ATTACKER"] S2 -- "dst MAC G:GG:GG:GG:GG:GG" --> S3["GATEWAY"] S3 -- "dst MAC A:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA" --> S2 S2 -- "dst MAC V:VV:VV:VV:VV:VV" --> S1 end ``` ## 4.3 TCP Session Hijacking — Sequence Number Injection Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CL as CLIENT participant AT as ATTACKER participant SV as SERVER CL->>SV: SYN SEQ x SV->>CL: SYN ACK y ACK x+1 CL->>SV: ACK SEQ x+1 ACK y+1 Note over CL,SV: Connection Established CL->>SV: Data SEQ x+1 ACK y+1 AT-->>SV: INJECTED PACKET SEQ x+1 ACK y+1 Note over AT,SV: Attacker spoofs source IP and uses predicted SEQ SV->>CL: Server now trusts attacker CL->>SV: Desync packet Note over CL,SV: Client is desynchronized and silently dropped ``` ## 4.4 Session Hijacking — Application Layer Token Theft ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["STEP 1 Victim logs in
Server issues Set-Cookie SESSIONID abc123"] --> P2["STEP 2 Attacker performs ARP Spoof
or XSS to position on path"] P2 --> P3["STEP 3 Attacker sniffs HTTP request
Extracts Cookie SESSIONID abc123"] P3 --> P4["STEP 4 Attacker replays cookie
via curl or Burp Suite"] P4 --> P5["STEP 5 Server trusts token
Grants full access to victim account"] P5 --> P6["STEP 6 Attacker performs actions
as victim transfers funds etc"] ``` ## 4.5 Defense-in-Depth Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ENDPOINT
Static ARP entries
DHCP Snooping"] --> B["SWITCH LAYER
Dynamic ARP Inspection DAI
Port Security"] B --> C["NETWORK LAYER
802.1X Authentication
VLAN Segmentation"] C --> D["APPLICATION LAYER
HTTPS TLS 1.3
HttpOnly Secure SameSite Cookies"] D --> E["SESSION LAYER
Regenerate Session ID on Login
Idle Timeout IP Binding"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B] **Define ARP Spoofing. How does an attacker poison the ARP cache of a victim?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** ARP Spoofing (or ARP Cache Poisoning) is a Layer 2 attack in which an attacker sends forged ARP Reply messages onto a Local Area Network, falsely binding the attacker's MAC address to a legitimate IP address (typically the default gateway), thereby misleading victim hosts. > **Mechanism (2 marks):** The attacker exploits the fact that ARP has no authentication. Using tools such as `arpspoof` or `ettercap`, the attacker sends a **Gratuitous ARP Reply** to the victim claiming: > *"IP 192.168.1.1 (Gateway) is at MAC-AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"* > The victim updates its ARP cache, and all subsequent traffic meant for the gateway is sent to the attacker's MAC. If the attacker also poisons the gateway's cache (claiming the victim's IP is at the attacker's MAC), a **Man-in-the-Middle** position is established, allowing the attacker to eavesdrop, modify, or inject traffic in transit. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A] **What is Session Hijacking? Differentiate between Network Level and Application Level Session Hijacking.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Session Hijacking is the act of taking over an active authenticated session between a client and server by stealing or predicting the session identifier, allowing the attacker to impersonate the legitimate user. > **Differentiation (2 marks):** > > | Parameter | Network Level (TCP Hijacking) | Application Level (Token Theft) | > |---|---|---| > | **Target** | TCP Sequence/Ack numbers | Session cookies / tokens | > | **OSI Layer** | Transport (Layer 4) | Application (Layer 7) | > | **Method** | Sequence prediction, packet injection | Sniffing cookies, XSS, fixation | > | **Visibility** | Mostly transparent to user | User may notice logout anomalies | > | **Mitigation** | Random ISN (RFC 6528) | HTTPS, HttpOnly cookies, session ID regeneration | --- ## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **(a) Explain the working of ARP Protocol in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how an attacker performs ARP Spoofing to launch a Man-in-the-Middle attack on a LAN. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (i): Legitimate ARP Operation (3 marks)** The Address Resolution Protocol (RFC 826) maps a 32-bit IPv4 address to a 48-bit MAC address within a broadcast domain. Operation proceeds in four steps: 1. **Need Arises**: Host A (IP: `192.168.1.2`) wants to send a packet to Host B (IP: `192.168.1.5`) but lacks B's MAC in its ARP Cache. 2. **ARP Request Broadcast**: Host A sends an Ethernet broadcast (destination MAC = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) containing the question: *"Who has 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.2."* 3. **ARP Reply Unicast**: Only Host B responds with a unicast reply: *"192.168.1.5 is at MAC-AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF."* 4. **Cache Update**: Host A stores this binding in its ARP cache for 15–45 seconds (Windows default). **Part (ii): ARP Spoofing Attack (3 marks)** The attacker exploits ARP's lack of authentication. The attack proceeds: - **Step 1 — Reconnaissance**: The attacker uses `Wireshark` to identify victim IPs and the default gateway IP (`192.168.1.1`). - **Step 2 — Forged Reply to Victim**: The attacker unicasts a forged ARP Reply to the victim: *"192.168.1.1 is at MAC-Attacker:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"* Victim updates cache → all gateway-bound traffic now flows to the attacker. - **Step 3 — Forged Reply to Gateway**: Symmetrically, the attacker tells the gateway: *"192.168.1.50 is at MAC-Attacker:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"* - **Step 4 — IP Forwarding**: The attacker enables `ip_forward=1` to silently relay traffic, avoiding a DoS that would alert the victim. **Part (iii): MitM Impact (1 mark)** The attacker can now sniff plaintext credentials, perform SSL stripping, inject malicious JavaScript, or alter DNS responses — all without the victim or gateway noticing. --- **(b) Describe TCP Session Hijacking. Explain how Sequence Number Prediction and the Desynchronization technique are used by attackers. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (i): TCP Session Hijacking Overview (2 marks)** TCP Session Hijacking is a Layer 4 attack where the attacker seizes control of an established TCP connection between a client and server. It relies on the attacker being able to inject packets with valid Sequence (SEQ) and Acknowledgment (ACK) numbers, causing the server to believe the injected packets originated from the legitimate client. **Part (ii): Sequence Number Prediction (3 marks)** For every byte of data, TCP uses an incrementing 32-bit Sequence Number. The Initial Sequence Number (ISN) is chosen during the three-way handshake. In older systems (pre-RFC 6528), the ISN followed predictable patterns such as simple increments. If an attacker observed: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ISN}_{\text{client}} &= X_0 \\ \text{ISN}_{\text{server}} &= Y_0 \\ \end{aligned} $$ …they could compute future values such as: $$ \text{SEQ}_{\text{next}} = \text{SEQ}_{\text{current}} + \text{Bytes Sent} $$ Modern OSes (Linux 2.6+) use cryptographic random ISNs (RFC 6528), making blind prediction infeasible. However, if the attacker is **on-path** (achieved via ARP Spoofing), they can observe the real-time SEQ values directly from sniffed packets — bypassing the need for prediction. **Part (iii): Desynchronization Technique (2 marks)** Desynchronization forces the client and server to compute different SEQ/ACK values, so they no longer accept each other's packets. The attacker achieves this by sending a large burst of data to the server with the **client's** spoofed source IP, which advances the server's expected SEQ. The legitimate client, unaware, sends packets with the old SEQ that the server now ignores. The attacker, who knows the new SEQ, is the only one the server trusts — achieving full hijack. **Defense (briefly)**: Random ISNs, encrypted payloads (TLS 1.3), and on-path-attack mitigation via ARP inspection. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary] **(a) Explain Application Layer Session Hijacking in detail. Discuss Session ID theft via cookie sniffing and the role of Secure Cookies as a defense. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (i): Application Layer Session Hijacking (2 marks)** In the Application Layer model, the server issues a **session token** (commonly a cookie like `JSESSIONID`, `PHPSESSID`, or `ASP.NET_SessionId`) after successful authentication. The browser automatically attaches this token to every subsequent HTTP request. If an attacker obtains this token, they can impersonate the user without ever knowing the password. **Part (ii): Cookie Sniffing Attack (3 marks)** 1. Attacker performs **ARP Spoofing** to position themselves on-path between victim and server. 2. Victim makes an HTTP (port 80) request to `http://bank.com/login`. 3. Server responds: `Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=abc123xyz; Path=/; HttpOnly` 4. Attacker sniffs the unencrypted HTTP response and extracts `SESSIONID=abc123xyz`. 5. Attacker crafts: `GET /dashboard HTTP/1.1\r\nCookie: SESSIONID=abc123xyz\r\n\r\n` 6. Server trusts the valid session token and grants full dashboard access to the attacker. **Part (iii): Defenses — Secure Cookies (2 marks)** Modern web servers mitigate this by setting cookie attributes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HttpOnly:} &\;\; \text{Prevents JavaScript access — blocks XSS theft} \\ \text{Secure:} &\;\; \text{Cookie sent only over HTTPS — blocks sniffing} \\ \text{SameSite=Strict:} &\;\; \text{Cookie not sent on cross-site requests — blocks CSRF} \\ \text{Domain and Path:} &\;\; \text{Restricts scope} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Additionally, servers must **regenerate the Session ID immediately after login** (defeats Session Fixation) and enforce **idle timeouts** plus **IP-binding** for high-security applications. --- **(b) What is Session Fixation? How does it differ from Session Hijacking? Explain the attack and its mitigation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (i): Session Fixation Defined (2 marks)** Session Fixation is an attack where the attacker **forces** a known session ID value onto a victim's browser **before** the victim logs in. When the victim subsequently authenticates, the server promotes this pre-set ID to an authenticated session — and the attacker, who already knows the ID, has effectively logged in as the victim. **Part (ii): Attack Walkthrough (3 marks)** 1. Attacker visits the target site and obtains a valid (unauthenticated) session ID, e.g., `SESSIONID=attack99`. 2. Attacker tricks the victim into using this ID — via a phishing link: `http://bank.com/login;jsessionid=attack99` or an XSS-injected cookie setter. 3. Victim's browser stores `SESSIONID=attack99` and proceeds to log in with valid credentials. 4. The server, seeing the pre-existing session, **promotes** `attack99` to an authenticated session — **without** rotating the ID. 5. Attacker, knowing `attack99`, refreshes their browser and finds themselves logged in as the victim. **Part (iii): Mitigation (2 marks)** The definitive defense is **Session ID Regeneration on Authentication**: $$ \text{SID}_{\text{pre-login}} \neq \text{SID}_{\text{post-login}} $$ The server must invalidate the pre-login ID and issue a fresh, cryptographically random ID the moment the user successfully authenticates. Additional defenses include rejecting any session ID supplied via the URL path (only accept cookies) and binding the session to the client's IP/User-Agent fingerprint. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing ARP Spoofing with DNS Spoofing** (3 marks lost in 14-mark Q): ARP is Layer 2 (MAC ↔ IP mapping on LAN); DNS is Layer 7 (Domain ↔ IP). Examiners will not give partial credit if the candidate describes DNS poisoning instead. > 2. **Forgetting to mention "no authentication in ARP"** (1–2 marks lost): This is the *root cause* of the vulnerability. Simply listing steps without explaining *why* ARP is exploitable loses conceptual marks. > 3. **Omitting IP Forwarding in MitM explanation**: Many students describe the attack but forget that without `ip_forward=1`, the connection dies and the victim is alerted. > 4. **Mixing up Session Fixation vs Hijacking**: Fixation = attacker *sets* the ID; Hijacking = attacker *steals* an existing ID. Swapping these definitions costs 2–3 marks. > 5. **Not mentioning Session ID Regeneration as the key defense against Fixation** — this is the textbook answer. > 6. **Forgetting the formula/symbolic representation** when explaining TCP SEQ prediction: examiners reward notation like $\text{SEQ}_{n+1} = \text{SEQ}_n + \Delta$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):** - [ ] **ARP** is a **Layer 2** protocol that maps **IP (32-bit) → MAC (48-bit)** on a LAN only. - [ ] ARP has **no authentication** — anyone can send forged ARP Replies. - [ ] **ARP Spoofing** poisons the ARP cache by claiming *"Gateway IP is at Attacker's MAC"*. - [ ] Attackers must enable **IP Forwarding** to keep the victim's connection alive during MitM. - [ ] **ARP Spoofing consequences**: eavesdropping, session hijack launchpad, DoS, MAC flooding. - [ ] **Defenses**: Static ARP entries, **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, DHCP Snooping, 802.1X, VLAN segmentation. - [ ] **Session Hijacking** is stealing/taking over an *active* authenticated session. - [ ] **Network-level hijacking** exploits **TCP SEQ/ACK prediction** (RFC 6528 randomizes modern ISNs). - [ ] **Application-level hijacking** steals **session cookies/tokens** via sniffing, XSS, or fixation. - [ ] **Session Fixation** = attacker *pre-sets* the ID; **Session Hijacking** = attacker *steals* an existing ID. - [ ] **Defense-in-Depth**: - TLS 1.3 + HTTPS (encrypts transport) - `HttpOnly` + `Secure` + `SameSite` cookies (blocks XSS & sniff-based theft) - **Session ID regeneration on login** (blocks fixation) - Idle timeout + IP/User-Agent binding (limits stolen-token usefulness) - [ ] Key tools: **arpspoof, ettercap, Cain & Abel, Wireshark, Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, Hunt, T-sight**. - [ ] Historical context: **ARP** → RFC 826 (1982); **TCP** → RFC 793 (1981); **ISN randomization** → RFC 6528 (2012). - [ ] TCP Three-Way: `SYN → SYN/ACK → ACK`; an attacker who can compute `SEQ+1` can blind-inject. - [ ] Session ID entropy should be **≥ 64 bits (NIST min)** and ideally **≥ 128 bits** — short IDs = brute-forceable. > **End of Notes — Module 3: ARP Spoofing and Session Hijacking**

topics.title: Capturing the Network Traffic: Promiscuous Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Capturing the Network Traffic: Promiscuous Mode # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Promiscuous Mode** is a non-standard operational mode of a Network Interface Card (NIC) in which the NIC disables its hardware-level destination MAC address filtering and accepts **every** Ethernet frame that physically reaches the network medium, regardless of whether the frame is addressed to that specific host, to a broadcast address, or to a multicast group. In the standard OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2) behavior, a NIC is designed to ignore any frame whose destination MAC address does not match its own burned-in address (BIA), unless the frame is broadcast (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) or matches an active multicast group. Promiscuous mode explicitly bypasses this filtering so that a host can be used as a passive network analyzer. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBCST604 – Module 3):** Capturing network traffic is the foundational skill required for Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), packet forensics, protocol analyzers (Wireshark, tcpdump), and network forensics investigations. Promiscuous mode is the enabling technology for all such passive capture. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you live in a row of houses on a street, and a **mail carrier** walks down the street delivering letters: - **Normal (Non-Promiscuous) Mode:** The mail carrier only stops at houses whose names are written on the envelopes. You, sitting inside House #5, can read only letters addressed to "House #5." If a letter says "House #7," the carrier simply walks past you. You have *no visibility* of any other house's mail. - **Promiscuous Mode:** You (House #5) install a super-sensitive **mail-sniffing antenna** on your roof. Now, even though the mail carrier still officially delivers only to the correct houses, your antenna *overhears every single piece of mail* moving along the street, including mail meant for House #7, House #12, and so on. The mail carrier does not even know you are listening. > [!TIP] > The key insight is **passive observation**. Promiscuous mode does *not* transmit extra packets; it only **silently listens** to whatever electrical/optical signals are already flowing on the shared medium. This is why detecting it requires special techniques (e.g., ARP-based, DNS-based, ping-based methods). ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Cyber Security Promiscuous mode is a **double-edged sword**: | Perspective | Implication | |---|---| | **Defensive (Blue Team)** | Enables IDS/IPS, network forensics, traffic baselining, anomaly detection. | | **Offensive (Red Team / Attacker)** | Enables credential sniffing, ARP poisoning prelude, lateral reconnaissance. | | **Compliance / Auditing** | PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 require monitoring of cardholder/regulated traffic. | | **Privacy** | Unregulated promiscuous sniffing on shared media is legally restricted in many jurisdictions. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 How a NIC Decides to Accept or Drop a Frame At the hardware (MAC) layer, every incoming frame goes through a three-step filter inside the NIC's controller: 1. **Destination MAC Match Test** — Does the destination MAC equal my BIA / unicast address? 2. **Broadcast Test** — Is the destination MAC `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`? 3. **Multicast Test** — Does the destination MAC match a MAC group address the host has joined (e.g., via IGMP)? If **any** of these tests pass, the frame is passed up to the OS kernel. Otherwise, it is silently discarded at the silicon level. > [!NOTE] > **Promiscuous Mode Flag:** The NIC firmware contains a single configuration bit — the *promiscuous enable* flag. When set, **tests 1, 2, and 3 are bypassed** and the frame is unconditionally forwarded to the kernel. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Promiscuous Mode - **Shared Media Problem:** In legacy hub-based Ethernet (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX with hubs), all traffic is broadcast to every port. A normal host *could* technically see everything, but its NIC discards it. Promiscuous mode simply tells the NIC to *not discard*. - **Switched Media Problem:** Modern switches use MAC-address tables to deliver frames only to the correct port. Promiscuous mode alone is **insufficient** on a switch — you only see broadcast, multicast, and your own unicast traffic. To capture full traffic on a switch, additional techniques such as **Port Mirroring (SPAN)**, **Network TAPs**, or **ARP poisoning** are required. - **Wireless Variant:** Wi-Fi cards use **Monitor Mode** (a superset of promiscuous mode) that additionally captures 802.11 management/control frames, which promiscuous mode on Ethernet does not. ## 2.3 Detection of Promiscuous Mode Hosts Three classical detection techniques (high-yield for KTU exams): | Detection Method | Mechanism | Limitation | |---|---|---| | **ARP-based (Dsniff / Sentinel)** | Send a non-broadcast ARP reply with a fake MAC; only a promiscuous NIC will cache it and reply. | Modern OSes drop invalid ARP. | | **DNS-based (Promiscuous Detector)** | Send traffic to a non-existent IP; sniff the network for reverse-DNS lookups triggered by the sniffing host. | Requires the sniffer to perform DNS resolution. | | **Ping-based (ICMP Echo Latency)** | Send pings with valid + invalid IPs; a promiscuous host may show timing differences. | Highly unreliable, OS-dependent. | | **Driver-level (ettercap)** | Detects the OS-level `IFF_PROMISC` flag via tools like `ifconfig`/`ipconfig`. | Only detects *local* host, not remote sniffers. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Term | Exact Definition / Value | |---|---| | **Broadcast MAC** | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (48 bits all ones) | | **Default NIC State** | Non-promiscuous (filtered) | | **Ethernet MTU** | $1500 \text{ bytes}$ (standard) | | **Jumbo Frame MTU** | up to $9000 \text{ bytes}$ | | **Promiscuous Mode Linux Command** | `ifconfig eth0 promisc` (legacy) / `ip link set eth0 promisc on` | | **Promiscuous Mode Windows** | WinPcap / Npcap driver required (e.g., Wireshark install) | | **Promiscuous Detection Tool (Linux)** | `ifconfig` (look for `PROMISC` flag) | | **Counterpart on Switch** | SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) / Port Mirroring | | **Wireless Counterpart** | Monitor Mode (rfmon) | | **Cabling Hardware Equivalent** | Network TAP (Test Access Point) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wireshark / tcpdump:** Both rely on libpcap (Linux) or Npcap (Windows) which sets the NIC into promiscuous mode. - **Intrusion Detection:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek in IDS mode sniff promiscuously. - **Forensics:** Tools like NetworkMiner reconstruct files from passively captured PCAPs. - **Network Performance Engineering:** Baseline traffic matrices, jitter analysis, QoS validation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Frame Acceptance Decision Flow (Exhaustive Walkthrough) We model the NIC's decision logic as a Boolean function over a 4-tuple of frame attributes. Let: - $D$ = Destination MAC of the incoming frame - $M_{BIA}$ = Burned-In MAC address of the host NIC - $M_{BCAST}$ = Broadcast MAC address (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) - $M_{MCAST}$ = Set of joined multicast MAC addresses - $P$ = Promiscuous-mode flag ($P = 1$ if enabled, $P = 0$ otherwise) **Step 1 — Define the filter predicate $\Phi(D)$ for non-promiscuous mode:** $$ \Phi_{\text{normal}}(D) = (D = M_{BIA}) \;\lor\; (D = M_{BCAST}) \;\lor\; (D \in M_{MCAST}) $$ **Step 2 — A frame is accepted by the NIC if and only if $\Phi(D) = 1$.** Otherwise, the frame is dropped at the silicon level and never reaches RAM. **Step 3 — Introduce the promiscuous mode override.** The NIC firmware implements: $$ \Phi_{\text{effective}}(D) = \Phi_{\text{normal}}(D) \;\lor\; P $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the four canonical cases:** | Case | $D$ | $P$ | $\Phi_{\text{effective}}$ | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = M_{BIA}$ | 0 | $1 \lor 0 \lor 0 \lor 0 = 1$ | Accepted | | 2 | $D = M_{BCAST}$ | 0 | $0 \lor 1 \lor 0 \lor 0 = 1$ | Accepted | | 3 | $D \in M_{MCAST}$ | 0 | $0 \lor 0 \lor 1 \lor 0 = 1$ | Accepted | | 4 | $D \neq$ any above | 0 | $0 \lor 0 \lor 0 \lor 0 = 0$ | **Dropped** | | 5 | $D \neq$ any above | 1 | $0 \lor 0 \lor 0 \lor 1 = 1$ | **Accepted (promiscuous)** | **Step 5 — Observation.** Setting $P = 1$ converts the strict Boolean filter into a tautology: every frame, including malformed frames and frames with CRC errors that some NICs would normally drop, may be accepted depending on driver behavior. ## 3.2 Quantitative Capture Analysis on a Shared Medium Suppose a 100 Mbps half-duplex hub has $n$ active hosts, each generating an average offered load of $L_i$ bps. The total wire-rate traffic visible to a promiscuous sniffer is: $$ T_{\text{observed}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i $$ Because Ethernet uses CSMA/CD, the maximum sustained throughput is bounded by: $$ T_{\text{observed}} \le 0.95 \times C_{\text{wire}} $$ where $C_{\text{wire}}$ is the nominal channel capacity (e.g., $C_{\text{wire}} = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ for Fast Ethernet, $C_{\text{wire}} = 1 \text{ Gbps}$ for Gigabit Ethernet). The factor $0.95$ accounts for inter-frame gap (IFG = $96$ bit-times), preamble ($64$ bits), and collision back-off overhead. **Worked Numerical Example (KTU exam-style):** A 100 Mbps hub interconnects 4 hosts generating loads $L_1 = 20$ Mbps, $L_2 = 15$ Mbps, $L_3 = 25$ Mbps, $L_4 = 10$ Mbps. A fifth host runs Wireshark in promiscuous mode. What is the maximum traffic it can observe? **Step 1 — Sum the offered loads:** $$ T_{\text{sum}} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 = 20 + 15 + 25 + 10 = 70 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 2 — Check against the wire capacity bound:** $$ T_{\text{max}} = 0.95 \times 100 = 95 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 3 — Compare and conclude:** $$ T_{\text{observed}} = \min(T_{\text{sum}},\; T_{\text{max}}) = \min(70,\; 95) = 70 \text{ Mbps} $$ The sniffer will capture **70 Mbps** of traffic (i.e., every frame on the wire), provided its NIC and disk can sustain that throughput — a common bottleneck known as **capture loss**. ## 3.3 Capture Loss Probability Let $\lambda$ be the average frame arrival rate (frames/sec) and $\mu$ be the service rate of the capture pipeline. The pipeline (NIC → kernel ring buffer → libpcap → disk) is modeled as an M/M/1 queue, and the steady-state capture loss probability is: $$ P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - \rho = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} $$ where the utilization $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ must satisfy $0 \le \rho < 1$ for stability. If $\rho \ge 1$, frames are dropped indefinitely (kernel buffer overflow). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** KTU theory questions often ask "why a sniffer may miss packets on a busy network." The answer is: **kernel ring buffer overrun** combined with a **slow disk write throughput**, mathematically expressed as $\rho \to 1$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 NIC Frame-Filter Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Frame Arrives on Wire] --> B{Promiscuous Mode P = 1?} B -- Yes --> Z[Accept Frame
Forward to Kernel] B -- No --> C{D = My BIA?} C -- Yes --> Z C -- No --> D{D = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF?} D -- Yes --> Z D -- No --> E{D in Joined Multicast Set?} E -- Yes --> Z E -- No --> F[Drop Frame
at Silicon Level] Z --> G[Kernel Network Stack] G --> H[libpcap or Npcap] H --> I[Wireshark or tcpdump] I --> J[PCAP File on Disk] ``` ## 4.2 Hub vs Switch Visibility for a Promiscuous Host ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HubNet[Hub Network - Full Visibility] H1[Host A] --- HUB((HUB)) H2[Host B] --- HUB H3[Host C] --- HUB SNIFF1[Sniffer P-Mode] --- HUB end subgraph SwNet[Switched Network - Limited Visibility] X1[Host X] --- SW((SWITCH)) X2[Host Y] --- SW X3[Host Z] --- SW SNIFF2[Sniffer P-Mode] --- SW end HUB -. "Sees all frames" .-> SNIFF1 SW -. "Only Broadcast,
Multicast, Unicast to SNIFF" .-> SNIFF2 ``` ## 4.3 Promiscuous Mode Enablement Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB APP[User Application
Wireshark tcpdump] --> LIB[libpcap Npcap API] LIB --> OS[OS Network Stack] OS --> DRV[NIC Driver] DRV --> REG[NIC Control Register
Promiscuous Bit] REG --> HW[NIC Hardware Filter Engine] HW --> WIRE[Physical Ethernet Medium] WIRE --> HW HW -. "Filtered Frames Dropped" .-> DROP[Silicon Drop] HW -. "Accepted Frames" .-> OS ``` ## 4.4 Detection Workflow (Promiscuous Host Identification) ```mermaid flowchart TD DET[Detection Tool] --> M1{Method Selection} M1 -- ARP --> ARP[Send Non-Broadcast ARP
Look for Replies] M1 -- DNS --> DNS[Trigger Reverse DNS
on Fake IP Traffic] M1 -- ICMP --> ICMP[Latency Differential
on Valid vs Invalid IPs] M1 -- Local --> LOC[Check ifconfig
PROMISC Flag] ARP --> RES{Reply Received?} DNS --> RES ICMP --> RES LOC --> RES RES -- Yes --> ALERT[Host is in Promiscuous Mode] RES -- No --> SAFE[No Evidence of Sniffing] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *What is promiscuous mode in a Network Interface Card (NIC)? Why is it required for packet sniffing?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Promiscuous mode is a non-default operational mode of a NIC in which the hardware-level destination MAC address filtering is disabled (1 mark). In this mode, the NIC accepts every frame present on the physical medium — including unicast frames addressed to other hosts — rather than dropping them (1 mark). It is required for packet sniffing tools like Wireshark and tcpdump because, by default, a NIC discards frames not addressed to it, preventing the sniffer from observing legitimate third-party traffic (1 mark). ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Differentiate between Promiscuous Mode and Monitor Mode.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Promiscuous mode is used on **wired Ethernet** NICs to capture all Layer-2 frames regardless of destination MAC (1 mark). Monitor Mode (rfmon) is the **wireless (Wi-Fi)** equivalent used on 802.11 adapters, and additionally captures low-level management, control, and 802.11-specific frames in raw radiotap format (1 mark). Monitor mode is a strict superset of promiscuous mode in terms of visibility into link-layer signaling (1 mark). ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Explain in detail how a NIC operates in normal (non-promiscuous) versus promiscuous mode. Include a Boolean decision model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe three techniques used to detect a host running in promiscuous mode on an Ethernet network. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Operational Model (7 marks):** In the default mode, the NIC firmware executes a destination-MAC filter (2 marks). Let the destination MAC of an incoming frame be $D$, the host's BIA be $M_{BIA}$, the broadcast MAC be $M_{BCAST}$, and the joined multicast set be $M_{MCAST}$. The acceptance predicate is: $$ \Phi_{\text{normal}}(D) = (D = M_{BIA}) \;\lor\; (D = M_{BCAST}) \;\lor\; (D \in M_{MCAST}) $$ If $\Phi_{\text{normal}}(D) = 0$, the frame is dropped at the silicon level (2 marks). When the user executes `ip link set eth0 promisc on`, the OS sets a single bit in the NIC control register. This bit, denoted $P$, when set to 1, ORs into the filter: $$ \Phi_{\text{effective}}(D) = \Phi_{\text{normal}}(D) \;\lor\; P $$ When $P = 1$, the predicate becomes a tautology and every frame — including malformed ones — is delivered to the kernel (2 marks). The frames are then passed to libpcap / Npcap, which exposes them to user-space sniffers (1 mark). **(b) Detection Techniques (7 marks):** 1. **ARP-based Detection (3 marks):** The detector sends a non-broadcast ARP reply (i.e., a unicast ARP reply to the suspect host) containing a fake IP-to-MAC mapping. A non-promiscuous NIC will drop this because it is not broadcast and the host did not request it. A promiscuous NIC, however, sees the frame, processes it, and may cache or respond to the mapping. Tools: *Sentinel*, *Arpwatch*. 2. **DNS-based Detection (2 marks):** The detector sends traffic (e.g., HTTP, SMTP) destined to a non-existent IP address within the suspect's subnet. If the suspect is sniffing, its sniffer may perform a reverse-DNS lookup on the captured destination IP. The detector watches its DNS server logs for the unexpected PTR query, identifying the promiscuous host. Tool: *Promiscuous Detector (PD)*. 3. **ICMP-based Latency Detection (2 marks):** The detector sends ICMP echo requests to two IPs — one valid and one invalid. A normal host replies only to the valid IP. A promiscuous host's NIC, which processes *all* traffic, may show a small but measurable latency difference. Tool: *LOPHT* (historical). Note: this method is unreliable on modern OSes. ### Question B — Option 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Explain why promiscuous mode alone is insufficient for traffic capture on a switched Ethernet network. What additional techniques can be used? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1 Gbps switch interconnects 6 hosts. Hosts generate loads of 50, 80, 120, 200, 90, and 60 Mbps. A network analyst attaches Wireshark in promiscuous mode to a SPAN port configured to mirror all six ports. Compute the maximum traffic visible to the analyst, and discuss whether the capture will be lossless. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Switched Ethernet & Promiscuous Mode (7 marks):** Unlike a hub, an Ethernet switch uses a **MAC address table** to forward each unicast frame only to the physical port that owns the destination MAC (2 marks). A promiscuous-mode sniffer attached to a non-SPAN port therefore sees only (i) broadcast frames, (ii) multicast frames it has joined, and (iii) unicast frames addressed to its own MAC — not other hosts' traffic (2 marks). To capture full-duplex switched traffic, the following techniques are used: - **Port Mirroring / SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer):** The switch is configured to copy all frames from the monitored ports to a designated mirror port where the sniffer is connected (1 mark). - **Network TAP (Test Access Point):** A hardware device inserted inline between two endpoints that splits the optical/electrical signal into two paths, one for the sniffer (1 mark). - **ARP Cache Poisoning / MAC Flooding:** Offensively, an attacker floods the switch's CAM table or poisons ARP caches to force the switch into fail-open hub-like behavior (1 mark). **(b) Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute the sum of offered loads:** $$ T_{\text{sum}} = 50 + 80 + 120 + 200 + 90 + 60 = 600 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the wire-capacity bound for full-duplex Gigabit Ethernet:** A SPAN port on a 1 Gbps switch typically supports up to the wire rate, but the SPAN destination port itself is rate-limited. For a 1 Gbps SPAN port: $$ T_{\text{cap}} = 0.95 \times 1000 = 950 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 3 — Take the minimum:** $$ T_{\text{observed}} = \min(600,\; 950) = 600 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 4 — Losslessness discussion (2 marks):** Because $T_{\text{sum}} = 600 \text{ Mbps} < 950 \text{ Mbps} = T_{\text{cap}}$, the SPAN port can sustain the traffic. However, the analyst's workstation must also have a NIC capable of $\ge 1 \text{ Gbps}$ capture, sufficient kernel ring buffer, and a disk write throughput $\ge 600 \text{ Mbps}$ (i.e., $\ge 75 \text{ MB/s}$). A standard 5400 RPM HDD cannot sustain this; an SSD or RAID array is mandatory for lossless capture. Otherwise, capture loss occurs per the M/M/1 model: $$ P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \quad \text{where} \quad \mu < 600 \text{ Mbps} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. Do **not** confuse *promiscuous mode* with *monitor mode* — they are wired vs wireless respectively. > 2. Do **not** claim that promiscuous mode alone captures switched traffic — it does not; the answer must mention SPAN / TAP. > 3. In numerical problems, students often forget to apply the **0.95 wire-efficiency factor**; this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if omitted. > 4. Many students write "sniffer captures everything" — wrong; it captures only what *physically reaches* the NIC. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Promiscuous mode = NIC ignores destination MAC filtering. - **Default State:** OFF (filtered). Activated via OS/driver command. - **Tooling:** Wireshark, tcpdump, TShark, Snort. - **Wired vs Wireless:** Promiscuous (Ethernet) vs Monitor (Wi-Fi). - **Switched Networks:** Promiscuous mode alone is insufficient → use SPAN / TAP / ARP poisoning. - **Detection:** ARP-based, DNS-based, ICMP-latency, local `ifconfig PROMISC` flag. - **Quantitative Bound:** $T_{\text{obs}} \le 0.95 \times C_{\text{wire}}$. - **Capture Loss:** M/M/1 model — $P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - \lambda / \mu$. - **Legal Note:** Unauthorized sniffing may violate IT Act 2000 (India) / wiretap laws. - **Exam Keywords:** `BIA`, `Broadcast MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`, `SPAN`, `libpcap`, `Npcap`, `Monitor Mode`, `CSMA/CD`, `Capture Loss`.

topics.title: Flooding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flooding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flooding Attack (DoS/DDoS Context):** A **Flooding Attack** is a class of Denial-of-Service (DoS) or Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) assault in which an attacker overwhelms a target system, server, network link, or application by transmitting a **volumetrically excessive** stream of malicious or crafted packets. The objective is to exhaust one or more finite resources — **bandwidth, connection state tables, CPU cycles, memory buffers, or application-handling capacity** — rendering the service unavailable to legitimate users.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, *Flooding* is positioned under the broader umbrella of **Network and Application Layer Threats**, where it is studied as the dominant operational pattern behind modern Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) incidents.

## 1.2 Key Vocabulary (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DoS (Denial of Service)** | Single-source attack denying service to legitimate users. |
| **DDoS (Distributed DoS)** | Multi-source (botnet-driven) flooding attack. |
| **Botnet** | Network of compromised "zombie" hosts under attacker control. |
| **Reflector** | A legitimate third-party server (e.g., DNS, NTP) abused to amplify traffic toward the victim. |
| **Amplification Factor** | Ratio of attack response bytes to attacker request bytes (e.g., **×30 to ×550**). |
| **SYN Backlog / Half-Open Queue** | Kernel buffer holding un-completed TCP connections. |
| **Throughput (T)** | Legitimate data successfully delivered, measured in **bps** or **pps**. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Phone-Bombing Prank:**
> Imagine a small restaurant that seats **30 customers** and has just **2 phone lines**. A prankster repeatedly calls both lines, places fake orders, and never hangs up properly. The lines stay "tied up." Real customers can no longer call to reserve a table — the restaurant is effectively **denied service**.
>
> - The **2 phone lines** = the server's **SYN backlog / connection table** (finite resource).
> - The **prankster's calls** = **SYN packets** that never finish the handshake.
> - Hiring **1000 pranksters** to call simultaneously = a **Distributed (DDoS)** version of the same attack.

## 1.4 Categories of Flooding (Official KTU Taxonomy)

1. **Volume-Based / Network Layer Flooding** — saturates link bandwidth.
2. **Protocol-Based / State-Exhaustion Flooding** — exhausts protocol state machines (e.g., TCP, ICMP).
3. **Application-Layer Flooding** — exhausts application resources (HTTP, DNS query handlers).
4. **Reflection / Amplification Flooding** — abuses third-party servers to multiply attack volume.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Resource Exhaustion Vectors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every flooding attack, regardless of type, targets **one of three finite resources**:
>
> 1. **Bandwidth (Link Capacity)** — the physical "pipe" between the victim and the Internet.
> 2. **State / Connection Tables** — finite kernel structures (e.g., TCP control blocks).
> 3. **Application Resources** — CPU, memory, file descriptors, threads, database connections.

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Major Flooding Attack Types

### A. SYN Flood (Protocol / State-Exhaustion)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Attacker sends a flood of **TCP SYN** packets to the victim, often with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
2. Victim allocates a **TCP control block (TCB)** in its SYN backlog, replies with **SYN-ACK**, and waits for the final **ACK**.
3. The spoofed source never sends the final ACK → connection remains **half-open**.
4. The backlog fills up. Legitimate SYNs are dropped → **service is denied**.

**Why it works:** Most OS kernels default to a **SYN backlog of 256 to 1024 entries**. Once filled, new connections are refused.

### B. UDP Flood (Volumetric)

1. Attacker sends massive volumes of **UDP datagrams** to random or specific ports of the victim.
2. Victim checks for listening applications; none → generates **ICMP Destination Unreachable** replies.
3. Bandwidth is consumed both by the inbound flood and the outbound ICMP replies.

### C. ICMP Flood / Ping Flood (Volumetric)

1. Attacker bombards the victim with **ICMP Echo Request** packets at maximum rate.
2. Victim obliges by sending **ICMP Echo Replies**.
3. Outbound bandwidth + CPU become saturated.

### D. Smurf Attack (Reflection + Amplification)

1. Attacker sends **ICMP Echo Request** to the **broadcast address** of an intermediate network, with the **victim's IP as the spoofed source**.
2. Every host on the broadcast network replies to the victim.
3. Result: **N hosts × echo reply = N-fold amplification**.

### E. Fraggle Attack (UDP variant of Smurf)

1. Uses **UDP echo (port 7)** directed at broadcast addresses.
2. All hosts on the subnet reply to the spoofed victim.

### F. HTTP Flood / Application-Layer Flood

1. Attacker (or botnet) sends **legitimately-formed HTTP GET/POST** requests to a web server.
2. Each request triggers **expensive server-side operations** (DB queries, crypto, file lookups).
3. Legitimate-looking traffic is hard to filter.

### G. DNS / NTP / SSDP Amplification Flood

1. Attacker sends **small DNS/NTP/SSDP queries** with **spoofed victim IP** to open resolvers / NTP servers / IoT devices.
2. The servers dutifully send **30× to 550× larger** responses to the victim.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are required for solving numerical / analytical KTU questions on Flooding.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $AF = \dfrac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}$ | Measures attacker's leverage per byte sent. |
| 2 | **Total Attack Bandwidth** | $B_{attack} = AF \times B_{request}$ | Bandwidth seen by victim per attacker request. |
| 3 | **Half-Open Connection Growth** | $H(t) = R_{syn} \cdot t - R_{timeout} \cdot t$ | Net new half-open connections over time *t*. |
| 4 | **Backlog Saturation Time** | $t_{sat} = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{R_{syn} - R_{ack}}$ | Time for SYN backlog of size $Q_{max}$ to fill. |
| 5 | **Effective Throughput (under attack)** | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{C_{link} - B_{attack}}{C_{link}} \times T_{legit}$ | Fraction of legitimate traffic that survives. |
| 6 | **SYN Cookie Verification** | $ISN = f(\text{SourceIP}, \text{DestIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstPort}, \text{Secret}, t)$ | Cryptographic ISN replacing TCB allocation. |
| 7 | **Botnet Attack Capacity** | $B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_{bot,i}$ | Aggregate bandwidth of N bots. |
| 8 | **Packet Rate (pps)** | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{S_{packet} \times 8}$ | Packets per second given bandwidth and packet size. |
| 9 | **Reflection Count** | $N_{reply} = N_{hosts} \times AF$ | Total reply packets generated by reflection. |
| 10 | **Detection Threshold (z-score)** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} > k$ | Anomalous traffic detection (e.g., k = 3). |

**Where:**
- $C_{link}$ = link capacity (bps).
- $B_{attack}$ = bandwidth consumed by attack (bps).
- $Q_{max}$ = maximum SYN backlog size.
- $R_{syn}$ = SYN arrival rate; $R_{ack}$ = legitimate ACK completion rate.
- $S_{packet}$ = average packet size in bytes.
- $N_{hosts}$ = number of hosts on broadcast network (Smurf).
- $k$ = detection threshold (typically 3 standard deviations).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flooding-attack analytics directly inform:
- **ISP Traffic Engineering** — over-provisioning links by factor 2–5×.
- **Data Center Defense-in-Depth** — designing scrubbing centers.
- **6G/IoT Security Research** — defending low-power reflective IoT devices.
- **Cyber Insurance Modeling** — quantifying DoS risk premiums.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time to Saturate a SYN Backlog

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A server with SYN backlog $Q_{max} = 512$ entries. Attacker injects SYNs at rate $R_{syn} = 200$ packets/sec. Legitimate users complete handshakes at $R_{ack} = 50$ packets/sec. **Find:** time for the backlog to become completely full.

**Step 1 — Define the net growth rate of half-open connections.**

The backlog grows when attackers send SYNs faster than legitimate clients can complete handshakes. So the **net fill rate** is:

$$
R_{net} = R_{syn} - R_{ack}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
R_{net} = 200 - 50 = 150 \; \text{half-open connections per second}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the backlog occupancy at time *t*.**

Assuming the backlog starts empty at $t=0$, the number of half-open connections grows linearly:

$$
Q(t) = R_{net} \times t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the saturation time.**

We want $Q(t) = Q_{max}$, therefore:

$$
t_{sat} = \frac{Q_{max}}{R_{net}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{sat} &= \frac{512}{150} \\
t_{sat} &= 3.4133... \;\text{seconds} \\
t_{sat} &\approx 3.41 \;\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (Examiner-Friendly Conclusion).**

> Within **3.41 seconds**, the SYN backlog of 512 entries will be completely filled. From that point onward, **all new TCP connection requests from legitimate users will be silently dropped**, achieving the attacker's goal of service denial.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amplification Factor for a DNS Reflection Attack

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** An attacker sends a DNS query of **60 bytes** (over UDP) to an open resolver. The resolver, believing the spoofed source IP is the victim, returns a DNS response of **3000 bytes**. **Find:** the Amplification Factor and the attack bandwidth seen by the victim for an attacker who injects 1 Mbps of request traffic.

**Step 1 — Compute the Amplification Factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF &= \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}} \\
AF &= \frac{3000}{60} \\
AF &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the victim's perceived attack bandwidth.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{attack} &= AF \times B_{request} \\
B_{attack} &= 50 \times 1 \text{ Mbps} \\
B_{attack} &= 50 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective legitimate throughput.**

If the victim's link capacity is $C_{link} = 100$ Mbps, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= \frac{C_{link} - B_{attack}}{C_{link}} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &= \frac{100 - 50}{100} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &= 0.50 \times T_{legit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

> A mere **1 Mbps** of carefully spoofed request traffic produces **50 Mbps** of inbound attack traffic at the victim, halving the bandwidth available to legitimate users.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Smurf Attack Amplification

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A /24 broadcast network contains **254** active hosts. The attacker sends **1 ICMP Echo Request (84 bytes)** with spoofed source IP. **Find:** the total echo-reply traffic received by the victim and the effective amplification factor.

**Step 1 — Number of replies generated.**

Each of the 254 hosts will send an ICMP Echo Reply (84 bytes) to the victim:

$$
N_{reply} = N_{hosts} = 254
$$

**Step 2 — Total reply volume in bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{reply} &= N_{reply} \times S_{reply} \\
V_{reply} &= 254 \times 84 \\
V_{reply} &= 21336 \;\text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective Amplification Factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF &= \frac{V_{reply}}{S_{request}} \\
AF &= \frac{21336}{84} \\
AF &= 254
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> The attacker **invests 84 bytes** and the victim receives **21,336 bytes** — a **254×** amplification. This is precisely why Smurf attacks are classified as *amplification* attacks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Botnet Aggregate Bandwidth

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A botnet of **5,000** compromised hosts, each capable of pushing **128 kbps** of attack traffic. **Find:** the aggregate bandwidth delivered to the victim.

**Step 1 — Aggregate bandwidth formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_{bot,i} \\
B_{total} &= N \times B_{bot} \\
B_{total} &= 5000 \times 128 \text{ kbps} \\
B_{total} &= 640{,}000 \text{ kbps} \\
B_{total} &= 640 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to a typical small-business link (e.g., 100 Mbps).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= \frac{100 - 640}{100} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &\to \text{NEGATIVE} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{complete saturation}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Negative result means the link is **fully saturated** — legitimate users receive **0% throughput**.

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Summary Matrix

| Attack | Resource Targeted | Mitigation | Amplification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SYN Flood** | Connection state table | SYN cookies, firewalls | None |
| **UDP Flood** | Bandwidth | Rate limiting, ACLs | None |
| **ICMP Flood** | Bandwidth + CPU | Disable ICMP echo, rate limit | None |
| **Smurf** | Bandwidth | Disable directed broadcast | **N** (hosts on subnet) |
| **Fraggle** | Bandwidth | Block UDP echo, disable broadcast | **N** |
| **HTTP Flood** | App threads, DB | WAF, CAPTCHA, JS challenge | None |
| **DNS Amp** | Bandwidth | BCP-38 (source validation) | **×30 to ×50** |
| **NTP Amp** | Bandwidth | Disable monlist, BCP-38 | **×550** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic Flooding Attack Architecture (DDoS)

flowchart LR

    A --> C[Botnet Bot 2]
    A --> D[Botnet Bot N]
    B --> E[Open Resolver / NTP / Smurf Reflector]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Victim Server]
    F --> G[Legitimate User Request DROPPED]
    F --> H[Service Unavailable]

    style B fill:#2c5f2d,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2c5f2d,color:#ffffff

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Att as Attacker (Spoofed IP)
    participant V as Victim Server
    participant Leg as Legitimate User
    Note over V: SYN Backlog (Qmax = 512)
    Att->>V: SYN [seq=x]  (flood begins)
    V-->>Att: SYN-ACK [seq=y, ack=x+1]
    Note over V: TCB allocated  (1 entry used)
    V--xAtt: waiting for ACK  (TIMEOUT)
    Att->>V: SYN [seq=x2]
    V-->>Att: SYN-ACK [seq=y2]
    V--xAtt: TCB allocated
    loop 511 more half-open connections
        Att->>V: SYN  (no ACK ever sent)
    end
    Note over V: Backlog FULL
    Leg->>V: SYN
    V--xLeg: Connection DROPPED
    Note over V: DENIAL OF SERVICE achieved
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Flooding Attack. List its three main categories.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Flooding Attack** is a Denial-of-Service assault in which the attacker overwhelms a target's finite resources — bandwidth, connection state, or application capacity — by sending a volumetrically excessive stream of packets, rendering the service unavailable to legitimate users.

**Three Main Categories:**
1. **Volumetric / Network Layer** — saturates link bandwidth (e.g., UDP flood, ICMP flood).
2. **Protocol-Based / State-Exhaustion** — exhausts protocol state machines (e.g., SYN flood).
3. **Application-Layer Flooding** — exhausts server-side resources via legitimate-looking HTTP/S requests (e.g., HTTP flood, Slowloris).

> A **4th** category — **Reflection / Amplification** — is often added separately and is **frequently asked** as a bonus distinction.

---

### Question 2: What is an Amplification Factor? Give one example with values.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Amplification Factor (AF)** is the ratio of the size of the response bytes sent to the victim to the size of the request bytes sent by the attacker:

$$
AF = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}
$$

**Example:** A DNS query of **60 bytes** sent to an open resolver produces a response of **3000 bytes** sent to the spoofed victim.

$$
AF = \frac{3000}{60} = 50
$$

Hence, a **50× amplification** is achieved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the **SYN Flood attack** in detail. Describe the role of the **SYN backlog**, the technique of **IP spoofing**, and **SYN Cookies** as a defense mechanism. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A server has a SYN backlog of size $Q_{max} = 1024$. An attacker sends SYNs at $R_{syn} = 400$ packets/sec, while legitimate clients complete handshakes at $R_{ack} = 80$ packets/sec. **Calculate the time for the backlog to saturate** and the **effective legitimate throughput** if the link capacity is 1 Gbps and the attack consumes 200 Mbps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**1. Definition and Operational Mechanism:**

A **SYN Flood** is a protocol-based, state-exhaustion DoS attack that exploits the **three-way handshake** of TCP. The attacker sends a torrent of TCP **SYN** packets with **spoofed source IP addresses**, never completing the handshake. The victim allocates a **TCP Control Block (TCB)** in the kernel's **SYN backlog** for each incomplete SYN, eventually exhausting the backlog.

**2. Role of the SYN Backlog:**

The SYN backlog is a finite kernel buffer (default size typically **256 to 1024** entries) that holds half-open connections. When the backlog is full, the kernel refuses to allocate new TCBs, and incoming SYNs are dropped — even from legitimate users. This is the **resource exhaustion vector**.

**3. IP Spoofing:**

The attacker forges the source IP of each SYN packet. Consequences:
- The victim's **SYN-ACK** goes to a non-existent or unwitting third party.
- The victim **cannot** rely on IP-based rate limiting alone.
- The attacker **hides** the true origin of the attack.

**4. SYN Cookies Defense:**

A defense proposed by **Daniel J. Bernstein** that **eliminates the need to allocate a TCB** for an incoming SYN. Instead, the server encodes the connection state into the **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** using a cryptographic hash of the source/destination IP, port pair, a secret, and a timestamp:

$$
ISN = f(\text{SrcIP}, \text{DstIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstPort}, \text{Secret}, t)
$$

When the legitimate client's final ACK arrives, the server **recomputes** the ISN and only then allocates the TCB. **SYN Cookies remove the state-exhaustion vulnerability** at the cost of disabling some TCP options.

#### Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the net growth rate of half-open connections.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{net} &= R_{syn} - R_{ack} \\
R_{net} &= 400 - 80 = 320 \; \text{half-open connections per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the saturation time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{sat} &= \frac{Q_{max}}{R_{net}} \\
t_{sat} &= \frac{1024}{320} \\
t_{sat} &= 3.2 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective legitimate throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= \frac{C_{link} - B_{attack}}{C_{link}} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &= \frac{1000 - 200}{1000} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &= 0.80 \times T_{legit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The backlog saturates in just **3.2 seconds**, after which all new connection requests are dropped. Concurrently, only **80%** of legitimate throughput survives the volumetric pressure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Choice 2]**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain **DNS Amplification Flood** and **Smurf Attack**. Compare their amplification behavior. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An attacker controls a botnet of 8,000 hosts, each generating 256 kbps of UDP flood traffic against a victim whose link capacity is 2 Gbps. **Compute the aggregate attack bandwidth, the packets-per-second rate** (assume 512-byte packets), and **deduce the effective legitimate throughput** as a percentage. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**1. DNS Amplification Flood:**

- Attacker sends small DNS queries (~60 bytes) over UDP to **open resolvers**, with the **victim's IP** spoofed as the source.
- Each open resolver, believing the victim made the query, returns a large DNS response (~3,000 bytes) to the victim.
- **Amplification Factor (AF) ≈ 30 to 50×** (or higher with DNSSEC, up to **×100**).
- *Mitigation:* BCP-38 ingress filtering to block IP spoofing; rate-limiting DNS responses.

**2. Smurf Attack:**

- Attacker sends an **ICMP Echo Request** to the **directed broadcast address** of an intermediate network, with the victim's IP as the spoofed source.
- Every host on the broadcast subnet (~254 hosts for a /24) replies with an ICMP Echo Reply to the victim.
- **Amplification Factor (AF) = N** (number of hosts on the broadcast network), typically **254×** for a /24.
- *Mitigation:* Disable **directed broadcast** at network routers.

**3. Comparison Table:**

| Property | DNS Amplification | Smurf Attack |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol | UDP / DNS | ICMP Echo |
| Vector | Open Resolvers | Broadcast Network |
| AF (typical) | **×30 to ×50** | **×N (hosts on subnet)** |
| Per-packet size | Small request → large reply | Equal size (84 bytes each) |
| Modern risk | **Still High** | **Mostly Mitigated** |

#### Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute aggregate attack bandwidth.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= N \times B_{bot} \\
B_{total} &= 8000 \times 256 \text{ kbps} \\
B_{total} &= 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ kbps} \\
B_{total} &= 2048 \text{ Mbps} \\
B_{total} &= 2.048 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute packet rate (pps).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rate} &= \frac{B_{total}}{S_{packet} \times 8} \\
P_{rate} &= \frac{2{,}048{,}000{,}000 \; \text{bits/sec}}{512 \times 8 \; \text{bits/pkt}} \\
P_{rate} &= \frac{2{,}048{,}000{,}000}{4096} \\
P_{rate} &= 500{,}000 \; \text{pps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective legitimate throughput.**

The victim's link is 2 Gbps and the attack consumes 2.048 Gbps, which **exceeds** link capacity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= \frac{C_{link} - B_{attack}}{C_{link}} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &= \frac{2.0 - 2.048}{2.0} \times T_{legit} \\
T_{eff} &\to \text{negative} \;\Rightarrow\; 0\% \text{ legitimate throughput}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The botnet produces **2.048 Gbps** of attack traffic — **2.4% more** than the link's full capacity. The victim suffers **complete (100%) service denial**. The packet rate is **500,000 packets per second**, which can also overwhelm router and server CPU resources.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing DoS vs. DDoS** — DoS is **single-source**; DDoS is **multi-source (botnet)**. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion in bandwidth** — 1 Gbps = 1000 Mbps = 1,000,000 kbps = $10^9$ bps. A common slip is mixing kbps with Kbps (kibibits).
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — In a 7-mark sub-question on SYN flood or Smurf, a labelled **sequence diagram or block diagram is mandatory** in KTU valuation. A text-only answer loses **1 to 2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the defense side** — Most KTU questions on flooding ask for **mitigation**. Students who describe only the attack lose marks.
> 5. **In amplification calculations** — Always express the final AF as a **dimensionless number** (e.g., "50×"), and explicitly state the **request/response sizes used**.
> 6. **Application-layer flood vs. volumetric flood** — Application-layer floods (HTTP GET/POST) are often **low-volume but high-cost per packet**; examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Flooding Attack** = overwhelming a victim by exhausting a finite resource (bandwidth / state / app).
- ✅ **Three categories:** Volumetric, Protocol (State-exhaustion), Application-layer.
- ✅ **Fourth bonus category:** Reflection / Amplification.
- ✅ **SYN Flood** exhausts the **SYN backlog** using spoofed IPs and never-completed TCP handshakes.
- ✅ **SYN Cookies** eliminate TCB allocation by encoding state in the **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)**.
- ✅ **Smurf Attack** abuses **directed broadcast** to multiply one packet into N replies (AF = N).
- ✅ **Fraggle Attack** is the **UDP** variant of Smurf.
- ✅ **DNS / NTP Amplification** abuse **open resolvers / NTP servers** with small spoofed queries generating massive replies.
- ✅ **Amplification Factor formula:** $AF = \dfrac{\text{Response Size}}{\text{Request Size}}$.
- ✅ **Backlog saturation time:** $t_{sat} = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{R_{syn} - R_{ack}}$.
- ✅ **Effective throughput:** $T_{eff} = \dfrac{C_{link} - B_{attack}}{C_{link}} \times T_{legit}$.
- ✅ **Botnet aggregate bandwidth:** $B_{total} = N \times B_{bot}$.
- ✅ **Key defenses:** BCP-38 ingress filtering, SYN cookies, rate limiting, scrubbing centers, WAFs, disabling directed broadcast.
- ✅ **Typical KTU exam marks split:** 3-mark question = definition + category listing; 7-mark sub-question = diagram + mechanism + defense; 7-mark numerical = amplification or saturation calculation.
- ✅ **Always label diagrams** in the KTU answer script — unlabeled diagrams lose marks.
- ✅ **Always mention "IP spoofing"** explicitly when explaining SYN flood or reflection attacks — it is the enabler.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DHCP Redirection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** DHCP Redirection

# DHCP Redirection — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DHCP Redirection** is a class of network-layer cyberattack in which an adversary manipulates the **Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)** exchange — specifically the standard **DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledgement)** handshake — to misdirect a legitimate client to a rogue network gateway, malicious Domain Name System (DNS) server, or unauthorized default gateway. The attack fundamentally exploits the **trust model** of DHCP, where clients blindly accept configuration parameters from the first responder on **User Datagram Protocol (UDP) port 67 (server)** and **UDP port 68 (client)** without cryptographic authentication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DHCP Redirection sits at the intersection of **Layer 2 (Data Link)** and **Layer 3 (Network)** attacks under the broader umbrella of **Infrastructure-Level Cyber Threats**. It is a prerequisite concept for understanding **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle)** attacks, **DNS poisoning**, and **network reconnaissance pivoting**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a hotel lobby and ask the receptionist for your room key. A fake receptionist (the rogue DHCP server) standing closer to you shouts back first, giving you a key to a *different room* — one with hidden cameras. You trust the response simply because it came first and looked legitimate. The real receptionist (the legitimate DHCP server) never gets a chance to respond.

In this analogy:
- **You (the client)** = any device joining the network (laptop, phone, IoT sensor)
- **Receptionist (DHCP server)** = the legitimate IP address allocator
- **Fake receptionist (rogue server)** = the attacker's machine
- **Wrong room key (malicious IP config)** = crafted DHCP offer with rogue gateway/DNS

The attacker essentially **redirects your network traffic** through their own infrastructure, enabling eavesdropping, credential theft, or session hijacking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (Industry Standards):**
> - DHCP Server UDP Port: **67**
> - DHCP Client UDP Port: **68**
