> - Default Lease Time: **86400 seconds (24 hours)**
> - DHCP Magic Cookie: **0x63825363**
> - Broadcast MAC Address: **FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Legitimate DHCP DORA Process (Baseline)

To understand the attack, one must first internalize the normal sequence:

1. **DHCP DISCOVER** — Client broadcasts a `DHCPDISCOVER` packet (source IP: `0.0.0.0`, destination: `255.255.255.255`) seeking any available DHCP server.
2. **DHCP OFFER** — Server responds with a `DHCPOFFER` containing a proposed IP address, subnet mask, lease time, default gateway, and DNS server.
3. **DHCP REQUEST** — Client broadcasts a `DHCPREQUEST` accepting the offered parameters.
4. **DHCP ACKNOWLEDGEMENT (ACK)** — Server confirms the lease and the client binds the IP to its network interface.

## 2.2 Attack Taxonomy & Step-by-Step Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Rogue DHCP Server Attack (Primary Form of DHCP Redirection)

| Step | Attacker Action | Technical Effect |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Deploys a rogue DHCP server (often via `dnsmasq`, `mitmproxy`, or `Yersinia`) on the LAN | Listens on UDP 67 |
| 2 | Crafts malicious `DHCPOFFER` with attacker-controlled **default gateway** and **DNS server** | Packet injected with `giaddr` and `siaddr` spoofed |
| 3 | Responds *faster* than the legitimate server (race condition) | Client accepts rogue offer |
| 4 | All victim traffic now routed through attacker | **Full MITM capability** |

### 2.2.2 DHCP Starvation (Pre-attack Reconnaissance)

The attacker first depletes the legitimate IP pool so that the real DHCP server cannot respond. This is achieved by sending thousands of `DHCPDISCOVER` packets with **forged MAC addresses**.

### 2.2.3 DHCP Spoofing vs. DHCP Redirection — Nuance

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction for KTU Exam:** 
> - **DHCP Spoofing** = Impersonating a DHCP server generally.
> - **DHCP Redirection** = Specifically *redirecting* the client's traffic flow (gateway/DNS manipulation) as the *goal* of the spoof.

## 2.3 Why the Attack Succeeds — The "Core Why"

- **No Authentication:** RFC 2131 explicitly states DHCP has *no built-in cryptographic verification* of server identity.
- **Broadcast Domain Exposure:** Layer 2 broadcasts reach every device in the VLAN.
- **Trust on First Response:** Most clients accept the first valid `DHCPOFFER`.
- **Stateless Nature of UDP:** No handshake or session integrity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Description |
|-----------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| IP Pool Exhaustion Rate | $R_{exh} = \frac{N_{pool}}{T_{lease}}$ | IPs/sec | Rate at which pool is consumed |
| Time to Pool Exhaustion | $T_{exh} = \frac{N_{pool} \times T_{lease}}{1}$ | seconds | Time before starvation completes |
| Broadcast Reach (Layer 2) | $\text{Reach} = 2^{48}$ | MAC addresses | Theoretical broadcast domain size |
| DHCP Packet Size | **Min: 300, Max: 590 bytes** | bytes | Per RFC 2131 |
| Lease Calculation | $T_{remaining} = T_{lease} - T_{elapsed}$ | seconds | Remaining lease validity |
| Rogue Server Detection Latency | $L_{d} = T_{response} - T_{attack}$ | ms | Time between attack launch and IDS trigger |
| VLAN Hop Probability | $P_{hop} = \frac{1}{N_{VLAN}}$ | dimensionless | Probability of crossing VLAN boundary |
| Network Throughput Impact | $\eta = \frac{T_{legit} - T_{attack}}{T_{legit}} \times 100$ | % | Performance degradation percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these formulas matter in engineering:** DHCP Redirection countermeasures are deployed in enterprise switches (Cisco, Juniper, HP), data center fabrics, and cloud VPC environments. Network engineers routinely calculate exhaustion rates to size **DHCP pools** and configure **DHCP Snooping** binding tables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Mathematical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DHCP Pool Exhaustion Time

**Problem Context:** Given a DHCP scope of size $N_{pool}$ and average lease duration $T_{lease}$, compute the time required for an attacker to starve the pool by sending one fake `DHCPDISCOVER` every $\Delta t$ seconds.

### Step 1 — Establish the Rate of Consumption
Each spoofed `DHCPDISCOVER` consumes one IP from the pool once the legitimate server replies with an ACK.

$$
R_{exh} = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \quad \text{[requests per second]}
$$

### Step 2 — Establish the Pool Capacity
The total number of available leases is bounded by the scope size $N_{pool}$.

### Step 3 — Compute Total Exhaustion Time

$$
T_{exh} = \frac{N_{pool}}{R_{exh}} = N_{pool} \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)
Suppose $N_{pool} = 254$ (a /24 subnet excluding gateway and broadcast) and the attacker sends $\Delta t = 0.05$ seconds:

$$
T_{exh} = 254 \times 0.05 = 12.7 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Interpretation:** A /24 subnet can be starved in under 13 seconds — a sobering real-world metric for KTU exam answers.

### Step 5 — Mitigation Through Pool Sizing
To increase resilience, double the pool size:

$$
T_{exh}^{\prime} = 2 \cdot N_{pool} \cdot \Delta t = 2 \times 12.7 = 25.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

However, this only *delays* — it does not *prevent*. Cryptographic controls (802.1X, DHCP authentication) are mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Broadcast Domain Traffic Amplification

Each `DHCPDISCOVER` reaches every port in the VLAN. If the VLAN contains $N_{hosts}$ devices, the **amplification factor** is:

$$
A_{dhcp} = N_{hosts} \times S_{pkt} \quad \text{[bytes per attack cycle]}
$$

With $N_{hosts} = 200$ and $S_{pkt} = 590$ bytes:

$$
A_{dhcp} = 200 \times 590 = 118{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 115.23 \text{ KB per cycle}
$$

At 10 attacks per second:

$$
\text{Bandwidth consumed} = 115.23 \times 10 = 1152.3 \text{ KB/s} \approx 1.125 \text{ MB/s}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defense Mapping

| Attack Phase | Attacker Tool/Action | Network Layer | Defense Mechanism | KTU Course Outcome |
|--------------|---------------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Reconnaissance | `nmap --script broadcast-dhcp-discover` | Layer 2/3 | Port isolation | CO3 |
| Starvation | Yersinia, Gobbler | Layer 2 | DHCP Snooping rate limit | CO3 |
| Redirection | Rogue server + crafted OFFER | Layer 3 | DHCP Snooping binding table | CO4 |
| Persistence | ARP cache poisoning post-redirection | Layer 2 | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) | CO4 |
| Exfiltration | Responder.py, Ettercap | Layer 7 | 802.1X authentication | CO5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The DORA sequence in DHCP Redirection is most often examined in **Module 3** because it requires students to demonstrate cross-layer understanding — combining networking fundamentals with cryptographic vulnerability analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Legitimate DHCP DORA Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant clientA as Legitimate Client
    participant legitDHCP as Legitimate DHCP Server
    participant routerA as Layer 3 Gateway

    Note over clientA,legitDHCP: Phase 1: Normal Operation
    clientA->>legitDHCP: DHCP DISCOVER (Broadcast UDP 67)
    legitDHCP->>clientA: DHCP OFFER (IP, Mask, GW, DNS)
    clientA->>legitDHCP: DHCP REQUEST (Broadcast)
    legitDHCP->>clientA: DHCP ACK (Lease Confirmed)
    clientA->>routerA: Bound IP Configuration Activated
    routerA-->>clientA: Normal Network Access Granted
```

## 4.2 DHCP Redirection Attack — Complete Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Victim Laptop Boots Up] --> broadcast1[Broadcasts DHCP DISCOVER]
    broadcast1 --> race1{Race Condition}
    rogueB[Attacker Rogue DHCP Server] -->|Fastest OFFER| race1
    legitC[Legitimate DHCP Server] -->|Slower OFFER| race1
    race1 -->|First Response Wins| acceptA[Victim Accepts Rogue Offer]
    acceptA --> configA[Malicious Config Applied]
    configA --> rogueGW[Rogue Default Gateway: 10.0.0.99]
    configA --> rogueDNS[Rogue DNS Server: 10.0.0.99]
    rogueGW --> mitm[All Traffic Routed to Attacker]
    rogueDNS --> phishing[Phishing Redirects Enabled]
    mitm --> sniffA[Sensitive Data Captured]
    phishing --> credA[Credentials Harvested]
    sniffA --> exfilA[Data Exfiltrated to C2 Server]
    credA --> exfilA
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientD[DHCP Client] --> switchA[Layer 2 Managed Switch]
    switchA -->|Trusted Port| legitServer[Legitimate DHCP Server]
    switchA -->|Untrusted Port Blocked| rogueBlocked[Attacker DHCP Server]
    switchA --> snoopingA[DHCP Snooping Engine]
    snoopingA --> bindingTableA[Binding Table: MAC to IP]
    snoopingA --> arpInsp[Dynamic ARP Inspection]
    arpInsp --> dot1x[802.1X Authentication Layer]
    dot1x --> aclLayer[ACL Enforcement]
    aclLayer --> secureNet[Verified Secure Network]
```

## 4.4 Attack Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    detectA{Is DHCP Snooping Enabled?}
    detectA -->|No| vulnA[High Vulnerability]
    detectA -->|Yes| checkB{Rogue Server on Trusted Port?}
    checkB -->|Yes| critA[Critical Compromise]
    checkB -->|No| rateCheck{Rate Limit Exceeded?}
    rateCheck -->|Yes| starvA[Possible Starvation Attack]
    rateCheck -->|No| normalOp[Normal Operation]
    vulnA --> actionA[Enable 802.1X Immediately]
    critA --> actionB[Isolate Switch Port]
    starvA --> actionC[Apply Port Security]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Aid:** Each Mermaid diagram above represents a validated academic schematic. The *race condition* in Figure 4.2 is the **most commonly tested visual concept** for KTU Module 3 questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define DHCP Redirection. Mention two UDP ports associated with DHCP communication.*

**Model Answer:**
DHCP Redirection is a cyberattack where an attacker manipulates the DHCP handshake to redirect a client's network traffic to a rogue gateway or DNS server by responding faster than the legitimate DHCP server. The two UDP ports are **port 67** (server-side) and **port 68** (client-side). This attack exploits the lack of authentication in the DORA process.

*(3 marks: Definition 2 + Ports 1)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*List any three countermeasures against DHCP Redirection.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **DHCP Snooping** — filters untrusted DHCP messages at the switch level.
2. **802.1X Port-Based Authentication** — ensures only authenticated devices receive IP configuration.
3. **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** — prevents post-attack ARP cache poisoning.
4. **Rate Limiting** on DHCP broadcast traffic per port.

*(3 marks: 1 per countermeasure)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of a DHCP Redirection attack, including the role of a rogue DHCP server and the DORA mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the defense mechanisms available at Layer 2 and Layer 3, with special emphasis on DHCP Snooping and 802.1X authentication. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A DHCP Redirection attack proceeds in the following structured steps:

1. **Reconnaissance Phase:** The attacker scans the LAN using tools such as `nmap` or `yersinia` to identify the existing DHCP server's IP, MAC, and lease pool size.

2. **Deployment of Rogue Server:** The attacker installs a rogue DHCP server (using `dnsmasq` on a Linux box or `dhcp.exe` utilities on Windows) on the same VLAN. This server listens on **UDP port 67**.

3. **Crafting the Malicious OFFER:** The rogue server prepares a `DHCPOFFER` packet with:
   - A valid IP from the subnet (to avoid client rejection).
   - A **default gateway** pointing to the attacker's machine.
   - A **DNS server** pointing to a malicious resolver (often for phishing).

4. **Race Condition Exploitation:** When a victim client broadcasts `DHCPDISCOVER`, both servers respond. The rogue server responds in **microseconds**, while the legitimate server has processing overhead. The client accepts the **first valid OFFER**.

5. **Traffic Capture:** All subsequent traffic from the victim (HTTP, DNS, FTP) now routes through the attacker's machine, enabling **MITM attacks**, credential theft, and session hijacking.

6. **Persistence:** The attacker may poison ARP caches to maintain the redirection beyond the DHCP lease cycle.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Layer 2 Defenses:**

- **DHCP Snooping:** A switch feature that classifies ports as *trusted* (uplink to legitimate server) or *untrusted* (end-user ports). DHCP messages from untrusted ports are dropped, and a binding table of (MAC → IP → VLAN → Port) is maintained.

- **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI):** Uses the DHCP Snooping binding table to validate ARP packets, preventing ARP spoofing post-redirection.

- **Port Security:** Limits the number of MAC addresses per port, mitigating DHCP starvation.

**Layer 3 Defenses:**

- **802.1X Authentication:** Requires EAP-based credential verification before a port is activated. Even if DHCP is spoofed, no IP is assigned until authentication succeeds.

- **Network Access Control (NAC):** Solutions like Cisco ISE enforce posture assessment before granting network access.

- **IPv6 SLAAC/DHCPv6 Shields:** Similar protections for IPv6 networks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse DHCP Snooping with Dynamic ARP Inspection. Remember: **Snooping validates DHCP; DAI validates ARP using the snooping table**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Many answers omit the **race condition** explanation — this is a critical 2-mark differentiator.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to mention that DHCP Redirection is the *enabler* for MITM, not the MITM itself. Examiners expect you to link the concepts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between DHCP Spoofing and DHCP Redirection. Explain DHCP Starvation as a precursor attack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the DHCP DORA process and indicate at which stage redirection occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline — Part (a):**

| Aspect | DHCP Spoofing | DHCP Redirection |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Scope | General impersonation of DHCP server | Specifically redirects traffic flow |
| Goal | Identity theft, reconnaissance | MITM, traffic interception |
| Outcome | Disrupts or misconfigures clients | Routes traffic through attacker |
| Tool | Yersinia, Ettercap | dnsmasq, mitmproxy |

**DHCP Starvation:** The attacker floods `DHCPDISCOVER` packets with spoofed MAC addresses until the legitimate DHCP pool is exhausted. New clients cannot obtain IP addresses, forcing them to either fail or accept a rogue server's offer.

**Model Solution Outline — Part (b):**
The DORA process diagram (see Section 4.1 of these notes) should be redrawn. **Redirection occurs at the OFFER stage**, where the rogue server's crafted parameters replace legitimate ones. The REQUEST and ACK proceed normally, but the malicious gateway/DNS is now bound to the client.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** DHCP Redirection = manipulating DORA to redirect traffic to rogue gateway/DNS.
- **Ports:** UDP **67** (server), UDP **68** (client).
- **Key Attack Types:** Rogue DHCP Server, DHCP Starvation, DHCP Spoofing.
- **DORA Stages Vulnerable:** Primarily the **OFFER** stage (race condition).
- **RFC Reference:** **RFC 2131** (no built-in authentication).
- **Critical Defenses:** DHCP Snooping, 802.1X, Dynamic ARP Inspection, Port Security.
- **Magic Cookie:** `0x63825363` (memorize if asked for trivia).
- **Default Lease:** 86400 seconds.
- **Layer of Attack:** Primarily Layer 2 (broadcast) escalating to Layer 3 (routing).
- **Mathematical Insight:** A /24 subnet can be starved in **under 13 seconds**.
- **MITM Link:** DHCP Redirection is a *prerequisite enabler* for Man-in-the-Middle attacks.
- **Enterprise Tool:** Cisco's DHCP Snooping binding table is the gold-standard defense.
- **Exam Tip:** Always mention **race condition** and **lack of authentication** in DHCP Redirection answers — these are examiner-favored keywords.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Redirection and Interception with ICMP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Redirection and Interception with ICMP # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 ICMP — The Internet's "Diagnostic & Alert" Protocol > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition** > The **Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)** is a network-layer supporting protocol (defined in **RFC 792**) used by hosts, routers, and gateways to communicate error reports, diagnostic information, and routing-control messages. ICMP does not carry application data; it is purely a *control* and *signalling* protocol operating directly over **IP (Internet Protocol)**. ICMP is encapsulated directly inside an **IPv4/IPv6 datagram** (Protocol Number = **1** for IPv4, **ICMPv6 = 58** for IPv6). Every ICMP packet contains a **Type** field, a **Code** field, a **Checksum** field, and a **Variable** payload that depends on the message class. The 8 most commonly referenced ICMP Type values (high-yield for KTU): | Type | Name | Purpose | |:----:|:-----|:--------| | 0 | Echo Reply | Ping response | | 3 | Destination Unreachable | Error reporting | | 5 | **Redirect** | **Routing correction / Attack vector** | | 8 | Echo Request | Ping request | | 11 | Time Exceeded | TTL expired (used by Traceroute) | | 13 | Timestamp | Time synchronization | | 14 | Timestamp Reply | Time synchronization | | 3/4 | Source Quench (deprecated) | Congestion control | --- ## 1.2 What is "Redirection & Interception with ICMP"? **Redirection** in the context of cyber security refers to the act of **diverting network traffic** from its legitimate, intended path to an attacker-controlled destination. **Interception** refers to the act of **eavesdropping, capturing, inspecting, modifying, or relaying** that diverted traffic as it passes through an unauthorized node. When ICMP is abused for these purposes, the attacker weaponises a *legitimate* protocol — making the attack extremely difficult for naïve firewalls to detect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The Module-3 focus is on understanding **ICMP Type 5 (Redirect)** messages, how they can be **forged by attackers** to corrupt a victim's routing table, and how this corruption enables **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks, **eavesdropping**, and **session hijacking**. --- ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Wrong Turn" Highway Sign Imagine you are driving on a highway. A highway sign (built by the road authority) tells you, *"If you want to go to City B, take Exit 7 instead of Exit 12 — Exit 7 is faster."* You trust the sign and take Exit 7. Now imagine a **criminal** who has forged an identical sign. The fake sign says the same thing, but Exit 7 actually leads through a narrow alley where the criminal watches your car, copies everything inside, and only then lets you continue to City B. > - The **legitimate router** sending an ICMP Redirect is like the **real highway authority**. > - The **attacker** sending a **forged ICMP Redirect** is like the **fake highway sign**. > - The **host's routing table** is like the **driver's mental map** that gets corrupted. > - The **interception** is the criminal watching through the alley. This is the essence of **ICMP Redirection & Interception** — a victim host is *politely tricked* into routing its traffic through an adversary, who silently inspects everything in transit. --- ## 1.4 Why ICMP Redirect Exists (Legitimate Purpose) When a host's default gateway receives a packet destined for a non-local network, but it knows that a **better first-hop router** exists on the same subnet, the gateway may send an **ICMP Redirect (Type 5)** to the source host: > *"Do not send packets for Network X through me — send them directly through Router R."* The host, on receiving this message, **updates its routing cache** so that future packets use the better route. This is a benign optimization feature — and *exactly* what attackers exploit. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an ICMP Redirect Packet (Type = 5) The ICMP Redirect message has the following structure (KTU high-yield): | Field | Size (bits) | Meaning | |:------|:-----------:|:--------| | Type | 8 | Always **5** for Redirect | | Code | 8 | Sub-type (see table below) | | Checksum | 16 | One's complement checksum (covers entire ICMP message) | | Gateway Internet Address | 32 | IP address of the *better* gateway | | Internet Header + 64 bits of Original Data | Variable | The original IP header + first 8 bytes of the offending packet (so the host can match the flow) | **ICMP Redirect Code Values (must memorise):** | Code | Meaning | |:----:|:--------| | 0 | Redirect Datagram for the **Network** | | 1 | Redirect Datagram for the **Host** | | 2 | Redirect Datagram for the **Type of Service and Network** | | 3 | Redirect Datagram for the **Type of Service and Host** | > [!NOTE] > A normal, **legitimate** ICMP Redirect can only be sent by a router to a host on the **same physical subnet** (RFC requirement). An attacker who is on the same broadcast domain can therefore forge this message trivially. --- ## 2.2 The Operational Logic of a Forged ICMP Redirect Attack The attack unfolds in **five logical stages** (high-yield for 14-mark answers): 1. **Reconnaissance & Co-Location** The attacker must first achieve **Layer-2 adjacency** with the victim — i.e., be on the same subnet, VLAN, Wi-Fi, or compromised switch port. This is essential because the host's OS will discard ICMP Redirects received from off-link sources. 2. **Packet Crafting** The attacker uses a raw-packet library (e.g., `Scapy`) to forge an ICMP packet with: - **Type = 5** - **Code = 1** (Redirect for Host — most effective) - **Gateway IP = Attacker's IP** (or attacker-controlled router) - **Original IP Header** of a flow the victim is actively using (e.g., a TCP connection to `8.8.8.8:443`) 3. **Injection** The forged packet is transmitted onto the local segment. Because the attacker spoofs the *real router's* MAC/IP as the source, the victim believes the message comes from its trusted gateway. 4. **Routing Table Poisoning** The victim's kernel updates its **routing cache** to add a **host-route entry** with `Next-Hop = Attacker's IP`. From this moment onward, traffic destined for that flow is sent to the attacker. 5. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** The attacker enables **IP Forwarding** on their machine so packets are silently forwarded to the real gateway. The victim is unaware, and the attacker can now **sniff, log, modify, or hijack** every byte of the conversation. --- ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why It Works | |:-----|:-------------| | Co-Location needed | ICMP Redirect is only honoured from a sender on the **same subnet** (link-local trust boundary). | | Original IP header required | The host OS matches the redirect to a *specific* flow already in its cache. | | Router IP spoofed as source | The host trusts ICMP messages from the gateway more than from arbitrary senders. | | IP Forwarding enabled | Without forwarding, the attack is a **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** — the victim loses connectivity. With forwarding, the attack becomes **silent and persistent**. | | Per-flow targeting | Modern OSes do *not* create a global default-route override; they create a *host-route* for that specific destination. So the attacker usually needs to **inject redirects for each active flow** (or use Code 0 for network-wide redirection). | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for the topic. Memorise it. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility | |:--------|:---------------|:--------------------| | ICMP Protocol Number (IPv4) | **Protocol = 1** | Identifying ICMP in IP headers via Wireshark / firewalls | | ICMPv6 Protocol Number | **Next Header = 58** | ICMPv6 in IPv6 packets | | Redirect Type | **Type = 5** | Detection signature for IDSes | | One's Complement Checksum | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i} W_{16,i}}$ (one's complement of the 16-bit one's complement sum) | Validates integrity of ICMP payload | | Subnet Co-Location Requirement | $d_{V,A} = 0$ (V=Victim, A=Attacker, link-local distance = 0) | Determines attack feasibility | | MITM Latency Penalty | $T_{MITM} = T_{legit} + 2 \cdot T_{proc} + 2 \cdot T_{prop}^{A}$ | Estimating detection probability | | Routing Cache TTL | $t_{cache} = 600\text{ s}$ (Linux default) | Attack persistence duration | | Netmask to Prefix Length | $n_{prefix} = \log_2(2^{32} - N_{hosts} - 2)$ | Determining broadcast domain size for attack scope | | Bandwidth for Mass Redirection | $B_{req} = n_{flows} \times (S_{icmp} + S_{eth})$ (in bytes/s) | Capacity planning for the attacker | | Passive Sniffing Range | $R_{sniff} = \text{collision domain size}$ | Switched networks require ARP poisoning or port mirroring | --- ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility The ability to **detect and prevent** ICMP redirection attacks is foundational in: - **Enterprise network design** — disabling ICMP Redirects at edge routers (`no ip redirects` on Cisco IOS). - **Host hardening** — `net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0` in Linux. - **Intrusion Detection Systems (Snort, Suricata)** — signature `alert icmp $EXTERNAL any -> $HOME any (msg:"ICMP Redirect"; itype:5; sid:...; )`. - **Forensics** — reconstructing a MITM attack from PCAP captures using Wireshark filters like `icmp.type == 5`. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ICMP Checksum Calculation (One's Complement) The ICMP checksum is computed by the **sender** and verified by the **receiver**. A mismatch indicates corruption or forgery. > **Worked Example:** Compute the checksum for a hypothetical ICMP Echo Request with the following 16-bit words: $W_1 = 0x4500$, $W_2 = 0x003C$, $W_3 = 0x1C46$, $W_4 = 0x4000$, $W_5 = 0x4001$, $W_6 = 0x0000$, $W_7 = 0x0000$. **Step 1 — Add all 16-bit words using one's complement arithmetic:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_1 &= W_1 + W_2 + W_3 + W_4 + W_5 + W_6 + W_7 \\ S_1 &= 0x4500 + 0x003C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 + 0x4001 + 0x0000 + 0x0000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the sum:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_1 &= 0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C \\ S_1 &= 0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182 \\ S_1 &= 0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182 \\ S_1 &= 0xA182 + 0x4001 = 0xE183 \\ S_1 &= 0xE183 + 0x0000 + 0x0000 = 0xE183 \end{aligned} $$ The sum fits in 16 bits — no carry wrap is needed. **Step 3 — Take the one's complement (flip all bits):** $$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{0xE183} = 0x1E7C $$ **Step 4 — Receiver verification:** The receiver sums the entire ICMP message *including* the checksum field. If the result is $0xFFFF$ (all 1's), the packet is intact; otherwise it is dropped or flagged. > [!NOTE] > This checksum is intentionally **weak** (no cryptographic protection). This is precisely why attackers can **forge** ICMP Redirects — the checksum protects against *random* bit-errors, not against *malicious* modification. A real attacker computes a correct checksum for their forged packet, so victims accept it. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Attack Feasibility via Subnet Distance For an ICMP Redirect to be honoured, the attacker and victim must share the same **Layer-2 broadcast domain**. We can formalise this: > **Theorem (Sub-Layer Constraint):** > Let $V$ = Victim's IP, $A$ = Attacker's IP, and $\text{Mask}$ = Subnet mask of length $n$. An ICMP Redirect from $A$ to $V$ is accepted by $V$'s kernel **if and only if**: $$ (V \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask}) \;=\; (A \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask}) $$ **Worked Example:** A network uses subnet mask **255.255.255.0** ($/24$). Victim = **192.168.1.50**, Attacker = **192.168.1.99**. $$ \begin{aligned} V \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask} &= 192.168.1.50 \ \text{AND} \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.1.0 \\ A \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask} &= 192.168.1.99 \ \text{AND} \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.1.0 \end{aligned} $$ Both yield **192.168.1.0** → attack is **feasible**. ✓ **Counter-Example:** Victim = **192.168.1.50**, Attacker = **192.168.2.99**, Mask = **255.255.255.0**. $$ A \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask} = 192.168.2.0 \neq 192.168.1.0 $$ Attack is **infeasible** from Layer-2. ✗ (Attacker would first need to compromise a host on the victim's subnet, e.g., via a pivot.) --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ICMP Attack Categories | Attack Class | Mechanism | Detection Difficulty | Impact | KTU Weight | |:-------------|:----------|:---------------------|:-------|:-----------| | **ICMP Redirect MITM** | Forged Type 5 → Routing-cache poisoning | Medium | Confidentiality breach | **High (14-mk candidate)** | | **ICMP Smurf** (Type 8 amplification) | Echo Request to broadcast with spoofed source | Low (volumetric) | DoS / DDoS | Medium | | **ICMP Ping of Death** | Oversized fragmented Echo Request | Low | DoS / crash | Low | | **ICMP Flood** | High-rate Echo Requests | Low (rate-based) | DoS | Medium | | **ICMP Tunneling (Covert Channel)** | Encapsulating data inside ICMP Echo payloads | **Very High** | Data exfiltration / C2 channel | **High (14-mk candidate)** | | **ICMP Router Advertisement** (Type 9, IPv6) | Fake default-gateway advertisement | Medium | MITM on IPv6 LANs | Medium | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth Cost of Mass Redirection Suppose an attacker wants to poison the routing caches of $n$ hosts on a $/24$ subnet, each with $f$ active TCP flows. The required injected bandwidth $B_{req}$ is: $$ B_{req} = n \cdot f \cdot S_{packet} $$ where $S_{packet}$ is the total wire-size of a forged ICMP Redirect (including Ethernet, IP, and ICMP headers + original IP header excerpt). **Numerical Example:** $n = 50$ hosts, $f = 10$ active flows each, $S_{packet} = 74$ bytes (Ethernet 14 + IP 20 + ICMP 8 + embedded IP+8 bytes 32 = 74). $$ \begin{aligned} B_{req} &= 50 \times 10 \times 74 \;\text{bytes} \\ &= 37{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \\ &= 37 \;\text{kB per poisoning campaign} \end{aligned} $$ Even for a modest campaign, the attacker only needs to inject **tens of kilobytes** to corrupt a subnet. This is why ICMP Redirect attacks are **silent, fast, and stealthy**. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Cache Persistence Window Most modern operating systems keep ICMP-Redirect-influenced routes in the routing cache for a bounded time. On Linux: $$ t_{cache} = 600 \;\text{seconds (default)} $$ For the attack to remain persistent, the attacker must re-inject the redirect at an interval: $$ T_{re-inject} < t_{cache} $$ This means the attacker needs a **low-bandwidth, periodic beacon** to maintain the MITM position — a useful signature for detection. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Legitimate vs. Forged ICMP Redirect Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Victim Host H1
IP 192.168.1.50] -- Packet destined for 8.8.8.8 --> B[Real Router R1
192.168.1.1] B -- ICMP Redirect Type 5 Code 1
Better gateway = R2 --> A A -- Updates routing cache
Next-hop for 8.8.8.8 = R2 --> C[Real Router R2
10.0.0.1] C -- Forwards to Internet --> D((Internet)) ``` > **Normal flow:** Router R1 optimises by telling H1 to use R2 directly. Legitimate optimisation. ## 4.2 Forged ICMP Redirect — MITM Attack Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Victim Host H1
192.168.1.50] -- 1 Sends packet to 8.8.8.8 --> R[Real Router R1
192.168.1.1] R -- 2 Forwards to Internet --> NET((Internet)) ATK[Attacker Machine
192.168.1.99] -- 3 SPOOFS ICMP Type 5
Code 1, Gateway = 192.168.1.99 --> V V -- 4 Routing cache poisoned
Next-hop for 8.8.8.8 = ATTACKER --> ATK ATK -- 5 Inspects logs modifies and forwards with IP FORWARD = 1 --> R R -- 6 Forwards to Internet --> NET NET -- 7 Return traffic back via R1 --> R R -- 8 Forwards to 192.168.1.99 --> ATK ATK -- 9 Inspects and forwards --> V V[Victim Host H1] -.-> ATK ATK -. Sniffing credentials session tokens plaintext .-> SN[Attacker Sniffer Logs] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **critical enabling step** is *enabling IP Forwarding* on the attacker's machine. Without it, the victim loses connectivity and the attack degrades into a noisy DoS. ## 4.3 ICMP Tunneling (Covert Channel) Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR APP[Client Application
Hidden Tunnel Client] -- 1 Wraps data in ICMP Echo payload --> A1[Attacker Boundary Host] A1 -- 2 Sends ICMP Echo Request to Public Server --> P1((Internet / Firewall Bypass)) P1 --> S1[External Covert Server
Listens for ICMP Echo] S1 -- 3 Extracts payload from ICMP --> C2[Command and Control Server] C2 -- 4 Replies via ICMP Echo Reply with response data --> A1 A1 -- 5 Delivers to local application --> APP ``` > **ICMP Tunneling** is a *separate* but related interception technique. The firewall sees only "ICMP pings" but is actually carrying an entire bidirectional TCP session in the payload. ## 4.4 Attack Decision Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([START Attacker on Same Subnet?]) --> Q1{Co-Located with Victim?} Q1 -- NO --> OUT1[Attack Infeasible
Requires Pivot] Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Enable IP Forwarding?} Q2 -- NO --> DOS[Result Denial of Service] Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Craft ICMP Type 5
with Spoofed Source = Real Router} Q3 --> Q4{Set Gateway IP = Self} Q4 --> Q5{Embed Original IP Header
of Active Victim Flow} Q5 --> Q6[Inject Packet onto LAN] Q6 --> Q7{Victim Kernel Honours Redirect?} Q7 -- NO --> OUT2[Attempt Failed
Check Host Hardening] Q7 -- YES --> Q8[MITM Established] Q8 --> Q9{Persist Cache?} Q9 -- Re-inject every 600 s --> Q8 Q8 --> SN[Sniff / Modify / Hijack] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **What is an ICMP Redirect message? In which scenario is it normally sent by a router?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **ICMP Redirect (Type 5)** is a control message used by a router to inform a host on the same subnet that a *better* first-hop gateway exists for a given destination. **(1 mark)** > > It is sent when a router $R_1$ receives a packet from a host $H$ on subnet $S$, and $R_1$ determines that another router $R_2$ on the *same* subnet $S$ is a more optimal first-hop for that destination. **(1 mark)** > > On receiving the redirect, the host updates its routing cache so future packets for that destination go directly to $R_2$. **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why is ICMP often described as a "supporting" protocol and not a "transport" protocol?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > ICMP is a **supporting/network-layer protocol** because it does not carry application data and is not used for end-to-end data delivery between user processes. **(1 mark)** > > It is encapsulated directly inside IP (Protocol = 1) and is used exclusively for **error reporting, diagnostics (ping, traceroute), and routing-control signalling** such as Redirects. **(1 mark)** > > It has **no port numbers, no sessions, and no reliability mechanisms** — distinguishing it from transport protocols like TCP and UDP. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. KTU provides internal choice — students answer **one of the two** alternatives. --- ### ✦ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Explain the structure of an ICMP Redirect packet. With a suitable diagram, describe how a legitimate ICMP Redirect is used for route optimisation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** How can an attacker abuse ICMP Redirects to perform a Man-in-the-Middle attack? List the necessary pre-conditions and describe the attack steps. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The **ICMP Redirect packet** has the following fields **(2 marks)**: - **Type = 5** - **Code** (0–3, indicating Network, Host, ToS+Network, ToS+Host) - **Checksum** (16-bit one's complement over the entire ICMP message) - **Gateway Internet Address** (32-bit IP of the better router) - **Internet Header + First 8 bytes** of the original datagram that triggered the redirect **Legitimate Use — Route Optimisation (Diagram in words):** **(3 marks)** 1. Host $H$ (192.168.1.50) sends a packet to 8.8.8.8 via its default gateway $R_1$ (192.168.1.1). 2. $R_1$ examines the packet and observes that $R_2$ (10.0.0.1, on the same 192.168.1.0/24 subnet as $H$ via a separate interface) is a better next-hop for 8.8.8.8. 3. $R_1$ forwards the packet to $R_2$ (so the user traffic still flows), and **also** sends an ICMP Redirect (Type 5, Code 1) to $H$, telling it: *"For 8.8.8.8, use gateway 10.0.0.1."* 4. $H$ adds a **host-route** entry in its routing cache: `8.8.8.8 → gw 10.0.0.1`. 5. Future packets from $H$ to 8.8.8.8 go **directly** to $R_2$, bypassing the sub-optimal hop through $R_1$. **Why this is safe (legitimate case):** **(2 marks)** — $R_1$ is a trusted on-link router; the redirect improves performance without changing security boundaries. #### Model Solution for (b): An attacker on the same subnet can **forge** an ICMP Redirect to redirect a victim's traffic through themselves, enabling a **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attack. **(1 mark)** **Pre-conditions (any 4 needed — 2 marks):** 1. Attacker is on the **same Layer-2 broadcast domain** as the victim (e.g., same Wi-Fi, VLAN, or compromised switch). 2. Attacker has a **packet-crafting tool** (Scapy, hping3, Nping). 3. Victim's OS has **ICMP Redirect acceptance enabled** (default in many older systems). 4. Attacker **enables IP Forwarding** (`/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward = 1`) so the victim doesn't lose connectivity. 5. Attacker knows or guesses at least one **active flow** of the victim (e.g., the destination IP and ports). **Attack Steps (4 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Crafting:** Build an ICMP Type 5 Code 1 packet with: - Source IP = Real Router's IP - Gateway IP = Attacker's IP - Embedded original IP header of the victim's active flow. 2. **Injection:** Send the packet onto the LAN. The victim's kernel updates its routing cache to route the flow through the attacker. 3. **Forwarding:** With IP Forwarding on, the attacker transparently relays the victim's traffic to the real gateway and back — the victim experiences no disruption. 4. **Exploitation:** The attacker now runs `tcpdump`, `Wireshark`, or an SSL-stripping tool on the in-transit stream, harvesting credentials, session cookies, or modifying content in real time. **Persistence:** The attacker must re-inject the redirect every ~600 seconds (Linux cache TTL) to maintain the poisoned route. --- ### ✦ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Differentiate between **ICMP Redirect attack** and **ICMP tunneling**. Explain how each can be used by an attacker for traffic interception. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What mitigations can a network administrator and a host administrator apply to defend against ICMP-based redirection and interception attacks? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): | Parameter | ICMP Redirect Attack | ICMP Tunneling | |:----------|:---------------------|:---------------| | **ICMP Type used** | Type 5 | Type 8 (Echo) / Type 0 (Echo Reply) | | **Goal** | Re-route victim's traffic through attacker | Carry arbitrary data inside ICMP payload | | **Co-location needed?** | Yes, same subnet | No — works across the Internet | | **Visibility** | Often silent, single packet per flow | Continuous ICMP traffic pattern | | **Mitigation difficulty** | Medium (disable redirects) | High (deep packet inspection required) | | **Typical use** | Eavesdropping on a LAN | Bypassing firewalls, C2 channels, data exfiltration | **ICMP Redirect Attack — Interception Mechanism:** The attacker poisons the victim's routing cache so that traffic flows through the attacker's machine (MITM), which silently logs and forwards packets. **(1.5 marks)** **ICMP Tunneling — Interception Mechanism:** Tools like `ptunnel`, `icmpsh`, or `icmptunnel` encapsulate TCP/UDP traffic inside the payload field of ICMP Echo Request/Reply packets. **(1.5 marks)** A firewall that allows "ping" will allow this traffic to pass unnoticed. The attacker can then exfiltrate data, establish reverse shells, or tunnel a full VPN — all while the IDS sees only "ICMP pings." **(1 mark)** #### Model Solution for (b): **Network-Administrator Mitigations (4 marks — 1 mark each minimum):** 1. **Disable ICMP Redirects on routers:** - Cisco IOS: `interface g0/0 → no ip redirects` - Juniper: `set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet no-redirects` 2. **Deploy ingress/egress ICMP filtering** at the perimeter — block all outbound ICMP from internal hosts to the Internet (or rate-limit to ~1 packet/second). 3. **Enable uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding)** on edge routers to drop packets with spoofed source addresses — defeats forged ICMP Redirects from off-link attackers. 4. **Use 802.1X port-based network access control** so only authenticated devices can join the broadcast domain — eliminates casual co-located attackers. **Host-Administrator Mitigations (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Disable ICMP Redirect acceptance on the host:** - Linux: `sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects=0` - Windows: Registry `EnableICMPRedirect = 0` under `HKLM\...\Tcpip\Parameters` 2. **Disable IP Forwarding** on user workstations (it should *only* be on, on dedicated routers). 3. **Deploy a Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** such as OSSEC or a modern EDR that alerts on the appearance of new host-routes or unexpected ICMP Type 5 packets. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the subnet co-location constraint** — A 14-mark answer that does not mention "the attacker must be on the same Layer-2 segment" will lose 2–3 marks. Examiners mark this as a critical omission. > > 2. **Confusing ICMP Redirect (Type 5) with ICMP Echo (Type 8)** — These are *different* attacks. Redirect = route poisoning. Echo (or large Echo) = tunneling or flooding. Examiners expect students to specify the **Type** number. > > 3. **Not mentioning IP Forwarding** — An attack that doesn't enable IP Forwarding is a DoS, not a MITM. Examiners deduct marks if the student describes a "stealthy silent attack" without this critical step. > > 4. **Omitting mitigations** — A 14-mark answer that only describes the attack but does not list **at least 3 mitigations** (with specific commands) is incomplete. Always end your answer with a "Defense / Mitigation" sub-section. > > 5. **Writing vague answers like "ICMP is used to attack networks"** — Examiners want the **Type number**, the **flow of packets**, the **role of the routing cache**, and the **post-attack impact**. Be specific. > > 6. **Confusing one-time DoS with persistent MITM** — ICMP Redirect attacks are **persistent** if re-injected; not one-shot. State this explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Bullets — Memorise Before Exam** - ✅ **ICMP = Type field + Code field + Checksum + Rest-of-header.** Type 5 = Redirect. - ✅ **ICMP Redirect (Type 5)** is sent by a router to inform a host of a *better* first-hop gateway. - ✅ **Four Code values:** 0 (Network), 1 (Host), 2 (ToS+Network), 3 (ToS+Host). - ✅ **Attacker must be on the same subnet** (link-local) for the redirect to be honoured. - ✅ The forged packet must include the **original IP header** of an active victim flow. - ✅ The **attacker enables IP Forwarding** to keep the attack silent (else it becomes a DoS). - ✅ The **routing cache TTL** is ~600 s on Linux — attacker re-injects periodically. - ✅ **Detection signatures:** Wireshark filter `icmp.type == 5`; Snort rule `itype:5`. - ✅ **ICMP Tunneling** uses Echo (Type 8/0) to carry covert payloads — different attack class. - ✅ **Mitigations:** `no ip redirects` (router), `accept_redirects=0` (host), uRPF, 802.1X, HIDS. - ✅ The **ICMP checksum is not cryptographic** — attackers compute valid checksums easily. - ✅ The attack is **silent, fast (kB-scale), and persistent** — high-impact and high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Port Scanning: TCP and UDP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Port Scanning: TCP and UDP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Port Scanning** is the systematic technique of probing a target host's network ports (logical endpoints numbered **0 to 65535**) to determine their **state** — whether they are *open*, *closed*, or *filtered* — and the underlying services (daemons/applications) listening on them. It is a foundational **reconnaissance** activity in the *Information Gathering / Scanning* phase of ethical hacking methodology and is regulated under KTU's Network Security and Penetration Testing modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Ready):**
> *"Port Scanning is a network probing technique used to identify open, closed, or filtered TCP/UDP ports on a target host, enabling enumeration of running services, operating system fingerprinting, and vulnerability assessment."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House With 65,536 Doors"

Imagine a massive apartment building with **65,536 numbered doors**. A delivery person (the *scanner*) wants to figure out **which doors are occupied, which are locked, and which are barricaded**.

- **Open door** → Someone is home and will answer (a service is running, e.g., web server on door 80).
- **Closed door** → Nobody lives there, but the building is open (host reachable, no service).
- **Filtered/Barricaded door** → A security guard blocks every approach with no answer (a firewall drops packets silently).

The scanner sends a polite **knock (packet)** at each door, observes the **response pattern (TCP flags / ICMP replies)**, and records the state. This *active reconnaissance* is the essence of port scanning.

## 1.3 Port Number Categories (KTU High-Yield Memory Table)

| Category | Port Range | Count | Example Services |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Well-Known Ports** | **0 – 1023** | 1024 ports | HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), SSH (22), FTP (21) |
| **Registered Ports** | **1024 – 49151** | 48,128 ports | MySQL (3306), RDP (3389) |
| **Dynamic/Private/Ephemeral** | **49152 – 65535** | 16,384 ports | Client-side temporary ports |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total theoretical ports = 2¹⁶ = 65,536**
> The TCP and UDP port spaces are *independent* — TCP port 80 and UDP port 80 are separate endpoints.

## 1.4 Why Port Scanning Matters in KTU Context

1. **Attackers** use it to find entry points (pre-attack reconnaissance).
2. **Defenders / Admins** use it to verify firewall ACLs and harden the network.
3. **Auditors** use it for compliance auditing (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).
4. KTU examiners frequently test scan type identification, packet flag behavior, and tool output interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TCP Flags — The Language of Scanning

A TCP header contains **6 control bits (flags)**. The scanner manipulates these flags to elicit differential responses from the target. This is the *core mechanism* of every TCP scan variant.

| Flag | Bit Position | Meaning | Scan Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SYN** | Bit 1 (0x02) | Synchronize — initiate connection | **SYN scan** (stealth) |
| **ACK** | Bit 4 (0x10) | Acknowledgment | **ACK scan** (firewall mapping) |
| **FIN** | Bit 0 (0x01) | Finish — graceful close | **FIN scan** (stealth) |
| **RST** | Bit 2 (0x04) | Reset — abort connection | **Response** to invalid packets |
| **PSH** | Bit 3 (0x08) | Push data | **Xmas scan** (FIN+PSH+URG) |
| **URG** | Bit 5 (0x20) | Urgent pointer | **Xmas scan** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**U**nforgettable **A**lligator **P**roperly **R**esponds **S**o **F**ast" → **U**RG, **A**CK, **P**SH, **R**ST, **S**YN, **F**IN

## 2.2 The TCP Three-Way Handshake (Foundation of Scanning)

Before any data transfer, TCP requires a handshake:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN (seq=x)}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Step 2: Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)}} \text{Client} \\
\text{Step 3: Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK (ack=y+1)}} \text{Server} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Connection ESTABLISHED}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight for Scanning:** The scanner's strategy depends on **how far it lets the handshake proceed**.

- *Full connect* → completes all 3 steps (noisy, logged).
- *Half-open* → sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK, **never sends final ACK** (stealth).

## 2.3 TCP Scan Types — Detailed Operational Logic

### (A) SYN Scan (Half-Open / Stealth Scan) ⭐ Most Used

**Logic Flow:**
1. Attacker → sends **SYN** to target port.
2. If port **OPEN** → Target replies **SYN-ACK** → Attacker sends **RST** (aborts handshake) ⟹ Port = OPEN.
3. If port **CLOSED** → Target replies **RST-ACK** ⟹ Port = CLOSED.
4. If port **FILTERED** → No response (or ICMP unreachable) ⟹ Port = FILTERED.

> [!NOTE]
> The connection is **never fully established**, so it is often *not logged by application-level logs* — this is why it is called "stealth." It is the **default scan** in the tool *Nmap*.

### (B) TCP Connect() Scan (Full Open Scan)

Uses the OS's native `connect()` system call to complete the **full 3-way handshake**.

- Pros: Works without raw socket privileges; high accuracy.
- Cons: **Easily logged** by the target's application; leaves forensic evidence.

### (C) NULL Scan

Sends a packet with **NO flags set**.

- OPEN port (Linux/BSD): **No response** (RFC 793 discard rule).
- CLOSED port: **RST** response.
- *OS fingerprinting technique* — Windows responds differently (RST for all), helping identify the OS.

### (D) FIN Scan

Sends a packet with **only the FIN flag set**.

- OPEN port: **No response**.
- CLOSED port: **RST** response.
- Bypasses basic stateless firewalls that only filter SYN packets.

### (E) Xmas Scan

Sends a packet with **FIN + PSH + URG** flags set — "lit up like a Christmas tree."

- OPEN port: **No response**.
- CLOSED port: **RST** response.
- Highly stealthy against non-stateful firewalls.

### (F) ACK Scan (Firewall Mapping)

Sends a packet with **only the ACK flag set**.

- Used to map **firewall rule sets**, not port state.
- Filtered port: ICMP unreachable.
- Unfiltered port: RST response.
- Helps determine whether the target is *stateful* or *stateless*.

## 2.4 UDP Scan — The Connectionless Challenge

UDP is **fire-and-forget** — no handshake, no session state. This makes scanning fundamentally different.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Attacker sends an **empty UDP packet** to the target port.
2. **If port OPEN** → No response, OR a UDP response from the service (rare).
3. **If port CLOSED** → Target sends **ICMP Type 3, Code 3 (Port Unreachable)**.
4. **If port FILTERED** → No response (packet dropped by firewall) OR different ICMP code (Type 3, Code 1/2/9/10/13).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** UDP scanning is **inherently slow and unreliable** because:
> - Many firewalls **rate-limit ICMP Port Unreachable** responses.
> - UDP services may not respond to empty/probe packets.
> - The scanner must use **timeouts** to detect "open|filtered" states, which is ambiguous.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Ports | $2^{16} = 65536$ | ports | 0 to 65535 |
| Well-Known Ports | $0 \text{ to } 1023$ | ports | Requires root to bind |
| Theoretical Scan Time | $T = N_{ports} \times t_{timeout}$ | seconds | $N$ = ports, $t$ = timeout |
| Bandwidth Cost (SYN) | $B = N_{ports} \times (H_{IP} + H_{TCP})$ | bytes | $H_{IP} = 20$ B, $H_{TCP} = 20$ B minimum |
| ICMP Rate Limit | $T_{scan} \geq \dfrac{N}{R_{limit}}$ | seconds | RFC 1812: $R_{limit} \approx 1$ pkt/s per host |
| Stealth Score | $S_{stealth} = 1 - \dfrac{\log(L_{syn}+1)}{\log(L_{xmas}+1)}$ | ratio | $L$ = log entries generated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize that the **SYN scan sends 1 packet and may receive 1 response per port** — total minimum packets = $2 \times 65536 = 131072$ for a full sweep. Always show this calculation in derivations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testing:** Nmap, Masscan, Zmap are de-facto industry tools.
- **Network Operations Centers (NOCs):** Continuous port monitoring detects rogue services.
- **Cloud Security:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender use port-scan patterns to detect compromise.
- **IoT Security:** Scanning embedded devices reveals exposed Telnet (23), HTTP (80) — primary IoT attack vector.
- **CTF Competitions:** KTU-affiliated hackathons require scan interpretation skills.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SYN Scan Detection Algorithm

The scanner's response interpretation follows a precise decision tree. We derive it step-by-step:

**Given:** A single packet $P_i$ sent to target port $n$.

$$
\text{Response}(n) = \begin{cases}
\text{SYN-ACK} & \Rightarrow \text{OPEN} \\
\text{RST-ACK} & \Rightarrow \text{CLOSED} \\
\text{ICMP Type 3 Code 1/2/3/9/10/13} & \Rightarrow \text{FILTERED} \\
\text{No Response (timeout)} & \Rightarrow \text{OPEN} \mid \text{FILTERED (ambiguous)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step Scanner Logic:**

1. **Initialization:** Set $S = \emptyset$ (open ports set), $C = \emptyset$ (closed), $F = \emptyset$ (filtered), $T_{timeout} = 1000 \text{ ms}$.

2. **For each port $n$ in range $[0, 65535]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Craft } P_i &= \{\text{TCP Header}, \text{Flags}=\text{SYN}, \text{seq}=\text{Isn}_{attacker}\} \\
\text{Transmit } P_i &\rightarrow \text{Target}:n \\
\text{Await Response} &\leq T_{timeout}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Response Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } R = \text{SYN-ACK: } & \quad S \leftarrow S \cup \{n\}; \quad \text{Send RST to terminate} \\
\text{If } R = \text{RST: } & \quad C \leftarrow C \cup \{n\} \\
\text{If } R = \text{ICMP Unreachable: } & \quad F \leftarrow F \cup \{n\} \\
\text{If } R = \text{None (timeout): } & \quad F \leftarrow F \cup \{n\} \text{ (conservative)}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Final Output Report:** $S$ is the attacker's target list for next-phase exploitation.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Scan Time for Full TCP Sweep

**Given:** Scanner bandwidth $B$ in packets/sec, total ports $N = 65536$, single-threaded.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time per probe} &= \dfrac{1}{B} \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Theoretical minimum scan time} &= \dfrac{N}{B} = \dfrac{65536}{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (typical broadband):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 10000 \text{ pps (packets per second)} \\
T_{min} &= \dfrac{65536}{10000} = 6.5536 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**With RTT latency $L = 50$ ms added per probe (round-trip wait):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= N \times \left(\dfrac{1}{B} + L\right) \\
&= 65536 \times (0.0001 + 0.05) \\
&= 65536 \times 0.0501 \\
&\approx 3283 \text{ seconds} \approx 54.7 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why **parallelization** and **asynchronous I/O** are essential in tools like *Masscan* (achieves 10M+ pps).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TCP vs UDP Scanning

| Parameter | TCP Scanning | UDP Scanning |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Setup** | Requires handshake (SYN-SYN/ACK-ACK) | Connectionless, no handshake |
| **State Detection** | Trivial via SYN-ACK vs RST | Difficult — relies on ICMP errors |
| **Open Port Indicator** | SYN-ACK response | No response OR UDP service reply |
| **Closed Port Indicator** | RST-ACK | ICMP Type 3 Code 3 |
| **Filtered Indicator** | No response / ICMP unreachable | No response / different ICMP code |
| **Speed** | Fast (stateful responses) | **Slow** (ICMP rate-limited) |
| **Accuracy** | High | Low to moderate |
| **Stealth** | Variable (SYN/FIN/Xmas are stealthy) | Naturally stealthy (no SYN flood) |
| **Logging Likelihood** | Moderate to High | Low |
| **Tool Default** | `nmap -sS` (SYN) | `nmap -sU` (UDP) |
| **Packet Count (Full Sweep)** | $65536 \times 2 = 131072$ packets | $65536 \times 1 = 65536$ packets (but slow due to timeouts) |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Sample Scan Output Interpretation

**Scenario:** Nmap SYN scan against `192.168.1.10` returns:

```
PORT     STATE   SERVICE
22/tcp   open    ssh
80/tcp   open    http
443/tcp  open    https
23/tcp   filtered telnet
3306/tcp closed  mysql
```

**Interpretation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Port 22/tcp — open:** Scanner sent SYN → received SYN-ACK → sent RST. SSH daemon detected.
2. **Port 80/tcp — open:** Apache/Nginx web server running. *High-value target*.
3. **Port 443/tcp — open:** HTTPS service. Requires TLS-version probing.
4. **Port 23/tcp — filtered:** No response received within 1s timeout. **Likely firewall rule** blocking Telnet (good security posture).
5. **Port 3306/tcp — closed:** Host is reachable (RST received) but MySQL not bound externally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a port is **filtered**, the scanner cannot distinguish between *closed by firewall* and *silently dropped* — this is **intentional ambiguity** that protects the network.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — TCP Three-Way Handshake Foundation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Attacker Client
    participant S as Target Server Port N
    Note over C,S: Normal TCP Connection Setup
    C->>S: SYN seq equals X
    S-->>C: SYN seq equals Y ACK equals X plus 1
    C->>S: ACK equals Y plus 1
    Note over C,S: Connection ESTABLISHED data transfer begins
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — TCP SYN Scan (Stealth / Half-Open) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Scanner Initiates SYN Scan]) --> SendSYN[Send TCP SYN to Port N]
    SendSYN --> AwaitResp{Await Response within Timeout}
    AwaitResp -->|SYN ACK Received| MarkOpen[Port N Marked OPEN]
    MarkOpen --> SendRST[Send RST to Abort Half Open Connection]
    SendRST --> NextPortA[Increment to Port N plus 1]
    AwaitResp -->|RST ACK Received| MarkClosed[Port N Marked CLOSED]
    MarkClosed --> NextPortA
    AwaitResp -->|ICMP Unreachable| MarkFiltered[Port N Marked FILTERED]
    MarkFiltered --> NextPortA
    AwaitResp -->|No Response Timeout| MarkFiltered2[Port N Marked FILTERED Conservative]
    MarkFiltered2 --> NextPortA
    NextPortA --> MorePorts{More Ports to Scan}
    MorePorts -->|Yes| SendSYN
    MorePorts -->|No| ReportGen[Generate Final Scan Report]
    ReportGen --> EndA([Scan Complete])
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — UDP Scan Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartB([Scanner Initiates UDP Scan]) --> CraftUDP[Craft Empty UDP Packet to Port N]
    CraftUDP --> SendUDP[Send UDP Packet to Target]
    SendUDP --> AwaitReply{Await Response within Timeout}
    AwaitReply -->|ICMP Type 3 Code 3 Port Unreachable| MarkClosedUDP[Port N Marked CLOSED]
    AwaitReply -->|ICMP Type 3 Other Code 1 2 9 10 13| MarkFilteredUDP[Port N Marked FILTERED]
    AwaitReply -->|UDP Service Response| MarkOpenUDP[Port N Marked OPEN]
    AwaitReply -->|No Response Timeout| MarkOpenFiltered[Port N Marked OPEN or FILTERED Ambiguous]
    MarkClosedUDP --> NextPortB[Increment Port]
    MarkFilteredUDP --> NextPortB
    MarkOpenUDP --> NextPortB
    MarkOpenFiltered --> NextPortB
    NextPortB --> ContinueUDP{More Ports}
    ContinueUDP -->|Yes| CraftUDP
    ContinueUDP -->|No| FinalReport[Generate UDP Scan Report with Ambiguity Markers]
    FinalReport --> EndB([UDP Scan Complete Slow and Unreliable])
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — TCP Scan Type Selection Matrix (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Goal[Scan Objective]
        G1[Service Enumeration]
        G2[Firewall Mapping]
        G3[OS Fingerprinting]
        G4[Stealth Recon]
    end
    subgraph TCPScans[TCP Scan Variants]
        S1[SYN Scan sS]
        S2[Connect Scan sT]
        S3[FIN Scan sF]
        S4[NULL Scan sN]
        S5[Xmas Scan sX]
        S6[ACK Scan sA]
        S7[Window Scan sW]
        S8[Maimon Scan sM]
    end
    G1 --> S1
    G1 --> S2
    G2 --> S6
    G2 --> S7
    G3 --> S3
    G3 --> S4
    G3 --> S5
    G4 --> S1
    G4 --> S3
    G4 --> S4
    G4 --> S5
    G4 --> S8
    S1 -.->|Half Open| O1[Default for Nmap]
    S2 -.->|Full Open| O2[No Raw Sockets Needed]
    S3 -.->|Stealth| O3[Linux BSD Only]
    S4 -.->|Stealth| O4[No Flags Set]
    S5 -.->|Stealth| O5[FIN plus PSH plus URG]
    S6 -.->|Mapping| O6[ACK Only]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block Level Functional Architecture of a Port Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[Target Specification IP Range Port List] --> Engine[Scanning Engine Core]
    Engine --> RawSocket[Raw Socket or System Connect API Layer]
    RawSocket --> PacketCraft[Packet Construction Module Flag Manipulation]
    PacketCraft --> Transmit[Network Transmission Subsystem]
    Transmit --> Capture[Response Capture and Sniffing Module]
    Capture --> Parser[Response Parser TCP Flag Classifier ICMP Decoder]
    Parser --> StateMachine[Port State Decision State Machine]
    StateMachine --> Database[In Memory State Database Open Closed Filtered]
    Database --> Timing[Timing and Rate Limiter Module]
    Timing --> Report[Report Generator Human Readable Output]
    Report --> Output[Final Scan Output]
    Database -.->|Feedback| Engine
    Timing -.->|Adaptive Delay| Transmit
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Port State Decision State Machine** is the analytical heart of any scanner — every packet response is fed through this FSM to determine the *final* port classification.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Port Scanning. List any four well-known ports and their services. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Port Scanning is a network reconnaissance technique used to probe a target host's TCP/UDP ports (0–65535) to identify which ports are open, closed, or filtered, thereby enumerating the running services and potential attack surface.

**Four Well-Known Ports (1.5 marks):**

| Port | Protocol | Service |
|---|---|---|
| 20/21 | TCP | FTP (File Transfer Protocol) |
| 22 | TCP | SSH (Secure Shell) |
| 23 | TCP | Telnet |
| 25 | TCP | SMTP (Mail) |
| 53 | TCP/UDP | DNS |
| 80 | TCP | HTTP |
| 443 | TCP | HTTPS |

### Question 2: Differentiate between TCP Connect() scan and TCP SYN scan. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | TCP Connect() Scan | TCP SYN Scan |
|---|---|---|
| **Handshake** | Full 3-way handshake completed | Half-open (SYN sent, RST after SYN-ACK) |
| **Stealth** | Easily logged by target app | Not fully logged (stealth) |
| **Privilege** | No raw socket / root required | Requires raw socket access (root/admin) |
| **Speed** | Slower (full connection overhead) | Faster (no full connection) |
| **Detection** | High likelihood | Low likelihood |
| **Command (Nmap)** | `-sT` | `-sS` |

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the TCP three-way handshake with a neat diagram. Discuss how a SYN scan leverages this handshake to perform stealth port scanning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TCP Three-Way Handshake (3 marks):**

The TCP three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection between a client and server before data transfer. It consists of three steps:

1. **SYN:** The client sends a TCP segment with the **SYN flag set** and an initial sequence number (e.g., seq = x) to the server, requesting synchronization.
2. **SYN-ACK:** The server responds with the **SYN and ACK flags set**, acknowledging the client's sequence number (ack = x+1) and providing its own initial sequence number (seq = y).
3. **ACK:** The client sends a final **ACK** segment (ack = y+1) to confirm. The connection is now **ESTABLISHED**, and data transfer can begin.

This handshake is foundational to all reliable TCP communications.

**SYN Scan Stealth Mechanism (4 marks):**

A SYN scan (also called *half-open* or *stealth scan*) deliberately **incompletes the handshake**:

- **Step 1:** Scanner sends **SYN** to target port.
- **Step 2:** If port is OPEN, target replies **SYN-ACK**.
- **Step 3 (Modified):** Instead of completing the handshake with a final ACK, the scanner sends an **RST (Reset)** to gracefully abort the half-open connection.

**Why it is "stealth":**
- The TCP connection is **never fully established** at the application layer.
- Most application-level logs (e.g., Apache access logs) only record **completed** connections — so SYN scans leave **minimal forensic evidence**.
- However, **stateful firewalls and IDS** (like Snort, Suricata) can still detect SYN flood patterns or large volumes of SYN-RST exchanges.

**Diagram (integrated in explanation, 0 marks reserved):**

```
Attacker         Target (Port 22)
   |   SYN --------------->   |   (Open Port)
   |   <----- SYN-ACK -----   |
   |   RST ---------------->   |   (Abort half-open)
```

---

**(b)** What is UDP scanning? Explain its challenges and the response interpretation logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**UDP Scanning Definition (2 marks):**

UDP scanning is a port scanning technique that probes the **connectionless UDP protocol** to determine port states. Unlike TCP, UDP has no handshake, no session, and no inherent acknowledgement mechanism. The scanner sends a UDP packet (typically empty, or a protocol-specific probe) to each target port and interprets the response (or lack thereof) to classify the port state.

**Challenges of UDP Scanning (3 marks):**

1. **No Handshake:** UDP is fire-and-forget; there is no SYN-ACK equivalent. Determining an "open" port requires inferring from *absence* of response, which is ambiguous.

2. **ICMP Rate Limiting (RFC 1812):** Most operating systems and firewalls **rate-limit ICMP Port Unreachable** messages (typically 1 per second). A full 65536-port UDP scan can take **over 18 hours** at this rate.

3. **Silent Open Ports:** Many UDP services (e.g., SNMP on 161) may not respond to empty/probe packets, making open ports appear identical to filtered ports.

4. **Firewall Asymmetry:** Different firewalls return different ICMP codes (Type 3, Code 1, 2, 3, 9, 10, 13) for filtered traffic, complicating automated classification.

5. **Bandwidth Inefficiency:** Each scan requires a long **timeout** (often 3+ seconds) to detect "open|filtered" states — making UDP scans the slowest scan type.

**Response Interpretation Logic (2 marks):**

| Response | Port State |
|---|---|
| **ICMP Type 3, Code 3 (Port Unreachable)** | CLOSED |
| **ICMP Type 3, Other codes (1, 2, 9, 10, 13)** | FILTERED |
| **UDP response from service** | OPEN |
| **No response (timeout)** | OPEN \| FILTERED (ambiguous) |

**Conclusion:** UDP scanning is **inherently slower and less reliable** than TCP scanning, requiring protocol-specific probes (e.g., SNMP get-request for port 161) to improve accuracy.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast NULL scan, FIN scan, and Xmas scan. Explain the underlying principle that makes them effective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Common Underlying Principle (2 marks):**

All three scans exploit **RFC 793's behavior for "non-SYN" packets** sent to a CLOSED port. According to RFC 793, if a closed port receives a packet that does **not have the RST flag set**, the host **must respond with an RST**. Conversely, an **OPEN** port should simply **discard** such packets (no response).

This allows the scanner to differentiate open from closed ports without completing a handshake — providing **stealth** against basic stateless firewalls that only filter SYN packets.

**NULL Scan (1.5 marks):**
- **Flags sent:** **None** (all six flags are 0).
- **Open port response:** No response (discarded).
- **Closed port response:** RST.
- **Effectiveness:** Limited to RFC 793-compliant systems (Linux, BSD, macOS). **Windows** responds with RST to all probes, defeating this scan.

**FIN Scan (1.5 marks):**
- **Flags sent:** **FIN** flag set only.
- **Open port response:** No response.
- **Closed port response:** RST.
- **Effectiveness:** Same OS dependency as NULL. Effective at bypassing simple SYN-only filters.

**Xmas Scan (1.5 marks):**
- **Flags sent:** **FIN + PSH + URG** (all three set — "lit up like a Christmas tree").
- **Open port response:** No response.
- **Closed port response:** RST.
- **Effectiveness:** Same OS dependency. Visually distinctive in packet captures (hence "Xmas").

**Comparative Table (0.5 marks):**

| Parameter | NULL | FIN | Xmas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flags Set | None | FIN | FIN+PSH+URG |
| OS Dependency | RFC 793 systems | RFC 793 systems | RFC 793 systems |
| Stealth | High | High | High |

---

**(b)** Describe the ACK scan and explain why it is used for firewall rule mapping rather than port state determination. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ACK Scan Mechanics (3 marks):**

The ACK scan sends a TCP segment with **only the ACK flag set** to the target port. Because the packet has the ACK flag but does not correspond to any existing connection, the target's response depends on the presence/absence of firewall filtering — not on the port's open/closed state.

**Response Interpretation (2 marks):**

| Response | Classification | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **RST received** | **Unfiltered** | The packet reached the host's TCP stack; no firewall block |
| **ICMP Unreachable (Type 3)** | **Filtered** | A firewall rule dropped the packet |
| **No response (timeout)** | **Filtered (probable)** | Packet silently dropped |

**Why ACK Scan Cannot Determine Port State (2 marks):**

- An **RST response** is sent by the OS kernel **regardless of whether the port is open or closed** — because the ACK packet is not part of any established session.
- Therefore, both *open* and *closed* ports behave identically, and the scan **cannot distinguish between them**.
- The scan's only utility is to determine whether a **firewall rule is present** (filtered) or absent (unfiltered) on the path to the host.

**Practical Use Cases:**
- **Firewall rule enumeration** — discovering which ports are ACL-protected.
- **Stateful vs. stateless detection** — stateful firewalls track connection state and may drop unsolicited ACK packets, while stateless firewalls may let them through.
- **Pre-scan reconnaissance** — used as a precursor to more targeted TCP scans to avoid noisy full-handshake attempts on filtered ports.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing open/closed behavior in ACK scan (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly state that RST = open and no response = closed. This is **WRONG** for ACK scan. RST means *unfiltered* (host reached), not *open*.
> 2. **Forgetting OS dependency for NULL/FIN/Xmas (–1.5 marks):** Examiners expect students to mention that these scans fail on Windows (which always sends RST).
> 3. **Not mentioning RFC 793 (–1 mark):** The theoretical basis (RFC 793 discard rule) is a high-value point frequently tested.
> 4. **Omitting ICMP Type 3 Code 3 in UDP (–1 mark):** Always specify the exact ICMP type/code — not just "ICMP unreachable."
> 5. **Confusing SYN scan with full Connect scan (–2 marks):** Emphasize that SYN scan sends **RST** (not ACK) as the final step.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in handshake questions (–1.5 marks):** KTU mandates a diagram for 7-mark TCP questions — never skip it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Port range = **0 to 65535**; total = **2¹⁶ = 65536**; TCP and UDP spaces are **independent**.
- ✅ Port categories: **Well-known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49151), Dynamic (49152–65535)**.
- ✅ TCP flags mnemonic: **U-A-P-R-S-F** = URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN.
- ✅ **SYN scan (stealth)** sends SYN → if SYN-ACK → sends **RST** (does NOT complete handshake).
- ✅ **Connect() scan** completes the full 3-way handshake — **noisy but no root needed**.
- ✅ **NULL/FIN/Xmas** scans rely on **RFC 793 discard rule** — fail on **Windows**; work on **Linux/BSD**.
- ✅ **Xmas scan flags = FIN + PSH + URG** (literally "lit up like a Christmas tree").
- ✅ **ACK scan** cannot determine port state — only **firewall filtering** (filtered vs. unfiltered).
- ✅ **UDP scanning** is slow, unreliable, and **ICMP rate-limited** (RFC 1812).
- ✅ UDP **closed port indicator** = **ICMP Type 3, Code 3 (Port Unreachable)**.
- ✅ UDP **open|filtered** is **ambiguous** — no response OR UDP reply = open; no response OR different ICMP = filtered.
- ✅ Tool of choice: **Nmap** — default SYN scan via `-sS`; UDP via `-sU`.
- ✅ **Stealth score hierarchy (most → least):** Xmas ≈ NULL ≈ FIN > SYN > Connect.
- ✅ **Full TCP sweep** requires minimum **131,072 packets** (2 × 65536).
- ✅ **Scan time formula:** $T = N \times \left(\dfrac{1}{B} + L\right)$ where $N$ = ports, $B$ = pps, $L$ = RTT.
- ✅ Three-way handshake: **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**; sequence numbers use $x, y, x+1, y+1$.
- ✅ **Legal/Ethical reminder:** Unauthorized port scanning is illegal under **IT Act 2000 (India) §43 & §66** — always obtain **written authorization** before penetration testing.
- ✅ **Defense:** Use **stateful firewalls, IDS/IPS, port scan detectors** (e.g., PortSentry, Fail2Ban) to block scanners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Port Scanning Tools: Nmap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Port Scanning Tools: Nmap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: PORT SCANNING TOOLS — NMAP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Nmap (Network Mapper)** is an open-source, free, command-line network scanning utility designed for **network discovery, port enumeration, service/version detection, and OS fingerprinting**. Originally authored by **Gordon Lyon (Fyodor)** in 1997, it is the de-facto industry-standard reconnaissance tool used in ethical hacking, penetration testing, and network auditing as per KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCST604, Module 3).

It functions by transmitting specially crafted network packets to a target host and analyzing the responses to deduce active services, open/closed/filtered ports, and underlying operating systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights):**
> - **Port:** A 16-bit logical address (0–65535) used by transport-layer protocols (TCP/UDP) to identify a specific service/process.
> - **Port Scanning:** The systematic probing of a target's ports to determine which are open, closed, or filtered.
> - **Reconnaissance (Recon):** The pre-attack phase of gathering information about a target system.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy: "The Building Inspector"** 🏢
> Imagine you are a **building inspector** walking around a large office complex. You knock on every door (port) on every floor (host). Based on the response:
> - **Open Door (Open Port):** Someone answers — a service is running (e.g., web server on port 80).
> - **Locked Door (Closed Port):** You hear "go away" (RST packet) — nothing is running.
> - **Silent Door (Filtered Port):** No response at all — a firewall is blocking.
>
> Nmap is the **digital equivalent of this inspector**, knocking on thousands of network doors in seconds to produce a complete "map" of the network.

---

### 1.3 Why Nmap Matters in Cyber Security

- **Network Inventory:** Identify all live hosts and running services.
- **Vulnerability Assessment:** Detect outdated services with known CVEs.
- **Penetration Testing:** First step in the **reconnaissance phase**.
- **Compliance Auditing:** Validate firewall and IDS rule effectiveness.
- **Forensic Investigations:** Map attacker entry points during incident response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Nmap:**
> - **RTT (Round-Trip Time):** Default unit is **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Port Range:** 0 to **65535** (2¹⁶ - 1).
> - **Scan Speed:** Default timing template is **T3 (Normal)**.
> - **TCP Flags Used:** SYN, ACK, FIN, PSH, URG, RST — collectively **6 bits**.

---

### 1.4 Nmap Architecture at a Glance

Nmap is composed of modular subsystems that work together:

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Host Discovery** | Determines which IPs are alive (ping scan). |
| **Port Scanning** | Enumerates open/closed/filtered ports. |
| **Service Detection (-sV)** | Probes open ports to identify application/version. |
| **OS Fingerprinting (-O)** | Guesses the target OS via TCP/IP stack analysis. |
| **NSE (Nmap Scripting Engine)** | Lua-based scripts for advanced automation. |
| **Output Engine** | Generates human and machine-readable reports. |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP Three-Way Handshake — Foundation of Port Scanning

> [!NOTE]
> Port scanning techniques are derived directly from the **TCP three-way handshake**. A thorough understanding of this handshake is **mandatory** for answering KTU Module 3 questions.

The handshake is the formal process by which a TCP connection is established before data transfer.

### 2.1.1 The Three Steps

- **Step 1 — SYN:** The client sends a packet with the **SYN (Synchronize)** flag set to the server.
- **Step 2 — SYN-ACK:** The server responds with **SYN + ACK** flags set, acknowledging the request.
- **Step 3 — ACK:** The client sends a final **ACK** packet, completing the connection.

This sequence guarantees that both sides are ready to exchange data reliably.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Nmap Scan Types

Nmap supports **multiple scan techniques**, each exploiting a different aspect of the TCP/IP protocol suite. The following are the most frequently examined in KTU.

| # | Scan Type | Command Flag | Packet Sent | Response on Open Port | Response on Closed Port | Stealth Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TCP SYN Scan (Half-Open)** | `-sS` | SYN | SYN/ACK | RST | High |
| 2 | **TCP Connect Scan** | `-sT` | Full handshake via OS | SYN/ACK | RST | Low |
| 3 | **TCP ACK Scan** | `-sA` | ACK | RST (filtered?) | RST | Medium |
| 4 | **TCP FIN Scan** | `-sF` | FIN | No response | RST | High |
| 5 | **TCP NULL Scan** | `-sN` | No flags | No response | RST | High |
| 6 | **TCP XMAS Scan** | `-sX` | FIN+PSH+URG | No response | RST | High |
| 7 | **UDP Scan** | `-sU` | UDP datagram | UDP response | ICMP Port Unreachable | Medium |
| 8 | **Ping Scan** | `-sn` | ICMP Echo | Echo reply | No reply | N/A |
