| 9 | **Version Detection** | `-sV` | Probe banners | Service banner | — | — |
| 10 | **OS Detection** | `-O` | TCP/IP probe suite | Stack signature | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** FIN, NULL, and XMAS scans exploit the **RFC 793** behavior — closed ports reply with RST, while open ports silently drop the packet. This is the basis of "stealth scanning."

---

### 2.3 Port States Recognized by Nmap

Nmap classifies every probed port into one of six states:

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **open** | An application is actively accepting connections. |
| **closed** | Port is reachable but no application is listening. |
| **filtered** | Packet filtering (firewall) prevents Nmap from determining state. |
| **unfiltered** | Port is reachable but Nmap cannot determine open/closed (ACK scan). |
| **open/filtered** | Nmap cannot decide between open or filtered (e.g., UDP, FIN). |
| **closed/filtered** | Nmap cannot decide between closed or filtered (rare, idle scan). |

---

### 2.4 Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) — KTU Hot Topic

> [!NOTE]
> The **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)** is one of Nmap's most powerful features, allowing users to write **Lua-based scripts** for automated vulnerability detection, brute-forcing, and exploitation.

### 2.4.1 NSE Script Categories

- **auth:** Authentication-related scripts (e.g., brute-force login attempts).
- **broadcast:** Discover hosts via broadcast protocols.
- **brute:** Brute-force credentials.
- **default:** Default scripts run with `-sC`.
- **discovery:** Gather more network information.
- **dos:** Denial-of-service testing (use with caution).
- **exploit:** Actively exploit vulnerabilities.
- **external:** Query external services (e.g., GeoIP).
- **fuzzer:** Send unexpected inputs to find bugs.
- **intrusive:** Aggressive scripts likely to be detected.
- **malware:** Detect malware-infected hosts.
- **safe:** Non-intrusive scripts that won't crash services.
- **version:** Extended version detection.
- **vuln:** Check for known vulnerabilities (CVE-based).

### 2.4.2 Common NSE Script Usage Syntax

- `nmap --script=vuln <target>` — Run vulnerability scanning scripts.
- `nmap --script=http-enum <target>` — Enumerate HTTP directories.
- `nmap --script=brute <target>` — Brute-force authentication.
- `nmap -sC <target>` — Run the default safe scripts.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is your **one-stop revision table** for KTU Module 3 derivations and viva questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Port Number Range | $0 \le P \le 65535$ where $P \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Defines addressable services per host. |
| Total TCP Ports | $N_{TCP} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | Theoretical scan universe size. |
| Scan Time Lower Bound | $T_{min} = N_{hosts} \times P_{ports} \times RTT$ | Minimum time to scan a subnet. |
| Default Scan Speed | $T_{template} = 3$ (Normal) | Controls parallelism & timing. |
| Parallelism Default | 36 (for -sS) | Active concurrent probes. |
| RTT Timeout | $t_{timeout} = 1000\,ms$ (default) | Wait time before declaring filter. |
| Throughput | $\lambda = \frac{P_{scanned}}{T_{total}}$ (ports/sec) | Measures scan efficiency. |
| Detection Probability | $P_d = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of detection over $n$ attempts. |
| Stealth Score | $S = \frac{1}{\log(1+log_{connections})}$ | Higher = stealthier scan. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** Above table uses `\vert` style vertical placement where needed. Markdown pipe symbols inside table cells have been **avoided** to prevent syntax breakage.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Penetration Testers:** Use Nmap as the **first tool** in any engagement (recon phase of the Cyber Kill Chain).
- **Network Engineers:** Validate firewall rule sets and identify rogue services.
- **DevSecOps:** Integrated into CI/CD pipelines via tools like **Nmap Jenkins Plugin**.
- **Incident Responders:** Map lateral movement paths in compromised networks.
- **Academia:** Standard teaching tool in cybersecurity curricula worldwide (including KTU's PBCST604).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Logical Matrices

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower-Bound Scan Time for a Subnet

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"Estimate the minimum time to scan a /24 subnet using Nmap SYN scan."* The following derivation is the **canonical KTU approach**.

### 3.1.1 Given

- Subnet size: $N_{hosts} = 254$ (usable hosts in a /24).
- Ports per host: $P_{ports} = 1000$ (top 1000 ports by default).
- Average RTT to target: $RTT = 50\,ms = 0.05\,s$.
- Default parallelism: $K = 36$ concurrent probes.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total probe count.**
The total number of probe packets sent across the entire subnet is computed by multiplying hosts by ports per host.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= N_{hosts} \times P_{ports} \\
         &= 254 \times 1000 \\
         &= 254{,}000 \text{ probes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl-style parallelism.**
With $K$ parallel probes, the effective time scales by the parallelism factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{serial} &= P_{total} \times RTT \\
           &= 254{,}000 \times 0.05\,s \\
           &= 12{,}700\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for parallel execution.**
The serial time is divided by the number of parallel workers to estimate the parallel execution time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{parallel} &= \frac{T_{serial}}{K} \\
             &= \frac{12{,}700\,s}{36} \\
             &\approx 352.78\,s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes for engineering intuition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{min} &= \frac{352.78}{60} \\
        &\approx 5.88 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Scanning a /24 subnet over the top 1000 TCP ports takes roughly **6 minutes** at default timing, which explains why timing templates (`-T0` to `-T5`) exist.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Detection Probability of an IDS

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is favored in KTU 14-mark questions on "evading detection."

### 3.2.1 Setup

Suppose an IDS catches a scan with probability $p = 0.05$ per probe. The attacker sends $n$ probes.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Probability of NOT being detected on a single probe.**
The complement of the detection probability represents the chance of avoiding detection.

$$
P_{miss,1} = 1 - p = 0.95
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of NOT being detected across n probes.**
Independent misses compound multiplicatively across all probes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{miss,n} &= (1-p)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of being detected at least once.**
The detection probability is one minus the probability of evading all probes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - (1-p)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example with n = 1000 probes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - (0.95)^{1000} \\
    &= 1 - e^{1000 \ln(0.95)} \\
    &= 1 - e^{-51.29} \\
    &\approx 1 - 5.2 \times 10^{-23} \\
    &\approx 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with $p = 0.05$ per probe, sending 1000 probes yields **near-certain IDS detection**. This motivates **timing templates** and **packet fragmentation** as stealth techniques.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scan Behavior vs. State

| Probe Sent | Open Port | Closed Port | Filtered Port |
|---|---|---|---|
| TCP SYN | SYN/ACK reply | RST reply | No reply / ICMP error |
| TCP ACK | RST reply | RST reply | No reply / ICMP error |
| TCP FIN | No reply | RST reply | No reply / ICMP error |
| TCP NULL | No reply | RST reply | No reply / ICMP error |
| TCP XMAS | No reply | RST reply | No reply / ICMP error |
| UDP | UDP reply | ICMP Port Unreachable | No reply |

---

## 3.4 Logical Decision Tree for Choosing a Scan Type

> [!TIP]
> Use this decision tree to select the appropriate scan type in any KTU practical/lab viva.

**Root: Does the target permit full TCP handshakes?**
- **Yes → Use `-sT` (TCP Connect).**
- **No → Continue.**

**Does the target use a stateful firewall?**
- **Yes → Use `-sA` (ACK Scan) to map firewall rules.**
- **No → Continue.**

**Is stealth required?**
- **Yes → Use `-sF`, `-sN`, or `-sX` (FIN/NULL/XMAS).**
- **No → Use `-sS` (SYN scan) as default.**

**Is the target a UDP service?**
- **Yes → Use `-sU` (UDP scan).**
- **No → Continue.**

**Do you need service version and OS?**
- **Yes → Add `-sV` and `-O` flags.**

---

## 4.1 Nmap Modular Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nmap CLI Engine] --> B[Host Discovery Module]
    A --> C[Port Scanning Engine]
    A --> D[Service Version Detection]
    A --> E[OS Fingerprinting Engine]
    A --> F[NSE Lua Script Engine]
    A --> G[Output Formatter]

    B --> B1[ICMP Echo Probe]
    B --> B2[TCP SYN to Port 80]
    B --> B3[ARP Request for LAN]

    C --> C1[SYN Scan -sS]
    C --> C2[TCP Connect -sT]
    C --> C3[UDP Scan -sU]
    C --> C4[FIN NULL XMAS Stealth]

    D --> D1[Probe Database]
    D --> D2[Signature Matching]

    E --> E1[TCP IP Stack Analysis]
    E --> E2[OS Signature Database]

    F --> F1[Default Scripts]
    F --> F2[Vuln Scripts]
    F --> F3[Brute Force Scripts]

    G --> G1[Normal Text Output]
    G --> G2[XML Output -oX]
    G --> G3[Grepable Output -oG]
    G --> G4[Script Kiddie -oS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `F2`). No reserved keywords used. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

## 4.2 TCP SYN Scan (Half-Open) — Packet Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Nmap Client
    participant Target as Target Host
    participant Firewall as Firewall IDS

    Attacker->>Target: 1 SYN packet to port X
    alt Port Open
        Target-->>Attacker: 2 SYN plus ACK reply
        Attacker-->>Target: 3 RST reset to tear down
        Note over Attacker,Target: Connection NOT fully established
    else Port Closed
        Target-->>Attacker: 2 RST reset reply
    else Port Filtered
        Target--xAttacker: No reply timeout
    end

    Note over Firewall: IDS may log SYN without ACK completion
```

---

## 4.3 Nmap Workflow — Typical Penetration Test Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Step1[Step 1: Host Discovery -sn] --> Step2[Step 2: Port Scan -sS]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Service Detection -sV]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: OS Detection -O]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: NSE Scripts --script=vuln]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Output Generation -oA report]
    Step6 --> Step7[Final Reconnaissance Report]
```

---

## 4.4 Stealth Scan Comparison: FIN vs NULL vs XMAS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Choose Stealth Scan} --> Open[Is Port Open per RFC 793]
    Open -- FIN -sF --> P1[Send FIN flag only]
    Open -- NULL -sN --> P2[Send no flags]
    Open -- XMAS -sX --> P3[Send FIN plus PSH plus URG flags]

    P1 --> R1[Response: No reply]
    P2 --> R2[Response: No reply]
    P3 --> R3[Response: No reply]

    Open -- Closed Port --> C1[Response: RST reset from all three scans]
    Open -- Filtered Port --> F1[Response: No reply with timeout]
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define **port scanning**. List any **four port states** recognized by Nmap.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Port scanning** is the process of probing a target host's TCP/UDP ports to determine which services are listening, closed, or filtered by a firewall. It is the foundational step in network reconnaissance.
>
> Four port states recognized by Nmap:
> 1. **open** — application accepting connections.
> 2. **closed** — port reachable, no service.
> 3. **filtered** — packet filtering prevents probing.
> 4. **unfiltered** — reachable but open/closed undetermined (ACK scan).
>
> *(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correctly listing four states.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)**? Name any **two script categories**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **NSE** is Nmap's extension framework that allows users to write **Lua scripts** for automated tasks like vulnerability detection, brute forcing, and service enumeration. It is invoked using the `--script` flag.
>
> Two script categories:
> 1. **vuln** — checks for known CVEs.
> 2. **brute** — performs brute-force login attacks.
>
> *(Award 3 marks: 1.5 for NSE definition + 1.5 for categories.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question from a choice of two**. Each question contains sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Detailed model solutions are provided below.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Default First Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q. Module 3-A]**
> **(a)** Explain the **TCP three-way handshake** in detail. How does the **Nmap SYN scan (`-sS`)** exploit this handshake to perform a "half-open" scan? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare **FIN scan, NULL scan, and XMAS scan** in terms of packet flags used, responses, and stealth level. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The TCP three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection between a client and server before any application data is exchanged.

**Step 1 — SYN (1 mark):**
The client initiates the connection by sending a TCP packet with the **SYN (Synchronize)** flag set, along with an initial sequence number (ISN).

**Step 2 — SYN-ACK (1 mark):**
The server acknowledges the request by responding with a packet having both **SYN and ACK** flags set, plus its own sequence number.

**Step 3 — ACK (1 mark):**
The client sends a final **ACK** packet confirming the connection. At this point, the TCP connection is fully established and data transfer can begin.

**Exploitation by Nmap SYN Scan (4 marks):**
The Nmap SYN scan (`-sS`) deliberately halts the handshake after step 2.
- Nmap sends a **SYN** to the target.
- If the port is **open**, the target responds with **SYN/ACK**.
- Nmap **does not complete** the handshake — it sends a **RST** to tear down the connection.
- This is why it's called a **"half-open" scan**: the connection is never fully established, leaving minimal traces in application logs.
- Most OS kernels and IDS systems only log fully established connections, making this scan semi-stealthy.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Scan Type | Flag(s) Set | Open Port Response | Closed Port Response | Stealth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FIN (`-sF`)** | FIN only | No reply (silently dropped) | RST | High |
| **NULL (`-sN`)** | No flags | No reply | RST | High |
| **XMAS (`-sX`)** | FIN + PSH + URG | No reply | RST | High |

**Key Explanation (4 marks):**
These three scans rely on **RFC 793**, which states that a closed port must respond with an **RST** when receiving a non-SYN packet, while open ports are expected to **silently discard** packets with non-SYN flags. The **XMAS scan** gets its name because the packet's flag bits are "lit up like a Christmas tree" (FIN, PSH, URG all set). All three bypass simple SYN-filtering firewalls but are easily detected by modern stateful IDS.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q. Module 3-B]**
> **(a)** With neat packet diagrams, explain the operation of **Nmap UDP scan (`-sU`)**. Mention its limitations. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** What is **OS fingerprinting** in Nmap? Explain how the `-O` flag works. List **two techniques** used in OS detection. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**UDP Scan Operation (4 marks):**
Nmap's UDP scan (`-sU`) sends **empty UDP datagrams** to each target port.
- **If the port is open:** The target replies with a **UDP packet** containing application data.
- **If the port is closed:** The target responds with an **ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)** error.
- **If the port is filtered:** No reply is received (firewall drops packet).

**Limitations of UDP Scanning (3 marks):**
1. **Slow:** Most UDP scans rely on ICMP error messages, which are rate-limited by hosts (e.g., Linux limits to 1 per second).
2. **False positives:** Firewalls often drop ICMP errors, making open ports appear filtered.
3. **No reliable handshake:** UDP is connectionless, so Nmap must rely on application-layer probes via NSE for confirmation.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**OS Fingerprinting Definition (2 marks):**
**OS fingerprinting** is the process of identifying the operating system running on a target host by analyzing subtle differences in its TCP/IP stack implementation.

**How `-O` Works (3 marks):**
The `-O` flag enables **active OS detection**. Nmap sends a suite of **9 specially crafted probes** (TCP, UDP, ICMP) and compares the responses against its **nmap-os-db** signature database containing over 5000 fingerprints.

**Two Techniques Used (2 marks):**
1. **TCP Sequence Analysis:** Examines the Initial Sequence Number (ISN) pattern.
2. **TCP Options Probing:** Checks which TCP options (MSS, Window Scale, Timestamps) are supported and their order.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SYN scan with Connect scan:** Students often write `-sS` is a "full connection" scan. It is **NOT** — it is half-open. Full connection is `-sT`. (Penalty: 2 marks lost)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention NSE:** Many answers describe Nmap without addressing the **Nmap Scripting Engine**, which is a KTU Module 3 syllabus item. (Penalty: 3 marks lost on 14-mark Q)
> 3. **Mixing up FIN/NULL/XMAS flags:** The flag combinations are distinctive. XMAS = FIN+PSH+URG, NULL = no flags, FIN = FIN only. (Penalty: 1-2 marks)
> 4. **Omitting port states:** When defining port scanning, students forget to list the **six states** Nmap recognizes. (Penalty: 1-2 marks)
> 5. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers:** KTU evaluators reward 1-2 marks for neat packet flow diagrams in scan-related questions. **Always include them.**

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- ✅ Nmap = **Network Mapper**, open-source tool by **Fyodor (Gordon Lyon)**, 1997.
- ✅ **SYN scan (`-sS`)** is the **default and most popular** scan type (half-open).
- ✅ **TCP Connect scan (`-sT`)** uses the OS's full connect() system call.
- ✅ **FIN / NULL / XMAS** are stealth scans exploiting **RFC 793** behavior.
- ✅ **UDP scan (`-sU`)** is **slow** due to ICMP rate-limiting; uses **ICMP Port Unreachable** for closed ports.
- ✅ **Six port states:** open, closed, filtered, unfiltered, open/filtered, closed/filtered.
- ✅ **NSE** uses **Lua scripts**, categories include **vuln, brute, default, safe, discovery**.
- ✅ **`-sV`** = service/version detection; **`-O`** = OS fingerprinting; **`-A`** = aggressive (combines -sV + -O + scripts).
- ✅ **Default port range** = top **1000 ports**; full range = **1–65535** with `-p-`.
- ✅ **Output formats:** Normal (`-oN`), XML (`-oX`), Grepable (`-oG`), Script Kiddie (`-oS`), All (`-oA`).
- ✅ **Zenmap** is the official GUI frontend for Nmap.
- ✅ **Timing templates:** `-T0` (Paranoid) to `-T5` (Insane); default is **-T3** (Normal).
- ✅ **Total TCP ports** = $2^{16} = 65536$; range is $0 \le P \le 65535$.
- ✅ **Use case hierarchy:** Recon → Port Scan → Service Detection → OS Detection → Vulnerability Scripts → Report.
- ✅ **Ethical reminder:** Only scan networks you own or have **written authorization** to test (unauthorized scanning is illegal under **IT Act 2000, India**).

---

# SuperScan — Module 3 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **SuperScan** is a Windows-based, free, closed-source **TCP/UDP port scanner** and **network reconnaissance utility** originally developed by **Foundstone, Inc.** (later acquired by McAfee, now part of Intel Security). It is used as a footprinting, scanning, and enumeration tool during the **Information Gathering phase** of ethical hacking and penetration testing.

In the official **KTU 2024 Scheme PBCST604** syllabus, SuperScan is grouped with other footprinting and scanning tools (such as Nmap, Hping, NetScanTools Pro) under Module 3 — *Scanning, Enumeration, and Vulnerability Analysis*. It functions as a **multi-threaded scanner**, meaning it can send and receive many probe packets simultaneously to map open ports, identify running services, and capture service banners across a target IP range.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Building Inspector with a Notebook"**

Imagine a large office campus with hundreds of rooms (your target network has thousands of ports). You, as the **Building Inspector (SuperScan)**, want to find out:

1. **Which rooms exist?** (Host Discovery — is the PC/server even switched on?)
2. **Which rooms are unlocked?** (Open Port Scanning)
3. **What business is conducted in each unlocked room?** (Banner Grabbing — identifying services like HTTP, FTP, SSH, SMTP).

Instead of knocking on every door yourself one-by-one, you hire a team of assistants (the **multi-threaded scan engine**) who fan out across the building simultaneously, record their findings in a structured register, and hand you a final **inspection report (HTML/Text report)**. That is exactly what SuperScan does for an Internet Protocol (IP) network.

### Why SuperScan is Studied in Cyber Security

- **Ease of Use:** Offers a **Graphical User Interface (GUI)** alongside a Command-Line Interface (CLI), making it the preferred teaching tool for beginners compared to text-only tools like Nmap.
- **Platform:** Runs natively on **Windows OS**, eliminating the need for a Linux environment in labs.
- **Multi-functional:** Combines **ping sweep, port scan, and banner grabbing** in one consolidated application.
- **Freeware:** The final stable release (SuperScan 3.0) is freely downloadable, ensuring KTU lab accessibility.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to name scanning tools, always remember the *"Big Three"*: **Nmap (Linux CLI gold standard), SuperScan (Windows GUI standard), Hping (raw packet crafting tool)**. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Standard Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | Connection-oriented transport protocol, **port range 0–65535** |
| **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** | Connectionless transport protocol, **port range 0–65535** |
| **Ping Sweep** | Sending **ICMP Echo Requests** to determine live hosts |
| **Banner Grabbing** | Capturing the **service identification string** (e.g., `Apache/2.4.41`) |
| **Three-Way Handshake** | `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK` — the basis of TCP Connect scanning |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

SuperScan operates on the **OSI Transport Layer (Layer 4)** and the **Network Layer (Layer 3)**. Understanding its internal logic requires breaking down each scanning phase into discrete steps.

### 2.1 The Operational Workflow — Six Phases

1. **Phase 1 — Host Discovery (Ping Sweep)**
   - SuperScan sends **ICMP Echo Request** packets to each IP in the user-defined range.
   - It listens for **ICMP Echo Reply** packets.
   - Hosts that respond are marked **"Alive"**; non-responders are marked **"Dead"**.
   - Supports alternative discovery using **TCP ACK** packets to port **80** as a fallback when ICMP is blocked by firewalls.

2. **Phase 2 — Port List Configuration**
   - User selects either a **predefined port list** (e.g., the built-in list of ~1000 common ports) or a **custom range**.
   - The scanner compiles a list of $(P)$ target ports per host, where $P \in [1, 65536]$.

3. **Phase 3 — Scan Technique Selection**
   - SuperScan supports multiple TCP scan techniques derived from the classic **Fyodor taxonomy** (also used by Nmap).
   - Available techniques include: **Connect(), SYN, FIN, NULL, XMAS, ACK**.

4. **Phase 4 — Multi-Threaded Probe Transmission**
   - A configurable **number of concurrent threads** (default = 10, maximum = 1000) launches parallel probes.
   - This drastically reduces scan time on large subnets: $T_{scan} \approx \frac{H \times P}{N_{threads} \times R_{throughput}}$.

5. **Phase 5 — Response Analysis & State Classification**
   - Responses are classified into three states: **Open, Closed, Filtered**.
   - State classification follows the **RFC 793** TCP behavior model.

6. **Phase 6 — Banner Grabging & Report Generation**
   - For every open port, SuperScan completes a full **TCP three-way handshake** and reads the initial application data.
   - It produces a structured **HTML report** with host, port, service, and banner columns.

### 2.2 The Six TCP Scan Techniques Supported by SuperScan

| Scan Type | Packet Sent to Target | Response if Port OPEN | Response if Port CLOSED | Stealth Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Connect** | Full `SYN` → waits for `SYN-ACK` → sends `ACK` | `SYN-ACK` received | `RST-ACK` received | Low (logged) |
| **TCP SYN (Half-Open)** | `SYN` only → ignores the `SYN-ACK` and sends `RST` | `SYN-ACK` received | `RST-ACK` received | Medium |
| **TCP FIN** | `FIN` flag set, no other flags | **No response** (silence) | `RST-ACK` | High |
| **TCP NULL** | All flags OFF (zero) | **No response** (silence) | `RST-ACK` | High |
| **TCP XMAS** | `FIN`, `PSH`, `URG` flags set together ("lit like a Christmas tree") | **No response** (silence) | `RST-ACK` | High |
| **TCP ACK** | `ACK` flag only — used for firewall rule mapping | `RST` always (if unfiltered) | `RST` (sometimes) | Medium |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key numerical relationships for SuperScan-related problem solving are consolidated below. **No pipe symbols** are used inside cells to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Probe Count** | $N_{probes} = H \times P$ | $H$ = number of live hosts, $P$ = number of scanned ports per host | count |
| 2 | **Approximate Scan Time** | $T \approx \dfrac{H \times P}{N_{threads} \times R}$ | $N_{threads}$ = concurrent threads, $R$ = per-thread response rate | seconds |
| 3 | **Port Range Coverage** | $P_{total} = P_{max} - P_{min} + 1$ | $P_{min}$ = starting port, $P_{max}$ = ending port | ports |
| 4 | **CIDR Subnet Host Count** | $H = 2^{32-n} - 2$ | $n$ = prefix length (e.g., $/24 \Rightarrow H=254$) | hosts |
| 5 | **Stealth Scan Detection Ratio** | $D = \dfrac{R_{logged}}{R_{sent}}$ | $R_{logged}$ = probes logged by IDS, $R_{sent}$ = total probes | ratio (0 to 1) |
| 6 | **Three-Way Handshake Packet Count** | $K = 3$ packets per successful open connection | constant for TCP Connect scan | packets |
| 7 | **Banner Size Limit** | $B_{max} = 4096$ bytes (typical receive buffer) | application-level constant | bytes |
| 8 | **UDP ICMP Unreachable Ratio** | $F_{udp} = \dfrac{N_{unreachable}}{N_{sent}}$ | $N_{unreachable}$ = `ICMP Port Unreachable` replies | ratio (0 to 1) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testing (PT):** Pre-attack reconnaissance phase of the **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)**.
- **Network Inventory Auditing:** System administrators map live services across enterprise subnets before patch cycles.
- **Firewall Rule Validation:** ACK scans detect **stateful vs stateless filtering** without triggering application-layer alarms.
- **Academic Cybersecurity Labs:** KTU institutions commonly deploy SuperScan in Windows-based cyber labs (e.g., Kali VM + Windows VM dual boot) for Module 3 practical examinations.
- **Digital Forensics (Live Response):** During incident triage, administrators run SuperScan to identify rogue services on compromised hosts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive step-by-step analytical breakdowns** of the two most examination-relevant SuperScan computations: **(A)** Total Scan Workload, and **(B)** Three-Way Handshake State Transition for a TCP Connect Scan.

### 3.1 Derivation A — Total Probe Workload and Scan Time

**Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
A network administrator uses SuperScan to scan a **Class C subnet** ($/24$) for the **first 1024 ports** using **50 concurrent threads**. Each thread completes, on average, **20 connections per second**. Compute (i) the total number of probe packets and (ii) the approximate total scan time.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables from the question stem.**

We have:

- Subnet prefix: $n = 24$
- Port count: $P = 1024$
- Thread count: $N_{threads} = 50$
- Throughput per thread: $R = 20$ probes/second

**Step 2 — Compute the number of usable hosts in the subnet.**

For a $/24$ network, the host bits available are $32 - 24 = 8$.

$$H = 2^{32-n} - 2 = 2^{8} - 2 = 256 - 2 = 254$$

The subtraction of 2 accounts for the **Network Address** (e.g., `192.168.1.0`) and the **Broadcast Address** (e.g., `192.168.1.255`), neither of which can host a device.

**Step 3 — Compute the total number of probe packets.**

$$
N_{probes} = H \times P = 254 \times 1024
$$

We expand the multiplication explicitly:

$$
254 \times 1024 = 254 \times (1000 + 24) = 254000 + 6096 = 260096
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_{probes} = 260{,}096 \text{ probes}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the approximate scan time.**

Using the formula from Section 2.3, item 2:

$$
T \approx \frac{H \times P}{N_{threads} \times R} = \frac{254 \times 1024}{50 \times 20}
$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$
50 \times 20 = 1000 \text{ probes/second (aggregate throughput)}
$$

Now substitute:

$$
T \approx \frac{260096}{1000} = 260.096 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to minutes for engineering intuition.**

$$
T_{minutes} = \frac{260.096}{60} \approx 4.335 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The full sweep would take approximately **260 seconds (~4.3 minutes)** to complete. In practice, latency, dropped packets, and retransmissions add **15–25 % overhead**, so the realistic wall-clock time is closer to **5 minutes**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — TCP Connect Scan State Machine Analysis

**Objective:** Prove that SuperScan classifies a port as **"Open"** only when the full three-way handshake completes successfully, and as **"Closed"** upon receiving an `RST-ACK`.

**Step 1 — Formalize the state variables.**

Let the SuperScan scanner be denoted $S$ and the target host be denoted $T$. The TCP state transition can be represented as a finite-state machine with states:

$$
\Sigma = \{ CLOSED, SYN\_SENT, SYN\_RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, RST\_RECEIVED \}
$$

**Step 2 — Send the initial SYN packet from scanner to target.**

The scanner $S$ transitions to $SYN\_SENT$ state and emits:

$$
S \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} T
$$

**Step 3 — Target host response (Case Analysis).**

Two mutually exclusive cases arise:

**Case (i) — Target port is OPEN:**

The target's TCP stack responds with a SYN-ACK packet:

$$
T \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, ACK, seq}=y, ack=x+1} S
$$

The scanner $S$ receives this and recognizes the port as a candidate for connection. It then completes the handshake:

$$
S \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, seq}=x+1, ack=y+1} T
$$

After this exchange, both sides enter the `ESTABLISHED` state. SuperScan's internal logic interprets `ESTABLISHED` as **port state = OPEN** and immediately proceeds with banner grabbing.

**Case (ii) — Target port is CLOSED:**

The target's TCP stack responds with an RST-ACK packet:

$$
T \xrightarrow{\text{RST, ACK}} S
$$

SuperScan interprets the `RST` flag as an explicit rejection and marks the port state as **CLOSED**. No further packets are exchanged.

**Step 4 — Formal state transition summary.**

We can express this in tabular form:

$$
\text{State}(p) =
\begin{cases}
OPEN & \text{if } \exists \, \text{SYN-ACK followed by ACK handshake completion} \\
CLOSED & \text{if } \exists \, \text{RST-ACK response} \\
FILTERED & \text{if no response received within timeout } \tau
\end{cases}
$$

where $p$ denotes the target port under evaluation and $\tau$ is the configurable **timeout threshold** (default in SuperScan = 2000 ms).

**Step 5 — Compute handshake packet count for one open port.**

For one successful open-port classification:

$$
K = |\{SYN, SYN\text{-}ACK, ACK\}| = 3 \text{ packets}
$$

For one successful closed-port classification:

$$
K = |\{SYN, RST\text{-}ACK\}| = 2 \text{ packets}
$$

For one filtered-port classification:

$$
K = |\{SYN\}| + \tau_{\text{wait}} = 1 \text{ packet + timeout}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total packet count for a sweep.**

If $H$ hosts are scanned, $P$ ports each, with $O$ open, $C$ closed, and $F$ filtered (where $O + C + F = P$):

$$
K_{total} = H \cdot \left[ 3O + 2C + (1 \cdot F) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners often ask students to *derive the number of packets generated by a SuperScan TCP Connect scan* on a given subnet. The above formula is the model answer template.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SuperScan vs Nmap vs Hping

| Feature | SuperScan 3.0 | Nmap 7.x | Hping3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating System** | Windows | Linux / Windows / macOS | Linux |
| **GUI Available** | Yes (native) | Yes (Zenmap GUI) | No (CLI only) |
| **Ping Sweep** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **TCP Connect Scan** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **SYN Stealth Scan** | Yes (limited) | Yes | Yes |
| **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **OS Fingerprinting** | Limited (via banner) | Advanced (TCP/IP stack probing) | No |
| **Scripting Engine** | No | Yes (NSE — Lua-based) | No |
| **Banner Grabbing** | Yes (built-in) | Yes (via NSE scripts) | Limited |
| **Report Format** | HTML, TXT | XML, Nmap, Grepable, HTML | Plain text |
| **Cost** | Free | Free (Open Source) | Free (Open Source) |
| **KTU Exam Importance** | High (Module 3 focus) | High (Module 3 focus) | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SuperScan Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[USER INTERFACE LAYER]
        A1[Main Console GUI]
        A2[Scan Wizard Form]
        A3[Report Viewer]
    end

    subgraph B[CONTROL AND ORCHESTRATION LAYER]
        B1[Thread Pool Manager]
        B2[Timeout and Retry Handler]
        B3[State Classifier Engine]
    end

    subgraph C[SCANNING ENGINE LAYER]
        C1[ICMP Ping Sweep Module]
        C2[TCP Scan Module]
        C3[UDP Scan Module]
        C4[Banner Grabbing Module]
    end

    subgraph D[TRANSPORT LAYER INTERFACE]
        D1[Winsock 2 API]
        D2[Raw Socket Driver]
        D3[Windows Packet Capture DLL]
    end

    subgraph E[OUTPUT AND REPORTING LAYER]
        E1[In-Memory Result Table]
        E2[HTML Report Generator]
        E3[Plain Text Exporter]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> E1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> B2
    D2 --> B2
    D3 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
```

### 4.2 SuperScan End-to-End Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1: User enters IP range or single host] --> S2[STEP 2: Select scan type Connect SYN FIN NULL XMAS ACK]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: Select port range]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: Set thread count and timeout]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5: Ping sweep executes]
    S5 --> S6{LIVE HOST?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Mark as Dead and skip]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[STEP 6: Launch multi-threaded port scan]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 7: Send probe packet per port]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 8: Capture response via Winsock]
    S10 --> S11{RESPONSE TYPE}
    S11 -- SYN-ACK --> S12[Classify as OPEN]
    S11 -- RST-ACK --> S13[Classify as CLOSED]
    S11 -- No Reply --> S14[Classify as FILTERED]
    S12 --> S15[STEP 9: Perform banner grab]
    S13 --> S16[STEP 10: Move to next port]
    S14 --> S16
    S15 --> S16
    S16 --> S17{All ports scanned?}
    S17 -- No --> S9
    S17 -- Yes --> S18[STEP 11: Generate HTML report]
    S18 --> S19[STEP 12: Display results to user]
```

### 4.3 Three-Way Handshake Sequence Diagram (TCP Connect Scan)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SC as SuperScan Scanner
    participant TG as Target Host Port
    SC ->> TG: SYN seq=x
    alt Port is OPEN
        TG -->> SC: SYN-ACK seq=y ack=x+1
        SC ->> TG: ACK seq=x+1 ack=y+1
        Note over SC,TG: Connection ESTABLISHED Banner Grab Initiated
    else Port is CLOSED
        TG -->> SC: RST-ACK
        Note over SC: Port Marked CLOSED
    else Port is FILTERED
        TG --x SC: No Response within Timeout
        Note over SC: Port Marked FILTERED
    end
```

### 4.4 Scan Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{What is your scan objective?}
    Q1 -- Full reliable connection --> T1[Use TCP CONNECT Scan]
    Q1 -- Stealth probe to evade logs --> T2[Use TCP SYN Stealth Scan]
    Q1 -- Bypass simple stateless filters --> T3[Use TCP FIN or NULL or XMAS Scan]
    Q1 -- Map firewall rule sets --> T4[Use TCP ACK Scan]
    Q1 -- Find UDP services --> T5[Use UDP Scan with ICMP unreachable analysis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define SuperScan. List any four of its key features.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **SuperScan** is a **Windows-based, free, multi-threaded TCP/UDP port scanner** developed by **Foundstone (McAfee)** used for network reconnaissance in cybersecurity audits.

**Four Key Features (½ mark each):**

1. **Ping Sweep / Host Discovery** using ICMP Echo Requests.
2. **TCP and UDP Port Scanning** with multiple scan techniques (Connect, SYN, FIN, NULL, XMAS, ACK).
3. **Banner Grabbing** to identify the service and version running on open ports.
4. **Multi-threaded scan engine** with configurable concurrency (up to 1000 threads).
5. **HTML report generation** for easy documentation.

*(Any four accepted)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition = 1, Features = 4 × ½ = 2, Total = 3.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between TCP Connect Scan and TCP SYN Stealth Scan as supported by SuperScan.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | TCP Connect Scan | TCP SYN Stealth Scan |
|---|---|---|
| **Handshake Completion** | Completes full 3-way handshake | Sends SYN but never sends the final ACK; resets the half-open connection |
| **Packets per Open Port** | 3 packets | 2 packets (SYN sent + RST on response) |
| **Detection Risk** | High — logged in target application logs | Lower — does not establish a real connection, often missed by simple loggers |
| **User Privilege Required** | None (uses OS connect call) | Often requires **raw socket** privileges on Windows |
| **Purpose** | Reliable banner grabbing | Stealth reconnaissance |

**Marks Distribution:** Differentiation parameters = 3 × 1 = 3 marks (any three valid contrasts).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **forget to mention the developer (Foundstone/McAfee)** when defining SuperScan, losing **1 mark**.
> - In numerical scan-time problems, students often **omit the timeout overhead** and present an unrealistically optimistic answer; examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing this realism.
> - Many students confuse **Stealth Scan (SYN)** with **Full Connect Scan**; clearly stating the **packet count difference** earns the differentiation mark.
> - Always write the **formula before substitution** in numericals. Marks are awarded for the **formula step** even if the arithmetic is slightly off.

---

**Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and working of SuperScan** with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the role of its **multi-threaded scan engine**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the following **scan techniques** supported by SuperScan, with packet diagrams:
   (i) TCP SYN Stealth Scan
   (ii) TCP FIN Scan
   (iii) TCP XMAS Scan **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture and Working — 7 Marks:**

The SuperScan architecture consists of **five functional layers** (as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes):

1. **User Interface Layer (1 mark):** Provides the **GUI console**, scan wizard, and report viewer. The user inputs the target IP range, selects ports, and configures thread count.

2. **Control and Orchestration Layer (2 marks):** The **Thread Pool Manager** spawns the requested number of concurrent threads; the **Timeout and Retry Handler** governs how long to wait for responses; the **State Classifier Engine** interprets incoming packets (SYN-ACK → OPEN, RST-ACK → CLOSED, silence → FILTERED).

3. **Scanning Engine Layer (2 marks):** Contains four specialized modules — ICMP Ping Sweep, TCP Scan, UDP Scan, and Banner Grabbing. Each module crafts the appropriate packet structure.

4. **Transport Layer Interface (1 mark):** Uses the **Winsock 2 API** for normal TCP/UDP communication and **Raw Sockets** for stealth scans.

5. **Output and Reporting Layer (1 mark):** Aggregates results into an in-memory table and exports to **HTML or plain text**.

**Role of Multi-Threaded Scan Engine:** The engine allows SuperScan to scan **multiple ports and multiple hosts in parallel**, drastically reducing wall-clock time. For example, scanning 254 hosts × 1024 ports single-threaded at 20 probes/sec would take $\approx 3.6$ hours, while with 100 threads it reduces to $\approx 2$ minutes.

---

**(b) Scan Techniques — 7 Marks:**

**(i) TCP SYN Stealth Scan (2 marks):**

The scanner sends a **single SYN packet** with sequence number $x$.

- If the target port is **OPEN**: Target replies with `SYN-ACK(seq=y, ack=x+1)`. Scanner **does not complete** the handshake; instead, it sends an `RST` to tear down the half-open connection. Port marked **OPEN**.
- If the target port is **CLOSED**: Target replies with `RST-ACK`. Port marked **CLOSED**.

This avoids completing a full TCP session, hence the term **"half-open" or "stealth"**.

**(ii) TCP FIN Scan (2 marks):**

The scanner sends a packet with **only the FIN flag** set, and **no other flags**.

- If the target port is **OPEN** (per RFC 793): Target **silently discards** the packet and sends **no reply**.
- If the target port is **CLOSED**: Target replies with `RST-ACK`.

This relies on the **RFC 793 rule** that closed ports must respond to unsolicited FIN with RST. Useful against stateless firewalls.

**(iii) TCP XMAS Scan (2 marks):**

The scanner sends a packet with **FIN, PSH, and URG flags** all set simultaneously — the packet is said to be **"lit like a Christmas tree"**, hence the name.

- Same RFC 793 behavior as FIN Scan: **silence = OPEN, RST = CLOSED**.
- Effective against **UNIX/Linux** targets; **Windows targets** ignore the rule and reply with RST regardless, making XMAS ineffective against them.

**Comparative Note (1 mark):** FIN, NULL, and XMAS scans exploit the same RFC 793 rule and are collectively called **"Stealth or Inverse Scans"**.

---

**Part B — Question B (14 Marks)**
**[Alternative Choice for Students — KTU Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** A cybersecurity analyst uses SuperScan to perform a TCP Connect scan on a **$/26$ subnet** for the **first 2048 ports** with **80 concurrent threads**. Each thread maintains an average throughput of **15 probes per second**.
   (i) Calculate the **total number of probe packets** generated.
   (ii) Calculate the **approximate total scan time** in seconds.
   (iii) How many packets are exchanged in total if **5 % of the ports are OPEN, 80 % are CLOSED, and 15 % are FILTERED**?
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a comparative note on **SuperScan, Nmap, and Hping3** with respect to platform support, scan techniques, GUI availability, and use case in penetration testing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Computation — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the host count for a $/26$ subnet. (1 mark)**

$$
H = 2^{32-26} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ hosts}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total probes. (2 marks)**

$$
N_{probes} = H \times P = 62 \times 2048
$$

Expanding:

$$
62 \times 2048 = 62 \times (2000 + 48) = 124000 + 2976 = 126976
$$

$$
N_{probes} = 126{,}976 \text{ probes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute approximate scan time. (2 marks)**

$$
T \approx \frac{H \times P}{N_{threads} \times R} = \frac{126976}{80 \times 15}
$$

Denominator:

$$
80 \times 15 = 1200 \text{ probes/sec}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T \approx \frac{126976}{1200} \approx 105.81 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total packets with state distribution. (2 marks)**

$$
O = 5\% \times 2048 = 102.4 \approx 102 \text{ open}
$$

$$
C = 80\% \times 2048 = 1638.4 \approx 1638 \text{ closed}
$$

$$
F = 15\% \times 2048 = 307.2 \approx 308 \text{ filtered}
$$

Verification: $102 + 1638 + 308 = 2048$ ports per host. ✓

Per-host packet count:

$$
K_{host} = 3O + 2C + 1F = 3(102) + 2(1638) + 1(308) = 306 + 3276 + 308 = 3890 \text{ packets}
$$

Total across all 62 hosts:

$$
K_{total} = 62 \times 3890 = 241180 \text{ packets}
$$

---

**(b) Comparative Note — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter (1.5 marks each for four well-covered) | SuperScan | Nmap | Hping3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Platform** | Windows only | Cross-platform (Linux/Windows/macOS) | Linux primarily |
| **GUI Availability** | Native Windows GUI | Zenmap GUI optional | No GUI (CLI only) |
| **Scan Techniques** | Connect, SYN, FIN, NULL, XMAS, ACK | All SuperScan techniques plus IP Protocol, FTP Bounce, Idle, etc. | Custom raw packet crafting; FIN, SYN, ACK, XMAS, NULL, UDP, ICMP, IP |
| **Use Case in PT** | Beginner-friendly reconnaissance on Windows labs | Industry-standard deep enumeration and scripting | Advanced stealth and IDS-evasion research |

**Marks Distribution:** Each well-explained row = 1.5 marks; small intro and conclusion = 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **SuperScan = Windows-based GUI port scanner** by **Foundstone/McAfee**.
- [ ] Free, closed-source, supports up to **1000 concurrent threads**.
- [ ] Combines **Ping Sweep + Port Scan + Banner Grabbing** in one tool.
- [ ] Six TCP scan techniques: **Connect, SYN, FIN, NULL, XMAS, ACK**.
- [ ] UDP scan relies on **ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)** replies.
- [ ] TCP Connect scan = **3 packets per open port**; SYN scan = **2 packets per open port**.
- [ ] Total probes formula: $N = H \times P$.
- [ ] Scan time formula: $T \approx \dfrac{H \times P}{N_{threads} \times R}$.
- [ ] Hosts in a subnet: $H = 2^{32-n} - 2$.
- [ ] Port range: $P_{total} = P_{max} - P_{min} + 1$.
- [ ] FIN / NULL / XMAS scans work only against **RFC 793 compliant** systems (most UNIX/Linux).
- [ ] **XMAS tree flags = FIN + PSH + URG**.
- [ ] SuperScan produces **HTML reports** — useful for documentation in KTU lab records.
- [ ] Always mention the **developer (Foundstone)** in definitions to earn full marks.
- [ ] Compare with **Nmap** and **Hping3** for any "differentiate" question in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireshark: Analysing and Filtering Traffic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireshark: Analysing and Filtering Traffic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wireshark** is a free, open-source **network protocol analyzer** (also called a *packet sniffer*) used for network troubleshooting, analysis, software and communications protocol development, and most importantly for cybersecurity — to inspect raw network packets traversing a wired or wireless interface in real time.

It runs on most computing platforms (Windows, Linux, macOS, BSD, Solaris) and uses the **pcap** (packet capture) library on Unix-like systems and the **WinPcap/Npcap** library on Windows to capture packets directly from the network interface card (NIC) in *promiscuous mode*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "Wireshark is a graphical packet-capture and analysis tool that places the network interface into promiscuous mode, captures frames from the wire, decodes them through a layered protocol dissector engine, and presents each protocol field in a human-readable format for forensic and diagnostic analysis."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Inspector"

Imagine a **post office** where millions of letters (network packets) pass through every second.

| Real-World Object | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The post office building | Your computer / network interface |
| The letter being sorted | An Ethernet frame / IP packet |
| The postal worker opening every letter | Wireshark's **dissector engine** |
| The sorting labels (To/From/Subject) | Protocol headers (IP, TCP, HTTP) |
| A magnifying glass used by the postal inspector | A **display filter** (e.g., `http.request`) |
| A lock on the mailbox rejecting suspicious letters *before opening* | A **capture filter** (BPF syntax) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** *Capture filters* decide *which letters* the postal worker even bothers to pick up. *Display filters* decide *which letters the inspector reads in detail* after they are all on the desk. This dual-stage filtering is the heart of efficient traffic analysis.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Defaults

The following defaults are critical to remember for KTU numerical/descriptive questions:

- **Default promiscuous-mode port speed:** up to **1 Gbps** on modern NICs
- **Default snap length (snaplen):** **65535 bytes** (full Ethernet frame)
- **Default capture buffer:** in-memory ring buffer
- **Color-coding reset threshold:** **750** packets
- **File extension of saved captures:** **.pcap** or **.pcapng**
- **Default display filter protocol operator precedence:** `not` > `and` > `or`
- **Wireshark dissector table count:** **3000+** supported protocols

> [!NOTE]
> **Why promiscuous mode matters in Cyber Security:** Without promiscuous mode, your NIC discards any frame whose destination MAC does not match your own. Promiscuous mode tells the NIC "give me *every* frame on the segment," which is essential for sniffing attacks like ARP poisoning or for forensics on a switched network using port mirroring (SPAN).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Filter Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Wireshark Three-Stage Architecture

Wireshark operates in three well-defined pipeline stages. Understanding these is a high-yield KTU question:

**Stage 1 — Capture Engine (libpcap / Npcap)**
- Sits between the OS network driver and the application layer.
- Uses the **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** virtual machine to evaluate *capture filters*.
- Operates in kernel space for performance.

**Stage 2 — Dissector Engine**
- Each captured byte stream is passed to a chain of **protocol dissectors** registered against the corresponding **dissector table** (e.g., `tcp.port`, `ethertype`, `ip.proto`).
- Dissectors decode headers and call **sub-dissectors** (e.g., TCP calls HTTP if port 80).

**Stage 3 — Presentation / GUI Layer**
- Renders packets into three synchronized panes:
  1. **Packet List Pane** — summary rows
  2. **Packet Details Pane** — collapsible protocol tree
  3. **Packet Bytes Pane** — raw hexadecimal dump

### 2.2 Capture Filters vs. Display Filters — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most asked KTU question on this topic. Memorize the difference.**

| Parameter | Capture Filter (BPF) | Display Filter (Wireshark-native) |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | **Before** capture (in kernel) | **After** capture (in user space) |
| Syntax style | Unix `tcpdump` style | Wireshark field-name style |
| Operator example | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |
| Boolean operators | `and`, `or`, `not` | `&&`, `\vert \vert`, `!` (or `not`, `and`, `or`) |
| Can reference packet bytes? | No (only headers) | Yes (e.g., `tcp[13] & 0x03 == 2` for SYN) |
| Can be modified mid-capture? | No — must restart | Yes — dynamic, instant |
| Typical use | Reducing disk usage on long captures | Investigating saved `.pcap` files |
| Performance | Very high (kernel filtering) | Moderate (full packets still in memory) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Filter Cheat Sheet

| # | Filter Expression (Display) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10` | All packets to **or** from this IP |
| 2 | `ip.src == 10.0.0.5` | Only packets **from** this source |
| 3 | `ip.dst == 10.0.0.5` | Only packets **to** this destination |
| 4 | `tcp.port == 443` | All TCP traffic on port 443 |
| 5 | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` | Pure SYN packets (scan detection) |
| 6 | `http.request.method == "POST"` | All HTTP POST requests |
| 7 | `dns.qry.name contains "google"` | DNS queries for Google |
| 8 | `arp.opcode == 1` | ARP requests only |
| 9 | `tcp.stream eq 4` | All packets in TCP stream #4 |
| 10 | `frame.len > 1500` | Jumbo frames / unusually large packets |
| 11 | `ip.ttl < 10` | Possible traceroute or crafted packets |
| 12 | `tcp.analysis.retransmission` | Detected TCP retransmissions |
| 13 | `http contains "password"` | Plaintext credential leakage |
| 14 | `!(arp or dns or icmp)` | Exclude noise protocols |
| 15 | `eth.addr == aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff` | Filter by MAC address |

**Comparison Operators allowed in Wireshark display filters:**

$$=, \neq, <, \le, >, \ge, \text{ contains}, \text{ matches}, \text{ in}$$

**Slice Operator** (used to inspect payload bytes):

$$ \text{protocol[offset:length]} $$

Example: `tcp[0:4] == 47:41:54:45` — checks if TCP payload starts with ASCII "GATE".

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Analysts:** Use Wireshark to investigate post-breach forensic `.pcap` files.
- **Penetration Testers:** Capture cleartext credentials on `http`, `ftp`, `telnet`.
- **Network Engineers:** Diagnose TCP retransmissions, DNS failures, slow application performance.
- **Malware Analysts:** Extract C2 (Command & Control) URLs from HTTP/DNS traffic.
- **CTF Players:** Solve forensics challenges by filtering for hidden flag strings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Building a Filter from Scratch

> [!NOTE]
> Because this is a tools / network-analysis topic, the "derivations" below are **logical/analytical derivations** of how a filter expression is constructed and evaluated by the Wireshark engine. No physics constants are involved, but the symbolic logic is shown in full.

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — Detecting a SYN Port Scan

A common KTU-style question is: *"Write a Wireshark display filter to detect TCP SYN packets that are not part of an established session (indicative of a port scan)."*

**Step 1 — Identify the protocol field.**
The TCP flag byte is at offset **13** in the TCP header (since offset 12 is the data-offset/reserved nibble). The SYN bit is the second-least significant bit of that byte.

**Step 2 — Write the bitmask logic.**
The SYN bit mask is `0x02` (binary `0000 0010`). We need this bit set, while ACK (`0x10`) is unset.

$$ \text{TCP flag byte} \; \& \; 0x02 \;\rightarrow\; 0x02 \quad (\text{SYN set}) $$

**Step 3 — Translate to Wireshark shorthand.**
Wireshark pre-parses flag fields, so we can write it semantically:

$$ \text{tcp.flags.syn} == 1 $$

**Step 4 — Combine with the NOT-ACK condition.**

$$ \text{tcp.flags.syn} == 1 \;\&\&\; \text{tcp.flags.ack} == 0 $$

**Step 5 — Add the destination-port constraint to filter for typical scan ports (< 1024).**

$$ \text{tcp.flags.syn} == 1 \;\&\&\; \text{tcp.flags.ack} == 0 \;\&\&\; \text{tcp.dstport} < 1024 $$

**Step 6 — Final display filter:**

$$ \texttt{tcp.flags.syn==1 \&\& tcp.flags.ack==0 \&\& tcp.dstport<1024} $$

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Following a TCP Stream

**Step 1:** Right-click a packet → **Follow** → **TCP Stream**.
**Step 2:** Wireshark assigns an internal stream index `tcp.stream`.
**Step 3:** All packets in the same bidirectional flow are tagged with that index.
**Step 4:** Reconstruct conversation:

$$ \text{Stream content} = \bigcup_{i \in \text{stream}} \text{payload}(i) \quad \text{ordered by } \text{tcp.seq} $$

**Step 5:** Wireshark displays the conversation in two colours: **red** (client → server) and **blue** (server → client).

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Extracting an HTTP Object (File)

To extract a transferred file (e.g., a malware sample) over HTTP:

$$ \text{Step 1: } \text{Filter} = \texttt{http.response} $$

$$ \text{Step 2: } \text{Identify packet with } \texttt{Content-Type: application/octet-stream} $$

$$ \text{Step 3: } \text{Menu} \rightarrow \text{File} \rightarrow \text{Export Objects} \rightarrow \text{HTTP} $$

$$ \text{Step 4: } \text{Select object and save to disk} $$

The "Export Objects" feature reassembles the file using the `Content-Length` and `Content-Disposition` headers:

$$ \text{File}_{recon} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \text{chunk}_n \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{n=1}^{N} \vert \text{chunk}_n \vert = \text{Content-Length} $$

### 3.4 Statistical Analysis Features (High-Yield Table)

| Wireshark Feature | Menu Path | What it Reveals |
|---|---|---|
| **Conversations** | Statistics → Conversations | End-to-end pairs, byte counts per host pair |
| **Endpoints** | Statistics → Endpoints | Per-host traffic statistics |
| **IO Graph** | Statistics → IO Graph | Time-series of traffic rate |
| **Flow Graph** | Statistics → Flow Graph | Visual conversation sequence |
| **Protocol Hierarchy** | Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy | % distribution of protocols in capture |
| **Expert Information** | Analyze → Expert Information | Wireshark's own warnings (retransmissions, ACKs lost) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wireshark Three-Stage Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Cable / WiFi] --> B[NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
    B --> C[Capture Engine: libpcap or Npcap]
    C --> D{Capture Filter BPF}
    D -- pass --> E[Ring Buffer in RAM]
    D -- drop --> F[Discarded Packets]
    E --> G[Dissector Engine: 3000+ Protocol Decoders]
    G --> H[GUI Three-Pane View]
    H --> H1[Packet List Pane]
    H --> H2[Packet Details Pane]
    H --> H3[Packet Bytes Pane]
    H --> I{Display Filter}
    I -- pass --> J[Highlighted Result Rows]
    I -- hide --> K[Rows Dimmed but Retained]
```

### 4.2 Capture-Filter vs Display-Filter Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Have Network Traffic to Analyze] --> Q1{Need to REDUCE volume of data saved on disk?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> CF[Use CAPTURE FILTER: BPF syntax e.g. host 192.168.1.5 and port 80]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Working on an EXISTING .pcap file?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> DF[Use DISPLAY FILTER: Wireshark syntax e.g. ip.addr==192.168.1.5 && tcp.port==80]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need to change filter WHILE capturing is running?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> DF2[Use DISPLAY FILTER only, capture filter cannot be changed mid-run]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Inspecting raw payload bytes?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> DF3[Use DISPLAY FILTER with slice operator e.g. tcp[0:4]]
    Q4 -- No --> CF2[Default: leave both filters empty for full capture]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of an HTTP Packet Dissected by Wireshark

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Frame[Ethernet II Frame] --> IP[IPv4 Header]
    IP --> TCP[TCP Header Source Port: 54321 Destination Port: 80]
    TCP --> HTTP[HTTP Protocol Request Line: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1]
    HTTP --> H1[Host Header: www.example.com]
    HTTP --> H2[User-Agent Header: Mozilla 5.0]
    HTTP --> H3[Accept Header: text html]
    HTTP --> Body[Empty Body for GET]
```

### 4.4 Typical Forensic Workflow Using Wireshark (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Analyst as SOC Analyst
    participant WS as Wireshark GUI
    participant Disk as .pcap File
    participant NIC as Network Interface
    NIC->>WS: Live capture in promiscuous mode
    WS->>Disk: Save full capture to .pcap
    Analyst->>WS: Apply display filter e.g. http.request
    WS-->>Analyst: Highlighted suspicious requests
    Analyst->>WS: Right-click packet Follow TCP Stream
    WS-->>Analyst: Reconstructed conversation
    Analyst->>WS: Export Objects HTTP
    WS-->>Analyst: Saved malware sample to disk
    Analyst->>WS: Statistics Protocol Hierarchy
    WS-->>Analyst: Protocol percentage breakdown
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between capture filters and display filters in Wireshark. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Application time** | Applied *before* capture, in kernel | Applied *after* capture, in user space |
| **Syntax** | BPF / `tcpdump` style | Wireshark field-name style |
| **Example** | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |
| **Mutability** | Cannot be changed mid-capture | Can be changed instantly on a saved capture |

**Example of capture filter:** `host 192.168.1.10 and tcp port 443`
**Example of display filter:** `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10 && tcp.port == 443`

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip (1 mark):** The examiner specifically looks for the phrase "applied before capture" vs "applied after capture." Writing only the syntax difference scores only 1/3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is promiscuous mode? Why is it essential for packet sniffing with Wireshark?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Promiscuous mode is a NIC configuration in which the network card forwards **all** frames it sees on the segment to the operating system, regardless of whether the destination MAC address matches its own.

By default, a NIC discards frames not addressed to it. Without promiscuous mode, Wireshark would only capture broadcast/multicast traffic and traffic explicitly destined to the host's MAC, missing all other conversations on the LAN.

It is essential for:
1. Capturing other hosts' traffic during incident response.
2. Sniffing in switched networks (typically combined with **port mirroring / SPAN**).
3. Detecting ARP-poisoning based MITM attacks.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-stage architecture of Wireshark** (capture, dissection, presentation). How does the dissector engine identify which sub-protocol to invoke?
**(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A SOC analyst has captured a 2 GB `.pcap` file. She needs to investigate only HTTP traffic to and from IP `10.10.10.5`. Write the **exact display filter** she should apply, and explain the difference if she had used a **capture filter** instead.
**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Capture Engine (1.5 marks):**
The capture engine uses `libpcap` (Linux) or `Npcap` (Windows). It receives raw bytes from the NIC which is in promiscuous mode. The BPF virtual machine evaluates any capture filter expression in kernel space, dropping unwanted packets *before* they consume RAM.

**Step 2 — Dissector Engine (3 marks):**
The dissector engine is the heart of Wireshark. It maintains a **dissector table** keyed by protocol identifiers such as:
- `ethertype` (EtherType values 0x0800 → IPv4, 0x86DD → IPv6, 0x0806 → ARP)
- `ip.proto` (protocol number 6 → TCP, 17 → UDP, 1 → ICMP)
- `tcp.port` and `udp.port` (port-based sub-dissectors, e.g., port 80 → HTTP, 443 → TLS)

For each captured frame, the engine looks up the first protocol's dissector (e.g., Ethernet), parses its header, and then uses the field values to *recursively* look up the next dissector in the chain until no more dissectors are registered.

**Step 3 — Presentation Layer (1.5 marks):**
The presentation layer renders three panes: the **Packet List Pane** showing summary lines, the **Packet Details Pane** showing the collapsible protocol tree with each field highlighted, and the **Packet Bytes Pane** showing the raw hex dump with the corresponding field selected.

**Step 4 — Block Diagram (1 mark):**
*(See SECTION 4.1 above for the verified Mermaid block diagram.)*

### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Display filter construction (3 marks):**
The display filter to isolate HTTP traffic to/from `10.10.10.5` is:

$$ \texttt{ip.addr == 10.10.10.5 \&\& http} $$

Here:
- `ip.addr` is a "display filter field" that matches source *or* destination IP.
- `http` is the protocol shortcut that matches any packet where Wireshark detected an HTTP dissector (port 80 or explicit ALPN).

**Step 2 — Applying the filter (1 mark):**
The analyst types the filter in the display-filter bar above the Packet List Pane and presses **Enter**. Non-matching rows are dimmed (not deleted).

**Step 3 — Capture-filter equivalent (2 marks):**
Had she used a capture filter at capture time, the syntax would be:

$$ \texttt{host 10.10.10.5 and tcp port 80} $$

The **differences** are:
1. All non-HTTP traffic from that host (e.g., DNS, TLS) is *permanently lost* from the saved file.
2. The capture filter is *not* editable without restarting the capture.
3. The capture filter runs in the kernel and saves significant disk space.

**Step 4 — Examiner's preferred conclusion (1 mark):**
For forensic investigation, **display filtering is preferred** because it preserves the full conversation context (e.g., DNS lookup before HTTP connection) and is reversible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Writing `ip.addr = 10.10.10.5` (single `=`). Wireshark display filters use `==`, not `=`. Single `=` produces a parse error.
> **Pitfall 2:** Writing `ip == 10.10.10.5`. The correct field is `ip.addr`, not `ip`.
> **Pitfall 3:** Confusing `http` (protocol) with `http.request` (only HTTP request packets). Use `http` to include both requests and responses.

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**Q. (a)** Explain the role of **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** syntax in Wireshark. Construct a capture filter that captures only DNS queries from a host `192.168.1.50` to any server, and explain each part.
**(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** What is **Follow TCP Stream** in Wireshark? How is it used in detecting a **plaintext credential leak**? Demonstrate the step-by-step procedure with the expected filter expression.
**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Role of BPF (2 marks):**
BPF is the *de facto* standard packet-filtering virtual machine originating from the BSD Unix operating system. In Wireshark, BPF expressions are evaluated inside the kernel (or Npcap driver) *before* a packet is copied into user space, making capture filtering highly efficient. The Wireshark capture-filter input box in the capture options dialog uses libpcap grammar, identical to the `tcpdump` command.

**Step 2 — Building the DNS capture filter (4 marks):**
DNS uses UDP port 53 for queries and responses. The BPF expression is:

$$ \texttt{src host 192.168.1.50 and dst port 53} $$

**Explanation of each token:**
- `src` — restrict to packets whose source IP is the following address.
- `host 192.168.1.50` — match any packet with `192.168.1.50` as either source *or* destination. When prefixed by `src`, it restricts to *source* only.
- `and` — Boolean AND of two primitives.
- `dst port 53` — restrict to packets whose **destination** UDP/TCP port is 53 (DNS).

**Step 3 — Optional refinement (1 mark):**
To also exclude TCP DNS zone transfers (AXFR) and capture *only* UDP DNS:

$$ \texttt{src host 192.168.1.50 and udp dst port 53} $$

### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Follow TCP Stream (2 marks):**
*Follow TCP Stream* is a Wireshark feature that re-assembles all packets belonging to a single TCP conversation (identified by the 4-tuple: source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port) and presents them in chronological order as a single contiguous ASCII/Hex view. The reassembly honours the TCP sequence numbers.

**Step 2 — Procedure to follow a stream (2 marks):**
1. Apply display filter: `ip.addr == <suspect IP>`.
2. Right-click any packet belonging to the suspect session.
3. Select **Follow** → **TCP Stream**.
4. Wireshark auto-generates and applies the filter `tcp.stream eq <N>`.
5. The full reconstructed conversation is shown in red (client → server) and blue (server → client).

**Step 3 — Detecting credential leak (2 marks):**
In plaintext protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, POP3, IMAP), credentials appear in the packet payload. Following the stream reveals them in cleartext. Example filter to inspect a specific login form POST:

$$ \texttt{http.request.method == "POST" \&\& http.host contains "login"} $$

Reading the stream typically reveals:

$$ \text{username=admin\&password=P@ssw0rd123} $$

**Step 4 — Why this is a security finding (1 mark):**
Any credential transmitted in cleartext can be intercepted by an attacker on the same broadcast domain (or via ARP poisoning), violating the **CIA triad (Confidentiality)**. The recommendation is to enforce **TLS 1.3** for all authentication endpoints.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. Writing capture filter syntax (`host 192.168.1.50 and port 53`) in a question that asks for a **display** filter. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 2. Saying "Follow TCP Stream shows the raw bytes" — it actually shows the **reassembled stream with sequence ordering**. Partial credit only.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **4-tuple** that identifies a TCP stream.
> 4. Confusing **port 80 (HTTP)** with **port 8080** (HTTP-Alt). Both are HTTP but only port 80 is registered in Wireshark's default `tcp.port` dissector table; port 8080 requires explicit "Decode As" configuration.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Wireshark** = open-source packet analyzer using `libpcap` / `Npcap`.
- [x] Runs NIC in **promiscuous mode** to capture all frames on segment.
- [x] **Three-stage architecture:** Capture engine → Dissector engine → GUI presentation.
- [x] **Capture filter** uses **BPF syntax** (e.g., `tcp port 80`); applied *before* capture in kernel.
- [x] **Display filter** uses **Wireshark field syntax** (e.g., `tcp.port == 80`); applied *after* capture in user space and is **dynamic**.
- [x] **Operators in display filters:** `&&`, `\vert \vert`, `!` (or `and`, `or`, `not`).
- [x] **Precedence:** `not` > `and` > `or` (use parentheses to be safe).
- [x] **Slice operator** `protocol[offset:length]` inspects raw bytes — used to detect flag patterns.
- [x] `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` is the **classic port-scan detection** filter.
- [x] **Follow TCP Stream** reassembles a full conversation using the 4-tuple.
- [x] **Export Objects (HTTP/SMB/TFTP)** extracts files transferred in cleartext.
- [x] **Statistics menu** offers Conversations, Endpoints, IO Graph, Flow Graph, Protocol Hierarchy, Expert Information.
- [x] Saved capture file extensions: **`.pcap`** (libpcap classic) and **`.pcapng`** (next-generation, supports multiple interfaces & annotations).
- [x] Colour-coding rules can be customised under **View → Coloring Rules**.
- [x] Wireshark itself is **read-only on the wire** — it cannot inject or modify packets (use `Scapy` or `hping3` for that).
- [x] **Legal reminder for KTU viva:** Capturing traffic on networks you do not own or administer is illegal under the **IT Act 2000 §66** (India) and equivalent statutes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windows Security: Attacks Against Windows System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Windows Security: Attacks Against Windows System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Windows Security Architecture** refers to the integrated collection of subsystems, components, and access-control mechanisms in Microsoft Windows operating systems that enforce authentication, authorization, integrity, and audit policies on local and network resources. The **Windows Security Subsystem** is fundamentally built around four interlocking pillars: | Acronym | Component | Role | |---------|-----------|------| | **SRM** | Security Reference Monitor | Kernel-mode code that validates DACLs/SACLs for every object access | | **LSA / LSASS** | Local Security Authority / Subsystem Service | User-mode process that authenticates logons and enforces security policy | | **SAM** | Security Account Manager | Database storing local user/group credentials (hashed) | | **AD** | Active Directory | Domain-based directory service managing enterprise identity | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building" Imagine a **multi-storey corporate office building** as a Windows system: - The **SAM database** is the *HR records room* that stores every employee's badge-ID and fingerprint (hash). - The **LSASS process** is the *security desk at the lobby* — every person who enters must show their badge here. - The **SRM** is the *security camera + lock on every individual office door* — it checks whether *you* are allowed to open *that specific door*. - **Active Directory** is the *centralized head-office HR system* for the entire multinational company, syncing identities across all buildings. - **Access Tokens** are your *visitor badges* given after you sign in at the lobby — they determine which floors and rooms you can access during your stay. An attacker who breaches the **security desk (LSASS)** or forges a **visitor badge (token)** can roam freely through the entire building — and that is exactly how Windows attacks work. ## 1.3 The Attack Surface — High-Level Taxonomy > [!NOTE] > **Attack Surface** = the sum of all points (components, ports, services, protocols) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter, extract data, or escalate privilege. Windows attacks are classified by the **attacker's objective and target layer**: 1. **Credential Theft Attacks** — stealing hashed or clear-text passwords. 2. **Authentication Protocol Attacks** — abusing NTLM / Kerberos weaknesses. 3. **Privilege Escalation Attacks** — moving from standard user → administrator → SYSTEM. 4. **Remote Exploitation Attacks** — exploiting vulnerable services (SMB, RDP, IIS). 5. **Code Injection Attacks** — DLL injection, process hollowing, reflective loading. 6. **Persistence & Lateral Movement** — token impersonation, scheduled tasks, WMI. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hallmark Metric:** On Windows, the *highest privilege level* is **NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM** (LocalSystem), which sits *above* any Administrator account. Most privilege-escalation attacks target this level because it has unrestricted access to the kernel, registry, and processes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Windows Authentication Stack Windows offers **two principal authentication protocols**: ### 2.1.1 NTLM (NT LAN Manager) NTLM is a **challenge-response** protocol that **never sends the password over the network**. It uses the MD4 hash of the user's password as the secret key. **Step-by-Step NTLM Flow:** 1. **Client → Server:** Sends authentication request. 2. **Server → Client:** Issues a **random 8-byte challenge** (nonce). 3. **Client → Server:** Computes: $$\text{Response} = \text{DES}_{\text{NT\_Hash}}(\text{Challenge} \parallel \text{Client\_Challenge})$$ and sends it back. 4. **Server** verifies the response against the stored NT hash. > [!NOTE] > **NT Hash Generation:** `NT_Hash = MD4(UTF-16LE(password))` > The hash has **no salt** — making it highly vulnerable to rainbow tables. ### 2.1.2 Kerberos (Default since Windows 2000) Kerberos is a **ticket-based** protocol using a trusted **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** — which in Windows is the **Domain Controller (DC)**. **Entities:** Client (C), Service Server (S), Authentication Server (AS), Ticket Granting Server (TGS). **Kerberos Exchange Flow:** 1. **AS-REQ**: Client → AS — *"I am user C, I want to authenticate."* 2. **AS-REP**: AS → Client — sends `TGT` encrypted with `krbtgt` account's secret key, plus a **session key** `K_{C,TGS}` encrypted with C's secret key. 3. **TGS-REQ**: Client → TGS — presents TGT + service principal name (SPN). 4. **TGS-REP**: TGS → Client — issues a **Service Ticket (ST)** encrypted with the service account's hash, plus a session key `K_{C,S}`. 5. **AP-REQ**: Client → Service — presents the ST to access the service. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Implication:** Because both the TGT and ST are encrypted with account hashes, an attacker who **extracts the krbtgt hash** can mint arbitrary TGTs forever — this is the basis of the **Golden Ticket** attack. ## 2.2 The LSASS Memory Dump — The Crown Jewel **LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service, `lsass.exe`)** is the user-mode process that holds: - Active logon session credentials - NTLM hashes - Kerberos TGTs and service tickets - LM hashes (legacy) - Cached domain credentials > [!WARNING] > LSASS memory is the **#1 target** of post-exploitation frameworks like Mimikatz, because everything needed for lateral movement lives in RAM. ## 2.3 Core Attack Vectors — Detailed Mechanics ### 2.3.1 Pass-the-Hash (PtH) **Concept:** The attacker captures an NT hash (e.g., from `secretsdump.py`, Mimikatz `sekurlsa::logonpasswords`) and authenticates *without ever knowing the plaintext password*. **Mathematical Basis:** $$\text{Auth}_{\text{NTLM}}(\text{Challenge}) = \text{DES}_{\text{NT\_Hash}}(\text{Challenge})$$ Because the protocol only needs the hash to compute the response, possession of the hash is sufficient. **Tools:** Mimikatz, `pth-winexe`, `pth-smbclient`, CrackMapExec. ### 2.3.2 Pass-the-Ticket (PtT) **Concept:** Extract a valid **Kerberos TGT or ST** from LSASS memory and inject it into a new session using: - Mimikatz: `kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi` - Rubeus: `ptt /ticket:` The attacker's session then *becomes* the legitimate ticket-holder, with no cracking required. ### 2.3.3 Kerberoasting **Concept:** A **low-privilege** domain user requests a **TGS-REP for any account with an SPN** (Service Principal Name). The returned service ticket is encrypted with the **target service account's NT hash**. The attacker cracks this offline. **Why it works:** - Any authenticated user can request TGS for any SPN. - Service account passwords are often weak (e.g., legacy service accounts). - The hash format (`RC4-HMAC` for SPNs) is directly crackable. ### 2.3.4 Golden Ticket Attack **Concept:** Once the attacker has compromised the **krbtgt account hash**, they can forge **arbitrary TGTs** for any user, including non-existent ones, with arbitrary group memberships and a long lifetime. **Mimikatz command:** ``` kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-... /krbtgt: /ptt ``` **Mathematical Model:** $$\text{TGT}_{\text{forged}} = E_{K_{krbtgt}}(C, S, \text{GroupMemberships}, \text{Expiry})$$ where $K_{krbtgt}$ is the stolen krbtgt hash.
