Anyone holding $K_{krbtgt}$ can encrypt valid TGTs. ### 2.3.5 Silver Ticket Attack A Silver Ticket forges a **Service Ticket (ST)** using the **service account's hash** instead of the krbtgt hash. It grants access only to the specific service but **bypasses the DC entirely**, making it stealthier than Golden Ticket. ### 2.3.6 EternalBlue (MS17-010) **Concept:** A **buffer overflow** in Windows' **SMBv1** driver (`srv.sys`) that allows **Remote Code Execution** with **SYSTEM** privileges via crafted `SMB_COM_TRANSACTION2` packets. **Trigger:** Send a malformed SMBv1 packet to TCP port **445**. **Used by:** WannaCry, NotPetya, EternalRocks, TrickBot loaders. ### 2.3.7 DLL Injection & DLL Hijacking **DLL Injection:** Forcing a running process to load a malicious DLL by: - `CreateRemoteThread()` + `LoadLibrary()` (classic injection) - `SetWindowsHookEx()` (hook-based) - AppInit_DLLs Registry value (persistence) - Process Hollowing (`NtUnmapViewOfSection`) **DLL Hijacking (Search Order):** When an application loads a DLL without a fully-qualified path, Windows searches in this order: 1. The application's directory 2. The current working directory 3. System directory 4. 16-bit system directory 5. Windows directory 6. Directories listed in `PATH` An attacker drops a malicious DLL with the expected name in step 2. ### 2.3.8 UAC Bypass (User Account Control) UAC is a **consent prompt** enforcing integrity levels. Common bypasses: - **Fodhelper.exe** — abuses `HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command` - **Eventvwr.exe** — auto-elevated binary - **COM elevation moniker** abuse ### 2.3.9 Token Impersonation After obtaining a privileged token (via `SeImpersonatePrivilege` — e.g., the `PrintSpoofer` or `Potato family` exploits), the attacker calls `DuplicateTokenEx()` and `ImpersonateLoggedOnUser()` to run code as SYSTEM. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Use | |---|---|---| | NT Hash | $\text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(\text{Password}))$ | NTLM authentication, pass-the-hash | | NTLMv2 Response | $\text{HMAC\_MD5}(K_{NT}, \text{Challenge} \parallel \text{Blob})$ | Modern NTLM | | Kerberos Session Key | $K_{C,S} = \text{Random}()$ | Securing C↔S traffic | | Golden Ticket Encryption | $E_{K_{krbtgt}}(\text{TGT})$ | Forging valid TGTs | | Account SID | `S-1-5-21--` | User identity in tickets | | Well-known SYSTEM SID | `S-1-5-18` | Identifies LocalSystem | | Built-in Admin RID | `500` | Default Administrator | | LSASS Process ID | Dynamic | Target for credential dumping | | SMB Default Port | **TCP 445** | EternalBlue, lateral movement | | RDP Default Port | **TCP 3389** | Brute force, BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) | | Kerberos Default Port | **TCP/UDP 88** | KDC communication | | Default Credential Formats | Domain\User or User@Domain | Pass-the-hash scripts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic — they are the *exact operations* performed by credential-extraction tools (Mimikatz, Rubeus, secretsdump) and the *exact test cases* that defenders use to validate intrusion-detection signatures (e.g., ETW providers for LSASS access). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NTLM Challenge-Response We start from first principles. Let $P$ be the user's plaintext password and $H$ the NT hash. **Step 1 — Derive the NT Hash** The NT hash is computed by applying MD4 to the UTF-16 little-endian encoded password: $$ H = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P)) $$ **Step 2 — Generate the 16-byte NT Hash Key** The 16-byte hash $H$ is split into three 7-byte keys ($K_1, K_2, K_3$), each zero-padded and used as DES keys: $$ K_1 = H[0..6] \parallel 0x00, \quad K_2 = H[7..13] \parallel 0x00, \quad K_3 = H[14..15] \parallel 0x00 \parallel 0x00 \parallel 0x00 \parallel 0x00 \parallel 0x00 $$ **Step 3 — Encrypt the Server Challenge** Let $C_S$ be the 8-byte server challenge. Compute three DES encryptions: $$ E_1 = \text{DES}_{K_1}(C_S), \quad E_2 = \text{DES}_{K_2}(C_S), \quad E_3 = \text{DES}_{K_3}(C_S) $$ **Step 4 — Concatenate to form 24-byte Response** $$ R_{\text{NTLM}} = E_1 \parallel E_2 \parallel E_3 $$ **Step 5 — Client Challenge Inclusion (NTLMv2)** For NTLMv2, a client challenge $C_C$ and timestamp $T$ are appended: $$ \text{Blob} = C_C \parallel T \parallel \text{Domain} \parallel \text{UserInfo} $$ $$ \text{NTLMv2\_Response} = \text{HMAC\_MD5}(H, \, C_S \parallel \text{Blob}) \parallel \text{Blob} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for attackers:** Because $H$ is unsalted and fixed for a given password, an attacker who captures $R_{\text{NTLM}}$ and $C_S$ can **brute-force $H$ directly** — there is no need for a rainbow table lookup if the dictionary is small. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kerberos Ticket Authentication Cost Model Define the computational cost $C$ for an attacker attempting ticket forgery given access to a hash $H_i$ of account $i$. $$ C_{\text{crack}}(H_i) = \text{Entropy}(H_i) \times \text{GuessRate}^{-1} $$ where: - $\text{Entropy}(H_i) = L \times \log_2(N)$ with $L$ = password length, $N$ = character-set size. - $\text{GuessRate}$ = hashes/sec by GPU cluster. **Service Account Vulnerability Score:** $$ V_i = \frac{\text{Privilege}_i}{\log_2(N_i^{L_i})} $$ Higher $V_i$ indicates the account is *more valuable per unit of cracking effort* — the *exact heuristic* Kerberoasting attackers use to prioritize targets. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack Comparison | Attack | Privilege Required | Targets | Detection Difficulty | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pass-the-Hash** | Any captured hash | NTLM services | Low (Event ID 4624 with NTLM) | Very High | | **Pass-the-Ticket** | Memory access to LSASS | Kerberos services | Medium | High | | **Kerberoasting** | Domain user (any) | Service accounts w/ SPN | Low (4769 events) | Very High | | **Golden Ticket** | `krbtgt` hash | Entire domain | Very Hard | High | | **Silver Ticket** | Service account hash | Single service | Very Hard | Medium | | **EternalBlue** | Network reachability of :445 | SMBv1 hosts | Medium (event logs) | High | | **DLL Injection** | Code execution in process | Process memory | Medium | High | | **UAC Bypass** | Local user in admin group | Local privilege | Medium | High | | **Token Impersonation** | `SeImpersonatePrivilege` | Local → SYSTEM | Hard | Medium | | **BlueKeep (RDP)** | Network reachability of :3389 | RDP service | Medium | Medium | ## 3.4 Worked Example — Golden Ticket Forgery **Scenario:** An attacker has compromised the `krbtgt` hash: `a1b2c3d4e5...` and the domain SID: `S-1-5-21-1234567890-123456789-123456789`. **Goal:** Forge a TGT for a non-existent user `CEO_Admin` belonging to `Domain Admins`, valid for 10 years. **Step 1 — Construct the PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate):** $$ \text{PAC} = \{ \text{UserId} = \text{CEO\_Admin}, \text{GroupIds} = \{512, 513, 518\}, \text{Expiry} = T + 10 \text{ years} \} $$ **Step 2 — Encrypt TGT with krbtgt key:** $$ \text{TGT}_{\text{forged}} = E_{K_{krbtgt}}(\text{SessionKey} \parallel \text{PAC} \parallel \text{Timestamp}) $$ **Step 3 — Inject into attacker's session:** ``` kerberos::ptt forged.tgt ``` **Step 4 — Request TGS for `cifs/DC01.corp.local`:** The DC accepts the TGT (because it decrypts successfully with $K_{krbtgt}$) and issues a Service Ticket granting full domain-admin access. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Defender Counter-Measure:** The **only** true remediation is **resetting the `krbtgt` password twice** (with a delay) because the DC retains the *previous* key for ticket validation until the second reset. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Windows Security Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph user["USER MODE - USER SPACE"] subgraph apps["APPLICATION LAYER"] appA["User Application 1"] appB["User Application 2"] winSrv["Windows Services"] end subgraph lsaCore["LSA CORE SUBSYSTEMS"] lsa["LSA - Local Security Authority"] lsass["lsass.exe - Process"] samDb["SAM Database - Local"] adClient["Active Directory Client"] end subgraph tokens["TOKEN LAYER"] primTok["Primary Token"] impTok["Impersonation Token"] end end subgraph kernel["KERNEL MODE - RING 0"] srm["SRM - Security Reference Monitor"] ntfs["NTFS File System Driver"] registry["Registry Driver"] objMgr["Object Manager"] end subgraph net["NETWORK AUTHENTICATION"] ntlm["NTLM Protocol"] kerb["Kerberos Protocol"] smb["SMB - TCP 445"] rdp["RDP - TCP 3389"] end appA --> lsa appB --> lsa winSrv --> lsa lsa --> lsass lsass --> samDb lsass --> adClient lsa --> srm srm --> ntfs srm --> registry srm --> objMgr lsa --> primTok lsa --> impTok appA --> ntlm appA --> kerb kerb --> smb ntlm --> smb winSrv --> rdp ``` ## 4.2 Kerberos Authentication Flow with Attack Injection Points ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client Workstation participant AS as Authentication Server participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server participant S as Service Server participant ATK as Attacker Note over C,AS: Phase 1 - Initial Authentication C->>AS: AS-REQ with Client Identity AS->>C: AS-REP with TGT encrypted by krbtgt key Note right of ATK: ATTACK POINT 1
Kerberoasting - request TGS
for service account C->>TGS: TGS-REQ with TGT and Target SPN TGS->>C: TGS-REP with Service Ticket Note right of ATK: ATTACK POINT 2
Offline crack of Service Ticket C->>S: AP-REQ with Service Ticket S-->>C: AP-REP - Access Granted Note over ATK,S: ATTACK POINT 3 - Pass-the-Ticket ATK->>ATK: Extract TGT from LSASS memory ATK->>C: Inject stolen TGT into attacker session Note over ATK,S: ATTACK POINT 4 - Golden Ticket ATK->>ATK: Forge TGT using stolen krbtgt hash ATK->>TGS: Submit forged TGT TGS->>ATK: Accept - Tickets issued ``` ## 4.3 Pass-the-Hash Attack Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR step1["1 - Compromise Workstation"] step2["2 - Dump LSASS Memory"] step3["3 - Extract NT Hashes"] step4["4 - Locate Admin Hash"] step5["5 - Authenticate as Admin via PtH"] step6["6 - Pivot to Domain Controller"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 subgraph tools["Common Tools"] m1["Mimikatz"] m2["secretsdump.py"] m3["CrackMapExec"] m4["pth-winexe"] end tools -.-> step2 tools -.-> step5 ``` ## 4.4 Privilege Escalation Path on Windows ```mermaid flowchart TB lvl0["LEVEL 0 - Unprivileged User"] lvl1["LEVEL 1 - Local Administrator"] lvl2["LEVEL 2 - LocalSystem NT AUTHORITY SYSTEM"] lvl3["LEVEL 3 - Domain Administrator"] lvl4["LEVEL 4 - Enterprise Admin / krbtgt"] lvl0 -->|"UAC Bypass
Fodhelper Exploit"| lvl1 lvl1 -->|"Token Impersonation
PrintSpoofer Potato"| lvl2 lvl2 -->|"Dump SAM or DC Sync"| lvl3 lvl3 -->|"Extract krbtgt hash"| lvl4 classDef target fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff class lvl2,lvl4 target ``` ## 4.5 DLL Search-Order Hijacking Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Application calls LoadLibrary missing.dll"] p1["Search 1 - Application Directory"] p2["Search 2 - Current Working Directory"] p3["Search 3 - System32"] p4["Search 4 - Windows Directory"] p5["Search 5 - PATH Directories"] start --> p1 p1 -->|"Not found"| p2 p2 -->|"ATTACKER PLANTED DLL
Triggers Execution"| exec["Malicious Code Runs"] p2 -->|"Not found"| p3 p3 --> p4 p4 --> p5 classDef attack fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005 class exec attack ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** — *Define the SAM database and explain its role in Windows authentication. Why is it a primary target for attackers?* **Model Answer:** The **Security Account Manager (SAM)** is a Windows database file (`%SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM`) that stores local user account credentials in the form of **LM and NT password hashes**, along with security identifiers (SIDs) and account policy data. **Role:** - Authenticates local logons when the machine is not domain-joined. - Provides the Local Security Authority (LSA) with hash material to compute NTLM challenge-responses. - Stores account lockout thresholds and password policies. **Why it is targeted:** 1. It contains **all local account hashes** in one file — a single exfiltration grants every local account. 2. It can be dumped offline using `reg save hklm\sam sam.hive` if `SeBackupPrivilege` is held, or via `secretsdump.py` from Volume Shadow Copies. 3. NT hashes stored here are directly usable for **Pass-the-Hash** attacks against any service that accepts NTLM. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the SAM (local database) with Active Directory (domain database). The SAM is for *local* accounts only; AD handles *domain* accounts through NTDS.dit on Domain Controllers. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — *What is Pass-the-Hash? Explain in 3 points why NTLM authentication is fundamentally vulnerable to this attack.* **Model Answer:** **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** is a post-exploitation technique in which an attacker uses a captured **NTLM hash** to authenticate to a remote service **without recovering the plaintext password**. **Three Reasons NTLM is Vulnerable:** 1. **No salt in the NT hash** — The same password always produces the same NT hash, so captured hashes are immediately replayable. 2. **Hash-only authentication** — NTLM's challenge-response protocol uses the hash as the encryption key directly, so possession of the hash = ability to compute valid responses. 3. **No mutual authentication in NTLMv1** — The server is not verified, so attackers can also perform NTLM relay attacks, capturing hashes from one service and replaying them at another. --- ## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Variant 1]** **(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the **Kerberos authentication protocol** used in Windows. Discuss the role of the KDC, TGT, and Service Ticket. **(7 Marks)*** **(b)** *Explain the **Golden Ticket Attack** with its mathematical basis. How can organizations defend against it? **(7 Marks)*** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Kerberos Authentication Protocol (Windows Implementation):** Kerberos is a ticket-based symmetric-key authentication protocol that uses a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** as a trusted third party. In Windows Active Directory, the **Domain Controller (DC)** acts as the KDC. **Key Entities:** - **Client (C)** — the user/workstation requesting access. - **Authentication Server (AS)** — authenticates the client. - **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** — issues service tickets. - **Service Server (S)** — the resource being accessed. **Working — Step by Step:** 1. **AS-REQ**: The client sends its identity to the AS (no password transmitted, plaintext only). 2. **AS-REP**: The AS returns two items: - A **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** encrypted with the **`krbtgt` account's secret key**. - A **session key $K_{C,TGS}$** encrypted with the client's secret key (derived from password hash). 3. **TGS-REQ**: The client requests a service ticket by presenting the TGT to the TGS along with the **Service Principal Name (SPN)**. 4. **TGS-REP**: The TGS verifies the TGT (by decrypting with `krbtgt` key) and issues a **Service Ticket (ST)** encrypted with the **service account's secret key**, plus a session key $K_{C,S}$. 5. **AP-REQ**: The client presents the ST to the service server. The server decrypts the ST with its own secret key, extracts $K_{C,S}$, and grants access. **Roles:** - **KDC**: Central trusted authority that issues and validates tickets. - **TGT**: Acts like a "passport" — proves the user already authenticated to the KDC. - **Service Ticket**: Acts like a "visa" for a specific service. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Golden Ticket Attack:** A **Golden Ticket** is a forged TGT created by an attacker who has extracted the **NT hash of the `krbtgt` account** from the domain controller's NTDS.dit database. **Mathematical Basis:** Let $K_{krbtgt}$ be the stolen krbtgt hash. The forged TGT is constructed as: $$ \text{TGT}_{\text{forged}} = E_{K_{krbtgt}}\big(\,K_{C,TGS} \,\|\, \text{PAC} \,\|\, \text{Timestamp} \,\|\, \text{Expiry} \,\big) $$ where PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate) contains the attacker's chosen user identity, SIDs (e.g., `S-1-5-32-544` for Domain Admins), and a custom expiration (often 10 years). Because the DC uses $K_{krbtgt}$ to decrypt and verify every TGT, any TGT encrypted with this hash is accepted as legitimate. **Attack Workflow:** 1. Attacker compromises DC and dumps NTDS.dit → extracts `krbtgt` hash. 2. Attacker uses **Mimikatz** with command: `kerberos::golden /user:FakeAdmin /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-XXX /krbtgt: /ptt` 3. The forged TGT is injected into the attacker's session. 4. Attacker requests TGS for any service → DC happily issues → full domain compromise. **Defensive Countermeasures:** 1. **Reset the `krbtgt` password twice** (with at least a 10-hour gap) — this invalidates all tickets encrypted with the old key. 2. Enable **Credential Guard** (uses VBS/Hyper-V to isolate LSASS). 3. Enable **Privileged Attribute Certificate (PAC) validation** enforcement. 4. Monitor Event ID **4769** for anomalous TGS requests with suspicious encryption types. 5. Restrict DC access using tiered administration and PAW (Privileged Access Workstations). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Variant 2]** **(a)** *Explain the architecture of the Windows Security Subsystem. Describe the roles of SRM, LSA, LSASS, SAM, and Active Directory. **(7 Marks)*** **(b)** *Discuss the various **DLL injection** and **DLL hijacking** techniques. How does Windows' DLL search order enable these attacks? **(7 Marks)*** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Windows Security Subsystem Architecture:** The Windows Security Subsystem operates in **two layers**: user-mode and kernel-mode, working together to enforce access control. **1. Security Reference Monitor (SRM):** - Runs in **kernel mode (Ring 0)** inside `ntoskrnl.exe`. - Validates **DACLs (Discretionary ACLs)** and **SACLs (System ACLs)** for every object access. - Decides whether to grant or deny a request based on the caller's **Access Token**. **2. Local Security Authority (LSA):** - A user-mode component running inside `lsass.exe`. - Enforces **local security policy**, **audit policy**, and **authentication protocols** (NTLM, Kerberos). - Generates **access tokens** upon successful logon. **3. LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service):** - The executable `lsass.exe` hosting the LSA. - Manages **logon sessions**, **credentials**, and **Kerberos tickets** in memory. - **Primary post-exploitation target** for credential dumping. **4. SAM (Security Account Manager):** - Database storing **local user account hashes** (`%SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM`). - Used when machine is **not domain-joined** or for local fallback authentication. - Locked exclusively by LSASS — direct reads return ACCESS_DENIED. **5. Active Directory (AD):** - Distributed directory service (NTDS.dit on DCs) managing **domain accounts, groups, GPOs, and trust relationships**. - The KDC for Kerberos authentication. - Replaces SAM as the authoritative store for domain credentials. **Interaction:** A user logs on → LSA validates credentials (against SAM locally or AD remotely) → LSA creates an Access Token → All subsequent resource access is validated by SRM using this token. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **DLL Injection vs DLL Hijacking:** **DLL Injection** is the technique of forcing a running process to load an attacker-controlled DLL into its address space. Common methods: 1. **Classic Injection (CreateRemoteThread + LoadLibrary):** - Attacker opens target process with `OpenProcess`. - Allocates memory with `VirtualAllocEx`. - Writes DLL path via `WriteProcessMemory`. - Executes remote thread calling `LoadLibraryA`. 2. **Reflective DLL Injection:** - DLL contains its own loader stub. - No file is dropped on disk → bypasses on-disk AV. - Used by Cobalt Strike, Metasploit's `reflective_dll_injection`. 3. **Process Hollowing:** - Create a suspended legitimate process. - `NtUnmapViewOfSection` to remove its image. - Map malicious payload and resume. 4. **AppInit_DLLs / LoadAppInit_DLLs Registry Value:** - DLL loaded into every process that uses User32.dll. - Persistent injection vector. **DLL Hijacking (Search-Order Exploitation):** When an application calls `LoadLibrary("helper.dll")` **without a fully-qualified path**, Windows searches directories in this fixed order: $$ \text{SearchOrder} = [\text{AppDir},\ \text{CWD},\ \text{System32},\ \text{WinDir},\ \text{PATH}] $$ An attacker who can write to the **Current Working Directory** (Search 2) drops a malicious `helper.dll` and tricks the user into running the legitimate executable from that directory. The malicious DLL is loaded *before* the real one. **Real-World Example:** - **iTunes Media Helper** (CVE-2015-3685): Dropbox-style folder placed DLLs before system DLLs. - **WinSxS DLL search-order hijacking** is a recurring Microsoft patch category. **Defense:** - Use **fully-qualified paths** in `LoadLibrary` calls. - Enable **Safe DLL Search Mode** (`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\SafeDllSearchMode = 1`). - Enable **CWDIllegalInDllSearch** group policy to exclude CWD from the search. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing **injection** (writing to a running process's memory) with **hijacking** (winning a file-search race), and (2) forgetting the actual numeric search order. Always list the order *as defined by Microsoft* — alphabetical improvisation costs marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Windows Attacks** - ⭐ **LSASS (`lsass.exe`) is the central credential store** — both NT hashes and Kerberos tickets reside here. Protect it with Credential Guard. - ⭐ **NTLM uses unsalted MD4 hash** → directly exploitable via Pass-the-Hash. - ⭐ **Kerberos relies on two encryption keys**: `krbtgt` (for TGT) and service account key (for ST). - ⭐ **Golden Ticket** = forge TGT using `krbtgt` hash → full domain compromise; only fix is **double krbtgt password reset**. - ⭐ **Kerberoasting** = request TGS for any SPN, crack offline → needs only Domain User privileges. - ⭐ **Silver Ticket** = forge ST using service account hash → single-service compromise, bypasses DC. - ⭐ **EternalBlue (MS17-010)** = SMBv1 buffer overflow on port 445 → RCE as SYSTEM; patch with MS17-010. - ⭐ **DLL injection** uses `CreateRemoteThread` + `LoadLibrary`; **DLL hijacking** exploits the search-order race. - ⭐ **UAC bypass** uses auto-elevated binaries (Fodhelper, Eventvwr) to escalate from admin group → actual SYSTEM. - ⭐ **Token impersonation** via `SeImpersonatePrivilege` (PrintSpoofer, JuicyPotato, GodPotato) → local privilege escalation to SYSTEM. - ⭐ **Default ports**: SMB=445, RDP=3389, Kerberos=88, LDAP=389, WinRM=5985. - ⭐ **SYSTEM SID** = `S-1-5-18`; **Domain Admins RID** = `512`; **Administrator RID** = `500`. - ⭐ **Defense triad**: Credential Guard (VBS isolation) + Protected Process Light + LSA Protection (`RunAsPPL`).

topics.title: Installing Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Installing Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber Security (KTU 2024 Scheme – PBCST604)**, *Installing Applications* refers to the systematic, security-audited process of deploying software programs, utilities, and system services onto a computing device (desktop, server, mobile, or IoT endpoint) through a controlled, verifiable, and integrity-preserving mechanism. The process encompasses package acquisition, authenticity verification, privilege mediation, dependency resolution, and post-installation hardening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Application installation is *not merely a "Next-Next-Finish" routine*. Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, it is treated as a **critical security perimeter event** — the moment a foreign binary gains execution privileges on a host.

The installation workflow is governed by three foundational security principles:

1. **Authentication of Source** – Verifying the publisher's identity via digital certificates.
2. **Integrity Verification** – Ensuring the binary has not been tampered with via cryptographic hashes.
3. **Least Privilege Mediation** – Granting only the minimum system permissions required for functionality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of installing an application as **hiring a stranger to work inside your house**:

- The **installer (.exe, .dmg, .apk, .deb)** is the *stranger* knocking at your door.
- The **digital signature / code certificate** is their *government-issued ID card* — you verify it before letting them in.
- The **hash checksum (SHA-256 / MD5)** is the *sealed background-verification report* that proves they are who they say they are and haven't been bribed.
- The **UAC prompt / sudo password** is the *homeowner's interview* where you grant specific room access.
- The **anti-virus scan during installation** is the *neighbourhood watch* doing a final pat-down.
- The **post-install firewall rule** is the *visitor badge* that limits which rooms they can enter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Parallel:** Just as you wouldn't let an unverified contractor rewire your home's electrical panel, you should never install an unsigned application with *Administrator / Root* privileges without integrity checks.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Package Manager** | A software tool (APT, YUM, DNF, Chocolatey, Homebrew) that automates installation, upgrades, and removal. |
| **Code Signing Certificate** | An X.509 digital certificate used by developers to sign executables and confirm authenticity. |
| **Trusted Root CA** | A Certificate Authority pre-installed in the OS trust store (e.g., VeriSign, DigiCert). |
| **UAC (User Account Control)** | Windows security feature that mediates privilege escalation during install. |
| **Sandbox** | Isolated runtime environment that restricts an app's access to system resources. |
| **Sideloading** | Installing an app from a source outside the official app store (high-risk in cyber-security). |
| **Whitelisting** | Security policy allowing ONLY pre-approved applications to execute. |
| **Dependency** | A secondary software component required for the primary app to function (DLLs, libraries). |
| **Repository** | A centralized, curated server hosting verified software packages. |
| **Manifest File** | A metadata file describing app version, permissions, publisher, and dependencies. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Installation Security Model

The KTU 2024 Cyber Security curriculum models application installation as a **five-phase pipeline**. Each phase is a potential attack surface.

### Phase 1 — Acquisition (Source Authentication)
- Software is downloaded from an **official repository**, **vendor portal**, or **app store**.
- Cyber-security concern: **Supply-chain attacks** (e.g., the infamous *SolarWinds* and *XZ Utils backdoor* incidents).
- Mitigation: Always prefer HTTPS-enabled official mirrors; reject third-party mirrors.

### Phase 2 — Integrity Verification (Cryptographic Hashing)
- A **hash function** $H(M)$ maps the binary $M$ to a fixed-length digest $h$.
- The user compares the locally computed hash with the **publisher-published hash**.
- If $H_{\text{local}}(M) = H_{\text{published}}(M)$, integrity is preserved.

### Phase 3 — Signature Validation (Public Key Infrastructure)
- The publisher signs the hash using their **private key**: $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$.
- The OS verifies the signature using the publisher's **public key** from the X.509 certificate: $\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(S) = H(M)$.
- A **valid chain of trust** must trace back to a Trusted Root CA in the OS keystore.

### Phase 4 — Privilege Elevation (Authorization)
- The installer requests elevated rights (Administrator on Windows, Root on Linux, Device Admin on Android).
- **UAC/sudo** acts as the gatekeeper.
- Principle of **Least Privilege** dictates that the runtime app should drop admin rights post-installation.

### Phase 5 — Post-Installation Hardening
- Configure firewall rules.
- Apply security templates (CIS Benchmarks).
- Enable automatic updates.
- Disable unnecessary background services and telemetry.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of literal pipes inside table cells to comply with the markdown-table integrity safeguard.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hash Integrity Check** | $H_{\text{local}}(M) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{published}}(M)$ | $M$ = binary, $H$ = hash function (SHA-256) | Detects tampering of installers. |
| **Digital Signature** | $S = E_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ | $E$ = signing algorithm, $K_{priv}$ = publisher's private key | Proves authorship and non-repudiation. |
| **Signature Verification** | $D_{K_{pub}}(S) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ | $D$ = verification, $K_{pub}$ = public key from cert | OS-level trust validation. |
| **Collision Resistance** | $P(H(M_1) = H(M_2)) \approx 2^{-n}$ | $n$ = hash output bits (e.g., 256 for SHA-256) | Quantifies hash security. |
| **Risk Score (CVSS-lite)** | $R = (C + I + A) \times E$ | $C$ = Criticality, $I$ = Impact, $A$ = Attack-surface, $E$ = Exposure | Prioritizing patches. |
| **Trust Chain Depth** | $T = d \leq 3$ | $d$ = certificate chain levels | Practical OS trust-store limit. |
| **Sandbox Memory Cap** | $M_{\text{sandbox}} \leq \alpha \cdot M_{\text{total}}$ | $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.25]$ typical | Mobile OS resource isolation. |
| **Least Privilege Rule** | $\text{Perm}(App) = \min\{P \mid App \text{ functional}\}$ | $P$ = permission set | Zero-Trust install policy. |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

- **Enterprise IT Management:** Organizations use **SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager)**, **Intune**, and **Jamf** to push vetted installers across thousands of endpoints.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines:** CI/CD systems (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) hash-verify every artifact before deployment to production servers.
- **Mobile Security:** Android's **Google Play Protect** and Apple's **App Review** process are large-scale, automated installation security gates.
- **IoT Firmware:** Routers and smart devices verify firmware signatures before flashing — a direct application of installation-time cryptographic verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hash-Based Integrity Verification

We will derive the complete verification logic a secure installer performs when comparing a downloaded binary against a publisher's published hash.

### Step 1 — Define the Binary Message
Let $M$ be the application installer binary (a sequence of bytes of length $L$):

$$ M = \{b_1, b_2, b_3, \dots, b_L\} $$

where each $b_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, 255\}$.

### Step 2 — Publisher Computes Hash
The publisher applies the SHA-256 hash function $H$ to $M$, producing a 256-bit digest $h_p$:

$$ h_p = H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M) $$

The publisher then publishes $h_p$ on their **HTTPS-secured official website**.

### Step 3 — User Downloads Binary
The user downloads $M'$ from the (potentially compromised) network. We assume:

$$ M' = M \oplus \Delta $$

where $\Delta$ represents any tampering (bit-flips, injected malware payload, etc.). If $\Delta = \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector), the file is intact.

### Step 4 — User Recomputes Hash Locally
The user runs the same hash algorithm on $M'$:

$$ h_u = H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M') $$

### Step 5 — Comparison
The secure installer performs a **constant-time comparison** to prevent timing attacks:

$$ \text{Result} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } h_u = h_p \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } h_u \neq h_p \end{cases} $$

### Step 6 — Probability of Collision (Why SHA-256 is Secure)
The probability that an adversary can craft a malicious $M''$ with $H(M'') = H(M)$ is, by the **birthday-bound theorem**:

$$ P_{\text{collision}} \leq \frac{q(q-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{256}} $$

For $q = 2^{128}$ attempts (more atoms than exist on Earth), this probability remains effectively zero. Hence SHA-256 is considered cryptographically secure for installation integrity.

### Step 7 — Summary Verification Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Integrity}(M') &:= \big[ H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M') = h_p \big] \\
&:= \big[ H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M \oplus \Delta) = H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M) \big] \\
&\iff \Delta = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(for a cryptographically strong } H\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The implication $\Delta = \mathbf{0}$ holds *only* because $H$ is a **pre-image resistant, second pre-image resistant, and collision-resistant** hash function. This is the mathematical foundation of secure installation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Installation Risk vs. Source Type

| Source Type | Trust Level | Hash Verification | Code Signed | UAC Prompted | Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Official App Store (Google Play, MS Store, App Store) | Very High | Yes (auto) | Yes (auto) | Yes | **Yes** |
| Vendor Official Site (HTTPS) | High | Manual (check) | Yes | Yes | **Yes** |
| Open-Source Repository (APT, YUM, Chocolatey) | High | Yes (GPG-signed) | Yes (maintainer key) | Yes (sudo) | **Yes** |
| Third-Party Mirror | Medium | Required | Variable | Yes | **Caution** |
| Peer-to-Peer / Torrent | Very Low | Unverifiable | Often unsigned | Often bypassed | **No** |
| Email Attachment | Very Low | Not applicable | Rarely | Often pre-granted | **No** |
| Sideloaded APK / IPA | Variable | Manual | Variable | Bypassed on dev devices | **No** |

## 3.3 Decision Tree: "Should I Install This Application?"

```
START
  │
  ├── Is the source an official app store or vendor HTTPS site?
  │     ├── NO  → ABORT. Treat as untrusted.
  │     └── YES ↓
  │
  ├── Is the binary digitally signed by a trusted CA?
  │     ├── NO  → ABORT. Signature missing.
  │     └── YES ↓
  │
  ├── Does the SHA-256 hash match the publisher's published value?
  │     ├── NO  → ABORT. File tampered.
  │     └── YES ↓
  │
  ├── Does the requested permission set follow Least Privilege?
  │     ├── NO  → DENY elevation. Install in user-space.
  │     └── YES ↓
  │
  └── PROCEED with installation + enable auto-updates.
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Secure Application Installation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Install] --> B[Acquisition Module]
    B --> C{Source Verification}
    C -->|Trusted| D[Download Binary M]
    C -->|Untrusted| X1[ABORT - Block]
    D --> E[Hash Computation Module]
    E --> F[Local Hash hu]
    F --> G{Hash Compare}
    G -->|Match| H[Signature Verification Module]
    G -->|Mismatch| X2[ABORT - Tampered]
    H --> I{Valid Signature}
    I -->|Yes| J[Privilege Elevation Request]
    I -->|No| X3[ABORT - Unsigned]
    J --> K[UAC / Sudo Prompt]
    K --> L[User Confirms]
    L --> M[Installer Executes]
    M --> N[Dependency Resolution]
    N --> O[Post-Install Hardening]
    O --> P[Firewall Rules Applied]
    P --> Q[Auto-Update Enabled]
    Q --> R[Installation Complete]
```

## 4.2 Threat-Surface Map: Where Attackers Strike During Installation

```mermaid

    end

    end

        E2[Cryptominer Dropper]
        E3[Backdoor Service]
    end
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5
```

## 4.3 Defence-in-Depth Layering Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Official Repos Only] --> L2[Layer 2: HTTPS Download]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Hash Verification SHA-256]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Code Signature Validation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: UAC / Sudo Mediation]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Anti-Malware Real-Time Scan]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Sandboxing / User Account]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8: Post-Install Patch Cycle]
    L8 --> L9[Layer 9: EDR / SIEM Telemetry]
```

```mermaid

        A4 --> A5[Install]
    end
    subgraph IOS[iOS Pipeline]
        I1[App Store] --> I2[Apple Review Team]
        I2 --> I3[Code Signing Mandatory]

        I4 --> I5[Install]
    end
    ANDROID -.Sideload Blocked by Default.-> BLOCK[Disable Unknown Sources]
    IOS -.Enterprise Cert Required.-> ENT[MDM Provisioning]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Define *code signing* and explain its role in secure application installation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Code signing is the process of digitally signing an executable or installer using the developer's **private key** bound to an X.509 certificate issued by a Trusted Certificate Authority. The signature $S = E_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ travels alongside the binary. During installation, the OS decrypts $S$ using the publisher's public key and compares the recovered hash with the freshly computed hash of the binary. A match confirms **(1) Authenticity** (publisher identity), **(2) Integrity** (no tampering), and **(3) Non-repudiation** (publisher cannot deny authorship). This prevents attackers from distributing malware disguised as legitimate software.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
What is *sideloading* and why is it considered a high-risk installation method?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sideloading is the practice of installing applications on a device from a source **outside the official, curated app store** — for example, installing an `.apk` file directly on Android after enabling "Install Unknown Apps." It is high-risk because: **(1)** the binary bypasses the platform's automated malware scanning (e.g., Google Play Protect), **(2)** the signature is often self-signed or unverified, removing the chain-of-trust guarantee, **(3)** the user typically disables UAC-equivalent prompts, granting excessive permissions, and **(4)** the dependency graph is manually maintained, increasing the risk of trojanized libraries. Hence cyber-security frameworks (NIST SP 800-167) classify sideloaded apps as untrusted by default.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing hashing with encryption, (b) omitting the *role of the Trusted Root CA*, (c) failing to mention *Least Privilege* in UAC discussions, and (d) writing flow diagrams without arrows/labels. Always show the **publisher-side** and **user-side** steps **separately**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five phases of secure application installation** as per the KTU Cyber Security framework. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the process of **digital signature verification** during installation. Include the role of the certificate chain of trust. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five Phases of Secure Application Installation (7 marks):**

1. **Acquisition (2 marks):** Software is obtained from an official, HTTPS-secured repository or vendor site. The threat here is the *supply-chain attack*; mitigation is to pin downloads to known-good URLs and verify TLS certificates.

2. **Integrity Verification (2 marks):** The user computes a SHA-256 (or SHA-3) hash of the downloaded file and compares it byte-for-byte with the publisher's published digest. Any mismatch implies tampering; installation must be aborted.

3. **Signature Validation (1 mark):** The OS inspects the embedded X.509 code-signing certificate, walks the chain up to a Trusted Root CA in the OS keystore, and cryptographically verifies the signature against the binary's hash.

4. **Privilege Elevation (1 mark):** The installer requests Administrator/Root rights via UAC or sudo. The user should grant the *minimum* rights necessary (Least Privilege Principle).

5. **Post-Installation Hardening (1 mark):** Firewall rules are configured, automatic update channels are enabled, unnecessary services are disabled, and the app is enrolled in EDR/SIEM telemetry if enterprise-managed.

**(b) Digital Signature Verification Process (7 marks):**

The publisher-side flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_p &= H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M) \\
S &= E_{K^{\text{publisher}}_{priv}}(h_p) \\
\text{Bundle} &= \{M,\; S,\; \text{Cert}_{X.509}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The user/OS-side flow during installation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_u &= H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M') \\
h_r &= D_{K^{CA}_{pub}}(S) \\
\text{Valid} &:= (h_u = h_r) \;\wedge\; \text{Cert Chain reaches Trusted Root}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **chain of trust** is constructed as follows:
- **Level 0:** Trusted Root CA (pre-installed in OS keystore — e.g., DigiCert, Sectigo).
- **Level 1:** Intermediate CA (signed by Root).
- **Level 2:** Code Signing Certificate (signed by Intermediate, issued to the publisher).

The OS walks this chain bottom-up, verifying each signature. If any link fails, or if the root is untrusted, the binary is rejected — typically triggering a SmartScreen warning on Windows or an "Unknown Developer" alert on macOS.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **package managers (APT, YUM, Chocolatey)** with **manual installer execution** from a security standpoint. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **principle of Least Privilege** in the context of UAC (User Account Control) and how it applies during application installation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Package Managers vs. Manual Installers (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Package Manager (APT/YUM) | Manual Installer (.exe/.dmg) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Trust** | Curated repositories with GPG-signed metadata. | User must independently verify the website. |
| **Hash Verification** | Automatic via package metadata. | Manual checksum comparison required. |
| **Dependency Handling** | Automatic resolution and conflict detection. | Manual; risk of *dependency hell*. |
| **Update Mechanism** | Centralized (`apt upgrade`). | Per-vendor; fragmentation risk. |
| **Rollback / Uninstall** | Clean removal via `apt remove`. | Often leaves registry entries and orphaned files. |
| **Audit Trail** | Logs every transaction to `/var/log/apt`. | Limited OS-level logging. |
| **Supply-Chain Risk** | Lower (small maintainer pool). | Higher (per-vendor trust). |
| **Privilege Footprint** | Elevated only during install/upgrade. | Often persistent elevation. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Package managers are the **security-preferred** mechanism in managed environments because they provide cryptographically signed, centrally audited, idempotent, and reversible software lifecycle management.

**(b) Least Privilege & UAC (7 marks):**

The **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** states that every process should operate with the *minimum set of permissions* necessary to perform its function — and **no more**. Formally:

$$
\text{Perm}(P) = \min\{ p \in \mathcal{P} \mid \text{Task}(P) \text{ is satisfiable} \}
$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the universe of available permissions and $P$ is the running process.

**Application to UAC (5 marks breakdown):**
1. **Standard User Mode (2 marks):** By default, Windows users run as *Standard Users* with limited privileges. Most daily applications (browsers, Office) function fully in this mode.
2. **Installer Detection Heuristic (1 mark):** When a binary requests elevation, UAC's *Installer Detection* examines file metadata (manifest, publisher, filename heuristics). If it identifies an installer, it auto-elevates *only the install operation*, not the post-install runtime.
3. **Consent Prompt (1 mark):** For unsigned or unfamiliar installers, UAC presents a *consent prompt* (username + password of an admin) — never auto-grants.
4. **Integrity Levels (1 mark):** Windows enforces Mandatory Integrity Control: even an elevated process runs at *Medium* or *High* IL, while user data resides at *Low* IL, providing OS-enforced privilege separation.

**Engineering Utility:** UAC embodies PoLP by ensuring that a malicious DLL-injected into a running app cannot silently gain SYSTEM-level access — it would need to trigger and survive a separate UAC prompt, which is auditable and user-mediated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in PBCST604 Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing hashing with encryption.** Hashing is *one-way* (no decryption key). Encryption is *two-way* (key-based). Digital signatures use **hashing + asymmetric encryption** together.
> 2. **Omitting the chain of trust.** Stating "the OS verifies the signature" without mentioning the **Trusted Root CA** and **X.509 chain validation** loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Least Privilege.** UAC questions always expect a *least-privilege* angle. Merely defining UAC as a "pop-up box" scores poorly.
> 4. **Skipping the publisher-side steps.** Signature verification is a *two-party* protocol. Forgetting to describe the publisher's signing process loses 3 marks.
> 5. **Treating sideloading casually.** Examiners expect the term *"untrusted source"* and a reference to *Play Protect / App Review* gatekeeping.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Installation is a security event**, not a routine utility operation.
- [x] The **five-phase pipeline**: Acquisition → Integrity → Signature → Privilege → Hardening.
- [x] **SHA-256** is the de-facto integrity hash; MD5/SHA-1 are deprecated.
- [x] **Code signing** = Publisher's private key signs the binary's hash.
- [x] **X.509 chain of trust** must terminate at a Trusted Root CA in the OS keystore.
- [x] **UAC / sudo** mediates privilege elevation; UAC uses *Installer Detection Heuristic* and *Integrity Levels*.
- [x] **Least Privilege Principle** = minimum permissions for functionality.
- [x] **Sideloading** is high-risk; prefer official stores and curated repositories.
- [x] **Package managers** (APT, YUM, Chocolatey) are security-superior to manual installers.
- [x] **Post-install hardening** includes firewall rules, auto-updates, EDR enrolment, and service minimization.
- [x] **Supply-chain attacks** (SolarWinds, XZ Utils) target the acquisition phase — pin and verify everything.
- [x] **Mobile platforms** (Android, iOS) implement signature + sandbox + permission review as defense-in-depth.
- [x] Remember: $H_{\text{local}}(M) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{published}}(M)$ is the integrity equation.
- [x] Always prefer **HTTPS** sources; reject HTTP downloads for installers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication and Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication and Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Authentication

**Authentication** is the security mechanism by which a system verifies the identity of a user, process, or device, typically as a prerequisite to granting access to resources. It answers the question: *"Who are you?"* — and operates on the principle of presenting and validating one or more **identity factors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (PBCST604 Module 4):** Authentication is the process of confirming the identity of a subject (user/process) through the presentation of credentials, which are then validated against a trusted authority before any authorization decision is rendered.

### The Three Pillars of Authentication Factors

| Factor Type | What it Verifies | Real-World Example | Security Strength |
|-------------|------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Knowledge Factor (Something You Know)** | Information stored in memory | Password, PIN, security question | Low–Medium (vulnerable to phishing/brute-force) |
| **Possession Factor (Something You Have)** | Physical/digital token in possession | OTP token, smart card, mobile authenticator app | Medium (vulnerable to theft/SIM-swap) |
| **Inherence Factor (Something You Are)** | Unique biological trait | Fingerprint, iris scan, facial geometry | High (requires liveness detection) |
| **Location Factor (Somewhere You Are)** | Geospatial context | IP geofencing, GPS coordinates | Supplementary |
| **Behavior Factor (Something You Do)** | Behavioral biometrics | Keystroke dynamics, mouse trajectory | Emerging / AI-augmented |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multifactor Authentication (MFA)** requires at least **two** factors from *different* categories (e.g., password + OTP = 2FA). Presenting two passwords is *not* MFA — it is merely two-step single-factor.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Airport Boarding Gate

Imagine you are at an international airport boarding gate. Three sequential checks occur:

1. **Passport check (Authentication - Identity):** The officer verifies your face against the photo and stamps your passport. *You prove who you are.*
2. **Boarding pass scan (Authorization - Permission):** The scanner confirms your seat class, flight number, and gate access. *The system decides what you may do.*
3. **TSA PreCheck lane (Access Control - Policy):** Only those who pre-registered may use the fast lane. *A rule enforces who may access what.*

Authentication and authorization are **distinct** operations: authentication happens *first*, and its success is the gate to authorization.

## 1.2 Access Control

**Access Control** is the policy-driven mechanism that determines *what* an authenticated subject is permitted to do — it answers the question: *"What are you allowed to do?"* It operates on the **AAA triad**: **Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (Auditing)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Control** = the selective restriction of access to a resource, governed by a formal policy that specifies which subjects can perform which operations on which objects.

### Intuitive Analogy: The Office Building

A corporate office is a perfect mental model:
- **Lobby badge check (Authentication):** The security desk verifies your employee ID.
- **Floor-access keycard (Authorization):** Your badge's embedded clearance level determines which floors open.
- **Audit log (Accounting):** Every badge swipe is timestamped and stored in the security database.

You are **authenticated once** at the lobby, but **authorized differently** on every floor you approach.

---

## 1.3 Relationship Between Authentication and Access Control

The two concepts are intertwined but operationally separable:

- **Authentication** = Identity verification (a 1:1 trust handshake).
- **Authorization / Access Control** = Right enforcement (a 1:N policy lookup).

In formal security models, the order is always: **Identification → Authentication → Authorization → Accountability (Logging)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently conflate authentication and authorization. A correct exam answer must always cite that *authentication precedes authorization* and that access control is the broader umbrella governing the entire post-authentication session.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Authentication Mechanisms — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Password-Based Authentication

The oldest and most ubiquitous mechanism. Security hinges on **entropy**, **storage hashing**, and **transit protection**.

**Entropy** is the measure of uncertainty (in bits) in a password. The standard formula for password entropy is:

$$
H = L \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

Where:
- $H$ = password entropy in **bits**
- $L$ = password length (number of characters)
- $N$ = size of the character pool (e.g., 26 lowercase, 52 mixed-case, 62 alphanumeric, 94 printable ASCII)

**Why this matters:** Each additional bit of entropy doubles the brute-force search space. A 6-character lowercase password has $H = 6 \cdot \log_2(26) \approx 28.2$ bits — crackable in seconds on a GPU rig. An 8-character mixed-case + digit + symbol password yields $H = 8 \cdot \log_2(94) \approx 52.4$ bits — centuries to brute-force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage best practice:** Never store passwords in plaintext. Use adaptive hashing algorithms: **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2id**. The adaptive cost factor forces attackers to pay CPU/memory per guess.

### 2.1.2 Biometric Authentication

Biometric systems use physiological or behavioral traits. The system operates on two error rates critical to KTU exams:

| Metric | Definition | Direction of Improvement |
|--------|------------|--------------------------|
| **FAR (False Accept Rate)** | Probability that an impostor is incorrectly accepted | Lower is better (security) |
| **FRR (False Reject Rate)** | Probability that a legitimate user is incorrectly rejected | Lower is better (usability) |
| **CER / EER (Equal Error Rate)** | The point where FAR = FRR | Lower is better (overall system quality) |

**Why FAR and FRR trade off:** Tightening the matching threshold (e.g., requiring 95% fingerprint match) lowers FAR but raises FRR, and vice versa. The **Equal Error Rate (EER)** is the canonical single-number quality benchmark for a biometric system.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

Combines factors from independent categories. A common implementation is **TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password)**, governed by **RFC 6238**:

$$
\text{OTP} = \text{HOTP}(K, T) = \text{Truncate}\bigl(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, T)\bigr) \pmod{10^6}
$$

Where $T = \lfloor (t_{\text{current}} - t_0) / X \rfloor$ with $X = 30$ seconds (the standard time step), $t_0$ is the Unix epoch, and $K$ is the shared 160-bit secret.

### 2.1.4 Single Sign-On (SSO) and Federated Identity

SSO permits a user to authenticate *once* and access multiple independent systems. **Kerberos** (MIT, 1988) and **SAML 2.0** are the canonical KTU-relevant protocols.

## 2.2 Access Control Models — Deep Analysis

The four classical models are universally tested in KTU boards.

### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

- The resource **owner** decides who can access it.
- Implemented via an **Access Control List (ACL)** per object.
- Vulnerable to **Trojan horse** and confused-deputy attacks because users can inadvertently leak rights.

### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

- Access is governed by **system-wide labels** (classifications) and a **central security policy** — owners cannot override.
- The **Bell-LaPadula** model enforces **confidentiality** (no read up, no write down).
- The **Biba** model enforces **integrity** (no read down, no write up).

### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

- Permissions are assigned to **roles**, and roles are assigned to users.
- The **NIST RBAC standard (INCITS 359-2012)** defines:
  - Flat RBAC
  - Hierarchical RBAC (roles inherit)
  - Constrained RBAC (separation of duties)
  - Symmetric RBAC (full bidirectional review)

### 2.2.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

- Decisions are dynamic, evaluated against **subject attributes, object attributes, action attributes, and environmental attributes**.
- The canonical formal model uses the predicate:

$$
\text{Access}(s, o, a) \iff \text{PolicyEval}\bigl(\text{Attrs}(s),\ \text{Attrs}(o),\ \text{Attrs}(a),\ \text{Attrs}(env)\bigr)
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision sheet** for Module 4. Memorize it before the exam.

| Concept | Key Formula / Property | Variables / Notes |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = pool size |
| Key Space Search Time | $T_{\text{crack}} = \dfrac{2^H}{R}$ | $R$ = guesses/sec, $H$ in bits |
| Biometric CER/EER | $\text{FAR}(t) = \text{FRR}(t)$ at $t = t_{\text{EER}}$ | Threshold parameter $t$ |
| TOTP Code | $\text{TOTP} = \text{HOTP}(K, \lfloor (t-t_0)/30 \rfloor) \bmod 10^6$ | $K$ = 160-bit shared secret |
| Bell-LaPadula | No Read Up (NRU), No Write Down (NWD) | Confidentiality model |
| Biba | No Read Down (NRD), No Write Up (NWU) | Integrity model |
| Clark-Wilson | Well-formed transactions + Separation of Duties | Commercial integrity |
| Brewer-Nash | No conflict-of-interest between subjects | Dynamic confidentiality |
| RBAC Constraint | $U \cap S = \varnothing$ (Static Separation of Duty) | $U$ = user, $S$ = mutually-exclusive role set |
| ABAC Decision | Boolean predicate over attribute tuples | XACML is the standard policy language |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Authentication and access control are foundational to *every* secure system: OS kernels (Windows Mandatory Integrity Control), cloud IAM (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC), database security (PostgreSQL GRANT/REVOKE), and zero-trust network architectures (BeyondCorp, ZTNA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Entropy and Brute-Force Time

We derive the expected number of guesses a brute-force attacker must try, and the corresponding wall-clock time, given an attacker's guess rate $R$ (guesses/second).

### Step 1 — Define the search space

For a password of length $L$ drawn uniformly from an alphabet of size $N$, the total key space is $N^L$.

$$
\text{KeySpace} = N^L
$$

### Step 2 — Convert the key space to bits (entropy)

By the information-theoretic definition, the entropy of a uniformly distributed random variable over $\text{KeySpace}$ is:

$$
H = \log_2(N^L) = L \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

> **Logic:** Each character contributes $\log_2(N)$ bits of uncertainty. $L$ independent characters multiply uncertainties.

### Step 3 — Average guesses to success

On average, an attacker probing uniformly at random will find the correct password after half the key space has been exhausted:

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{N^L}{2} = 2^{H-1}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert average guesses to wall-clock time

$$
T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{\bar{G}}{R} = \frac{2^{H-1}}{R}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked numerical example

Consider an 8-character password using the 94-character printable ASCII set, attacked by a GPU rig at $R = 10^{11}$ guesses/sec.

$$
H = 8 \cdot \log_2(94) = 8 \cdot 6.5546 = 52.44\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
\bar{G} = 2^{52.44 - 1} = 2^{51.44} \approx 3.04 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{3.04 \times 10^{15}}{10^{11}} = 3.04 \times 10^{4}\ \text{seconds} \approx 8.4\ \text{hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A "complex" 8-character password is still crackable in under a day by a modern offline attacker. This is why KTU Module 4 emphasizes **MFA, passphrases, and adaptive hashing** as the real defenses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TOTP Verification Logic

We derive the verification equation that a server uses to validate a TOTP code typed by a user.

### Step 1 — Define the time counter

The server and client share an epoch $t_0$ (Unix time zero by default) and a step $X = 30$ s. At the current Unix time $t$:

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{t - t_0}{X} \right\rfloor
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the HMAC

The client computes an HMAC-SHA-1 of the 8-byte big-endian counter $T$ using the shared secret $K$:

$$
\text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, T) \quad \text{(20 bytes output)}
$$

### Step 3 — Dynamic truncation

The last nibble of the HMAC is an offset $\text{off}$. We extract 4 bytes starting at $\text{off}$, mask the sign bit, and reduce modulo $10^6$ to get a 6-digit decimal code:

$$
\text{code} = \bigl(\text{HMAC}[\text{off}..\text{off}+3]\ \text{AND}\ 0x7FFFFFFF \bigr) \bmod 10^6
$$

### Step 4 — Server validation with drift window

To tolerate clock skew, the server typically verifies against $[T-1, T, T+1]$ and accepts if any match. The effective security factor is $10^6$ per 30-second slot — equivalent to a 20-bit entropy contribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality:** TOTP is *only* secure because the secret $K$ is never transmitted. A network observer sees only the 6-digit code, which becomes useless within 30 seconds. The factor that **prevents** replay is the time step $X$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Access Control Models

| Property | DAC | MAC | RBAC | ABAC |
|----------|-----|-----|------|------|
| **Policy Authority** | Resource owner | System / Security officer | Administrator (role definitions) | Policy engine (predicate rules) |
| **Granularity** | Per-user / per-object | Coarse (label-based) | Coarse to medium (role-based) | Fine-grained (attribute-based) |
| **Flexibility** | High (owner can grant) | Low (rigid) | Medium | Very high |
| **Scalability** | Poor (ACL explosion) | Good for small closed systems | Excellent (industry standard) | Excellent (cloud-native) |
| **Failure Mode** | Trojan horse, confused deputy | Covert channel leakage | Role explosion, role mining complexity | Policy conflict, evaluation latency |
| **Sample Standard** | POSIX ACLs, Windows NTFS DACLs | SELinux, Trusted Solaris | NIST INCITS 359-2012, AWS IAM Roles | XACML 3.0, NIST NGAC |
| **Confidentiality?** | Optional | Yes (Bell-LaPadula) | Optional | Optional |
| **Integrity?** | Optional | Yes (Biba) | Optional (Clark-Wilson compatible) | Optional |
| **Best-fit Domain** | Collaborative file systems | Military / government | Enterprise IT, SaaS | Cloud, healthcare, IoT zero-trust |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** When asked to "justify" an access control model in a 7-mark question, always anchor the answer in **threat model → policy authority → granularity → failure mode**. Examiners award 7 marks for **systematic reasoning**, not a one-line definition.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Bell-LaPadula Mandatory Access Control

Suppose a system has two security levels: **PUBLIC (L0)** and **SECRET (L1)**.

A subject at level **L1** with clearance *Top Secret* on an object labelled *Secret*:

- **Read operation (NRU — No Read Up):** Subject clearance $\geq$ Object classification. Here L1 $\geq$ L1, so **read is permitted**.
- **Write operation (NWD — No Write Down):** Subject clearance $\leq$ Object classification. Here L1 $\leq$ L1, so **write is permitted**.

If the same subject writes to a *Public* object:

- Subject at L1, Object at L0 → NWD violated (L1 $\not\leq$ L0). **Write is denied.**

> This *star-property (✱)* prevents a SECRET-cleared process from leaking classified data into a PUBLIC object — the foundation of MLS (Multi-Level Security) operating systems like SELinux.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AAA Operational Flow (Authentication → Authorization → Accounting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User attempts access] --> B[Identification: presents username/ID]
    B --> C[Authentication: validates factor 1]
    C --> D{Factor 2 required?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Authentication: validates factor 2]
    D -- No  --> F
    E --> F{Identity verified?}
    F -- No  --> X1[Access Denied: log to audit trail]
    F -- Yes --> G[Authorization: look up policy DB]
    G --> H{Policy permits request?}
    H -- No  --> X2[Access Denied: log to audit trail]
    H -- Yes --> I[Session Token Issued]
    I --> J[Resource Access Granted]
    J --> K[Accounting: every action logged with subject ID, timestamp, object, action]
    K --> L[Audit trail stored in SIEM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every decision diamond ends in either an *Access Denied* path that still produces an **audit log entry**, or a permitted path. KTU examiners will mark *missing audit* answers as incomplete.

## 4.2 Access Control Model Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Organization threat model] --> B{Centralized authority?}
    B -- Yes --> C{High-integrity data?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Mandatory Access Control]
    C -- No  --> E{Large user base?}
    B -- No  --> F[Discretionary Access Control]
    E -- Yes --> G[Role-Based Access Control]
    E -- No  --> F
    G --> H{Dynamic context needed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Attribute-Based Access Control]
    H -- No  --> G
```

## 4.3 Kerberos Authentication Exchange (Simplified)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant V as Target Service

    C->>AS: 1. Request TGT (plaintext user ID, no password)
    AS-->>C: 2. Encrypted TGT + session key (keyed with user password hash)
    C->>TGS: 3. Request Service Ticket (TGT + service ID)
    TGS-->>C: 4. Service Ticket + session key for service
    C->>V: 5. Present Service Ticket + authenticator
    V-->>C: 6. Mutual authentication established
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for KTU:** The user's password *never* traverses the network. The AS encrypts the TGT with a key derived from the user's password, so only the legitimate user (who knows the password) can decrypt it.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ABAC Policy Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PIP[Policy Information Point]
        ATTR1[Subject Attributes]
        ATTR2[Object Attributes]
        ATTR3[Action Attributes]
        ATTR4[Environment Attributes]
    end

    subgraph PEP[Policy Enforcement Point]
        REQ[Incoming Access Request]
        DEC[Permit / Deny Decision]
    end

    subgraph PDP[Policy Decision Point]
        EVAL[Boolean Predicate Evaluator]
        RULES[(Policy Set XACML)]
    end

    subgraph PAP[Policy Administration Point]
        ADMIN[Policy Authoring Tool]
    end

    REQ --> EVAL
    ATTR1 --> EVAL
    ATTR2 --> EVAL
    ATTR3 --> EVAL
    ATTR4 --> EVAL
    RULES --> EVAL
    ADMIN --> RULES
    EVAL --> DEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **XACML reference architecture:** The four components — **PEP, PDP, PAP, PIP** — are the standardized building blocks of the OASIS XACML 3.0 specification. A 14-mark KTU question may ask you to label this flow precisely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between authentication and authorization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Authentication** is the process of *verifying the identity* of a user or process. It answers "Who are you?" Example: A user entering a username and password on a banking portal login page, which the server validates against its credential database.
- **Authorization** is the process of *enforcing access rights* after identity has been established. It answers "What are you allowed to do?" Example: The same user, once logged in, is permitted to view their own account balance but is denied access to another customer's account because their **ACL** does not include the target object.
- **Key distinction:** Authentication *precedes* authorization; a user cannot be authorized without first being authenticated. (1 mark for distinction, 1 mark each for the two examples.)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three authentication factors defined in the ISO/IEC 27000 series and state which factor is strongest against remote credential theft.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Knowledge factor** — something the user knows (password, PIN).
2. **Possession factor** — something the user has (smart card, hardware token).
3. **Inherence factor** — something the user is (fingerprint, iris).

**Strongest against remote credential theft:** The **inherence factor (biometrics)**, because a remote attacker cannot replay a fingerprint. However, modern MFA combining **possession + knowledge** (e.g., password + TOTP) is the practical best defense in distributed systems. (1 mark per factor + 0.5 + 0.5 for the strength reasoning.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four classical access control models: **DAC, MAC, RBAC, and ABAC**, with one real-world system implementing each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Bell-LaPadula confidentiality model** and the **Biba integrity model** in detail. Construct a 3×3 access matrix showing permitted operations for a subject at level SECRET reading and writing objects at levels {PUBLIC, CONFIDENTIAL, SECRET}. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution (Question A)**

#### Part (a) — Four Access Control Models (7 marks)

- **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** The resource owner controls access. Implemented as per-object **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**. Example: **Unix/Linux file permissions** (`rwx` triplet per user/group/other) or **Windows NTFS DACLs**. Mark: 1.5
- **MAC (Mandatory Access Control):** Access governed by a system-enforced security label hierarchy. Owners cannot override. Example: **SELinux** (NSA-developed, used in Red Hat Enterprise Linux and Android). Mark: 1.5
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Permissions are bound to roles; users acquire permissions by being assigned to roles. Standardized by **NIST INCITS 359-2012**. Example: **AWS IAM Roles** or **Microsoft Active Directory Groups**. Mark: 2
- **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):** Decisions evaluated dynamically by predicates over subject/object/action/environment attributes, defined in **XACML 3.0** (OASIS). Example: **AWS IAM with attribute-based conditions** (`aws:RequestTag`, `aws:PrincipalTag`). Mark: 2

#### Part (b) — Bell-LaPadula and Biba + 3×3 Matrix (7 marks)

**Bell-LaPadula Model** enforces *confidentiality* through two rules (3 marks):

- **No Read Up (NRU / Simple Security Property):** A subject at level $L_s$ may read an object at level $L_o$ only if $L_s \geq L_o$.
- **No Write Down (NWD / Star Property ✱):** A subject at level $L_s$ may write to an object at level $L_o$ only if $L_s \leq L_o$.

**Biba Model** enforces *integrity* through two inverse rules (2 marks):

- **No Read Down (NRD):** A subject may read an object only if subject's integrity level ≤ object's integrity level.
- **No Write Up (NWU):** A subject may write to an object only if subject's integrity level ≥ object's integrity level.

**3×3 Access Matrix (Bell-LaPadula rules, Subject = SECRET):**

| Operation \ Object Level | PUBLIC | CONFIDENTIAL | SECRET |
|--------------------------|--------|--------------|--------|
| **READ** (NRU: $L_s \geq L_o$) | ✅ Permitted (SECRET ≥ PUBLIC) | ✅ Permitted (SECRET ≥ CONF) | ✅ Permitted (SECRET ≥ SECRET) |
| **WRITE** (NWD: $L_s \leq L_o$) | ❌ Denied (SECRET ≰ PUBLIC) | ❌ Denied (SECRET ≰ CONF) | ✅ Permitted (SECRET ≤ SECRET) |

> **Insight:** The matrix is **not** symmetric — the NWD rule blocks any "write down" that would leak classified information to a lower compartment. This is why MLS databases are always built around Bell-LaPadula. (2 marks for the matrix with correct symbols and reasoning.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Kerberos authentication protocol**. List two attacks Kerberos defends against and one attack it does *not* defend against. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A banking application uses password-based authentication. The password policy is: 6–20 characters, at least one uppercase, one digit, one special character. Calculate the **entropy range** and the **average brute-force time** at a guess rate of $10^{10}$ guesses/sec. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution (Question B)**

#### Part (a) — Kerberos Protocol (7 marks)

Kerberos is a **trusted third-party authentication protocol** developed at MIT (RFC 4120). It uses **tickets** (time-limited, encrypted credentials) instead of transmitting passwords.

**Phases (with diagram, 4 marks):**

1. **Authentication Service Exchange:**
   - Client → AS: plaintext user ID (no password).
   - AS → Client: TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) encrypted with the user's password-derived key + a session key.
2. **Ticket Granting Service Exchange:**
   - Client → TGS: TGT + requested service ID.
   - TGS → Client: Service Ticket encrypted with the service's secret key + new session key.
3. **Client/Server Exchange:**
   - Client → Service: Service Ticket + authenticator (timestamp encrypted with session key).
   - Service → Client: Mutual authentication reply (timestamp + 1, encrypted).

*(Insert the sequence diagram from Section 4.3 of these notes for full marks.)*

**Attacks Defended Against (2 marks):**
- **Replay attacks:** Timestamps and authenticators with bounded validity windows (typically 5 minutes) make captured tickets unusable.
- **Eavesdropping on passwords:** Passwords never traverse the network; only encrypted tickets do.

**Attack *Not* Defended Against (1 mark):**
- **Compromise of the KDC (Key Distribution Center):** If the KDC itself is breached, an attacker can mint arbitrary tickets for any service — Kerberos has *no* defense against insider compromise of the central trust anchor.

#### Part (b) — Entropy and Brute-Force Time (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Determine the minimum entropy (1 mark):**

The minimum character pool is the largest constrained set after policy filters. The *effective* pool size is bounded by the *full* printable ASCII set $N = 94$ (since the policy does not exclude lowercase). The minimum length is $L_{\min} = 6$.

$$
H_{\min} = 6 \cdot \log_2(94) = 6 \times 6.5546 = 39.33\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the maximum entropy (1 mark):**

$$
H_{\max} = 20 \cdot \log_2(94) = 20 \times 6.5546 = 131.09\ \text{bits}
$$

**Entropy range: $H \in [39.33,\ 131.09]$ bits.**

**Step 3 — Average brute-force time at the weakest (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{G}_{\min} = 2^{39.33 - 1} = 2^{38.33} \approx 3.55 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{3.55 \times 10^{11}}{10^{10}} \approx 35.5\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Average brute-force time at the strongest (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{G}_{\max} = 2^{131.09 - 1} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{39}
$$

$$
T_{\max} = \frac{2.74 \times 10^{39}}{10^{10}} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{29}\ \text{seconds} \approx 8.7 \times 10^{21}\ \text{years}
$$

> **Engineering conclusion (2 marks):** The *minimum* password in the policy can be cracked in **under a minute** — the policy is dangerously weak at the lower bound. The *maximum* password is computationally infeasible to crack. Therefore, the policy should enforce both a **strong minimum length (≥12)** and **rate-limiting / account lockout** to push the worst-case attacker cost above economic viability. Password *complexity rules alone* are insufficient — this is precisely why NIST SP 800-63B now recommends **length-based** policies over composition rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **No units in final answer** → 1 mark deduction. Always state $H$ in **bits** and $T$ in **seconds/years**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\div 2$ in average guesses** → You will get $T$ off by a factor of 2. State explicitly "average, so divide by 2."
> 3. **Using $N = 62$ (alphanumeric only)** instead of $N = 94$ → The policy permits special characters, so the upper bound is 94.
> 4. **Skipping the policy critique** → Part (b) explicitly asks for engineering judgment, not just arithmetic. The concluding paragraph about NIST SP 800-63B is worth 2 marks and is the difference between a 5/7 and a 7/7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Module 4 — Authentication and Access Control:**

- **Authentication ≠ Authorization.** Authentication verifies *identity*; authorization enforces *rights*. Order: Identification → Authentication → Authorization → Accounting.
- **Three authentication factors:** Knowledge, Possession, Inherence. MFA requires factors from *different* categories.
- **Password entropy formula:** $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$; brute-force time: $T = 2^{H-1} / R$.
- **Storage rule:** Never plaintext — use **bcrypt / scrypt / Argon2id** with a per-user salt.
- **Biometric benchmarks:** **FAR**, **FRR**, **EER** — lower is better for all three; EER is the single-number quality index.
- **TOTP (RFC 6238):** 6-digit code, 30-second step, HMAC-SHA-1 with shared 160-bit secret; the secret never travels the network.
- **Kerberos (RFC 4120):** Trusted third-party (KDC) using tickets; defends against replay and password sniffing; vulnerable to **KDC compromise** and **pass-the-ticket** if not rotated.
- **Four access control models:** DAC (owner decides), MAC (label hierarchy, Bell-LaPadula/Biba), RBAC (NIST INCITS 359-2012), ABAC (XACML 3.0).
- **Bell-LaPadula rules:** NRU + NWD → **confidentiality**.
- **Biba rules:** NRD + NWU → **integrity**.
- **RBAC constraints:** **Static Separation of Duty (SSD)** and **Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD)** prevent fraud by forbidding a user from holding two mutually-exclusive roles.
- **ABAC building blocks:** **PEP, PDP, PIP, PAP** — memorize the OASIS XACML reference architecture.
- **Engineering truth:** Length beats complexity. A 16-character passphrase from a 26-letter lowercase alphabet ($H \approx 75$ bits) is stronger and more memorable than an 8-character `P@ssw0rd!` ($H \approx 52$ bits).
- **Common KTU trap questions:** "Is MAC stronger than DAC?" → *It depends on the threat model.* "Is RBAC always better than ABAC?" → *No — RBAC suffers from role explosion in fine-grained scenarios.*
- **Exam time budget:** A 14-mark question in this module should consume **~25 minutes**. Allocate 12 minutes to diagrams and 13 minutes to written derivation. Always end with a **one-line engineering implication**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upgrades and Patches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Upgrades and Patches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Upgrades** are comprehensive, version-level enhancements to a software system that introduce new features, improve performance, modify architecture, or significantly extend the existing functionality of the application. **Patches** are small, targeted software updates released by vendors to fix specific bugs, security vulnerabilities, or stability issues without fundamentally changing the underlying software's feature set.

According to the KTU 2024 Cyber Security syllabus framework, upgrades and patches constitute the **operational backbone of defensive software hygiene**. They are the primary, vendor-issued mechanisms for remediating known vulnerabilities catalogued under the **Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE)** system and managed through standards like the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)**.

### 1.1.1 Distinguishing Upgrades from Patches

| Attribute | Patches | Upgrades |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Targeted (single bug/fix) | Broad (entire version change) |
| **Frequency** | Weekly/Monthly (Patch Tuesday) | Quarterly/Annually |
| **Risk of Deployment** | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| **Mandatory Nature** | Often critical (Security) | Often optional (Feature) |
| **System Restart** | Sometimes | Frequently |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Car Maintenance Analogy:** Imagine your car is software, and you are driving it on a highway (the internet).
