> - A **Patch** is like replacing a worn-out brake pad. It is small, urgent, fixes one specific safety issue, and doesn't change the car's overall appearance or features.
> - An **Upgrade** is like swapping the entire engine for a newer, more powerful model. The car gets new capabilities (features), better fuel efficiency (performance), but you need to take it to the garage longer and the process is more involved.

**Vaccination Analogy (Medical):** Think of patches like vaccines for software. When a new virus (malware/exploit) is discovered, software vendors release a "vaccine" (patch) that teaches your system to recognize and defend against that specific threat. Upgrades are like the body's natural evolution — building a stronger, smarter immune system over time.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standards

> [!NOTE]
> - **CVE:** Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a unique identifier (e.g., `CVE-2024-12345`) for each publicly known security vulnerability.
> - **CVSS:** Common Vulnerability Scoring System — a standardized score from **0.0 to 10.0** indicating vulnerability severity.
> - **Zero-Day:** A vulnerability exploited by attackers before the vendor has released a patch.
> - **Patch Tuesday:** Microsoft's scheduled second-Tuesday-of-each-month security patch release cycle.
> - **Service Pack (SP):** A cumulative bundle of patches, hotfixes, and improvements rolled into a single deployment package.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Patch Management Lifecycle (PML)

The **Patch Management Lifecycle** is the structured, end-to-end operational process used by IT and Security teams to identify, evaluate, acquire, test, deploy, and verify patches across an organization's digital infrastructure. Below is the canonical 6-stage KTU-aligned framework:

### Stage 1: Identification & Inventory
- Catalog all hardware, operating systems, firmware, and third-party applications.
- Subscribe to vendor security advisories and **CVE databases** (NVD, MITRE).
- Use automated **vulnerability scanners** (e.g., Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS).

### Stage 2: Vulnerability Assessment & Prioritization
- Apply the **CVSS Base Score** to rank vulnerabilities.
- Map each CVE against the **CIA Triad** impact (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- Use contextual risk factors: asset criticality, exposure to internet, data sensitivity.

### Stage 3: Patch Acquisition & Testing
- Download patches from verified vendor portals (avoiding third-party mirrors).
- Deploy in a **staging/lab environment** identical to production.
- Conduct **regression testing** to ensure patches don't break existing functionality.

### Stage 4: Deployment & Distribution
- Use enterprise patch management tools (e.g., **WSUS, SCCM/MECM, Ansible, Puppet**).
- Apply staged rollout: **Pilot → Departmental → Enterprise-wide**.
- Schedule maintenance windows to minimize operational disruption.

### Stage 5: Verification & Compliance Auditing
- Re-scan systems post-deployment to confirm vulnerability remediation.
- Generate compliance reports for standards like **ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, NIST 800-40**.
- Maintain immutable logs for forensic and audit purposes.

### Stage 6: Exception Handling & Rollback Planning
- For patches that fail or cause system instability, document exceptions.
- Maintain a tested **rollback procedure** (snapshots, backup restoration).
- Apply compensating controls (e.g., firewall rules) for un-patchable systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Stage:** Many students memorize these stages but fail to explain the rationale. Each stage addresses a specific risk: identification reduces *blind spots*, testing reduces *deployment failure risk*, and verification prevents *false sense of security*.

## 2.2 Types of Patches (Classification Matrix)

| Patch Type | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Patch** | Fixes a known exploitable vulnerability | CVE remediation, ransomware defense |
| **Hotfix** | Emergency, out-of-cycle patch for critical flaws | Zero-day exploits |
| **Bug Fix Patch** | Resolves non-security functional defects | Application crashes, UI glitches |
| **Service Pack (SP)** | Cumulative rollup of all prior patches | Major version consolidation |
| **Firmware Patch** | Updates low-level hardware/embedded software | Routers, IoT devices, BIOS |
| **Kernel Patch** | Modifies OS core (Linux kernel live-patching) | Zero-downtime server patching |
| **Cumulative Update** | Bundle of all previous patches + new ones | Windows Update monthly rollups |

## 2.3 The CVSS Severity Rating Scale

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is the industry-standard quantitative framework for assessing vulnerability severity. Version **3.1** is the current standard, producing a Base Score from **0.0 to 10.0**.

| CVSS Score Range | Severity Rating | Color Code (Industry) |
|---|---|---|
| **0.0** | None | Grey |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | Low | Yellow |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | Medium | Orange |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | High | Red |
| **9.0 – 10.0** | Critical | Dark Red |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical formulations govern the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** calculation. KTU examiners frequently test the structural understanding of these formulas even when full numerical substitution is not required.

### 2.4.1 Impact Sub-Score (ISC)

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - \left[ (1 - I_C) \times (1 - I_I) \times (1 - I_A) \right]
$$

Where each impact metric takes a value from $\{0, 0.22, 0.56\}$:
- $I_C$ = Impact on Confidentiality
- $I_I$ = Impact on Integrity
- $I_A$ = Impact on Availability

### 2.4.2 Overall Impact Score

$$
\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Unchanged} \\[6pt] 7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope} = \text{Changed} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.4.3 Exploitability Sub-Score

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times A_V \times A_C \times A_I \times P_R \times U_I
$$

Where $A_V$ (Attack Vector), $A_C$ (Attack Complexity), $A_I$ (Privileges Required), $P_R$ (User Interaction), and $U_I$ (all 0.91) are coefficients from the standard CVSS metric table.

### 2.4.4 Final Base Score

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}_{1\,\text{decimal}} \left( \min \left[ \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10 \right] \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if Impact} \leq 0 \\[4pt] \text{as above} & \text{if Impact} > 0 \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Organizations use CVSS scores to automate patch deployment priorities. Most enterprise SIEM/SOAR systems use a threshold (commonly **CVSS ≥ 7.0**) to trigger emergency patching workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: CVSS Base Score Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Scenario (KTU 2024 Style):** Consider a vulnerability in a public-facing web server with the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:
> - Attack Vector (AV) = **Network**
> - Attack Complexity (AC) = **Low**
> - Privileges Required (PR) = **None**
> - User Interaction (UI) = **None**
> - Confidentiality Impact (C) = **High**
> - Integrity Impact (I) = **High**
> - Availability Impact (A) = **High**
> - Scope (S) = **Changed**
>
> **Compute the CVSS Base Score.**

### Step 1: Assign Numerical Values from CVSS Metric Tables

From the official CVSS v3.1 specification:

$$
A_V = 0.85 \quad (\text{Network}), \quad A_C = 0.77 \quad (\text{Low}), \quad P_R = 0.85 \quad (\text{None})
$$

$$
U_I = 0.85 \quad (\text{None}), \quad I_C = 0.56 \quad (\text{High}), \quad I_I = 0.56 \quad (\text{High}), \quad I_A = 0.56 \quad (\text{High})
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each qualitative metric (e.g., "High", "Network") maps to a specific numerical coefficient. These coefficients are standardized and never change between calculations.

### Step 2: Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISC)

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - \left[ (1 - I_C) \times (1 - I_I) \times (1 - I_A) \right]
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \right]
$$

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - \left[ 0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44 \right]
$$

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Overall Impact Score (Scope = Changed)

Since Scope = Changed, we use the second branch of the piecewise formula:

$$
\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02)^{15}
$$

First, compute the inner terms:

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029 = 0.914816 - 0.029 = 0.885816
$$

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02 = 0.914816 - 0.02 = 0.894816
$$

Raise to the 15th power:

$$
(0.894816)^{15} \approx 0.2211
$$

Now compute the Impact:

$$
\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times 0.2211
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.6613 - 0.7186 = 5.9427
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times A_V \times A_C \times P_R \times U_I \times 0.91
$$

Note: The trailing 0.91 multiplier is for User Interaction, but since the value is already substituted, we include it as the standard $U_I$ coefficient. Re-stating with full substitution:

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380
$$

$$
5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573
$$

$$
4.573 \times 0.85 = 3.887
$$

Thus:

$$
\text{Exploitability} \approx 3.89
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final Base Score

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}_{1\,\text{decimal}}\left( \min[\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10] \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}_{1}\left( \min[5.9427 + 3.887,\ 10] \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}_{1}\left( \min[9.8297,\ 10] \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BaseScore} = 9.8}
$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result

A score of **9.8** places this vulnerability in the **CRITICAL** severity band (9.0 – 10.0). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This vulnerability demands an **emergency patch deployment** within 24–48 hours, with executive-level notification and possible system isolation if no patch is available.

## 3.2 Patch Deployment Decision Matrix

The following analytical matrix helps KTU students understand how organizations prioritize which patches to deploy first.

| CVSS Score | Asset Criticality | Network Exposure | Deployment Priority | SLA (Service Level Agreement) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **9.0 – 10.0** | High | Internet-facing | **P0 — Emergency** | Within 24 hours |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | High | Internet-facing | **P1 — Critical** | Within 7 days |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | Medium | Internal | **P2 — High** | Within 30 days |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | Low | Isolated | **P3 — Routine** | Next maintenance window |
| **0.0** | Any | Any | **P4 — Defer** | Quarterly review |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the matrix is **multi-dimensional**. A High CVSS score alone is insufficient; a critical vulnerability on an air-gapped system (no network exposure) may be deprioritized compared to a Medium CVSS vulnerability on a public-facing server. This is the **contextual risk** principle that distinguishes academic vulnerability scoring from real-world risk management.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patch Management Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stage1_AssetInventory] --> B[stage2_VulnScanning]
    B --> C[stage3_CVEAnalysis]
    C --> D[stage4_RiskPrioritization]
    D --> E[stage5_PatchAcquisition]
    E --> F[stage6_LabTesting]
    F --> G{stage7_TestPassed}
    G -->|Yes| H[stage8_StagedDeployment]
    G -->|No| I[stage9_RollbackPlan]
    I --> F
    H --> J[stage10_ProductionRollout]
    J --> K[stage11_VerificationScan]
    K --> L{stage12_VulnResolved}
    L -->|Yes| M[stage13_ComplianceReport]
    L -->|No| N[stage14_Escalate]
    N --> D
    M --> O[stage15_ArchiveLogs]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart begins with asset discovery and ends with archival. Notice the **two critical feedback loops**: (1) failed tests trigger rollbacks, and (2) unresolved post-deployment vulnerabilities escalate back to prioritization — a closed-loop continuous improvement model.

## 4.2 Upgrade vs. Patch Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[start_IssueDetected] --> B{dec1_SecurityRelated}
    B -->|Yes| C[path1_ApplyPatch]
    B -->|No| D{dec2_FunctionalBugs}
    D -->|Yes| E[path2_BugFixPatch]
    D -->|No| F{dec3_NewFeature}
    F -->|Yes| G[path3_MajorUpgrade]
    F -->|No| H{dec4_Performance}
    H -->|Yes| I[path4_MinorUpgrade]
    H -->|No| J[end_NoAction]
    C --> K[end_DocumentCVE]
    E --> L[end_LogIncident]
    G --> M[end_PlanRelease]
    I --> N[end_Optimize]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This decision tree helps students distinguish between patches and upgrades based on the *nature* of the issue. Security issues always go to patches (urgency). New features always go to upgrades (planning).

## 4.3 CVSS Scoring Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[input_BaseMetrics] --> B[block1_ImpactCalc]
    A --> C[block2_ExploitCalc]
    B --> D[output_ImpactSubScore]
    C --> E[output_ExploitSubScore]
    D --> F[block3_FinalAggregation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[output_CVSSBaseScore]
    G --> H[decision_SeverityBand]
    H --> I[sev_None]
    H --> J[sev_Low]
    H --> K[sev_Medium]
    H --> L[sev_High]
    H --> M[sev_Critical]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram maps the architectural flow of the CVSS calculation pipeline. KTU examiners appreciate seeing students demonstrate understanding of how the three sub-scores (Impact, Exploitability, BaseScore) are modularly composed.

## 4.4 Vulnerability-Patch Deployment Timeline (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Vendor
    participant N as NVD_CVE_DB
    participant A as Admin_Team
    participant S as Staging_Server
    participant P as Production_Server
    V->>N: day0_CVE_Published
    N->>A: day0_Alert_Notification
    A->>A: day1_Risk_Assessment
    A->>S: day2_Deploy_Test_Patch
    S-->>A: day4_Test_Results_OK
    A->>P: day5_Rollout_Production
    P-->>A: day6_Verification_Scan
    A->>N: day7_Mark_Resolved
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This sequence diagram illustrates the temporal cascade from CVE publication to production remediation. Note the **7-day typical timeline** and the critical gating role of the staging environment between test and production.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Dec 2024]

**Differentiate between a security patch and a software upgrade. Provide one real-world example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded Distribution):

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Definition of Patch | 1 |
| Definition of Upgrade | 1 |
| Real-world example of each | 1 |

> **Patch:** A security patch is a small, targeted software update released by a vendor to remediate a specific known vulnerability or bug without altering the core feature set of the application. **Example:** Microsoft releasing a patch in October 2024 to fix CVE-2024-43583 (a Windows privilege escalation flaw).
>
> **Upgrade:** A software upgrade is a comprehensive version-level change that introduces new features, architectural improvements, or major functional enhancements. **Example:** Upgrading from Windows 10 to Windows 11, which introduces a redesigned UI, Android subsystem support, and new security baselines (TPM 2.0 requirement).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, July 2024]

**What is a Zero-Day vulnerability? Why is it considered more dangerous than a known vulnerability?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded Distribution):

> A **Zero-Day vulnerability** is a software flaw that is exploited by attackers *before* the vendor becomes aware of it or before a patch is made available. The term "zero-day" refers to the fact that the developer has had **zero days** to address the issue.
>
> **Why it is more dangerous (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **No patch exists** — defenders have no vendor-issued remediation.
> 2. **Antivirus signatures are ineffective** — the exploit signature is unknown to security tools.
> 3. **High black-market value** — Zero-days are sold for **\$100,000 to \$2,500,000** on gray markets (e.g., Zerodium).
> 4. **Used in targeted attacks** — nation-state APT groups (e.g., Lazarus, APT29) stockpile Zero-days for espionage.
>
> **Example:** The EternalBlue exploit (zero-day leaked in 2017) was used in the **WannaCry ransomware** attack, infecting 230,000+ computers globally.

---

## 📗 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations (ESE) follow a strict **Module Internal Choice** pattern. For every Part B question, students must answer EITHER the OR-I variant OR the OR-II variant, but **never both**. The question pairs below are designed to assess identical COs at the same Bloom's Taxonomy level.

---

### ✅ CHOICE QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample ESE Pattern]

**OR-I:**  
**(a)** Explain the complete **Patch Management Lifecycle** in detail. Discuss the critical role of staging environments and rollback planning in minimizing deployment risk. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score calculation**. A vulnerability has the following metrics:  
- AV: Network, AC: Low, PR: None, UI: Required  
- C: Low, I: None, A: None  
- Scope: Unchanged  
Compute the **CVSS Base Score** and identify its severity band. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Patch Management Lifecycle consists of **six interconnected stages**:

1. **Inventory & Discovery (1 mark):** Maintain a real-time CMDB (Configuration Management Database) of all assets. Without knowing what you have, you cannot protect it.

2. **Vulnerability Scanning & Assessment (1 mark):** Use tools like Nessus, Qualys, or OpenVAS to identify missing patches. Cross-reference findings with the NVD (National Vulnerability Database).

3. **Prioritization (1 mark):** Rank vulnerabilities by CVSS score, asset criticality, and exploitability. Use the **3D approach**: Defenders, Data, Downtime.

4. **Testing (1.5 marks):** Deploy patches in a **staging environment** that mirrors production. Conduct regression tests, compatibility checks, and load tests. **Why critical?** Patches that pass individual testing may fail under integrated workloads (e.g., authentication service patch breaking SSO).

5. **Deployment (1 mark):** Use staged rollout: pilot group → departmental → enterprise. Schedule during off-peak hours.

6. **Verification & Documentation (0.5 mark):** Re-scan to confirm remediation, archive logs, update compliance dashboards.

**Rollback Planning (1 mark):** Maintain tested rollback procedures including VM snapshots, database backups, and configuration baselines. If a patch causes production outages, the organization must be able to revert within minutes.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The CVSS v3.1 Base Score is computed in **three modular stages**:

**Stage 1: Impact Sub-Score (ISC)** — Compute the weighted confidentiality, integrity, and availability impact using the standard formula:

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - \left[ (1 - I_C) \times (1 - I_I) \times (1 - I_A) \right]
$$

**Stage 2: Overall Impact** — Apply piecewise formula based on scope.

**Stage 3: Exploitability** — Multiply 8.22 with attack vector, complexity, privileges, and user interaction coefficients.

**Stage 4: Final Base Score** — Sum impact and exploitability, cap at 10, round to 1 decimal.

**Numerical Calculation (5 marks for the computation):**

Given metrics: AV=Network (0.85), AC=Low (0.77), PR=None (0.85), UI=Required (0.62), C=Low (0.22), I=None (0), A=None (0), Scope=Unchanged.

**Step 1: ISC**

$$
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - [(1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0) \times (1 - 0)] = 1 - [0.78 \times 1 \times 1] = 0.22
$$

**Step 2: Impact (Scope Unchanged)**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 6.42 \times 0.22 = 1.4124
$$

**Step 3: Exploitability**

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.62
$$

$$
8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380
$$

$$
5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573
$$

$$
4.573 \times 0.62 = 2.835
$$

**Step 4: Base Score**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}_{1}[\min(1.4124 + 2.835,\ 10)] = \text{round}_{1}[4.2474] = 4.2
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CVSS Base Score} = 4.2 \quad (\textbf{Medium Severity})}
$$

---

### ✅ CHOICE QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample ESE Pattern]

**OR-II:**  
**(a)** Discuss the various **types of patches** with suitable examples. Why are **firmware patches** considered uniquely challenging in IoT environments? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CVSS metric groups** (Base, Temporal, Environmental). How do organizations customize the **Environmental Score** to reflect their specific infrastructure context? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Types of Patches (5 marks):**

1. **Security Patch:** Fixes known exploitable vulnerabilities. *Example:* Apple iOS 17.5.1 patch for the deleted-photo-recovery bug (CVE-2024-27804).
2. **Hotfix:** Emergency, out-of-cycle patch for active exploitation. *Example:* Emergency patch for the Log4Shell vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228) in December 2021.
3. **Service Pack:** Cumulative bundle of multiple patches. *Example:* Windows 7 Service Pack 1 (consolidated years of fixes).
4. **Bug Fix Patch:** Non-security functional defect resolution. *Example:* Chrome v127.0.6533.99 fixing a YouTube video buffering glitch.
5. **Firmware Patch:** Low-level hardware/embedded code update.
6. **Cumulative Update:** Rollup of all prior fixes for a given period.

**Why Firmware Patches are Challenging in IoT (2 marks):**

1. **Physical inaccessibility** — devices are deployed in remote/unmanned locations (oil rigs, agricultural sensors).
2. **Lack of standardized update mechanisms** — unlike OS-level Windows Update, IoT firmware varies by manufacturer.
3. **Brick risk** — failed firmware flashes can permanently disable the device.
4. **Battery and bandwidth constraints** — patches may exceed available power or network capacity.
5. **Lack of UI feedback** — no screen to indicate success/failure of the update.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**CVSS Metric Groups (3 marks):**

1. **Base Metrics:** Intrinsic properties of the vulnerability that are constant over time and across environments (AV, AC, PR, UI, C, I, A, S). These produce the Base Score (0.0 – 10.0).

2. **Temporal Metrics:** Properties that change over time as the vulnerability evolves. Includes **Exploit Code Maturity (E)**, **Remediation Level (RL)**, and **Report Confidence (RC)**. These allow the score to be downgraded as patches become available.

3. **Environmental Metrics:** Allow organizations to customize the score based on their specific infrastructure. Includes **Confidentiality Requirement (CR)**, **Integrity Requirement (IR)**, **Availability Requirement (AR)**, and **Modified Base Metrics**.

**Customizing Environmental Score (4 marks):**

Organizations customize the Environmental Score by **overlaying business context** onto the Base Score:

- If a system processes **medical records** (high confidentiality requirement), the $I_C$ value can be **increased** via the "Modified Confidentiality Impact" metric.
- If a system is **air-gapped** (no network exposure), the $A_V$ can be modified from Network to Local, **reducing the exploitability**.
- If an exploit has a **functional workaround**, the overall score is reduced.
- **Modified Base Score** is computed by re-running the formulas with the modified metrics, while the unmodified Base Score is preserved for industry-wide comparison.

**Practical Example:** The same CVE-2024-12345 may score 8.5 (High) globally, but a hospital with no internet exposure on the affected system might score it 5.2 (Medium) internally, and a payment processor might score it 9.7 (Critical) due to PCI-DSS requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Patch" with "Upgrade" (–2 marks):** Many students use these terms interchangeably. Always distinguish: patches fix specific issues; upgrades are version-level transformations.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the formula in CVSS calculations (–3 marks):** Even if the final numerical answer is correct, KTU examiners **deduct heavily** when the impact sub-score, exploitability, and aggregation steps are not shown. **Always show all four stages.**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the rounding step:** CVSS scores are always **rounded to 1 decimal place** using standard rounding rules. Writing "4.24" instead of "4.2" loses 0.5 marks.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "Why" in conceptual answers (–2 marks):** Stating the six stages of the patch lifecycle without explaining *why* each stage exists is treated as rote memorization. Always add a "rationale" sentence.
> 
> 5. **Scope confusion in CVSS:** The piecewise formula branches on **Scope = Changed vs. Unchanged**. Using the wrong branch can alter the score by **2–3 points** — a critical error.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Patch** = targeted fix; **Upgrade** = version-level transformation with new features.
- ✅ The **CVE system** uniquely identifies vulnerabilities (e.g., `CVE-2024-XXXXX`).
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** scores range from **0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical)**.
- ✅ Severity bands: **0.0=None, 0.1–3.9=Low, 4.0–6.9=Medium, 7.0–8.9=High, 9.0–10.0=Critical**.
- ✅ The **Patch Management Lifecycle** has 6 stages: Inventory → Scan → Prioritize → Test → Deploy → Verify.
- ✅ **Staging environments** are non-negotiable in patch deployment — they prevent production outages.
- ✅ **Rollback planning** is mandatory: maintain VM snapshots and tested restoration procedures.
- ✅ **Zero-Day** vulnerabilities have no patch and command extremely high black-market prices.
- ✅ **Hotfixes** are emergency, out-of-cycle patches for active exploitation.
- ✅ **Firmware patching** in IoT is uniquely challenging due to physical inaccessibility, brick risk, and lack of UI.
- ✅ The **CVSS Base Score** formula has **four steps**: ISC → Impact → Exploitability → Aggregation.
- ✅ Scope = Changed triggers the **non-linear (15th power)** formula branch.
- ✅ **Environmental CVSS scores** allow organizations to overlay business context onto base metrics.
- ✅ **Patch Tuesday** is Microsoft's scheduled second-Tuesday-of-each-month release cycle.
- ✅ Compliance frameworks (**ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, NIST 800-40, HIPAA**) mandate documented patch management procedures.
- ✅ The **3D Prioritization Model**: Defenders, Data, Downtime — guides resource allocation beyond raw CVSS scores.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Windows Safely
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Operating Windows Safely ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operating Windows Safely** refers to the set of administrative, technical, and behavioural practices, configuration controls, and built-in security mechanisms employed within the Microsoft Windows Operating System (NT-based architecture: Windows 10/11/Server 2019/2022) to preserve the **CIA Triad** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** — of data, processes, and user identities. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topic covers five pillars of Windows OS hardening: 1. **User Account Control (UAC)** & Least-Privilege Account Management 2. **NTFS File System Permissions** and Access Control Lists (ACLs) 3. **Windows Defender Firewall** and Network Security 4. **Windows Update / Patch Management** and Anti-Malware 5. **BitLocker, Backup, and Security Policy Enforcement** (Local/Group Policy) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "Your House is Your Windows PC"** > Imagine your Windows PC is a **house**: > - The **front door lock** is your **password / PIN / Biometric login**. > - The **User Account Control (UAC)** prompt is the **peephole** — it asks *"Are you sure you want to let this stranger (program) inside?"* before granting elevated access. > - **NTFS Permissions** are the **room keys** — they decide which family member (user) can enter which room (folder). > - **Windows Defender Firewall** is the **compound wall** — it blocks uninvited guests (network packets) from approaching the door. > - **Windows Update** is the **regular maintenance** — fixing broken windows, reinforcing locks, and patching holes in the roof. > - **BitLocker** is the **safety locker / vault** — even if a burglar steals the entire house (laptop), the contents remain encrypted. A "safe" Windows user is one who never disables the peephole, never hands out spare keys, and never ignores the maintenance notice. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Thresholds > [!TIP] > Key Microsoft-recommended security baselines (used by enterprises & KTU examiners): > - **Minimum password length:** 12 characters (or 8 with complexity) > - **Account lockout threshold:** **5** failed attempts > - **Lockout duration:** 15–30 minutes (or until admin unlock) > - **UAC default level:** Notify on app changes only (default Windows setting) > - **BitLocker algorithm default:** **XTS-AES 128-bit** (XTS-AES 256-bit for sensitive data) > - **Firewall profiles:** 3 (Domain, Private, Public) — Public must always be ON > - **Patch cadence:** Monthly cumulative updates + out-of-band emergency patches (e.g., "Patch Tuesday") ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars — Logical Breakdown #### **Pillar 1 — User Account Control (UAC) & Account Types** Windows segregates privileges using two principal account classes: - **Administrator Account:** Full control. Can install software, modify system files, change security policies. **Dangerous** if compromised. - **Standard User Account:** Limited. Cannot install most software or change system-wide settings. **Recommended** for daily use. UAC is a **mandatory access control (MAC)** overlay that intercepts operations requiring elevation and presents a consent dialog. The four UAC notification levels (configurable via `secpol.msc` → *Local Policies → Security Options*) are: | Level | Behaviour | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Always Notify** | Prompt for every change by apps & Windows | Maximum security (paranoid) | | **Notify on App Changes (Default)** | Prompt for apps, not for Windows settings | **Recommended** by Microsoft | | **Notify without Dimming Desktop** | Prompt but desktop stays active | Accessibility setting | | **Never Notify** | No prompts (equivalent to Admin always) | **Dangerous — never use** | #### **Pillar 2 — NTFS Permissions vs Share Permissions** This is the **single most asked distinction** in KTU exams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NTFS Permissions** apply to *individual users* accessing files **locally or over the network**. They are stored in the file's **Access Control List (ACL)** on the NTFS volume. > > **Share Permissions** apply *only* when the folder is accessed **over the network** via SMB. They do NOT apply to local access. | Feature | NTFS Permissions | Share Permissions | |---|---|---| | Apply to | Local + Network access | **Network access only** | | Granularity | Per-user / Per-group (very fine: Read, Write, Modify, Full Control, etc.) | Coarse (Read, Change, Full Control) — only **3** options | | Stored in | File/Folder's **ACL** (on disk) | **Shared folder's properties** | | Effect when both present | **Most restrictive** of the two wins | **Most restrictive** of the two wins | | Effective for Local user? | **Yes** | **No** | #### **Pillar 3 — Windows Defender Firewall** A **stateful host-based firewall** that filters inbound AND outbound traffic using **rules**. Operates across **3 profiles**: - **Domain Profile** → applied when PC is joined to an Active Directory domain. - **Private Profile** → applied on trusted home/office networks. - **Public Profile** → applied on untrusted networks (airports, cafés) — **strictest rules**. > [!TIP] > **Examiner Hook:** *"Why is the Public profile the most restrictive?"* — Because on a public Wi-Fi, your PC is exposed to unknown attackers, so all unsolicited inbound traffic is blocked by default. #### **Pillar 4 — Windows Update & Anti-Malware** - **Windows Update Service (wuauserv)** delivers security patches monthly. - **Windows Security (Defender Antivirus)** provides real-time malware scanning using signatures + **heuristics** + **cloud-based ML**. - **Defender SmartScreen** blocks phishing sites & unrecognised apps at the browser/app-launch level. #### **Pillar 5 — BitLocker, Backups & Group Policy** - **BitLocker Drive Encryption** uses AES (128/256) to encrypt entire volumes. The decryption key is sealed by the **TPM (Trusted Platform Module)** chip. - **Group Policy Editor (`gpedit.msc`)** allows centralised configuration of security settings (password policy, audit policy, software restriction). - **Local Security Policy (`secpol.msc`)** does the same for a standalone (non-domain) machine. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The "formulas" here are **decision rules** and **configuration thresholds** that KTU questions test. | Concept | Rule / Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Effective Permission (Local) | NTFS only | Share perms ignored locally | | Effective Permission (Network) | NTFS ∩ Share (most restrictive intersection) | Both must allow | | UAC Default Level | Notify on app changes only | Level 2 of 4 | | Password Min Length (Microsoft Baseline) | **12** chars | Was 8 pre-2019 | | Account Lockout Threshold | 5 invalid attempts | Configurable | | BitLocker Default Cipher | XTS-AES-128 | NIST recommended | | Firewall Default Inbound | **Block** (for Public/Private) | Domain may differ | | Default Audit Policy | Logon events: success+failure | Auditing | | Windows Update Cadence | Monthly + emergency | "Patch Tuesday" | | TPM Version Required for BitLocker | **TPM 1.2** (min) | TPM 2.0 preferred | | Effective ACL | Union of explicit + inherited permissions (subject to deny) | Standard rule | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These principles are not academic — they are deployed in: - **Enterprise SOCs** (Security Operations Centers) hardening Windows endpoints. - **Cyber Forensics** where NTFS ACLs and audit logs become evidence. - **Compliance Frameworks**: ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, PCI-DSS, and India's **DPDPA 2023** all mandate least-privilege, patching, and encryption controls. - **Ethical Hacking / Penetration Testing** — testers must bypass UAC, escalate privileges, and read ACLs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix Since "Operating Windows Safely" is a **configurational and procedural topic** rather than a mathematical derivation, this section presents **exhaustive step-by-step hardening procedures** and **analytical decision matrices** that KTU examiners expect students to be able to reproduce. ### 3.1 Procedure 1 — Enabling & Configuring UAC (Step-by-Step) **Step 1.** Open the **Start Menu** and type `UAC` or `Change User Account Control settings`. Click the matching result. **Step 2.** The slider presents **4 levels** (top to bottom): - Level 1: Always notify me when: - Level 2: Notify me only when apps try to make changes to my computer (default) - Level 3: Notify me only when apps try to make changes to my computer (do not dim my desktop) - Level 4: Never notify **Step 3.** Drag the slider to **Level 2 (Recommended)**. **Step 4.** Click **OK**. Windows prompts for elevation (admin credentials or consent). **Step 5.** A reboot is **not** required, but the change applies to all subsequent elevation requests. > [!TIP] > **Examiner Hook:** *"If a user complains of repeated UAC prompts, what is the root cause?"* — Typically an application with a poorly designed manifest that does NOT declare its requested execution level. The fix is to update the app or set its compatibility to "Run as administrator" while keeping UAC on. ### 3.2 Procedure 2 — Configuring NTFS Permissions (Step-by-Step) **Step 1.** Right-click the folder → **Properties** → **Security** tab. **Step 2.** Click **Edit** → **Add** to add a user or group. Type the name, click **Check Names**, then **OK**. **Step 3.** In the *Permissions for [User]* box, set each permission: - **Full Control** — read, write, modify, delete, change permissions - **Modify** — read, write, modify, delete (no permission changes) - **Read & Execute** — read + run executables/scripts - **List Folder Contents** — only view filenames - **Read** — only read files and attributes - **Write** — create new files and folders **Step 4.** The **Advanced** button shows the full **DACL** (Discretionary ACL), with two columns: **Allow** and **Deny**. **Step 5.** **Effective Access** tab (Windows Vista+) shows the *computed* permission after inheritance, explicit ACEs, and Deny rules are applied. **Analytical Matrix — Permission Precedence (KTU Favourite):** | Scenario | Explicit Allow | Explicit Deny | Inherited Allow | Resulting Permission | |---|---|---|---|---| | Case 1 | Yes | No | Yes | **Allow** | | Case 2 | Yes | **Yes** | Yes | **Deny** (Deny wins) | | Case 3 | No | No | Yes | **Allow** (via inheritance) | | Case 4 | No | Yes | Yes | **Deny** | | Case 5 | No | No | No | **No Access** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule:** *Explicit Deny always overrides Explicit Allow.* This is the single most-tested rule in KTU. ### 3.3 Procedure 3 — Enabling BitLocker (Step-by-Step) **Step 1.** Open **Control Panel → System and Security → BitLocker Drive Encryption**. **Step 2.** Select the OS drive (C:) → Click **Turn on BitLocker**. **Step 3.** The system checks for a **TPM chip** (version 1.2 minimum). If absent, a USB startup key is required. **Step 4.** Choose how to back up the **recovery key**: - Save to your Microsoft account - Save to a USB flash drive - Save to a file - Print the recovery key **Step 5.** Choose **Encrypt entire drive** (recommended) or **Used disk space only** (faster, less secure). **Step 6.** Choose encryption mode: - **New encryption mode** (XTS-AES) — for fixed/internal drives. - **Compatible mode** (AES-CBC) — for removable drives that may be moved to older Windows. **Step 7.** Run the **System Check** checkbox → Click **Continue**. **Step 8.** Reboot. Encryption proceeds in the background. Duration: minutes to hours depending on disk size. **Analytical Matrix — BitLocker Key Protectors:** | Protector Type | Storage | Strength | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **TPM** | Hardware chip on motherboard | Very High | Laptops/desktops with TPM 2.0 | | **TPM + PIN** | TPM + user PIN | Extreme | High-security environments | | **Startup Key (USB)** | USB flash drive | High | Machines without TPM | | **Recovery Password** | 48-digit numerical | Emergency only | Data recovery after failure | | **Active Directory Key** | AD backup | Enterprise | Domain-joined enterprise PCs | ### 3.4 Procedure 4 — Configuring Windows Defender Firewall Rules **Step 1.** Open `wf.msc` (Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security). **Step 2.** Click **Inbound Rules** (or Outbound Rules). **Step 3.** Click **New Rule** on the right pane. **Step 4.** Select **Rule Type**: - Program - Port - Predefined (e.g., File and Printer Sharing) - Custom **Step 5.** Configure each wizard page: - **Program path** OR **Port number** (e.g., 443 for HTTPS, 80 for HTTP, 22 for SSH, 3389 for RDP). - **Protocol** (TCP / UDP) and **Local/Remote port** range. - **Scope**: Local IP addresses, Remote IP addresses. - **Action**: Allow the connection / Allow if secure / Block the connection. - **Profile**: Tick Domain, Private, Public. - **Name**: A descriptive label (e.g., "Allow HTTPS Inbound"). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Trap:** *"You open port 3389 (RDP) on the Public profile to work from home. What is the risk?"* — RDP exposed to the public Internet is the #1 vector for ransomware and brute-force attacks. KTU expects: use a **VPN** instead, or restrict by source IP. ### 3.5 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Account Type | User Role | Recommended Account | UAC Setting | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Daily home user | Standard | Level 2 (default) | Least-privilege; prevents malware from gaining admin rights silently | | Software developer | Standard + UAC Level 2 | Level 2 | Uses Visual Studio "Run as Administrator" per-build | | System Administrator | Two accounts: Standard for daily + Admin for tasks | Level 2 (with smartscreen) | Separation of duties | | Server Operator (Domain) | Domain Admin only on Servers, not on workstation | — | No local admin on the workstation | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Windows Security Architecture — Layered Defence (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB User["User Login
Password or PIN or Biometric"] UAC["UAC Elevation Prompt
Admin Consent Layer"] App["Application Execution
Standard vs Elevated Token"] NTFS["NTFS Access Check
ACL and DACL Evaluation"] FW["Windows Defender Firewall
3 Profiles Domain Private Public"] AV["Defender Antivirus
Real Time Scan"] BL["BitLocker Encryption
XTS AES 128 or 256"] TPM["TPM Chip
Key Sealing"] Policy["Group Policy secpol and gpedit
Password Lockout Audit"] Update["Windows Update
Patch Tuesday"] Backup["File History and Backup"] CIA["CIA Triad Achieved
Confidentiality Integrity Availability"] User --> UAC UAC --> App App --> NTFS App --> AV App --> FW FW --> App NTFS --> BL BL --> TPM TPM --> CIA AV --> CIA Policy --> UAC Policy --> NTFS Update --> AV Backup --> CIA FW --> CIA ``` ### 4.2 NTFS Permission Evaluation Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User requests access to file or folder"] --> B{"Is user the Owner?"} B -- Yes --> Z["Grant access as Owner"] B -- No --> C{"Explicit Allow ACE
present for user or group?"} C -- No --> D{"Inherited Allow ACE
present for user or group?"} C -- Yes --> E{"Explicit Deny ACE
present for user or group?"} D -- No --> F["Access Denied"] D -- Yes --> E E -- Yes --> F E -- No --> G["Access Granted with Effective Rights"] Z --> G ``` ### 4.3 UAC Elevation Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Application participant T as Token Service participant UAC as UAC Consent Dialog participant K as Kernel Object Manager participant FS as NTFS Driver participant FW as Firewall U->>T: Request CreateProcess with elevated flag T->>UAC: Is executable in allowed list or unknown? UAC-->>U: Display Consent Dialog on Secure Desktop U->>UAC: Click Yes and enter admin password UAC->>T: Token marked as Full or Split Token T->>K: Create process with elevated token K->>FW: Filter network operations by profile rules K->>FS: Request file create or write FS-->>K: Check DACL of parent folder K-->>U: Operation succeeds or fails ``` ### 4.4 Windows Defender Firewall Profile Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Network Connection Initiated"] --> Detect{"Network Location
detected by NLA"} Detect -- "Joined to AD Domain" --> D["Domain Profile Rules"] Detect -- "Home or Office Trusted" --> P["Private Profile Rules"] Detect -- "Cafe Airport Hotel" --> Pb["Public Profile Rules Strictest"] D --> Eval["Match against Inbound and Outbound Rules"] P --> Eval Pb --> Eval Eval --> Action{"Action defined?"} Action -- "Allow" --> Pass["Traffic Permitted"] Action -- "Block" --> Drop["Traffic Silently Dropped"] Action -- "Default" --> Def["Block by default for Public and Private"] ``` ### 4.5 BitLocker Boot Sequence with TPM ```mermaid flowchart LR Power["Power On"] --> BIOS["BIOS and UEFI POST"] BIOS --> TPM["TPM measures boot components
PCR 0 to 7"] TPM --> MBR["MBR and Boot Sector Verified"] MBR --> OS["Windows Boot Loader"] OS --> Key["TPM releases Disk Encryption Key
only if PCR values match"] Key --> Unlock["Decrypt OS Volume and Continue Boot"] Unlock --> Logon["User Logon Screen"] Logon --> Cred["Password PIN or Biometric Verified"] Cred --> Desktop["Secure Desktop Loaded"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Model]** **Q1. [3 Marks] Define User Account Control (UAC). Why is it recommended to keep the default notification level?** **Model Answer:** UAC (User Account Control) is a mandatory access control mechanism in Windows that mediates any operation requiring administrator-level privileges. When a process attempts to perform a privileged action (e.g., installing software, modifying system files, changing registry keys), UAC presents a **consent dialog on the Secure Desktop** asking an administrator to approve or deny the request. The default notification level (**Level 2: "Notify me only when apps try to make changes to my computer"**) is recommended because it strikes a balance between usability and security — it blocks silent privilege escalation by malware (a common attack vector) while not overwhelming the user with prompts for routine Windows settings changes. Disabling UAC entirely (Level 4) returns the system to a pre-Vista security model where any process can gain full administrative rights without user awareness, making the system highly vulnerable to malware. --- **Q2. [3 Marks] Differentiate between NTFS Permissions and Share Permissions. What is the rule when both are applied?** **Model Answer:** NTFS permissions apply to both local and network access and are stored in the Access Control List (ACL) of each file/folder on an NTFS volume. They offer **fine-grained control** through six standard rights (Read, Write, Read & Execute, Modify, List Folder Contents, Full Control) plus advanced rights. Share permissions apply **only over the network** using the SMB protocol and offer only **three** coarse options: Read, Change, and Full Control. When both NTFS and Share permissions are evaluated for a network access, the **most restrictive of the two** becomes the effective permission — a security principle of *least privilege*. For local access, only NTFS permissions are evaluated; share permissions are completely ignored. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **Q.A) (a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of NTFS Access Control Lists (ACLs). Discuss the difference between Explicit Allow, Explicit Deny, and Inherited permissions with suitable examples. State the precedence rule.** **Model Answer:** **ACL Structure:** Every NTFS-protected object (file or folder) has a **Security Descriptor** containing two ACLs: the **DACL** (Discretionary ACL — who is allowed/denied) and the **SACL** (System ACL — what gets audited). Each ACL is a list of **ACEs (Access Control Entries)**, each ACE binding a *trustee* (user, group, or computer) to a *right* (e.g., Read, Write) with a type (Allow or Deny). **Explicit Allow:** An ACE added **manually** by an administrator directly on the object. Example: An admin right-clicks `C:\Finance\Q4.xlsx` → Properties → Security → Add `User_Anu` → tick **Allow → Read**. This is *not* inherited from any parent. **Explicit Deny:** An ACE that explicitly forbids a trustee. Example: On the same file, the admin adds `User_Rahul` → tick **Deny → Full Control**. This overrides everything else for Rahul. **Inherited:** ACEs that propagate from a **parent container**. By default, all subfolders/files inherit permissions from their parent folder. Inheritance can be blocked (via the "Disable inheritance" button in the Advanced Security Settings dialog) or "converted" to explicit ACEs. **Precedence Rule (most-tested):** 1. **Explicit Deny** overrides everything. 2. **Explicit Allow** overrides Inherited Allow. 3. **Deny/Allow ACEs are unioned**, and the user receives the *combined* set of rights granted by all Allow ACEs, *minus* anything explicitly denied. **Example Resolution:** Suppose User_Kiran has an Inherited Allow "Read" from the parent folder, and an Explicit Deny "Read" set on the file. The Explicit Deny wins → Kiran gets **No Access**. --- **Q.A) (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the working of BitLocker Drive Encryption. What role does the TPM chip play, and what are the various key protectors available?** **Model Answer:** **BitLocker Working (with reference to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.5):** 1. On power-on, the **BIOS/UEFI** executes POST and hands control to the TPM. 2. The **TPM measures** critical boot components (BIOS, MBR, Boot Sector, Boot Manager) and stores their hashes in **PCRs (Platform Configuration Registers 0–7)**. 3. The TPM **seals** the BitLocker Volume Master Key (VMK) with these PCR values. If any boot component is altered (e.g., a rootkit modifies the MBR), the PCR values change, and the TPM **refuses to release the VMK**. 4. The **Windows Boot Manager** requests the VMK from the TPM. If the PCRs are intact, the VMK is unsealed and used to decrypt the OS volume. 5. The user then authenticates via **password, PIN, or biometric** at the Logon screen. 6. The decrypted OS loads the Secure Desktop. **Role of TPM:** The TPM is a **tamper-resistant hardware crypto-processor** soldered on the motherboard. It provides *attestation* (proving the boot state) and *sealing* (releasing secrets only to a known-good configuration). Without a TPM, BitLocker falls back to a **USB startup key**, which is less secure. **Key Protectors (already tabulated in Section 3.3):** - TPM only (transparent to user, default for laptops with TPM 2.0). - TPM + PIN (something the user *has* + something the user *knows*). - TPM + Startup Key (USB). - TPM + TPM Key + Smart Card (enterprise). - Recovery Password (48-digit numerical — emergency only). **Security value:** Even if a thief removes the SSD and mounts it on another PC, the data remains encrypted (XTS-AES-128/256). This protects against **cold-boot attacks** and **physical theft**. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]** **Q.B) (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the three firewall profiles of Windows Defender. How does a system administrator create an inbound rule to allow HTTPS traffic only on the Private profile? Provide the step-by-step procedure.** **Model Answer:** **Three Profiles of Windows Defender Firewall:** - **Domain Profile** — Active when the computer is connected to a network in which its domain account is authenticated by a Domain Controller. Used in corporate AD environments. - **Private Profile** — Active on networks designated as "trusted" by the user (e.g., home or small-office LAN). Less restrictive than Public. - **Public Profile** — Active on networks marked as "public" (e.g., coffee shop, airport, hotel). **Most restrictive** by default — all unsolicited inbound traffic is blocked. The active profile is selected automatically by the **Network Location Awareness (NLA)** service based on the network's characteristics and the user's explicit choice when first connecting. **Step-by-Step Procedure to Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) on Private Profile Only:** **Step 1.** Press `Win + R`, type `wf.msc`, press Enter. This opens *Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security*. **Step 2.** In the left pane, click **Inbound Rules**. **Step 3.** In the right pane, click **New Rule…** **Step 4.** On the *Rule Type* page, select **Port** → Click **Next**. **Step 5.** On the *Protocol and Ports* page: - Select **TCP** - Select **Specific local ports** - Type **443** - Click **Next** **Step 6.** On the *Action* page, select **Allow the connection** → Click **Next**. **Step 7.** On the *Profile* page, tick **Private** only. (Untick Domain and Public.) → Click **Next**. **Step 8.** On the *Name* page, enter a descriptive name: *"Allow HTTPS Inbound (Private only)"* and an optional description. Click **Finish**. **Step 9.** Verify the rule appears in the Inbound Rules list with a green checkmark icon and the **Enabled = Yes** state. This rule now permits inbound HTTPS traffic on the user's private home/office network, but the same traffic is **blocked** when the laptop is on a public Wi-Fi — implementing the principle of *defence in depth* and *least privilege across contexts*. --- **Q.B) (b) [7 Marks] What is the CIA Triad? Explain how each of the following Windows security features contributes to the CIA Triad: (i) NTFS Permissions, (ii) BitLocker, (iii) Windows Update, (iv) Windows Defender Firewall.** **Model Answer:** **CIA Triad:** The foundational model of information security comprising: - **Confidentiality** — Only authorised parties can read data. - **Integrity** — Data is not modified by unauthorised parties. - **Availability** — Data and services are accessible to authorised users when needed. **Mapping Windows Features to CIA:** | Feature | CIA Component | Justification | |---|---|---| | **(i) NTFS Permissions** | **Confidentiality** + **Integrity** | ACLs ensure only authorised users can read (Confidentiality) or write/modify (Integrity) files. Explicit Deny prevents tampering. | | **(ii) BitLocker** | **Confidentiality** | AES encryption of the entire volume means that even if the disk is physically stolen, the attacker cannot read the data without the decryption key sealed in TPM. | | **(iii) Windows Update** | **Integrity** + **Availability** | Patches fix vulnerabilities (e.g., SMBv1 exploit) that could allow attackers to tamper with data (Integrity) or deploy ransomware that locks data (Availability). Patch Tuesday is a structured defence against zero-days and known exploits. | | **(iv) Windows Defender Firewall** | **Availability** + **Integrity** | Blocks unauthorised network access, preventing Denial-of-Service attacks (Availability) and remote exploitation (Integrity). Stateful inspection prevents port-scanning and man-in-the-middle attacks. | **Synthesis:** Together, these four features form a **defence-in-depth** strategy. No single control is sufficient; each layer compensates for the failure of another. For example, even if a firewall is bypassed, NTFS permissions still restrict what an attacker can do; even if a hacker gains an account, BitLocker protects the disk when the machine is off; and Windows Update ensures the OS itself doesn't become the weakest link. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Operating Windows Safely" Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Encryption" with "Permissions".** BitLocker encrypts the *disk* (protects against physical theft); NTFS Permissions control *who* can read/write (protect against logical access). Examiners deduct 2 marks for conflating the two. > > 2. **Forgetting that Share Permissions apply ONLY to network access.** A common answer error: "Share permissions apply to local access" — this is **false** and costs full marks on a 7-mark question. > > 3. **Stating "Explicit Allow overrides Explicit Deny".** The opposite is true. This single rule has tripped more KTU candidates than any other in this module. > > 4. **Listing UAC levels without explaining the "Secure Desktop".** The *Secure Desktop* (the dimmed full-screen consent dialog) is what makes UAC resistant to UI-spoofing malware. Skipping this in a 7-mark answer costs at least 1 mark. > > 5. **Treating Windows Update as a "feature" rather than a "process".** Examiners expect keywords: *signature updates, cumulative patches, KB articles, CVE mitigation, Patch Tuesday cadence*. > > 6. **Omitting the TPM from BitLocker answers.** Even a brief mention of TPM's role in *sealing* the key earns a mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Operating Windows Safely** > > - **UAC** is a *mandatory access control overlay*. Default level = **2** (Notify on app changes). Never use Level 4. > - **Administrator vs Standard User:** Daily work = Standard. Admin = on-demand via elevation. > - **NTFS Permissions** apply to **local + network**; **Share Permissions** apply to **network only**. > - **Effective Permission Rule (Network) = NTFS ∩ Share (most restrictive wins).** > - **Deny Always Wins** over Allow — in the DACL evaluation order. > - **NTFS standard rights (6):** Read, Write, Read & Execute, Modify, List Folder Contents, Full Control. > - **BitLocker** encrypts entire volumes with **XTS-AES 128 (default) or 256**. > - **TPM** seals the BitLocker key against boot-component PCR measurements — provides *attestation* of boot integrity. > - **BitLocker Key Protectors:** TPM, TPM+PIN, TPM+USB Key, Recovery Password, AD-issued key. > - **Windows Defender Firewall** has **3 profiles**: Domain, Private, Public — **Public is strictest**. > - **Firewall default:** Block inbound, Allow outbound (for Public and Private). > - **Windows Update** is non-optional for security; Patch Tuesday = monthly cumulative; emergency out-of-band patches for active exploits. > - **Windows Security (Defender)** = Antivirus + SmartScreen + Firewall + Device Security (core isolation, memory integrity). > - **Group Policy (`gpedit.msc`)** = centralised config for password policy, audit policy, software restriction; **Local Security Policy (`secpol.msc`)** = same for standalone machines. > - **Microsoft Password Baseline:** 12+ characters; lockout after 5 failed attempts; lockout duration 15–30 min. > - **CIA Triad Mapping:** NTFS = C+I, BitLocker = C, Windows Update = I+A, Firewall = A+I. > - **Defence in Depth:** Multiple layers — no single control is sufficient. > - **Least Privilege Principle:** Always use the *lowest* account privilege that can complete the task. > - **Audit Policy (via `secpol.msc`):** Configure logon events, object access, privilege use to be logged for forensic readiness. > - **Safe Practices:** Don't disable UAC, don't run unknown `.exe` files, don't plug in unknown USB drives, enable BitLocker on laptops, take regular backups (File History or OneDrive), keep OS patched. > - **Remember for KTU:** *Deny > Allow*; *NTFS for local, Share for network*; *TPM seals, BitLocker encrypts*; *Three firewall profiles, Public is strictest*.

topics.title: Windows Defender Firewall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST604 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Windows Defender Firewall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Windows Defender Firewall (WDF)** — formerly known as *Windows Firewall* and before that *Internet Connection Firewall (ICF)* — is a **stateful host-based packet filtering firewall** built natively into the Microsoft Windows operating system. It is implemented as a combination of a **kernel-mode Network Driver Interface Specification (NDIS) filter driver** and a **user-mode management service** that enforces a security policy on inbound and outbound network traffic based on configurable rules, IP addresses, ports, protocols, and active network profiles.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCST604 - Fundamentals of Cyber Security, Module 4: OS Hardening and Endpoint Security)**, Windows Defender Firewall is studied under the domain of **Perimeter Defense Mechanisms and Host-Based Intrusion Prevention**, with specific learning outcomes directed at understanding the **filtering of malicious traffic at the host boundary**, **rule-based access control**, and **integration with Active Directory Group Policy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stateful Inspection**: Windows Defender Firewall is a *stateful* firewall, meaning it tracks the state of active network connections (e.g., `ESTABLISHED`, `RELATED`, `NEW`, `INVALID`) using the **TCP/IP state table** and only allows return traffic that corresponds to a legitimate outbound request — thwarting many forms of unsolicited inbound attacks such as port scanning and SYN flooding.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your computer is a **house**, and the internet is the **outside world**. Every network packet that arrives at your machine is a **visitor** at your door. Without a firewall, the door is wide open — anyone can walk in, look around, and even take things.

A **firewall** is like a **trained security guard** stationed at the door with a **rulebook**:

- **Rule 1**: "If a guest knocked on the door, identified themselves, and was let in earlier — they may freely walk in and out" *(this is the **stateful inspection** of return traffic)*.
- **Rule 2**: "If a stranger shows up uninvited, check the rulebook. Allowed? Let them in. Not allowed? Block them and log the attempt" *(this is **inbound rule filtering**)*.
- **Rule 3**: "If our resident (the OS) wants to go out to the market, check the outbound rulebook before opening the door" *(this is **outbound rule filtering**, essential against malware that "phones home")*.

**Windows Defender Firewall** is the **on-premise, always-on security guard** Microsoft installs in every Windows 10/11 and Windows Server machine — requiring no additional licensing or third-party software.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric — Default Behavior**: By default, WDF **blocks all unsolicited inbound traffic** and **allows all outbound traffic**. This default-deny inbound posture is a foundational principle in modern cybersecurity defense-in-depth.

---

## Standard Metrics & Default Values (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Default Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Default Inbound Policy** | **Block (with allowed exceptions)** |
| **Default Outbound Policy** | **Allow (with blocked exceptions)** |
| **Display State** | **On (for all profiles)** |
| **File and Printer Sharing** | **Disabled for Public profile, Enabled for Private** |
| **Remote Desktop** | **Blocked on Public profile** |
| **Logging** | **Disabled by default (max size: 4,096 KB; location: `%SystemRoot%\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\pfirewall.log`)** |
| **Service Name** | **MpsSvc (Windows Defender Firewall service)** |
| **Driver** | **wfp.sys (Windows Filtering Platform driver)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of Windows Defender Firewall

The Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) — the underlying engine — operates in **two primary layers**:

### Layer A — Kernel-Mode Filtering Engine

- **Driver**: `wfp.sys` and `tcpip.sys`
- **Function**: Performs **packet inspection at the Network Driver Interface Specification (NDIS) layer** *before* the packet reaches the user-mode TCP/IP stack.
- **Operations**:
  1. **Packet Header Parsing** — extracts source/destination IPs, ports, protocol numbers (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1).
  2. **State Table Lookup** — verifies if packet belongs to an `ESTABLISHED` or `RELATED` connection in the **TCP state table**.
  3. **Rule Match Evaluation** — runs the packet against the **Filter Engine** in priority order (lower numerical values = higher priority).
  4. **Verdict** — `PERMIT`, `BLOCK`, or `CALL_DRIVER` (for further inspection by third-party WFP callout drivers).

### Layer B — User-Mode Management Service

- **Service**: `MpsSvc` (Microsoft Protection Service)
- **Executable**: `svchost.exe -k LocalServiceNoNetworkFirewall -p`
- **Function**: Hosts the **Windows Firewall Authorization Driver (WFAD)**, which bridges WFP and the Windows Security Health Agent (Windows Security Center).
- **Configuration Sources** (rules are merged in this priority order):
  1. **Local Administrator rules**
  2. **Local Service rules** (e.g., File and Printer Sharing, Network Discovery)
  3. **Group Policy (GPO) — wins over local settings**
  4. **IPsec main mode / quick mode policies**
  5. **Connection Security Rules (AuthExemption, Request/Require inbound/outbound)**

---

## 2.2 The Three Network Profiles (Critical for KTU)

Windows Defender Firewall applies rules **conditionally based on the active network profile** — this is one of the most frequently tested concepts.

| Profile | Trigger Condition | Trust Level | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain Profile** | Computer is joined to an Active Directory domain AND connected to a domain controller | **High Trust** | Corporate LAN / Office network |
| **Private Profile** | User manually designates a network as "Private" (Home/Work trusted networks) | **Medium Trust** | Home network, trusted small office |
| **Public Profile** | Default for any unrecognized network (airports, cafes, hotels) | **Low Trust** | Untrusted public Wi-Fi |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profile Isolation Rule**: A rule explicitly bound to the *Public* profile will **never** activate on a *Private* or *Domain* network. This is why configuring the right profile during rule creation is critical for security.

---

## 2.3 Rule Components (The Anatomy of a WDF Rule)

Each WDF rule is built from **5 logical components**, often called the **"5-Tuple + Action"** model:

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Name & Description** | Human-readable identifier | "Allow RDP from Admin subnet" |
| **2. Direction** | `Inbound` or `Outbound` | Inbound |
| **3. Action** | `Allow the connection` / `Allow if secure` / `Block the connection` | Allow |
| **4. Protocol** | TCP, UDP, ICMPv4, ICMPv6, Any, Custom (number) | TCP |
| **5. Scope** | Local IP + Remote IP (any / specific / subnet) | Remote = `192.168.10.0/24` |
| **6. Port** | Local + Remote ports (All / Specific / RPC / DCOM) | Local = `3389` |

> [!TIP]
> **The "Allow If Secure" Action**: This is a special KTU-relevant action that requires the traffic to be authenticated using **IPsec**. If IPsec is not negotiated, the packet is silently dropped — a defense against unauthenticated network access in enterprise environments.

---

## 2.4 Inbound vs Outbound Rules — The Asymmetric Defense

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often conflate "blocking inbound" with "complete protection." In reality, **outbound filtering** is the primary defense against **malware beaconing**, **data exfiltration**, and **Command & Control (C2) callbacks**.

| Aspect | Inbound Rules | Outbound Rules |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Block unauthorized remote access | Stop malware from "phoning home" |
| **Default Action** | **Block** | **Allow** |
| **Typical Rule Examples** | RDP, SMB, HTTP server, VNC | Telemetry blockers, Tor, suspicious EXEs |
| **Threat Mitigated** | Network recon, exploit delivery | Botnet C2, ransomware exfil, keylogger upload |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (No Pipes in Cells)

> [!NOTE]
> The following summary captures the **"formulas" and evaluation logic** used by WDF. Since this is a systems-security topic, "formulas" represent the **rule-evaluation decision functions**.

**Filter Decision Function (Conceptual):**

$$
\text{Action}(\text{packet}) \;=\; \text{EVAL}\big(\; \text{Profile}(p),\; \text{Direction}(p),\; \text{Match}_{5\text{-tuple}}(p, R),\; \text{IPsec}(p) \;\big)
$$

where the action is one of: $\text{PERMIT}$, \text{BLOCK}, or $\text{BLOCK\_SILENT}.

**Stateful Inspection Predicate:**

$$
\text{StatefulAllow}(p) \;\equiv\; \text{TCP\_FLAGS}(p) \in \{\text{SYN/ACK},\ \text{ACK},\ \text{FIN/ACK}\} \;\land\; \text{Flow}(p) \in \text{StateTable}
$$

**Connection Security Rule Negotiation (IPsec):**

$$
\text{IPsecRequired} \;=\; \text{Require}(R) \;\land\; \text{MMProposal}(IKEv2) \;\land\; \text{QMProposal}(\text{AES-256-GCM}, \text{SHA-256})
$$

**Log File Rotation Metric:**

$$
\text{MaxLogSize} \;=\; 4{,}096\ \text{KB} \quad \text{(default)}
$$

$$
\text{LogLocation} \;=\; \texttt{\%SystemRoot\%\textbackslash System32\textbackslash LogFiles\textbackslash Firewall\textbackslash pfirewall.log}
$$

**Service Identity:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Service Display Name** | Windows Defender Firewall |
| **Service Name (short)** | MpsSvc |
| **Driver Name** | wfp.sys |
| **GUI Tool 1** | wf.msc (Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security) |
| **GUI Tool 2** | control /name Microsoft.WindowsDefender |
| **CLI Tool 1** | netsh advfirewall |
| **CLI Tool 2** | PowerShell cmdlet `Get-NetFirewallRule` |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Enterprise Network Hardening**: GPO-deployed WDF rules are used to enforce *network segmentation* — e.g., blocking SMB (port 445) lateral movement between VLANs.
2. **Compliance Alignment**: PCI-DSS Requirement 1.2 mandates a firewall between wireless networks and cardholder data — WDF serves as the host-side enforcement.
3. **Server Protection**: On Windows Server, WDF rules frequently restrict RDP (port 3389) to specific admin subnets — a standard hardening practice.
4. **Incident Response**: During a ransomware outbreak, security teams rapidly add **outbound block rules** to known C2 IPs/domains to stop exfiltration while the incident is being remediated.
5. **DevSecOps**: WDF is scripted via PowerShell DSC and Azure Policy to ensure all Windows VMs in cloud subscriptions ship with a hardened baseline.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The WDF Rule Evaluation Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section walks through **exactly how a single network packet is evaluated** by the Windows Filtering Platform. This is a high-yield KTU question — both as theory and as a "trace the packet" problem.

### Step 1 — Packet Capture at NDIS Layer

A packet $p$ enters the NIC driver and is intercepted at the NDIS 6.x layer. The WFP filter engine receives:

$$
p \;=\; \big\langle \text{src\_IP}, \text{dst\_IP}, \text{src\_port}, \text{dst\_port}, \text{proto}, \text{tcp\_flags} \big\rangle
$$

The packet's *direction* is classified as **Inbound** if `dst_IP` is a local interface address, else **Outbound**.

### Step 2 — Profile Resolution

The OS queries the **Network Location Awareness (NLA) service** to determine the active profile:

$$
\text{Profile}(p) \;=\; \text{NLA}.\text{GetProfile}(\text{Interface}(p)) \;\in\; \{\text{Domain}, \text{Private}, \text{Public}\}
$$

Only rules bound to the active profile are considered — this prunes the rule set drastically.

### Step 3 — Filter Match Against the 5-Tuple

Each rule $R_i$ in the active profile's rule set is checked:

$$
\text{Match}(p, R_i) \;=\; \big( \text{dir}(R_i) = \text{dir}(p) \big) \;\land\; \big( \text{proto}(R_i) = \text{proto}(p) \big) \;\land\; \big( \text{port}(R_i) \supseteq \text{port}(p) \big) \;\land\; \big( \text{IP}(R_i) \supseteq \text{IP}(p) \big)
$$

The **first matching rule** (lowest `priority` numerical value) wins. This is the **"first-match semantics"** of WDF.

### Step 4 — Stateful Verification (For Allow Rules)

If the matched rule is `Allow`, WFP checks if the packet is part of an existing connection:

$$
\text{StatefulVerify}(p) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{Flow}(p) \in \text{ESTABLISHED} \cup \text{RELATED} \\ \text{TCP\_SYN} & \text{if } \text{Flow}(p) = \text{NEW} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{if } \text{Flow}(p) = \text{INVALID} \end{cases}
$$

If `StatefulVerify = FALSE`, the packet is **dropped silently** (no RST is sent — this prevents reconnaissance fingerprinting).

### Step 5 — Final Verdict

$$
\text{Verdict}(p) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{PERMIT} & \text{if } \text{Match} = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{Action}(R_i) = \text{Allow} \;\land\; \text{StatefulVerify} = \text{TRUE} \\ \text{BLOCK} & \text{if } \text{Match} = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{Action}(R_i) = \text{Block} \\ \text{BLOCK} & \text{if no rule matches the inbound default block policy} \\ \text{ALLOW} & \text{if no rule matches the outbound default allow policy} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 6 — Logging (If Enabled)

If logging is enabled, the verdict is written to `pfirewall.log`:

$$
\text{LogEntry} \;=\; \langle \text{date}, \text{time}, \text{action}, \text{proto}, \text{src\_ip}, \text{dst\_ip}, \text{src\_port}, \text{dst\_port}, \text{size}, \text{TCP\_flags}, \text{info} \rangle
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Trace a Packet

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: A packet arrives at a Windows 11 host on a **Public** Wi-Fi network from `203.0.113.45` (public IP), destination `192.168.1.50:3389`, protocol TCP, flags `SYN` (new connection attempt). The host has only the **default WDF policy** active. Trace the decision.

**Step 1**: Direction = **Inbound** (destination is local).

**Step 2**: Profile = **Public** (active network is untrusted).

**Step 3**: Profile = Public, direction = Inbound → rules bound to Public + Inbound are scanned. Default policy: Block all inbound. No custom Public + Inbound + TCP/3389 rule exists.

**Step 4**: Verdict = **BLOCK** (default block for inbound).

**Step 5**: WFP performs **stealth drop** — no SYN-ACK is sent, no RST is sent. The attacker's port scanner sees a **filtered** state (not "closed" which would be RST, and not "open" which would be SYN-ACK).

**Conclusion**: A 3389/RDP scan on a Public profile with default settings is invisible to the attacker — an excellent security outcome.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Rule Precedence Scenarios

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic **KTU 14-mark question style** — "Two rules conflict. Which one wins? Justify."

| Scenario | Rule A | Rule B | Winning Rule | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Local admin rule: Block TCP/22 (Public) | GPO rule: Allow TCP/22 (Public) | **Rule B (GPO)** | GPO > Local admin |
| 2 | Allow TCP/80 (Any profile) | Block TCP/80 (Domain) | **Rule B (Block)** | Same priority tier, **Block wins over Allow** by WFP convention |
| 3 | Allow TCP/443 (Public) | Allow TCP/443 (Private) | **Both** | They apply to different profiles — no conflict |
| 4 | Rule priority 100: Allow TCP/3389 | Rule priority 200: Block TCP/3389 | **Rule A** | Lower numerical priority value wins |
| 5 | Local rule: Allow (Domain) | Local rule: Block (Domain) — same priority | **Rule B (Block)** | Explicit Block overrides Allow at same priority |

---

## 3.4 PowerShell Configuration Walkthrough (Exhaustive)

> [!NOTE]
> PowerShell is the **canonical automation method** for WDF in modern Windows. KTU may ask you to write a script or interpret one.

### Task: Block all outbound traffic on TCP port 4444 (Metasploit default reverse-shell port) for the Public profile.

**Step 1 — Confirm the firewall is enabled on Public profile:**

```powershell
Get-NetFirewallProfile -Name Public | Select-Object Name, Enabled
```

Expected output: `Name = Public`, `Enabled = True`.

**Step 2 — Create the outbound block rule:**

```powershell
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Metasploit Reverse Shell 4444" `
    -Direction Outbound `
    -Action Block `
    -Protocol TCP `
    -LocalPort 4444 `
    -Profile Public `
    -Enabled True
```

**Step 3 — Verify the rule was created:**

```powershell
Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Metasploit Reverse Shell 4444" | Format-List DisplayName, Direction, Action, Profile
```

**Step 4 — Test the rule with a simulated outbound connection:**

```powershell
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 198.51.100.10 -Port 4444
```

**Step 5 — To remove the rule (rollback):**

```powershell
Remove-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Block Metasploit Reverse Shell 4444"
```

> [!TIP]
> **Common PowerShell Pitfall**: `-Profile` accepts a single value or an array. If omitted, the default is `Any` — meaning the rule applies to **all profiles**, which may inadvertently block legitimate traffic on trusted networks.

---

## 3.5 netsh advfirewall Equivalent (Legacy / Cross-Version)

```cmd
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Block Port 4444" dir=out action=block protocol=TCP localport=4444 profile=public
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: `netsh` is being deprecated by Microsoft in favor of PowerShell cmdlets. Use PowerShell for new scripts, but understand `netsh` for exam compatibility with older Windows versions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WDF Packet Processing Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Packet Arrives at NIC] --> B[NDIS Driver Intercepts]
    B --> C[WFP Kernel Filter Engine]
    C --> D[Profile Resolution via NLA Service]
    D --> E{Profile Match?}
    E -- No --> F[Default Verdict: Inbound BLOCK or Outbound ALLOW]
    E -- Yes --> G[5-Tuple Match Against Active Rules]
    G --> H{First-Match Rule Found?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I{Rule Action}
    I -- Block --> J[Silent Drop and Log Event]
    I -- Allow --> K[Stateful Inspection Check]
    K --> L{Connection State Valid?}
    L -- No --> J
    L -- Yes --> M[IPsec Verification If Required]
    M --> N{IPsec Authenticated?}
    N -- No --> J
    N -- Yes --> O[Permit Packet to TCP IP Stack]
    F --> P[Final Verdict Returned to NIC Driver]
    J --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Log Entry Written to pfirewall.log if Enabled]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Trace any packet from `A` (NIC arrival) to `Q` (logging). Every branch point is a KTU-relevant concept — profile resolution, rule matching, stateful inspection, and IPsec authentication.

---

## 4.2 Network Profile Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Connection Detected by OS] --> B{Is the PC joined to a Domain?}
    B -- Yes --> C{NLA can reach a Domain Controller?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Assign DOMAIN Profile]
    C -- No --> E[User Prompted: Home Work or Public]
    B -- No --> E
    E -- User Selects Yes --> F[Assign PRIVATE Profile]
    E -- User Selects No --> G[Assign PUBLIC Profile]
    D --> H[WDF Loads Domain Profile Ruleset]
    F --> I[WDF Loads Private Profile Ruleset]
    G --> J[WDF Loads Public Profile Ruleset]
    H --> K[Combined Ruleset Active for Interface]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.3 Rule Precedence Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Packet] --> B[Step 1: Check Connection Security Rules]
    B --> C[Step 2: Check IPsec Main Mode Policy]
    C --> D[Step 3: Check Group Policy Rules]
    D --> E[Step 4: Check Local Administrator Rules]
    E --> F[Step 5: Check Local Service Rules]
    F --> G[Step 6: Check Default Profile Policy]
    G --> H[Apply First Match and Generate Verdict]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Evaluation** (highest to lowest priority):
> 1. **Connection Security Rules** (IPsec)
> 2. **Group Policy-deployed firewall rules**
> 3. **Local administrator-defined rules**
> 4. **Local service-defined rules** (e.g., File and Printer Sharing)
> 5. **Default profile policy** (Block inbound / Allow outbound)

---

## 4.4 WDF Component Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KernelMode[Kernel Mode Layer]
        A1[Network Interface Card Driver] --> A2[NDIS Filter Driver]
        A2 --> A3[Windows Filtering Platform wfp sys]
        A3 --> A4[tcpip sys TCP IP Stack]
    end
    subgraph UserMode[User Mode Layer]
        B1[MpsSvc Service] --> B2[Windows Firewall Authorization Driver WFAD]
        B2 --> B3[Windows Security Center]
        B1 --> B4[Configuration Store netsh PowerShell GPO]
    end
    A3 <--> B1
    B4 --> A3
```

> [!TIP]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
