Explain its three operational modes.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux) is a Linux kernel security module that implements **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** through a mechanism of **security policies**. It was developed by the **NSA** and provides fine-grained access control beyond traditional UNIX Discretionary Access Control (DAC). **Three Operational Modes:** | Mode | Behavior | |------|----------| | **Enforcing** | SELinux actively enforces the policy. Any unauthorized access is **denied AND logged** to the audit subsystem. | | **Permissive** | SELinux does **NOT deny** any operation, but **logs all policy violations**. Used for debugging and policy development. | | **Disabled** | SELinux is completely **turned off**. The system reverts to standard UNIX DAC permissions only. This is **not recommended** for production. | The mode is controlled via the `SELINUX=` directive in `/etc/selinux/config` (permanent) or the `setenforce` command (runtime). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **What is a Security Context in SELinux? Explain its four components with an example.** **Model Answer:** A **Security Context** (also called a **security label**) is a string of four colon-separated fields attached to every subject (process) and object (file, socket, port) in SELinux. It is the **primary mechanism** used for access decisions. **Four Components of Security Context:** $$ \texttt{user : role : type : level} $$ | Field | Purpose | Example Value | |-------|---------|---------------| | **SELinux User** | Identity in SELinux (not Linux login user). Examples: `system_u`, `unconfined_u`, `user_u` | `system_u` | | **Role** | Defines which domains a user can transition into (RBAC component) | `object_r` | | **Type (Domain)** | The core of Type Enforcement. For processes = **domain**; for files = **type** | `httpd_sys_content_t` | | **Level** | MLS sensitivity level (e.g., `s0` = lowest, `s15` = highest) | `s0` | **Example of a complete security context:** ``` system_u:object_r:httpd_sys_content_t:s0 ``` This represents: An **SELinux user** `system_u` with role `object_r` accessing a **file of type** `httpd_sys_content_t` at MLS level `s0`. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between **Question A and Question B** of the same module. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of SELinux. Discuss the role of the **Linux Security Module (LSM) framework**, the **Access Vector Cache (AVC)**, and the **Policy Database** in enforcing security policies. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Type Enforcement (TE)** in SELinux with a suitable example. How does TE differ from traditional Discretionary Access Control (DAC)? --- #### Model Answer for Q.A (a): **SELinux Architecture:** SELinux is implemented as a **kernel security module** that integrates with the Linux Security Module (LSM) framework. The architecture consists of the following components: **1. Linux Security Module (LSM) Framework:** The LSM framework provides **hook points** throughout the kernel (in the VFS, network stack, process management) where security modules like SELinux can intercept operations. Examples of LSM hooks include `security_file_open()`, `security_socket_connect()`, and `security_task_create()`. When a process issues a system call, the corresponding LSM hook transfers control to the SELinux hook function. **2. SELinux Hook Functions:** These are SELinux-specific implementations registered with the LSM framework. For example, `selinux_file_open()` is invoked whenever a file is opened. These functions extract the security contexts of the subject and object, then query the AVC. **3. Access Vector Cache (AVC):** The AVC is a **high-speed in-memory cache** of access decisions. Checking the policy database for every operation would be prohibitively expensive. The AVC stores recent decisions, so the most common path is a fast cache lookup. On a cache miss, the full policy database is consulted and the result is cached. **4. Policy Database:** This is the binary representation of the security policy (typically `policy.31` for the targeted policy). It contains: - **Type Enforcement (TE) rules:** `allow` and `dontaudit` rules between types - **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) rules:** Role transition permissions - **MLS constraints:** Bell-LaPadula rules - **Boolean conditions:** Tunable switches **5. Security Context Storage:** Contexts are stored in **extended attributes** (`security.selinux` xattr) on filesystems, and in **task_struct** fields for processes. In-memory mappings are maintained by the kernel. **6. Audit Subsystem:** All access decisions, especially denials in Enforcing mode and violations in Permissive mode, are logged to `/var/log/audit/audit.log` with `type=AVC` records. Tools like `ausearch` and `sealert` interpret these logs. **Working Flow:** $$\text{Syscall} \rightarrow \text{LSM Hook} \rightarrow \text{SELinux Hook} \rightarrow \text{Context Extraction} \rightarrow \text{AVC Lookup} \rightarrow \text{Policy Check} \rightarrow \text{Decision} \rightarrow \text{Audit Log}$$ --- #### Model Answer for Q.A (b): **Type Enforcement (TE) in SELinux:** **Definition:** Type Enforcement is the **primary access control mechanism** in SELinux. It assigns a **type** to every object and a **domain** to every process, then enforces rules about which domains can access which types and with what permissions. **Key Principle: Default Deny** In TE, access is **denied by default**. An operation is permitted **only if there is an explicit `allow` rule** in the policy. **Structure of a TE Rule:** ``` allow source_type target_type : object_class { permissions }; ``` **Practical Example:** Consider a web server scenario: - Apache HTTPD process runs in domain: `httpd_t` - Web content files have type: `httpd_sys_content_t` - Apache log files have type: `httpd_log_t` The targeted policy contains the following rules: ``` allow httpd_t httpd_sys_content_t : file { read open getattr }; allow httpd_t httpd_log_t : file { write create append open getattr }; ``` **Interpretation:** - The `httpd` process **CAN** read/open web content files. - The `httpd` process **CAN** write to log files. - If `httpd` tries to read `/etc/shadow` (type `shadow_t`), there is **no rule** for `httpd_t → shadow_t`, so the access is **DENIED**, even if the file has `world-readable` DAC permissions. **Difference between TE and DAC:** | Aspect | DAC (Traditional Linux) | TE (SELinux) | |--------|------------------------|--------------| | **Policy Owner** | The **object owner** decides permissions (e.g., via `chmod`) | The **central policy** decides; owner cannot override | | **Default** | Permissive (owner can grant access easily) | **Deny by default** | | **Granularity** | Coarse (user/group/other, rwx) | Fine-grained (per-type, per-class, per-permission) | | **Privilege Escalation** | A compromised root process has full power | A compromised process is **confined to its domain** | | **Example** | `chmod 644 file.txt` | `allow httpd_t httpd_sys_content_t : file read;` | **Engineering Benefit:** If a web application is compromised via a vulnerability (e.g., SQL injection), the attacker cannot use the web server process to read sensitive files like `/etc/shadow` or other users' home directories. This **containment** is the core security value of SELinux. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE] **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **SELinux Policies** (Targeted, Strict, MLS). Which policy is the default in RHEL/CentOS systems, and why? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the various **SELinux administrative commands** used for: (i) checking status, (ii) managing file contexts, (iii) managing Booleans, and (iv) managing port labels. Give one example command for each. --- #### Model Answer for Q.B (a): **SELinux Policies:** A **policy** is the complete set of rules that SELinux enforces. There are three main policy types: **1. Targeted Policy (Default in RHEL/CentOS/Fedora):** - Only **selected (targeted) daemons** are confined to specific domains (~200 processes including `httpd`, `named`, `sshd`, `nfsd`, `snmpd`, `mysqld`). - All other processes run in the **unconfined domain** (`unconfined_t`), which has minimal restrictions (essentially similar to DAC). - **Advantage:** Easy to administer; existing applications work without modification. - **Use case:** General-purpose production servers. **2. Strict Policy:** - **EVERY** process and user is confined. - There is **no unconfined domain** — even login shells are restricted. - **Disadvantage:** Extremely high administration overhead; many applications break unless custom policy modules are written. - **Use case:** Highly secure environments where administration overhead is acceptable. **3. MLS (Multi-Level Security) Policy:** - Implements the **Bell-LaPadula model** with sensitivity labels (e.g., Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret). - Enforces **"No Read Up"** and **"No Write Down"** rules. - Designed for **government and military** environments. - **MCS (Multi-Category Security)** is a practical variant using categories (c0–c1023) instead of strict sensitivity levels, widely used for **virtualization (sVirt)** and **container isolation**. **Default Policy Comparison:** | Policy | Default In | Confinement Scope | Administration | |--------|------------|-------------------|----------------| | Targeted | RHEL, CentOS, Fedora | ~200 daemons | Low | | Strict | Rarely used | All processes | Very High | | MLS | RHEL MLS variant | All processes + levels | High | **Why Targeted is Default:** Targeted provides a **balanced trade-off** between security and usability. It confines the most commonly exploited services (web, DNS, mail, SSH) while leaving user applications unconfined, ensuring broad compatibility with existing software. Most real-world security breaches exploit daemon vulnerabilities, so confining these yields maximum benefit with minimal administrative pain. --- #### Model Answer for Q.B (b): **SELinux Administrative Commands:** **(i) Checking SELinux Status:** | Command | Purpose | |---------|---------| | `getenforce` | Displays current mode (Enforcing/Permissive/Disabled) | | `sestatus` | Detailed status including policy, mode, and policy version | | `sestatus -v` | Lists security contexts of files in `/etc` | **Example:** ```bash $ getenforce Enforcing $ sestatus SELinux status: enabled SELinuxfs mount: /sys/fs/selinux SELinux root directory: /etc/selinux Loaded policy name: targeted Current mode: enforcing Mode from config file: enforcing Policy MLS status: enabled Policy deny_unknown status: allowed ``` **(ii) Managing File Contexts:** | Command | Purpose | |---------|---------| | `ls -Z file` | View security context of a file | | `ps -eZ` | View security context of processes | | `chcon -t type file` | Temporarily change file context | | `restorecon -R /path` | Restore default context based on policy | | `semanage fcontext -a -t type 'pattern'` | Permanently add file context mapping | **Example:** If Apache cannot read a new web file: ```bash $ restorecon -R /var/www/html/ # OR permanently add a new web directory: $ semanage fcontext -a -t httpd_sys_content_t '/myweb(/.*)?' $ restorecon -R /myweb ``` **(iii) Managing Booleans:** | Command | Purpose | |---------|---------| | `getsebool -a` | List all booleans and their states | | `getsebool boolean_name` | Check specific boolean | | `setsebool boolean_name on/off` | Change boolean (runtime only) | | `setsebool -P boolean_name on` | Change boolean **permanently** | **Example:** Allow HTTPD to make network connections (e.g., for proxy): ```bash $ setsebool -P httpd_can_network_connect on ``` **(iv) Managing Port Labels:** | Command | Purpose | |---------|---------| | `semanage port -l` | List all port labels | | `semanage port -a -t type -p proto portnum` | Add a port label | **Example:** Allow Apache to listen on port 8080 (non-standard port): ```bash $ semanage port -a -t http_port_t -p tcp 8080 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing DAC and MAC:** Students often write that SELinux *replaces* DAC. **Correct answer:** SELinux *complements* DAC. Both checks must pass for access to be granted. > 2. **Forgetting Default Deny:** Always state that SELinux is **deny by default** — do not assume access is allowed. > 3. **Mixing MLS with MCS:** MLS uses sensitivity levels (s0–s15); MCS uses categories (c0–c1023). They are related but distinct. > 4. **Chcon vs Restorecon:** `chcon` is **temporary** (lost on relabel); `restorecon` uses **policy-defined defaults**. > 5. **Forgetting `-P` flag:** `setsebool` without `-P` does **not persist** across reboots. Examiners deduct marks for missing `-P`. > 6. **Confusing `user` and `type`:** SELinux **user** (`system_u`) ≠ Linux login user (`root`). The **type** (`httpd_t`) is what Type Enforcement uses. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — SELinux (Module 4)** - **Full Form:** Security-Enhanced Linux, developed by the **NSA**, merged into kernel in **2.6**. - **Core Principle:** **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** with **default-deny** policy. - **Three Modes:** **Enforcing** (blocks + logs), **Permissive** (logs only), **Disabled** (off — avoid). - **Configuration File:** `/etc/selinux/config` (`SELINUX=enforcing|permissive|disabled`). - **Security Context Format:** `user : role : type : level` (4 colon-separated fields). - **Type Enforcement (TE):** Core mechanism using **types** for files and **domains** for processes. Default deny. - **TE Rule Syntax:** `allow source_type target_type : class { permissions };` - **Policies:** **Targeted** (default, ~200 daemons), **Strict** (all confined), **MLS** (Bell-LaPadula). - **RBAC:** Roles control domain transitions (e.g., `unconfined_r` → `unconfined_t`). - **MLS:** Sensitivity levels enforce No-Read-Up / No-Write-Down (Bell-LaPadula). - **MCS:** Categories (c0–c1023) for sVirt / container isolation. - **Key Commands:** - `sestatus`, `getenforce` → status - `ls -Z`, `ps -eZ` → view contexts - `chcon` (temp) / `restorecon` (policy default) → set file context - `semanage fcontext` → permanent file context mapping - `getsebool -a` / `setsebool -P` → Booleans (remember `-P` for persistent) - `semanage port` → manage port labels - **Log Location:** `/var/log/audit/audit.log` (look for `type=AVC` entries). - **DAS vs SELinux:** Both checks must pass; SELinux is **in addition to**, not **instead of** DAC. - **Most Common Real Issue:** **Mislabeled files** (e.g., content created in user's home and moved to web root without `restorecon`). - **Containers & sVirt:** MCS is the foundation for VM/container isolation in QEMU, Docker, Podman. - **Booleans:** ~2000+ available; tunables allow policy adjustment without recompilation. - **DAC vs MAC Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):** DAC = owner decides; MAC = central policy decides; owner cannot override. - **Why SELinux matters:** **Containment** — a compromised process cannot escape its domain, drastically limiting damage from exploits.
